Authors: Zhang, W.; Chen, B.; Wu, X.; Jia, H.; Jiang, A.; Tang, Z.
Title: Integrated design of variable-geometric inward-turning inlet and waverider via accurate capture profile matching
Abstract: The integration of a waverider forebody and inward-turning inlet is a key configuration characteristic of combined-cycle air-breathing vehicles. Under the forebody’s influence, the inlet’s actual incoming flow parameters deviate from the designed values based on a uniform flowfield. Meanwhile, conventional variable-geometry inward-turning inlets modify the original streamline-tracking profile and alter the external compression stream-surface, thereby impairing flow capture and degrading the aerodynamic performance of the integrated design. This paper proposes an integrated design method for variable-geometric inward-turning inlets and waveriders with precise capture profile matching. By iteratively determining the inlet’s three-dimensional (3D) spatial capture profile position, parameters on this profile are matched to the specified designed values. Then the root mean square error (RMSE) of streamline deviation is introduced to quantify discrepancies between the streamline-tracking and target profiles, enabling adjustable design by directly constraining the profile while preserving the external compression stream-surface. The capture area and flow splitting device are tailored to engine requirements for wide-speed-range flow matching. Under viscous conditions, at the design point (Ma=7.0) of the integrated configuration designed via this method, the mass-flow-weighted average Mach number on the actual capture profile is 5.485, with a deviation of only 0.27 % from the designed incoming flow parameter (Ma=5.5), effectively improving design input accuracy. The RMSE of streamline deviation at the adjustable rotating shaft, denoted as RMSE(Δ d), is 0.717 mm, meeting the rotating shaft’s engineering design requirements via the local stream-surface cross-section. The inlet operates normally at Mach numbers 2.0 to 7.0, with well-organized internal flow and satisfied flow demand. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, J.; Zhang, C.; Zheng, C.; Bao, J.; Zheng, Z.
Author full names: Wang, Jun (58771111900); Zhang, Cheng (57199501811); Zheng, Chenming (57208860626); Bao, Jiayu (59223925200); Zheng, Zhangyao (60029981000)
Title: Fuzzy adaptive prescribed performance fault-tolerant control of hypersonic vehicles with multiple faults and input saturation
Abstract: In this paper, a fuzzy adaptive prescribed performance attitude fault-tolerant control method for hypersonic vehicles is proposed to address full-state constraints, actuator and sensor faults, actuator saturation, and external disturbances. A fuzzy neural network-based fault estimation algorithm is proposed to estimate sensor faults. A fuzzy neural network extended state observer is designed to estimate system states and disturbances, thereby alleviating the effects of sensor faults and external uncertainties. To address the state constraint problem, a new prescribed performance control method with unknown initial error is designed to ensure that the tracking error remains within the preset range. Furthermore, a new anti-saturation compensation mechanism is designed to mitigate the effects of actuator saturation. The stability of the system is demonstrated through Lyapunov stability analysis. Simulation results show that the proposed control scheme is capable of achieving attitude tracking of hypersonic vehicles under multiple failure scenarios, thereby providing a reliable control strategy for the safety of hypersonic vehicles. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Lemma, T.A.; Hashmi, M.B.; Salilew, W.M.; Ahsan, S.; M.
Author full names: Lemma, Tamiru Alemu (55143260600); Hashmi, Muhammad Baqir (57214804479); Salilew, Waleligne Molla (57876513900); Ahsan, Shazaib (57196439498)
Title: Transient performance and control strategy of three shaft variable geometry industrial gas turbines
Abstract: Industrial gas turbines are gaining substantial momentum in decentralized energy scenarios owing to wide range of benefits. In line with global measures to address climate change, industrial gas turbines are also integrated with intermittent renewable energy systems (solar and wind) as a hybrid power generation source. To respond to the fluctuating nature of renewables, gas turbines undergo frequent ramping, cycling and rapid load change operations. These transient regimes lead to several thermal, aerodynamic, and mechanical stresses that are very adverse for engine’s reliability, availability, overall health, and performance. To ensure resilient operation flexibility, a transient model of three shaft (GE LM 1600) variable geometry industrial gas turbine was developed using a commercially available tool GasTurb 12. To simulate load change phenomenon through acceleration and deceleration two kinds of fuel flow schedules were incorporated. Moreover, a variable inlet guide vane (VIGV) schedule and variable bleed schedule were also incorporated for low pressure compressor (LPC) to maintain stability and reliability. An optimization approach was employed to adapt the design point results with the OEM data. The design point and off design steady state results were compared with the already OEM performance data and showed good agreement. Additionally, the dynamic performance was also incorporated with a PID control scheme to assure a surge free compressor operation along with overall engine stability. It became evident from this research that, during transient operation, burner exit temperature depicts an overshoot while low pressure (LP) and high pressure (HP) spool speeds show a time delay that are deemed inappropriate as they can cause low cycle fatigue in hot parts such as combustor, HP turbine blades and casing. Hence, this transient model will help the future researchers in real time predictive condition monitoring and fault detection and diagnostics (FDD) models by developing advance reliability models. © 2026 The Author(s).

Authors: Wang, N.; Zhang, G.; Yu, S.C.M.
Author full names: Wang, Nannan (60016798300); Zhang, Guoqing (57192541290); Yu, Simon Ching Man (59652663400)
Title: Classification of unstart flow in a two-dimensional hypersonic inlet
Abstract: In this paper, attempts have been made to the classification of hard unstart flow in a two-dimensional hypersonic inlet based on the characteristics of mass flow capture ratio vs freestream Mach number and the corresponding flow field structures obtained by numerical simulations. It is found that hard unstart can be broadly classified into four categories: 1) cowl angle unstart (CAU), 2) upper separation unstart (USU), 3) throat compression unstart (TCU), and 4) throat expansion unstart (TEU). As the internal contraction ratio (ICR) increases, the mass flow capture ratio for CAU remains constant because the disturbance from throat is blocked by a fully supersonic region. USU can be regarded approximately as a special form of CAU with the influence from the separation bubble at upper wall. TEU is equivalent to introducing throat expansion wave effect to CAU by reducing ICR. TCU is dominated by throat high pressure with a large subsonic region at bottom wall as a medium for the pressure signal propagation. Different unstart categories exhibit distinct geometric sensitivities, with a particularly pronounced difference observed between CAU and TCU. Under large cowl angle, it is found that two novel flow regimes, namely the degenerated TCU and the quasi CAU, may arise due to system bifurcation. Finally, based on appropriate assumptions, empirical formulas for predicting the boundaries between different unstart categories are constructed. The prediction agrees well with the simulation results. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Samad, A.; Ullah, I.; Qasem, N.A.A.
Author full names: Samad, Abdallah (57203432241); Ullah, Izhar (57209106399); Qasem, Naef A.A. (55936151600)
Title: An experimental study to characterize the effect of winglet-like geometries on the performance of UAV propellers
Abstract: Blade tip geometries strongly influence the aerodynamic performance of small Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) propellers operating at low Reynolds numbers (Re), where tip-vortex-induced losses become significant. Inspired by the effectiveness of winglets on fixed-wing aircraft, this study experimentally investigates the aerodynamic impact of winglet-like tip geometries applied to UAV propellers. Seven configurations were tested, including a baseline propeller and propellers equipped with upward- and downward-facing winglets of varying heights (4–8 mm). Static thrust and torque measurements were conducted over a rotational speed range of 2000 to 5500 RPM, and performance was evaluated using nondimensional coefficients and figure of merit. In addition, 2D Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) measurements were employed to characterize near-wake flow behavior. The results show that a 4 mm upward-facing winglet produced an approximately 3% increase in thrust relative to the baseline propeller while maintaining comparable efficiency. PIV measurements indicate that this configuration weakened the tip vortex, reduced near-wake vorticity, and decreased the turbulent kinetic energy. In contrast, larger or downward-facing winglets introduced additional flow losses and degraded performance. The observed performance trends highlight the inherently nonlinear aerodynamic response of low -Re propellers, where small geometric modifications such as winglet-inspired tip geometries lead to non-proportional changes in thrust, efficiency, and wake dynamics. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jia, X.; Dong, X.; Xiang, J.; Hong, Y.; Zhang, Y.; Lu, X.
Title: Numerical study of transonic fan flutter and acoustic propagation characteristics at high-altitudes
Abstract: High-performance engines require fans to operate stably in a wide airspace. The study investigates a transonic fan through numerical simulations, conducting a series of steady and unsteady calculations to reveal the effect of high-altitude conditions on flutter. The traveling wave method based on the energy method was employed to compute unsteady pressure and aerodynamic damping. Under high-altitude conditions, radial flow on the pressure side intensifies, while leading-edge/secondary separation and reattachment occur on the suction side. The relative variation in aerodynamic damping correlates with the relative change in total pressure and exhibits acoustic mode dependence. Under upstream cut-on and downstream cut-off mode, out-of-phase variations in unsteady pressure within the radial flow region on the pressure side enhance the negative aerodynamic damping. Under different acoustic modes, the combined effect of changes in unsteady pressure amplitude and phase in the leading-edge/secondary separation and reattachment regions on the suction side has a minor impact on aerodynamic damping variations. The study reveals that the sensitivity of unsteady pressure on the pressure side to acoustic modes is the key mechanism responsible for the acoustic mode dependence of flutter stability with varying altitudes. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhou, G.; T.; M.; Wu, Z.; Wang, L.; Zhang, Y.; Tan, H.
Title: Parametric analysis of the propeller interference characteristics for a branched turboprop inlet
Abstract: Turboprop aircraft constitute a critical component in both civil and military transport fields. Central to their operation, the propeller has a complex effect on engine inlet performance. In this paper, the propeller interference characteristics for a full-sized turboprop inlet were investigated by using the overset mesh technique and solving Unsteady Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (URANS) equations. The overset mesh approach handles the relative motion between the rotating propeller and the stationary inlet through overlapping mesh zones and data interpolation. The influences of freestream air speed, Aerodynamic Interface Plane (AIP, located between the inlet and the engine compressor, its aerodynamic performance determines the quality of air entering the engine) Mach number, angle of attack, and propeller speed are comprehensively discussed. The results showed that both the time-averaged total pressure recovery coefficient (σ¯) and the time-averaged total pressure distortion index (|DC60|‾) at the AIP surface of the inlet increased in the presence of the propeller compared to their absence. Furthermore, due to the presence of the propeller, the variation law for the aerodynamic performance of the inlet with the internal and external flow parameters differs from that observed in the absence of a propeller. As the freestream Mach number increases from 0.15 to 0.25, the σ¯ decreases by 0.16% and |DC60|‾ increases by 1.14 times without a propeller. Whereas when the propeller is present, the σ¯ decreases by 0.73% and |DC60|‾ decreases by 11.52%. Numerical data presented here provide methodological and data support for the development of high-performance branched turboprop engine inlets. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Higginbotham, E.C.; Bettinger, R.A.; Webb, E.L.
Author full names: Higginbotham, Erica C. (59230589800); Bettinger, Robert A. (36865935200); Webb, Emma L. (59186921900)
Title: Preliminary 3- and 6-Degree-of-Freedom investigation of aerobraking to support cislunar operations
Abstract: The first aerobraking experiment occurred in 1991 as part of the Hiten satellite’s cislunar survey mission. Although making only two trans-atmospheric passes to reduce its apogee altitude, the success of Hiten’s experiment spurred the implementation of aerobraking by Magellan in 1993 and subsequent Mars missions starting in the late 1990s. This paper investigates the Hiten aerobraking experiment from the perspective of both 3- and 6-degree-of-freedom analysis with the inclusion J <inf>4</inf> gravitational and lunisolar perturbations in order to understand the complexity and sensitivity of aerobraking maneuvers in Earth’s atmosphere. Alternative satellites of varying size are also studied to determine their capability of maintaining the two Hiten aerobraking flight profiles. So as to examine a wide range of vehicle mass and drag reference area options, the Magellan and Hubble Space Telescope (HST) satellites are modeled and their aerobraking performance analyzed with varying target perigee altitudes for hypothetical cases of cislunar orbital operations in the Earth-Moon system. 3DOF and 6DOF analysis yields minimal deviation when reconstructing the historical Hiten aerobraking maneuvers with the Hiten and Magellan models. However, larger vehicles in the mass and size class as HST require a shallower depth of perigee transit in order to achieve target post-pass apogee altitudes due to greater drag and associated losses in orbital energy during aerobraking. Overall, aerobraking is posited as a viable cislunar maneuver option for adjusting semi-major axis if the satellite performing the maneuver is capable of surviving deceleration loads of approximately 9 g’s and countering aerodynamic torques in the range of 2–11 N · m depending on vehicle size and initial attitude orientation. © 2026 .

Authors: Liu, H.; Xiao, H.; Wang, C.; Zhao, R.; Yang, G.; Guo, H.; Liu, R.; Deng, Z.
Author full names: Liu, Hualiang (59155580000); Xiao, Hong (57220866877); Wang, Chunlong (57200387463); Zhao, Runchao (57221869977); Yang, Guang (58597539400); Guo, Hongwei (59661645500); Liu, Rongqiang (7404552019); Deng, Zongquan (55536964400)
Title: Bio-inspired design and analysis of multidimensional morphing linkage for aircraft forebody
Abstract: Cross-domain vehicles are critical roles for the next generation of aerospace transportation systems. Morphing forebody technologies offer clear advantages in enhancing performance across multiple flight domains, wide speed ranges, and maneuvering flight conditions. Therefore, the bio-inspired design of morphing forebody aircraft holds breakthrough significance. This paper presents a design method for bio-inspired multidimensional morphing ( BMM ) linkage, inspired by Cavernularia obesa . The BMM linkage is composed of the dual-slider six-bar morphing ( DSSBM ) linkages, a 12- SS transmission linkage, and a 6- UPS actuated linkage. The forward and inverse kinematic of the BMM linkage are systematically analyzed. A novel inverse kinematic method for the 12- SS linkage is proposed by incorporating P joints to restructure a 12- PSS linkage to solves its forward kinematics. This method can solve the locally unique pose for the moving platform by determining whether the Jacobian matrix of the linkage is full rank. A pose selection method for the moving platform is proposed to achieve various morphing types. The application of the linkage in an aircraft morphing forebody is explored, and suitable flight profiles are identified based on the aerodynamic benefits of various morphing types. A morphing forebody prototype is developed and tested. For four morphing types, the morphing error in the Y -direction remains below 0.5%. The maximum vertical morphing ratio exceeds 20%, validating both the accuracy of the theoretical analysis and the feasibility of achieving multidimensional morphing in the aircraft forebody. The BMM linkage design and analysis method proposed in this paper can achieve multidimensional concave or convex morphing of the aircraft forebody. This study provides theoretical support for the development of morphing forebody technologies in aerospace vehicles. © © 2026. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Mecklem, S.A.; Curran, D.; Landsberg, W.O.; Veeraragavan, A.
Author full names: Mecklem, Sarah A. (57205587007); Curran, Damian R. (57204548528); Landsberg, Will O. (57140548700); Veeraragavan, Ananthanarayanan (16644293400)
Title: Evaluation of a distributed fuelling scheme for a Mach 8 axisymmetric scramjet using large eddy simulation
Abstract: This paper examines supersonic turbulent combustion in an ethylene-fuelled Mach 8 axisymmetric scramjet using US3D with wall-modelled large eddy simulation (LES). A tandem cavity scramjet combustor with distributed fuelling was compared against a single cavity design with one fuelling location, which also served as a validation study. LES successfully replicated experimentally observed combustion and extended beyond the experimental test window to reveal the potential of domain unstart. Each LES was initialised from Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) solutions using turbulent Schmidt numbers ( Sc<inf>t</inf> ) of either 0.3 or 0.75. While Sc<inf>t</inf>=0.30 produced a steady-state RANS solution matching experimental ‘steady’ pressure profiles, only the Sc<inf>t</inf>=0.75 LES captured the experimentally observed transient combustion behaviour resulting in domain unstart. These Sc<inf>t</inf> -varied initialisations were applied to the tandem cavity domains which found the Sc<inf>t</inf>=0.3 domain produced robust scram-mode combustion, while Sc<inf>t</inf>=0.75 yielded marginal ignition behaviour. Combustion regime and Damköhler number analyses indicated that an ignition aid could enable the validated Sc<inf>t</inf>=0.75 condition to evolve and replicate the Sc<inf>t</inf>=0.3 case. LES demonstrated that distributed fuelling in tandem cavities can achieve stable, scram-mode combustion with reduced susceptibility to thermal choking and scramjet unstart. Although the ignition-supported LES predicted lower combustion efficiency (40%) than RANS (65%), the design’s distributed heat release supports improved flow stability and operational resilience. © 2026 The Authors.

Authors: X.; Xu, Y.; Gao, C.
Title: Effects of stall pattern and intensity on the single-degree-of-freedom aeroelastic behavior of airfoils
Abstract: At high angles of attack (AOAs), stall produces unsteady, nonlinear aerodynamic force. Such force can trigger stall flutter and large structural oscillations, endangering the performance and safety of structure. Although airfoils of different thickness exhibit distinct stall patterns and intensities depending on the angle of attack, their specific effects on stall flutter dynamics remain inadequately understood. This study investigates the aeroelastic response of a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) pitching oscillator for a leading-edge-stall thin airfoil (NACA0012) and a trailing-edge-stall thick airfoil (DU 91-W2-250) using two-dimensional CFD-CSD coupled simulations and spectral analysis. In this study, the AOAs are categorized into three distinct regimes: linear, light stall and deep stall, based on variations in airfoil aerodynamic characteristics, thereby representing different levels of stall intensity. The simulation results indicate that the thin, leading-edge-stall airfoil is prone to structural instability in the light-stall regime due to the emergence of a low-frequency flow mode. However, the thick, trailing-edge-stall airfoil is more likely to exhibit structural instability in the deep-stall regime. A concise criterion from a quasi-steady aerodynamic model, dC<inf>m</inf>dα|<inf><inf>α</inf><inf>=</inf>α<inf>0</inf></inf><0, predicts instability regions well across the test cases, and remains effective even in the deep stall regime. The finding implies that time-averaged flow quantities remain a key factor controlling coupled-response stability in stall flutter. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xie, Q.; Huang, H.; C.; Yu, P.
Title: Oscillation characteristics of the Mach disk and wall boundary flow on extremely underexpanded supersonic impinging jets
Abstract: Extremely underexpanded impinging jets, prevalent in scenarios like rocket launches and nuclear reactor leaks, exhibit complex flow dynamics with significant implications for structural impact and noise generation. This study examines the influence of nozzle pressure ratio (NPR) on the flow and oscillation characteristics of such jets using high-fidelity numerical simulations. Simulations reveal that increasing NPR (from 5 to 15) significantly weakens recirculation within the stagnation region, with suppression intensifying at higher NPRs. At NPR = 15, high-frequency oscillations, exceeding typical Mach disk frequencies, emerge in the stagnation region. Additionally, higher NPRs shift the peak wall friction force away from the impingement center, while reducing its amplitude and diminishing the contributions of boundary layer turbulence. However, turbulent kinetic energy increases with NPR, amplifying flow instability. Spectral Proper Orthogonal Decomposition and flow field reconstruction identify the dominant oscillation mechanism as a coupled interaction between the Mach disk and the wall jet, characterized by an oscillating wall jet driven by shock wave-boundary layer interactions. These findings elucidate the role of NPR in modulating jet dynamics and provide a theoretical foundation for assessing structural impacts and noise generation in high-pressure jet applications. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, Z.; Li, Z.; Wang, M.; Wu, W.
Title: Unstable flow mechanisms guiding early warning in axial compressors: A mechanism-feature-model framework for enhanced robustness
Abstract: Axial compressor early stall warning techniques are essential for expanding stable operating ranges through active control. The trend toward high-loading designs introduces complex tip flow instability, posing significant challenges to the early stall warning techniques. At present, early stall warning techniques integrated with data-driven algorithms exhibit strong potential. However, the research bias toward data-driven approaches results in insufficient support from physical mechanisms. It is necessary to establish a link and framework between mechanism research and early stall warning techniques to support the subsequent research. To address this gap, this review adopts a "unstable flow mechanism-feature extraction-discriminative model" logical framework, dividing early stall warning techniques into feature extraction and discriminative models. It establishes links between mechanism research and warning techniques, systematically summarizing previous studies on origin of flow instabilities, and warning techniques. The relationship between stall triggering and unstable tip flow is summarized in detail, with the unstable tip flow classified according to dominant factors, including tip leakage flow, suction surface separation, and rotating instability. Feature extraction methods encompass those targeting disturbances directly involved in stall, time-frequency domain-based signal analysis techniques, and data-driven approaches. Discriminative models include threshold-based and data-driven methods. Finally, the applicability of different methods and underlying flow mechanisms is discussed, along with the potential and prospects for integrating unstable mechanisms to guide more robust warning techniques within this framework. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Fu, L.; Zhai, Z.; Chen, H.; Liu, J.; Sun, R.; Ma, M.
Title: Koopman theory assisted transfer learning for anomaly detection of liquid rocket engines in frequency domain
Abstract: As the foundation for various fault diagnosis work, Anomaly Detection plays an important role in ensuring the safety and reliability of various complex systems, Liquid Rocket Engine, for example. However, real-world signals exhibit intrinsic non-stationary characteristics, due to the inherent noise present in actual work situations, resulting in a significant challenge for time-series based anomaly detection. On the other hand, the scarcity of actual working data for Liquid Rocket Engine also brings certain challenges. In this study, a novel Furior-Koopman based neural Network (FKN) is proposed for time-series anomaly detection of Liquid Rocket Engine. The proposed Furior-Koopman models time-series data in the frequency domain via Koopman operator, which has significant advantages in reducing noise interference. A transfer learning method from simulated data to actual data is also applied to handle the problem of insufficient actual data quantity. In order to meet the demand for anomaly detection of liquid rocket engine, we have established a dataset for anomaly detection of rocket engines and several experiments are conducted on established dataset. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms several recent methods. Several experiments are also conducted on common datasets to show the effectiveness of proposed method. All data and code realted can be found at https://github.com/minussix/FKN_for_LRE . © © 2026. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kuhn, L.; Meindl, M.; Bentheimer, C.; Maerz, M.
Author full names: Kuhn, Leonard Ferdinand Ulrich (60071530800); Meindl, Markus (58119783500); Bentheimer, Christian (57216036545); Maerz, Martin (8379907900)
Title: Hardware-based electric drive train fault detection unit enabling fail-operational capabilities for aviation applications
Abstract: The aircraft electrification process imposes safety requirements to ensure the reliability and availability of the electric drive train. Integrating multiple three-phase structures in the motor and inverter is a possible solution to meet these prerequisites. A critical condition to successfully implement such multiple three-phase structures is the ability to isolate faulty subsystems effectively from the overall system. The usage of traditional melting fuses is limited due to possible low short-circuit currents, as they cannot guarantee reliable isolation in such a case. A solution is the use of an active fuse in the DC link of the drive train. For the actuation of such an active fuse, a system is required that detects possible faults in the electrical drive train. This leads to a hardware-based electric fault detection unit (FDU). Therefore, this work aims to develop and simulative evaluate a redundant hardware-based FDU for an active fuse, in this case, a pyrofuse. A new solution that closed the gap in large scale electric propalsion aircrafts fault management concepts. © 2026 The Authors.

Authors: Bai, R.; Song, H.; Zhang, J.; Yan, C.
Title: Mechanistic insights into rotational curvature correction for turbulence modeling in high angles of attack separated flows
Abstract: Accurately simulating rotational and curvature effects remains a central challenge in turbulence modeling. This study investigates the effect of rotational curvature correction on the SST-DDES method in high angles of attack separated flow over the NACA4412 airfoil and its underlying mechanism. We reveal a novel physical mechanism whereby the rotational and curvature correction suppresses vortex stretching-particularly in streamwise and spanwise directions-via the curvature-sensitivity function F <inf> r 1</inf>, leading to reduced enstrophy. Key consequences include decreased vorticity growth, lower turbulent kinetic energy, and inhibited dissipation. Consequently, non-physical dissipation in the vortex core is significantly reduced, yielding sharper vortical structures and more accurate unsteady flow evolution. The identified chain of mechanisms-suppressed stretching, reduced enstrophy, and rebalanced production/dissipation-provides profound insight into curvature-sensitive flows. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, X.; Wu, Y.; Zhang, Z.; Wang, D.; Liu, C.
Title: Vortex breakdown suppression in a compressor cascade using an l-shaped groove via flow deflection
Abstract: Flow blockage arising from tip leakage vortex breakdown is a crucial factor in effecting the performance and stability of compressors. Based on the concept of flow deflection, an L-shaped groove—comprising both pitchwise and streamwise components—is proposed to mitigate this issue in a compressor cascade, which has the phenomenon of tip leakage vortex breakdown verified by experiments. The flow control effect of L-shaped groove is concentrated within the major loss region of tip leakage vortex breakdown. Compared to the baseline configuration, the optimized design achieves a 36.5% reduction in flow loss and an 82% elimination of the flow blockage. The flow control mechanism of an L-shaped groove is attributed to an entrainment-deflection effect. The endwall boundary layer is entrained into the pitchwise groove and subsequently redirected into the streamwise groove, ensuring that the redirected flow adheres to the suction surface, thereby suppressing the tip leakage vortex breakdown. Subsequently, the effects of six groove design parameters are investigated individually and a design guideline is established. For the groove width, a convergent cross-section is recommended to accelerate the redirected flow and enhance the boundary layer entrainment. Regarding the groove length, an appropriate pitchwise length is essential, while the streamwise length should approximate the axial chord length. Both the pitchwise location and the groove depth significantly influence the control effectiveness. The pitchwise location should be close to the suction surface for a better suppression of tip leakage vortex breakdown. A deeper groove provides a better flow capacity, thereby reducing the flow blockage. Further analysis of tip vortex dynamics confirms that the redirected groove flow effectively confines the high-vorticity scraping vortex to the area near suction surface, and both of them jointly suppress the breakdown of tip leakage vortex. © © 2026. Authors: Wei, X.; Yang, C.; Shi, H.; Yang, J.
Title: Design and loss mechanisms analysis of supersonic turbine in turbopump application
Abstract: As the demand for high thrust-to-weight ratios in rocket engines increases, improving the performance of supersonic turbines in turbopumps has become a critical technical challenge. However, there is currently no effective methodology for designing the supersonic stator blade profile. To address this gap, the study proposes a novel supersonic stator design method based on the method of characteristics. This method generates expansion contours by solving the governing flow equations and integrating them into parameterized blade profiles. This enables efficient and precise generation of blade geometries that meet Mach number specifications and structural constraints. In a fuel turbopump case study, three-dimensional numerical simulations demonstrate that the designed supersonic stator provides circumferentially uniform flow to the rotor inlet, resulting in a 25 % increase in efficiency across the entire tip clearance range. Flow field analysis at the design point reveals that the stator design significantly reduces exit shock losses, with wake losses and endwall boundary layer friction losses as the dominant contributors. Additionally, tip leakage vortices and upper/lower passage vortices in the rotor occupy nearly the entire flow path at the turbine outlet, contributing to 76 % of total turbine losses. Off-design flow field analysis indicates that increasing rotational speed raises the critical pressure ratio, whereas reducing the pressure ratio amplifies adverse pressure gradients in the passage, significantly enlarging separation zones. The proposed parameterized supersonic stator design methodology enables the development of lightweight, high-performance turbopump turbines while providing valuable blade profile samples for subsequent aerodynamic optimization. © © 2026. Authors: Lungu, M.; GRIGORIE, T.L.; Butu, F.A.; Chen, M.; Flores, G.
Author full names: Lungu, Mihai Aureliu (35778476100); GRIGORIE, Teodor Lucian (14019389000); Butu, Florentin Alin (57192541609); Chen, Mou (55595837400); Flores, Gerardo Ramon (49361581500)
Title: Integrated software and hardware platform for the guidance of the rockets with backstepping controller and strap-down inertial navigation system
Abstract: This work presents a novel technological hardware and software platform, with applications in the monitoring, guidance, and control of suborbital rockets. The software and hardware platform has two experimental components: 1) a rocket virtual model (RVM), implemented as a Java software simulator in a dedicated computer, together with a reconfigurable software for simulating the disturbances induced by the inertial sensors instrumenting the space vehicle, as well as a configuration, execution, and visualization interface for modifying the “thrust” profiles and choosing the type and the characteristics of the rocket; 2) a Raspberry Pi Single-Board Computer (RP-SBC), equipped with onboard I/O hardware, and dedicated software, that implements a backstepping controller and a strap-down inertial navigation system (SD-INS) using an inertial navigation algorithm based on the quaternion algebra and a dedicated numerical integration method which minimizes the commutation errors. The rocket dynamics is enriched with disturbances using Orekit (Java space dynamics library enabling the design of numerical/semi-analytic propagators for space vehicles). The validation is done in two steps: i) Model-in-the-loop (MIL) – the rocket simulator, the backstepping controller, and the SD-INS run on the same computer; ii) Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) – the controller and the SD-INS are embedded in an RP-SBC communicating with the RVM over TCP/IP. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kang, J.; Song, F.; Wu, Y.; Li, H.; Chen, Q.; Qiu, H.; Zhang, D.; Chen, X.
Title: Research on the upstream chamber pressure and intake blockage characteristics of the intake-adjustable rotating detonation combustor
Abstract: To enable the rotating detonation combustor (RDC) applied to a turbine-based continuous detonation engine (TBCD) to operate continuously and stably under variable complex inflow conditions across a wide flight envelope and multiple operational states, an intake-adjustable rotating detonation combustor (IARDC) with a continuously variable throat geometry was designed and developed. This study systematically investigated the variations in combustor operational parameters, detonation wave propagation characteristics, upstream chamber pressure behavior, and intake blockage characteristics during both fixed-geometry operation at different throat widths and the continuous throat adjustment process of the IARDC. The results indicate that in the intake-adjustable configuration, since the combustor initiates detonation at a high inlet total pressure condition (A₈/A₃.₁ = 2.80) and then transitions to a lower total pressure inflow (A₈/A₃.₁ = 1.49), it effectively suppresses the longitudinal pulsed detonation (LPD) mode induced by pressure overshoot during the ignition transient. This effectively broadens the fuel-rich detonable limit of the combustor. Regarding the upstream chamber pressure characteristics, increasing the intake throat area reduces the upstream chamber pressure. However, because changes in the throat area affect the intake blockage ratio, the upstream chamber pressure exhibits a non-linear variation trend with throat area adjustments. After the throat adjustment ceases, the upstream cavity pressure in the intake-adjustable configuration equals that of the fixed-geometry configuration. This indicates that the intake adjustment process does not affect the working state of the combustor after the adjustment is terminated. Nevertheless, during the throat adjustment process, the thermal environment within the combustor cannot change instantaneously in sync with the mechanical throat variation. This leads to a delayed response in detonation wave strength adjustment, causing the upstream chamber pressure and intake blockage ratio during the transition to be higher than those observed in the corresponding fixed-geometry condition at the same throat area. In summary, this work proposes a novel RDC structure suitable for complex inflow conditions and explores the operational characteristics of an intake-adjustable RDC. This research is of great significance for expanding the flight envelope of turbine-based continuous detonation engines and promoting the engineering application of rotating detonation propulsion technology. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chandrasekaran, R.; Hodges, D.H.
Title: Resonance elimination for a variable speed helicopter rotor blade using a moving-mass system
Abstract: Variable speed rotors (VSR) provide several advantages with regard to performance improvements, noise reduction, and high-speed capabilities for a helicopter. Hence, the feasibility of using a variable speed rotor system has been a topic of interest for helicopter design engineers. Several vertical flight vehicles in the Urban Air Mobility landscape also include variable speed rotors in their architecture. The primary challenge of a VSR system is its vibration issues, especially when the rotor operates close to or passes through a resonance region. Detailed studies on the characterization of these resonance vibrations have been performed previously. In this study, we explore the concept of an anti-resonance system based on a moving mass that actively changes the resonance points of the blade. The moving mass is located inside the rotor blade and is moved along the span. Thus, changing the blade’s inertial properties and its natural frequencies. The rotor model is built in DYMORE, a multi-body dynamics simulation tool. The rotor blades are modeled as one-dimensional beams using the geometrically exact beam theory (GEBT). Peters et al’s unsteady aerodynamics model with finite-state inflow and dynamic stall are used to obtain the aerodynamic loads acting on the rotor. The moving mass is modeled as a point mass and slid along the beam using a prismatic joint. The mass was chosen to be 25% of the total weight of the blade to have a desired range of natural frequencies movement. For most of the cases in our study, the moving mass system was able to move the resonance points out of the rotor operating range and thus, eliminating the resonance vibrations. Some of the unfavorable cases are also discussed. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Pu, Q.; Yang, L.; Li, T.
Title: Dynamic task allocation for multi-UAVs in disaster scenarios: A novel two-level optimization method with forecasting
Abstract: In disaster relief operations, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) play a critical role in tasks such as search and rescue, material delivery, and monitoring, significantly improving rescue efficiency. However, the large-scale, dynamic, and diverse nature of these tasks poses substantial challenges to task allocation, as traditional methods often suffer from limited accuracy and slow response times. This study proposes a novel two-level task allocation mechanism that integrates a three-dimensional self-organizing map (3D-SOM) for initial task clustering and an auction-based optimization algorithm for refined allocation. Additionally, this research introduces a radial basis function multi-kernel extreme learning machine (RBF-MKELM), which achieves high-precision prediction of supply and demand changes even with limited data. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms nine benchmark algorithms, achieving an average performance improvement of 34.5% in single-type task scenarios and 33.9% in multi-type task scenarios. Compared to five other time-series forecasting models, the RBF-MKELM model improves prediction accuracy by 48.6%, highlighting its robustness in data-scarce environments. Simulation validations confirm the framework’s effectiveness in dynamic task reallocation for large-scale disaster scenarios, providing a scalable and practical solution for UAV swarm operations. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Bolker, A.; Gouzman, I.; Tordjman, M.; Atar, N.; Riggs, B.E.; Carmiel, Y.; Verker, R.; Grossman, E.; Kalish, R.; Minton, T.K.
Author full names: Bolker, Asaf (25627362700); Gouzman, Iirina (7003303903); Tordjman, Moshe (55211125800); Atar, Nurit (55615584500); Riggs, Brian E. (58988161300); Carmiel, Yacov (56150700600); Verker, Ronen (15752258400); Grossman, Eitan (15750694900); Kalish, Rafael (57205842992); Minton, Timothy K. (7004677249)
Title: Real-time atomic oxygen detection using transition metal oxide coated hydrogen-terminated diamond surface
Abstract: Atomic oxygen (AO) is a major cause for the deterioration of spacecraft materials, such as polymers, composites and optical coatings, in low Earth orbit (LEO). AO exposure can degrade thermal, mechanical, or optical system performance, potentially leading to premature mission failure. Future missions, especially for remote sensing, are designed for very low Earth orbit (VLEO) where the variations of thermospheric density and AO flux are more significant than in higher orbits. Therefore, accurate real-time assessment of the AO fluence impinging upon spacecraft surfaces becomes a crucial issue for mission success, as well as for improving current thermospheric density models. We present a compact, solid-state sensor with high sensitivity and fast response to AO. The sensor is based on two semiconductor components that exhibit unique electrical properties when assembled together: hydrogenated diamond substrate and transition-metal oxide (TMO) coating. The Diamond:H-Transitional Metal Oxide AO sensor (DiMO) was characterized using RF plasma-based and laser detonation AO facilities. Tungsten oxide, WO<inf>3</inf>, with thickness ranging from 6 nm to 30 nm was used as TMO coating of choice. The results showed a linear increase in electrical resistance as a function of AO fluence of up to 2 × 1020 O-atoms∙cm-2, as tested in a laser detonation AO beam facility. The sensitivity of the sensor was found to be tunable, ranging from 10–14 to 10–15 Ω∙O-atoms-1∙cm2, and inversely dependent on the coating thickness. This work demonstrates the potential usage of diamond-based devices for real-time AO flux monitoring. Furthermore, compact dimensions and minimal power consumption of the DiMO sensor make it an ideal low-cost solution for the emerging "new-space" era, including nanosatellites applications. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hu, N.; Liu, H.; Gao, P.; Gao, H.; Qu, Y.; Meng, G.
Title: Nonlinear flutter of tandem curved panels subject to supersonic flows
Abstract: Tandem structures in supersonic flow present unique aeroelastic behaviors that remain poorly understood. To address this gap, particularly the lack of knowledge on curved panels in tandem, this paper develops a coupled partitioned fluid-structure interaction model for predicting nonlinear aeroelastic flutter behaviors of tandem curved panels exposed to supersonic flow. The numerical model integrates the compressible Euler equations solved using a finite volume method in an Arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian framework, with nonlinear dynamics equations of large deformable curved beam computed by a finite element method. To accurately describe the nonlinear deformation of the curved panel, a higher-order shear deformable beam theory is employed in conjunction with the von Kármán strain assumptions. The numerical model is validated by comparing the present results with reference data available in literature, encompassing both isolated and tandem-positioned panels. Typical nonlinear aeroelastic phenomena of tandem curved panels in supersonic flow are examined, including frequency synchronization, period-doubling bifurcations, quasi-periodicity and chaotic oscillations. A dynamic mode decomposition-based analysis reveals how upstream wake modes contribute to the onset of frequency synchronization of the downstream panel. This synchronization occurs under specific combinations of stiffness and thickness ratios. As the thickness ratio increases beyond a critical threshold, the downstream panel desynchronizes and exhibits beat-type oscillations, even though the upstream wake continues to promote earlier flutter onset. This work thus provides the first systematic insight into the interplay of curvature and wake effects in tandem flutters, significantly extending the predictive capabilities beyond traditional isolated or flat-panel models and offering valuable tools for the practical design of advanced aerospace structures. © © 2026. Authors: Xiang, J.; Zhang, X.; Jia, X.; Sheng, X.; Dong, X.; Lu, X.; Zhang, Y.
Author full names: Xiang, Jiahui (60227646900); Zhang, Xiaodan (59672248900); Jia, Xingbiao (60018769100); Sheng, Xiaoying (58547465300); Dong, Xu (57212727007); Lu, Xingen (59939737800); Zhang, Yanfeng (57214254845)
Title: Discrete adjoint-based optimization and analysis of a tandem axial supersonic through-flow fan cascades
Abstract: With continuous advancements in aeroengines, the stage pressure ratio of fan blades must be continuously increased, while also ensuring stable operation under supersonic conditions. Therefore, this study selects a tandem supersonic through-flow fan (STFF) as the research object, where the blade design variables are parameterized using the free-form deformation (FFD) method, and optimization is performed via the discrete adjoint method. In the unconstrained optimization case, the total pressure loss coefficient is reduced by up to 13.5 %. When constraints on the mass flow rate and outlet flow angle are applied, the difference in the outlet flow angle decreases to 0.47°, whereas the total pressure loss coefficient is reduced by 9.87 %. The reduction in total pressure loss mainly results from alterations in the shock structures and shock angles within the flow field, which weaken shock–boundary–layer interactions and reduce shock-induced losses. Differences in the exit flow angle are attributed primarily to the deflection of the trailing-edge shock, resulting in changes in flow turning. Overall, the present results indicate that adjoint-based optimization provides an effective and physically interpretable approach for controlling shock structures and flow deflection in tandem supersonic cascades, guiding the aerodynamic design of STFFs with increased efficiency, robustness, and interstage matching. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Samuels, K.E.; Baccarella, D.
Title: Nonequilibrium analysis of emission spectra in the plenum chamber of an arcjet wind tunnel
Abstract: This work presents spectroscopic measurements of nonequilibrium molecular emission from the plenum chamber of a Mach 6 arc-heated wind tunnel operating with pure nitrogen (N<inf>2</inf>) at three distinct enthalpy levels, 2.4, 4.0, and 7.3 MJ/kg. For each condition, high-resolution spectra in the 275–475 nm range were assembled by concatenating five sequential spectral acquisitions from single tunnel runs. Within this range, emissions from the second positive system of N<inf>2</inf> (C3Π<inf> u </inf> → B3Π<inf> g </inf>) and the first negative system of N<inf>2</inf>+ (B2Σ<inf>u</inf>+→X2Σ<inf>g</inf>+) were captured with high signal-to-noise ratio, allowing for detailed resolution of rotational and vibrational features. Spectral fitting was performed over the full wavelength range using the SPARK line-by-line radiation code and a custom fitting algorithm, based on a two-temperature nonequilibrium model. The analysis revealed that vibrational temperatures were consistently lower than rotational temperatures across all tested conditions, indicating possible thermodynamic nonequilibrium in the plenum chamber. Furthermore, the fitted temperatures were largely insensitive to changes in bulk enthalpy and remained significantly higher than estimated bulk temperatures, suggesting that the measured spectra are dominated by emission from a localized hot core. The diameter of this core region was estimated using a steady-flow energy balance. Finally, quantitative molar fractions of both N<inf>2</inf> and N<inf>2</inf>+ at each condition suggest that variations of the dissociation and ionization levels are more sensitive to the changes in local core temperature rather than bulk temperature. The proportionality between these two populations also suggests nonequilibrium between ionization and vibrational relaxation that, in a pure two-temperature model, would be governed by the same temperature. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jessen, J.; Larsson, J.; Brehm, C.
Author full names: Jessen, John (60342251500); Larsson, Johan (8399727500); Brehm, Christoph (15123994400)
Title: Comparative optimization of hypersonic waveriders using analytical and computational methods
Abstract: This work presents a methodology for designing waverider geometries that incorporate leading edge rounding and parameterized surface perturbations, and evaluates their performance using two distinct aerodynamic prediction approaches: a reduced-order analytical/empirical model and a GPU-based Euler CFD solver, both applied with an empirical approach to estimate the viscous contributions. Geometry optimization was performed using a global pattern search algorithm, and optimal trajectories were computed using a direct collocation method to assess the impact of aerodynamic modeling fidelity on mission performance. Cross-comparisons between these aerodynamic performance prediction methods revealed that CFD-based optimization produces geometries that capture off-design flow phenomena that are absent from analytical-based optimizations. As a result, CFD-optimized waveriders demonstrated higher average lift-to-drag ratios than those derived from the analytical model. The analytical trajectories had lower angle-of-attack control and degraded in accuracy for vehicles with high pressure losses. These findings highlight the limitations of traditional analytical methods and underscore the feasibility of using modern GPU-accelerated CFD for resource-intensive tasks such as vehicle optimization and trajectory analysis. © 2026 .

Authors: Wang, K.; Li, J.; Wang, Y.
Author full names: Wang, Kunkun (60342209600); Li, Jieping (60342120000); Wang, Yanrong (35801453600)
Title: Reduction of blade vibration stress by vane asymmetry in a high-pressure turbine
Abstract: In the field of turbomachinery, forced vibrations induced by unsteady aerodynamic forces on blades have a significant impact on fatigue life. Asymmetric vane is an effective design approach to reduce unsteady excitation forces of downstream blades. However, experimental studies indicate that the classical numerical prediction methods for the vibration reduction factor of asymmetric vane need further development and refinement. This paper investigates a 2.5-stage high-pressure turbine, originally designed with uniform inlet guide vanes, where the first-stage rotor blades experienced flow-induced vibration failures during experiments. To address this issue, seven asymmetric vane designs were proposed, including three practical non-uniform vane spacing schemes and four exploratory non-uniform vane camber angle schemes. The time-marching method was used to calculate the full-wheel unsteady flow field and obtain unsteady aerodynamic loads. The modal force method was then employed to compute the vibration stress of the downstream bladed disk excited by the wake. The results demonstrate that asymmetric vane designs can disperse a single resonance point into multiple adjacent resonance points, thereby reducing vibration stress. In the non-uniform vane spacing schemes, the optimal design reduced vibration stress by 47.8% with minimal aerodynamic performance change. In the non-uniform vane camber angle schemes, reducing the camber angle of half vanes effectively lowered the vibration stress of the downstream rotor blades while incurring small aerodynamic performance loss. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Title: Radar characterization of hypersonic wake plasma in a high-enthalpy shock-tunnel flow: RCS, micro-doppler and electron-density analysis
Abstract: The plasma sheath that forms around vehicles at hypersonic speed can change the radar signature substantially. A common approach to predict the impact of plasma on radar observation is to combine computational fluid dynamics (CFD) and computational electromagnetics (CEM). Due to the lack of experimental reference data, however, these predictions often remain unvalidated and their accuracy unclear. As part of an initiative to improve this situation, this paper presents radar signatures of a turbulent flow around an 8 cm diameter sphere measured at high enthalpy conditions in the High Enthalpy Shock Tunnel Göttingen (HEG) at the site of the German Aerospace Center (DLR) in Göttingen. The radar return and microwave transmission of this instationary and turbulent hypersonic flow were acquired with the wideband Multi-application Software Defined Radar (Masodera) system. Besides a strong radio blackout on the transmission channel, characteristic Micro-Doppler features were also observed. Reference CFD and CEM simulations were carried out and are compared with the measured data. © 2026 The Author(s).

Authors: Sun, L.; Wang, Y.; Chen, M.
Title: Dynamic time-window nash equilibrium strategies for UAV pursuit-evasion games under incomplete strategies
Abstract: In the domain of autonomous unmanned aerial systems, pursuit-evasion games are critically challenged by adversarial information asymmetry and real-time operational constraints. Traditional methods often fail under the evader’s active multi-domain interference relying on fixed time-windows or passive noise assumptions which induces non-stationary perceptual uncertainty and strategic incompleteness. To address this, a dynamic time-window Nash equilibrium framework is proposed that adaptively adjusts strategy-update intervals based on real-time interference intensity, effectively balancing computational efficiency with strategic optimality. The evader employs a variety of multi-dimensional interference tactics, such as electromagnetic suppression, infrared decoys, and laser blinding. These tactics are modeled via Pignistic probability divergence and Jensen-Shannon metrics, which serve to characterize the extent of degradation in the pursuer’s situational awareness. Polar-coordinate kinematics are integrated to characterize UAV dynamics, while a variational distributionally robust optimization (VDRO) method is employed to resolve bounded uncertain parameters. The existence and consistency of local and global Nash equilibria is rigorously established under conditions of continuity, convexity, and bounded uncertainty. Numerical simulations demonstrate the framework’s superiority, achieving a 98.3% capture success rate and reducing the average interception time by 8.08% compared to fixed time-window methods, under high-interference scenarios. This work integrates game-theoretic equilibrium theory with adaptive robustness, offering a scalable solution for the pursuit-evasion games in highly contested environments characterized by significant interference. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Author full names: Liu, Shuaipeng (57416735200); Geng, Shaojuan (8954113900); Zhang, Hongwu (57189391891)
Title: A data-driven airfoil generative design method and its application in compressor throughflow optimization design
Abstract: To enhance the intelligence and efficiency of compressor design, a data-driven compressor airfoil generative design method within a throughflow optimization framework is proposed. The method enables automatic airfoil geometry generation directly from design feature parameters. First, a two-dimensional airfoil parameterization method based on superimposing thickness on the suction surface is developed. A two-dimensional airfoil optimization platform is constructed by integrating a surrogate-based optimization approach. Based on six airfoil feature parameters—design inflow Mach number, relative maximum thickness, design inflow angle, design flow-turning angle, solidity, and axial velocity density ratio (AVDR)—160 airfoils are defined and optimized to establish an optimized airfoil database. Subsequently, a Kriging surrogate model is trained to form an “Airfoil Predictor” driven by the optimized airfoil database. This predictor establishes a mapping between airfoil feature parameters and geometric modeling parameters, enabling real-time airfoil geometry generation and significantly improving design efficiency and automation. Airfoil loss and outflow angle correlations consistent with the optimized airfoil database characteristic parameters are developed, enabling data-driven airfoil performance description within a classical throughflow framework. The proposed method is applied to the throughflow optimization of a 1.5-stage transonic compressor, where the “Airfoil Predictor” instantaneously generates airfoil geometries consistent with the optimized flow conditions. CFD validation shows that, under a constant pressure ratio, the optimized configuration achieves a 0.6% increase in isentropic efficiency and a 3.1% improvement in stall margin. The aerodynamic characteristics of the generated airfoils are highly consistent with the optimized results, demonstrating the accuracy and feasibility of the proposed data-driven methodology for improving compressor design efficiency and intelligence. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Osman, M.; Xia, Y.; Mahdi, M.; MANZOOR, T.; Abro, G.E.M.; Bajodah, A.H.
Author full names: Osman, Mohammed (55954199500); Xia, Yuanqing (55916672500); Mahdi, Mohammed (59548867200); MANZOOR, Tayyab (57216806355); Abro, Ghulam E.Mustafa (59910377500); Bajodah, Abdulrahman H. (9243910200)
Title: Physics-informed sparse reinforcement learning for hybrid VTOL UAV control: HILS verification and tethered hover benchmarking
Abstract: Hybrid Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL) UAVs present significant challenges for control design, particularly due to nonlinear dynamics, actuator coupling, and complex hover-cruise transitions. Conventional controllers, such as PID and gain-scheduled MPC, often lack robustness to uncertainties, while recent reinforcement learning (RL) methods remain computationally demanding and offer limited interpretability, restricting their suitability for embedded platforms. To address these limitations, this paper introduces a physics-informed sparse RL framework that combines Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) with RL. Unlike black-box policies, the proposed approach constructs symbolic models of dynamics, reward, and policy, yielding interpretable controllers that are lightweight, data-efficient, and dynamically consistent. The framework is validated on a hybrid UAV with blended quadrotor-fixed-wing dynamics using high-fidelity simulations, hardware-in-the-loop testing, and tethered prototype bench experiments executed in real time on an NVIDIA Jetson platform (no free-flight experiments are reported in this work). Results demonstrate reliable trajectory tracking, robust mode transitions, and efficient real-time execution, confirming the potential of SINDy-RL as a scalable pathway for physics-aware autonomy in next-generation VTOL UAV systems. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Choi, I.; Park, G.; Park, S.-H.
Author full names: Choi, Ilsung (57221803184); Park, Gisu (55759619200); Park, Seong-hyeon (57194493953)
Title: Free-flight measurement for a small-scale model via synthetic image-based template matching technique
Abstract: This Short Communication proposes a synthetic image-based template-matching technique to track the free-flight motion of small-scale models in hypersonic impulse facilities using a single high-speed camera. The approach iteratively minimizes the zero-mean normalized sum of squared differences (ZNSSD) between measured shadowgraph images and synthetic images. Synthetic images account for partial-area effects (PAE) and a pillbox point-spread function to approximate defocus blur, enabling subpixel sensitivity for models as small as ∼ 10 pixels. Applied to a 3 mm diameter, 3 mm long cylinder in a shock tunnel, the approach yields positions consistent with a corner-point tracking approach while substantially improving size and orientation estimates. The measured diameter (13.71 ± 0.18 pixels) aligns with independent calibration (13.26 ± 0.68 pixels), whereas the corner-point method underestimates size (11.55 ± 0.47 pixels) by 2.16 pixels. The proposed method also captures small attitude changes up to −7.5∘ pitch and 2.8 ∘ yaw that the corner-point approach fails to resolve. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yang, K.; Bai, C.; Quan, Q.
Title: Task allocation without direct communication: Graphical game based swarm interception allocation
Abstract: The interception of hostile UAV swarms in communication-denied environments poses a significant challenge for modern defense systems. Traditional methods, such as physical capture or frequency interference, are ineffective against coordinated swarms, while existing multi-robot task allocation strategies rely heavily on communication. This paper addresses this gap by proposing a game-theoretic framework for swarm interception allocation based on active observation. We formulate the problem as a graphical game, enabling interceptors to make decentralized decisions without direct communication. Specifically, we investigate Correlated Equilibrium (CE) to resolve conflicts in target selection among interceptors. The proposed method achieves task coverage and global payoff comparable to centralized approaches like the Hungarian algorithm, as validated by software-in-the-loop simulations and real-flight experiments. This work demonstrates a robust, communication-free solution for swarm interception in complex scenarios. Video of the experiments is available at youtu.be/twUSc8zJ9so . © © 2026. Authors: Li, B.; Lyu, B.
Title: Sound generated by the interaction between shock and instability waves in supersonic round jets
Abstract: In this paper, we develop an analytical model to investigate the sound generated by the shock-instability interactions (SII) in supersonic round jets, extending our previous two-dimensional planar study to circular configurations. The jet is represented by a vortex sheet, with its motion modeled by the Euler equations. Shock and instability waves are modeled using Pack’s approach and the linear stability theory, respectively, while their interaction is calculated by solving an inhomogeneous wave equation. Using the Fourier transform and steepest descent method, we obtain a closed-form solution for the resulting acoustic field. Results due to the interaction between the instability waves and one interaction cell capture the key directivity features of screech reported in experiments and numerical simulations, indicating that the classic monopole assumption may be inadequate. In particular, the screech-tone intensity due to multiple shock cells decays rapidly as the observer angle approaches 180 degrees, which is in better agreement with the experimental data measured by Norum. We further analyze how the instability wave growth rate influences these directivity patterns and examine the sound generation efficiency of the broadband shock-associated noise. Finally, an examination of near-field pressure fluctuations due to the SII reveals that noise is produced primarily via the Mach wave radiation mechanism. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Rotherham, D.; Filippone, A.; Bojdo, N.
Author full names: Rotherham, Daniel (58036856000); Filippone, Antonio (8665392600); Bojdo, Nicholas (35728197500)
Title: Aircraft engines dust ingestion in the Middle East
Abstract: Commercial aircraft operations in the Middle East are projected to grow at an annual rate of 5.1% over the next 20 years. However, aircraft operating in this region are frequently exposed to high concentrations of mineral dust, which can cause significant engine damage and reduce component lifetimes. This study presents a methodology that integrates real-world flight trajectory data with a numerical weather model to quantify the amount of dust ingested by aero engines. A large-scale analysis was conducted on 30,000 flights operated by a narrow-body aircraft across 12 Middle Eastern airports to assess regional variability in dust ingestion. The vertical distribution of ingested dust was examined, revealing a peak in the core dust ingested between 2000 and 5000 feet, with a secondary peak around 10,000 feet. These patterns are primarily driven by the climb phase, where significant time is spent within dust-laden altitude bands, as the aircraft increases the forward flight velocity, thereby contributing significantly to overall dust exposure. Holding patterns were identified in less than 1% of flights, with the primary driver for variability in ingested dust mass attributed to the radar vectored approaches and deviations from published arrival paths. © 2026 The Author(s).

Authors: Wang, J.; Liu, X.; Fan, J.; Tang, Z.
Title: Investigation on the central combustion of solid-fuel dual-mode ramjet combustor in ramjet mode
Abstract: This study investigates the central combustion characteristics of a solid-fuel dual-mode ramjet (SF-DMRJ) combustor operating in ramjet mode through direct-connect experiments and numerical simulations. Two-phase fuel-rich gas, generated by combusting a boron-containing oxygen-deficient solid propellant in a solid fuel gas generator (SFGG), is centrally injected into the combustor. The combustor operates under Mach 6 flight conditions at an altitude of 26 km, with equivalence ratios ( ϕ ) of 0.75, 1.10, and 1.35. Results show that the present high-speed, centrally injected SF-DMRJ combustor operates in a supersonic inlet-ramjet mode, where shock trains extend from the isolator into the combustor and interact with mixing and combustion processes, accompanied by a quasi-thermal throat near the exit. The combustor features a non-uniform flow structure with a central subsonic high-temperature region flanked by low-temperature supersonic air streams. The downstream supersonic flow evolution is governed by the competing mechanisms between heat conduction and shear/thermal expansion acceleration, with the dominant mechanism undergoing two shifts as ϕ increases from 0.75 to 1.1 and further to 1.35. Increasing ϕ deteriorates particle combustion and thus reduces the overall combustion efficiency. However, owing to a self-balancing mechanism between the total pressure losses induced by the shock train intensification and the spatial redistribution of combustion heat release, together with the compensatory effect of the high-pressure fuel jet, the total pressure recovery is moderately enhanced at higher ϕ . © © 2026. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Lu, M.; Su, Z.; Liang, H.; Zong, H.; Wu, Y.; Wei, B.; Tang, M.; Zhang, D.; Kong, X.; Zhang, F.
Title: Experimental study on incident shock wave/boundary layer interaction controlled by pulsed arc discharge
Abstract: Shock wave/boundary layer interaction (SWBLI) is a prevalent phenomenon in high-speed aircraft. It leads to issues including inlet flow distortion and structural damage, which are induced by low-frequency vibrations. Wind tunnel tests at Mach 4 were conducted to investigate the influence of pulsed arc discharge (PAD) on an incident SWBLI. 12 discharge channels are involved based on a U-shaped electrode design. Results demonstrate that the PAD induces a shock wave array upstream of the SWBLI region. This array subsequently interacts with the SWBLI. Consequently, a break point is generated on the separation shock wave. In contrast, the structure of the incident shock wave remains largely unchanged. The break point on the separation shock wave emerges at 100 µs after each discharge pulse. Meanwhile, the overall temperature of the expansion wave system increases significantly by 200 µs following a pulse. In the frequency domain, PAD forces the characteristic frequency of the SWBLI to synchronize with the discharge frequency or its integer multiples. When a high discharge frequency is applied, the low-frequency pulsation energy of the SWBLI is transferred to a higher frequency domain. Quantitative analysis finds that, this energy transfer effectively mitigates the structural vibrations resulting from SWBLI low-frequency instability. Specifically, the structural vibrations are reduced by 12.87% and 25.63% at discharge frequencies of 2.5 kHz and 5 kHz, respectively. The flow control mechanism encompasses both the thermal effect (via the controlling gas bulb, CGB) and the shock effect (via shock wave arrays) produced by the plasma discharge. Furthermore, energy accumulation effects are present during this control process. © © 2026. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zheng, Z.; Lu, Y.; Qu, S.; Wang, X.
Title: A study of the correlation between FSI flow behaviors and losses for the supersonic turbine cascade with millimeter-level ultra-thin blades and a high inlet flow angle
Abstract: The pursuit of higher performance in advanced gas turbine propulsion systems has driven the adoption of ultra-thin blade in the final turbine stage. These blades operate at high inlet flow angle under supersonic conditions. This complex environment causes strong nonlinear coupling between the flow field and blade deformation. As a result, traditional one-way FSI simulations become inaccurate. This inaccuracy poses a risk to both performance optimization and operational safety. This study evaluates the predictive accuracy of one-way and two-way FSI methods. The numerical results were validated using experimental data obtained through linear cascade aerodynamic measurements and Schlieren visualization techniques. The investigation reveals that the two-way FSI method significantly outperforms the one-way approach in aerodynamic predictions. Compared to the one-way FSI method, the two-way FSI method achieves excellent consistency with experimentally obtained shock structures while reducing aerodynamic prediction errors by over 50%. Conversely, the one-way method is found to substantially overestimate the structural dynamic response, predicting stress and strain values higher than the two-way results, and erroneously indicating yield failure due to the lack of a real-time feedback mechanism. Furthermore, the results elucidate the intrinsic aero-structural coupling as a continuous feedback loop where blade deformation reshapes the effective flow passage and migrates the boundary layer separation, thereby governing the downstream flow field evolution. In conclusion, for supersonic thin-blade cascades operating at high inlet flow angles, the two-way FSI method is identified as the essential technology for accurately predicting aero-structural performance and ensuring operational safety. © © 2026. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jin, Y.; Li, H.; Lu, S.; Bai, Y.; Teng, J.
Title: Numerical investigation of the partition rib design and loss reduction mechanism in squealer blade tip
Abstract: This study conducts a systematic investigation into the flow topologies and leakage control mechanisms of four distinct squealer tip configurations. It elucidates how partition rib designs influence tip leakage flow and mixing losses, and proposes optimized design principles for partition ribs. Compared to conventional squealer tips, the partition-rib design induces the formation of a second scraping vortex (SV<inf>2</inf>), creating a cooperative multi-vortex flow structure in the upstream region that enhances the aerodynamic labyrinth sealing effect. However, the partition rib simultaneously constricts the development space of the first scraping vortex (SV<inf>1</inf>), reducing its scale and causing premature ejection from the clearance. Consequently, the low-leakage zone extent dominated by SV<inf>1</inf> is significantly contracted, leading to substantially increased mixing losses in mid-to-downstream regions. Through optimization of the rib's length and curvature, vortex evolution can be effectively controlled: providing adequate space for SV<inf>1</inf> development delays its detachment, while refined rib-end geometry reduces the mixing angle when SV<inf>1</inf> interacts with the mainstream flow. The research ultimately demonstrates that an optimized rib geometry can maintain effective upstream sealing performance while significantly suppressing additional losses in mid-to-downstream regions. These findings provide theoretical foundations and engineering guidance for high-performance squealer tip designs. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Sessini, F.; Donnini, D.; Chella, A.; Schwarz, T.; Yin, J.; Bellani, G.; de Angelis, E.L.
Author full names: Sessini, Francesco (60212832500); Donnini, Diego (60212571100); Chella, Aldo (60213089400); Schwarz, Thorsten (7201638090); Yin, Jianping (36984757500); Bellani, Gabriele (35090069700); de Angelis, Emanuele L. (55617968200)
Title: Directional noise reduction via rotor phase–shifting for multirotor aircraft
Abstract: This study investigates rotor phase synchronization as a strategy for directional noise reduction in multirotor vertical take–off and landing (VTOL) aircraft employing collective pitch control. The application of this approach enables the redirection of acoustic emissions away from noise–sensitive regions without degrading aerodynamic performance or requiring alterations to the flight trajectory. A dedicated simulation framework integrates the mid–fidelity aerodynamic solver UPM with the aeroacoustic prediction model APSIM, developed at the German Aerospace Center (DLR), Institute of Aerodynamics and Flow Technology. To reproduce representative flight conditions, a trimming procedure determines the required control inputs and vehicle attitude. Such simulation methodology was validated by reproducing available experimental data for hovering fixed–pitch propellers. To identify optimal rotor phasing for noise redirection in full–scale quadrotor and hexarotor configurations under trimmed flight conditions, two distinct optimization strategies are employed. In forward flight, optimization is conducted using the complete high–fidelity simulation environment. For hover conditions, an analytical aeroacoustic model based on compact source theory provides a computationally efficient alternative that reduces the demands placed on the optimizer. Acoustic pressure evaluations on a ground plane beneath the vehicle confirm that optimized rotor phasing successfully redirects noise away from designated regions. © 2026 The Author(s).

Authors: Han, Q.; Cui, J.; Fan, M.; Zheng, Y.; Ye, S.; Guo, Y.; Liu, C.; En-Hou, E.-H.
Title: Bridging a Cu layer enables decent metallurgical bonding for wire-arc directed energy deposited Mg-Ti bimetals
Abstract: In this study, TA15/AZ91D bimetallic materials with Cu as the intermediate layer were prepared by wire arc directed energy deposition. The interfacial microstructe and mechanical properties were systematically studied. The results show that under the best process parameters, an intermetallic compound (IMC) layer of 179 μm thickness (mainly TiCu and Ti<inf>2</inf>Cu phases) was formed at the Ti-Cu interface, and an IMC layer of 44 μm thickness (mainly TiAl, α-Mg, MgCu and MgAlCu ternary phases) was formed at the Cu-Mg interface, realizing a good metallurgical bonding. Due to the lower temperature difference, the residual stress between Ti and Cu is reduced, the thickness of the IMC layer decreases, thereby eliminating cracks. The thermodynamic analysis reveals the reaction mechanism of Cu-Mg interface: TiAl phase and MgCu phase tend to form preferentially, and Cu element promotes the formation of TiAl phase. The increase of TiAl phase is related to the activation of more Cu atoms when the heat input increases. Due to the increase of heat input, the concentration of Ti atoms in the Cu layer increases. The Cu dendrites in the Cu layer disappear and the TiCu phases are generate, and the microhardness reaches 480 ± 6.4 HV (10 times that of pure Cu). It is particularly noted that the strength of the bimetal increased by 27 MPa because of the increase in the thickness of the Cu layer (from 1357.90 μm to 1851.96 μm). The fracture mechanism of the bimetal is brittle fracture. Under the best process, the tensile strength of the bimetal can reach up to 106 MPa, and the bending strength can reach up to 138.9 MPa. This study provides important process references for the manufacturing of Mg-Ti bimetals. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yang, T.; Wang, W.; Wang, B.; Chen, X.; Zhao, Q.
Title: Numerical investigation of rotor transient maneuver noise in forward flight
Abstract: Helicopter climb/descent maneuvering control is mainly achieved by adjusting the rotor collective pitch in forward flight conditions. The aeroacoustic characteristics of helicopter rotors during aperiodic variations in collective pitch under forward flight conditions are calculated and analyzed based on the CFD method and FW-H equations. First, a set of analytical methods for aperiodic rotor aeroacoustic characteristics is established. The AH-1 G rotor in forward flight and the NACA rotor under a ramp increase in collective pitch are simulated, and the numerical analysis method employed is validated by comparison with experimental data. Then, the aeroacoustic characteristics of the AH-1 G rotor during a ramp increase in collective pitch are analyzed, with a focus on the sound pressure peak in the blade-vortex interaction state. Finally, a detailed analysis is conducted on the BO-105 rotor in forward flight during aperiodic variations in collective pitch. In addition, parameters such as the collective pitch change rate are quantified, leading to several conclusions: during ramp increases and decreases in collective pitch, positive and negative overshoot phenomena occur respectively, with both cases showing a significant increase in acoustic levels. © © 2026.
