Authors: Kong, M.; Pei, B.; Liu, Q.; Xu, Y.
Title: Dynamics and reliability design of rotor-nacelle-wing coupled structures under non-white random gusts
Abstract: This paper investigates the dynamics and reliability of rotor-nacelle-wing coupled structures with cubic nonlinearity under non-white random gusts. Random gusts will induce the coupled structures to exhibit unexpected high-amplitude limit-cycle oscillations or catastrophic instability, directly shortening the service life and threatening flight safety. By modeling non-white random gusts as Gaussian colored noises with correlation time, this study reveals the impact of correlation time on structural reliability. We observe that a shorter correlation time and larger intensity significantly increases the risk of stochastic jumping, structural fatigue damage and catastrophic instability. The current work proposes a reliability framework that integrates first passage criteria for sudden overload and fatigue failure criteria for cumulative damage. Two nonlinear energy sink structures are put forward to reduce vibrations by redirecting energy flow. Indeed, the parameters of the nonlinear energy sinks in the rotor-nacelle-wing coupled structure are optimized using a multi-objective particle swarm method. Compared to merely tuning structural parameters, our vibration control strategy achieves at least a 54.68% improvement in reliability, with maximum enhancements exceeding 95.32% in a certain parameter domain. This research is the first to consider bistable stochastic dynamics of rotor nacelle-wing coupled structures under non-white random gusts to improve reliability, which will provide new insights into the structural safety design of tilt-rotor aircraft. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hou, Y.; Wang, W.; Zhou, Y.; You, R.
Title: Evaluating different control modes for the gasper system in an economy-class aircraft cabin with personalized displacement ventilation
Abstract: Maintaining both thermal comfort and air quality in aircraft cabins is essential for air passengers and crew members. The gasper system, widely used to provide personalized ventilation, plays a major role in regulating individual thermal comfort and potentially influencing contaminant transport. However, these two aspects have been addressed separately in existing studies. Whether it is feasible for a control mode to simultaneously mitigate airborne infection risk while maintaining thermal comfort remains unclear. To address the knowledge gap, this study first conducted experimental measurements in a three-row, single-aisle aircraft cabin mock-up with personalized displacement ventilation. The obtained experimental data was then used to validate a CFD model. Subsequently, the validated CFD model was employed to simulate different gasper control modes in a seven-row section of a single-aisle, fully occupied, economy-class aircraft cabin with personalized displacement ventilation. Three gasper control modes were considered: a no-control mode, full control of engaged gaspers, and partial control of engaged gaspers. The results show that under full control of engaged gaspers, the number of relatively high-risk passengers was reduced by at least 79% compared to the no-control mode. However, the gasper operations significantly differed from passengers' preferences under the no-control mode. Partial control of engaged gaspers also achieved meaningful risk reduction, reaching at least 66%. Meanwhile, it maintained the comfort preferences of most passengers. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of the partial-control mode in achieving both airborne infection mitigation and comfort preference maintenance in real-life cabin environments. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ren, P.; Xie, X.; Zhang, K.; Peng, D.; Wang, H.
Title: Aero-engine bearing anomaly detection framework based on adaptive wavelet transform network and neighborhood angle factor analysis
Abstract: The reliability of aero-engines, the core power units of modern aircraft, is paramount for flight safety. However, the harsh operating environment—characterized by high speeds and variable loads—makes rolling bearings susceptible to faults that are difficult to detect using traditional methods. Existing signal processing techniques rely heavily on manual parameter tuning, while purely data-driven deep learning models often struggle to extract stable features under fluctuating operating conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes AWTN-NAFA, a novel unsupervised anomaly detection framework integrating an Adaptive Wavelet Transform Network (AWTN) and Neighborhood Angle Factor Analysis (NAFA). The AWTN module embeds the physical mechanism of the fast discrete wavelet transform into a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture. By employing learnable filters and activation functions, AWTN adaptively extracts compact, stable, and discriminative latent representations that remain robust against domain shifts. Subsequently, the NAFA module performs anomaly detection on these features. Unlike traditional distance-based detectors sensitive to feature scale, NAFA exploits the geometric properties of the data manifold, utilizing the angular variance of nearest neighbors to construct a robust, adaptive threshold. Extensive experiments on a real-world aero-engine bearing dataset demonstrate that AWTN-NAFA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving superior detection accuracy and robustness across diverse complex operating conditions. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, Y.; Zheng, X.; Xiang, G.
Author full names: Zhang, Yichen (57222365127); Zheng, Xiaojing (7404091252); Xiang, Gaoxiang (56909590900)
Title: Active control of oblique detonation waves through engineered wall properties
Abstract: Oblique Detonation Engines (ODEs) offer a promising propulsion solution for future hypersonic flight systems. However, the stability of oblique detonation waves (ODWs) is highly sensitive to boundary conditions, particularly the physical and chemical properties of the confinement wall. This study establishes a concrete link between fundamental wall material properties and macroscopic flow dynamics by systematically investigating the effects of wall roughness, temperature, and catalytic activity. Through numerical simulations based on the multi-species reactive Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) equations with a detailed hydrogen reaction mechanism, we demonstrate that wall roughness—modeled via an equivalent sand-grain approach—significantly modulates flow behavior by enhancing turbulence, amplifying pressure fluctuations, and altering reaction initiation. Increased roughness height delays reactions due to mechanisms such as mass transfer limitations, whereas a higher roughness coefficient promotes earlier ignition via intensified turbulent mixing. Variations in wall temperature show negligible effects on pressure distributions but accelerate reaction onset due to thermal activation. Catalytic walls induce distinct species concentration profiles, with fully catalytic walls notably reducing the height of the oblique shock wave front. These findings offer new insights into the behavior of ODWs under complex wall boundary conditions and highlight the potential of engineered surfaces for active flow control. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tekin, A.; Xiao, T.; Yu, X.
Author full names: Tekin, Abdurrahman (60107715300); Xiao, Tianhang (22036571800); Yu, Xiongqing (7404115848)
Title: A hybrid deep learning framework for efficient airfoil design optimization
Abstract: Navigating the high-dimensional and multi-modal design space of airfoils presents a formidable challenge for traditional optimization algorithms, which are often susceptible to convergence in suboptimal local minima. To address this, the present study proposes a novel hybrid optimization framework that synergistically couples a deep neural network (DNN) inverse design model with a local search algorithm. The DNN, trained on a comprehensive dataset of 80,000 unique airfoils, functions as a computationally inexpensive surrogate to perform a global search, rapidly identifying promising design candidates. These candidates are then refined with high precision by a 'Nelder-Mead' optimizer, effectively bridging the gap between global exploration and local exploitation. The framework’s performance was demonstrated on a series of increasingly complex design challenges at a cruise condition defined by a Reynolds number of 3×106, a Mach number of 0.1, and a zero-degree angle of attack. In an unconstrained lift-to-drag (C<inf>L</inf>/C<inf>D</inf>) maximization, the strategy discovers an airfoil with a superior C<inf>L</inf>/C<inf>D</inf> of 316.08. The framework's versatility is highlighted in two constrained cases: it achieves a nearly identical performance of C<inf>L</inf>/C<inf>D</inf> = 316.0 while satisfying a practical minimum thickness constraint, and yields an airfoil with a C<inf>L</inf>/C<inf>D</inf> of 68.5 when constrained to a near-zero pitching moment for tailless aircraft configurations. Furthermore, the practical viability of the optimized design was confirmed through a rigorous uncertainty quantification, which demonstrated that the airfoil's performance is highly robust to geometric variations representative of manufacturing tolerances. This work establishes the proposed hybrid strategy as a powerful paradigm for solving complex, constrained optimization problems, demonstrating how the integration of data-driven surrogates with physics-based solvers provides a validated, robust, and highly efficient pathway for the future of aerodynamic design. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liang, H.; Yin, Z.; Wei, C.; Xie, Y.; Chen, X.
Author full names: Liang, Hao (59323763400); Yin, Zeyang (57192682496); Wei, Caisheng (57192677895); Xie, Yongfang (55609506600); Chen, Xiaofang (55739065200)
Title: Cooperative encirclement hunting for space non-cooperative targets based on real-time reachable area prediction
Abstract: This paper investigates the encirclement hunting problem of a non-cooperative target by multiple pursuit spacecraft. An encirclement hunting strategy is proposed to capture the non-cooperative target based on predicting its reachable area under maneuver uncertainty. First, a dynamic model of the non-cooperative target is developed, in which the uncertainty in its maneuver is considered and modeled. Then, the reachable area of the non-cooperative target is predicted by analyzing the uncertainty propagation and computing the covariance matrix of the target’s deviation based on the state transition tensor method. To continue, the dynamic encirclement configuration of the multiple pursuit spacecraft is designed based on the virtual structure method. A hybrid control strategy combining artificial potential field and sliding mode control (APF-SMC) is developed, ensuring the flight safety of the pursuit spacecraft while effectively executing the encirclement hunting tasks. Finally, the comparison experiment results show that the proposed method can acquire the same reachable area with only a single-round calculation as the Monte Carlo methods with 1500-round simulations, thereby reducing computational costs and improving hunting effectiveness. Meanwhile, the designed APF-SMC controller ensures fast convergence in finite time, enabling the multiple pursuit spacecraft to safely and rapidly form the encirclement configuration. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Taşkaya, G.; Erdoğan, B.
Title: Taguchi optimization and CFD analysis for Bézier-based airfoil geometry
Abstract: In this study, a new airfoil consisting of 16 control points, 8 each on the upper and lower curves of the airfoil geometry, was designed by modeling the Bézier curve with the MATLAB program. The Taguchi Method, which provides an effective and systematic approach in determining the optimum parameter combinations, was preferred in order to evaluate the effects of the parameters affecting the airfoil performance. Within the scope of the Design of Experiments (DOE) methodology, the analyses were conducted at a Reynolds number of Re=200,000 and an angle of attack of 8°. The aim is to determine the optimal design conditions (maximum camber, maximum camber position, maximum thickness, leading edge radius, and trailing edge thickness) by considering five factors consisting of control points representing five basic parameters and to offer a high-performance original airfoil profile that can be used in general aerodynamic systems (Re=175,000-500, 000 Reynolds number and 0°-21° angle of attack). In this direction, the data obtained from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations based on the Taguchi L16 orthogonal array were statistically evaluated using Analysis of Variance (ANOVA). The performance ratio (C<inf>L</inf>/C<inf>D</inf>) of the new airfoil, obtained as a result of parametric studies on the optimum experimental parameter combination determined by the Taguchi method, was compared with the NACA 0012, NACA 6412, and NASA 747A315 airfoils, which are frequently used in the literature, and with 16 other configurations included in the analysis. The new airfoil, obtained with Bézier curves, shows that it provides approximately 32% performance improvement compared to the NASA 747A315 airfoil, approximately 13% compared to the NACA 6412 airfoil, and approximately 20% compared to the NACA 0012 airfoil. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wu, L.; Du, P.; Ning, F.
Title: Effects of high-temperature steam ingestion-induced inlet distortion on transonic compressor performance and stability
Abstract: This study numerically investigates the adverse effects of high-temperature steam ingestion on aero-engine compression systems. A multi-component flow simulation method was developed to account for inter-species interactions of the steam-air mixture. Using NASA Rotor 67 as a test case, steady-state single-passage simulations and a time-space collocation method were employed to analyze the influence of saturated humid air under both uniform and distorted inlet conditions. Under uniform inflow, humid air significantly reduces corrected speed and work capability while maintaining stall margin. Flow fields show remarkable similarity between humid and dry air at matched corrected conditions. Under distortion condition, the time-space collocation method achieves results consistent with conventional time-marching approaches while obtaining a 7 times speed-up. Steam-induced inlet distortion, comprising both total temperature and thermodynamic property variations, collectively reduces corrected speed and stall margin. Compared to dry-air distortion, the humid-air working fluid further diminishes work input capability across all spanwise positions. The resultant decrease in local Mach number weakens shock strength and boundary layer separation, reducing shock and profile losses. Under near-stall conditions, severe boundary layer separation and tip blockage cause a sharp rise in aerodynamic loss above 40 % span. Despite reduced absolute leakage mass flow under humid air, the increased leakage-to-inflow ratio indicates an enhanced destabilizing effect that compromises compressor stability. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhou, Z.; Bai, B.; Li, Z.; Li, J.
Title: Investigation of tip impingement cooling scheme on film cooling and aerothermal performance of turbine blade squealer tips with hollow ribs
Abstract: Blade tip is under degradation threat from the increasing blade tip thermal load. Squealer tip with film holes is widely applied to alleviate the danger of tip erosion at the engine operating aerodynamic conditions. The cooling approach of film holes can be generalized the coolant leaves the film hole and then adheres to the tip surface. However, this cooling performance of coolant from the tip film holes is significantly limited by the flow pattern within the tip cavity. This study proposes a tip impingement cooling scheme to address the problem which lacks effective tip thermal protection in the conventional film hole scheme, while pondering the effect of coolant supplying conditions on tip cooling performance. In the tip impingement cooling scheme, coolant is able to directly impinge on the tip cavity bottom via a unique hollow rib which is installed at the leading edge (LE), the middle region and the trailing edge (TE). The tip film cooling and heat transfer performance are examined and compared among different tip cooling schemes by numerical simulation. The aerodynamic and tip leakage mass flow characteristics are also illustrated and analyzed in depth. The observations reveal that the direct coolant impingement approach effectively enhances the local film cooling performance in contrast to the tip film hole cooling scheme. Additionally, the hollow rib structure plays a key role in controlling the flow pattern within the tip cavity. Benefiting from this, the impingement cooling scheme has the superior heat transfer performance while also leading to more aerodynamic losses and leakage mass flow rate. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Guo, H.; Wang, R.; Guo, Z.; Ding, Y.; Yuan, R.; Guo, J.; Hu, G.
Title: Gradient-enhanced PINN-based hybrid attitude control for reentry vehicle under uncertainties
Abstract: Reentry vehicle attitude control faces critical challenges from model uncertainties, strong nonlinear dynamics, and fast response requirements. To address the limitations of traditional methods, which suffer from strong parameter sensitivity and lack of optimality guarantees in physical models, this paper combines physical modeling with data-driven approaches. It proposes a hybrid attitude tracking control framework based on gradient-enhanced physical information neural network (PINN). Gradient-enhanced PINN employs a dual-branch neural network architecture to explicitly output both the value function and its gradient, thereby eliminating costly numerical differentiation during rapid inference. Concurrently, a hybrid strategy is established in network training that aligns the physical information residual from the Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs equation with the gradient supervision loss, enhancing computational efficiency and convergence speed. Furthermore, the explicit gradient outputs are integrated into the dual-loop attitude control structure, driving stable outer-loop attitude tracking and inner-loop angular velocity stabilization without computational overhead. Considering practical multi-actuator coordination challenges, a gradient-enhanced torque allocation algorithm is proposed, dynamically balancing control authority between aerodynamic surfaces and thrust vectoring using state sensitivity information. Extensive comparative simulations and Monte Carlo robustness analysis validate the framework’s superior performance over existing methods in tracking accuracy, computational efficiency, and adaptive resource allocation under uncertainties. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, Y.; Li, Y.; Zhou, C.; Tian, X.; Yang, Z.
Title: Influence of loading distribution on corner separation in high-subsonic compressor cascades under low Reynolds number condition
Abstract: In the domain of modern high-load aero-engine design, corner separation is prone to occur in the compressor, which can significantly influence the aerodynamic performance of compressor blades at low Reynolds numbers. This study adopts a quantitative modeling approach to blade loading distribution and aims to examine the influence of loading distribution on corner separation. Adjustments are performed to the V103-B blade profile curvature, and compressor blade cascades with diverse loading distribution coefficients (Ω) are obtained. Numerical simulations are conducted to investigate the influence of loading distribution on corner separation of compressor stator under low Reynolds number condition. The simulation results reveal that a front-loaded blade configuration can effectively reduce stator losses. Nevertheless, as the loading distribution shifts forward, laminar flow separation on the suction surface occurs earlier. When the loading distribution is excessively forward (Ω = 1.36), the axial adverse pressure gradient region at the onset of laminar flow separation merges with that near the leading edge, forming a cross-channel axial adverse pressure gradient region. This significantly hinders the incoming flow from entering the cascade passage, causing corner separation to evolve into corner stall and reducing the range of stable operating angles for the cascade. This discovery demonstrates the influence of loading distribution on three-dimensional corner separation, offering insights for subsequent compressor blade profile design. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tarek, F.; Safwat, N.E.-D.; Sabatini, R.
Author full names: Tarek, Farah (58796621600); Safwat, Nour El Din (58982653900); Sabatini, Roberto (56962744800)
Title: GNSS performance monitoring and augmentation for Advanced Air Mobility
Abstract: Navigation systems that are adequate for Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) safety-critical applications must achieve levels of robustness, flexibility and reliability that are unprecedented in the realm of aviation for civil use. With the increase in the accessibility of new airspaces such as urban environments, the performance (i.e. accuracy, integrity, availability and continuity) of the navigation system has become of paramount importance. Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) are, thus far, among the most promising in meeting the performance requirements of those applications but not without sufficient operation-based augmentation. An Aircraft-Based Augmentation System (ABAS) allows for performance monitoring without reliance on external equipment. This paper proposes an evolution of the ABAS concept with deterministic predictive integrity monitoring capabilities suitable for highly challenged AAM applications. Raytracing-based models for antenna masking and multipath, which are highly dependent on the topographical surroundings, vehicle dynamics and airframe shape are integrated in the augmentation system. A simulation-based system verification is included assessing the achievable protection levels in a representative urban environment with flights below and above skyline as well as the feasibility of the system’s implementation. Results show promising integrity monitoring performance with high rates of detection exceeding 90% and low rates of false alarms below 5% for horizontal positioning. Best performance is observed at an operating altitude close to the average building height in the studied region (75 m height) with a 100% detection rate and 0% false alarm rate. The system showed great potential for its implementation and usage for AAM flight path and mission planning. Moreover, findings support the identification of a PBN requirement framework that would support high-integrity flight operations in future urban and suburban environments. © © 2026. Authors: Yang, J.; Li, Y.; Wang, L.; Xie, F.; Li, C.
Title: Mechanism of coupled energy transfer and pressure drop in cryogenic propellant sloshing through improved phase change modeling
Abstract: Heat and mass transfer at the vapor-liquid interface is significantly enhanced when rocket tanks experience sloshing, thereby accelerating the pressure drop. To investigate this phenomenon, this study first develops a CFD model for simulating liquid methane sloshing with higher phase change prediction accuracy by applying an improved temperature recovery model. Then, the real-time energy transfer between the vapor and liquid phases is quantified by integrating the developed CFD model with a lumped-parameter thermodynamic model of tanks. Based on energy analysis and the equivalent numerical decomposition method, three pressure drop mechanisms during the cryogenic tank sloshing process are studied in depth. The results show that the maximum error between the simulation results of the developed CFD model and cryogenic fluid sloshing experiments is 7.0 %, confirming the accuracy of the model. Liquid temperature is the dominant factor governing both the interphase energy transfer and the tank pressure drop. Vapor temperature has little effect on energy transfer, but determines the proportion of energy allocated to vapor condensation versus vapor cooling. Among the three pressure drop mechanisms during the rocket tank sloshing process, the phase change-dependent pressure drop depends solely on the intensity of vapor condensation, whereas the expansion-related pressure drop proves to be also influenced by the adiabatic index. When a greater fraction of the liquid's cold energy is allocated to vapor cooling rather than condensation and results in a larger heat-transfer-driven pressure drop, the total pressure drop in the tank becomes higher. This study advances the simulation and analysis of cryogenic propellant sloshing dynamics, and can provide insights for enhancement of cryogenic propellant management in rocket systems. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Guo, Y.; Wang, Y.; Wang, X.; Li, L.; Zhang, D.; Mu, H.
Title: Active vibration control of a variable-thickness FGM plate under aerodynamic load
Abstract: This study addresses the problem of aeroelastic instability of plates under high Mach number airflow by considering a variable-thickness functionally graded material (FGM) plate. Macro fiber composite (MFC) actuators are embedded on the plate surface to suppress vibration under aerodynamic excitation. To accurately describe the response of the FGM plate under load, this paper studies the large deformation modeling theory of the plate based on the absolute nodal coordinate formulation (ANCF). A dynamic model of the variable-thickness FGM plate element with attached MFC is established. Using the piston theory to describe the aerodynamic load, the aerodynamic load is expressed in the ANCF, and the aerodynamic responses of the plate under different parameters are discussed. A proportional-derivative (PD) control strategy is employed to suppress the unstable reaction of the plate under aerodynamic loading. The results show that although PD control can stabilize the plate, a noticeable static deformation remains. An integral term is introduced to overcome this limitation, forming a PID control strategy. The effects of control gains, actuator coverage area, and placement position on the effectiveness of deformation suppression are further investigated. As a theoretical research, this study offers a theoretical foundation for the modeling and vibration suppression of advanced engineering structures, such as helicopter rotors, wind turbine blades and aircraft wings, operating under aerodynamic loads. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, B.; Wang, C.; Huang, D.; Song, L.; Yang, C.; Liu, H.
Title: Persistent active target protection with multiple nonlinear players in arbitrary space dimensions
Abstract: This paper presents an active target protection approach that accommodates multiple defenders and attackers with nonlinear dynamics operating in arbitrary space dimensions. To establish a formal connection between the defense-winning conditions and the control inputs of nonlinear players, the m-sphere pursuit enclosure function (mSPEF) is proposed. The mSPEFs enable the explicit construction of control barrier functions (CBFs), which are further composed to form the forward invariant condition. This formulation allows the multi-player defense-winning condition to be transformed into linear constraints on the control inputs of the defenders and the target. A CBF-based quadratic programming (CBF-QP) controller is developed, ensuring real-time applicability with the defense-winning guarantee. Two implementing examples in both two-dimensional and three-dimensional scenarios are provided with specific controller parameters. Experiments conducted on real-world robots validate the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed approach. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS

Authors: Comunian, P.; Montagner, S.; Serpieri, J.; Cafiero, G.
Author full names: Comunian, P. J. (59308734000); Montagner, S. (59309025500); Serpieri, Jacopo (55848721100); Cafiero, Gioacchino (6507050265)
Title: Aerodynamic interaction of side-by-side propellers in forward flight operating at low-Reynolds numbers
Abstract: An experimental investigation is conducted to study the interaction of side-by-side propellers operating in forward flight at low Reynolds numbers. The effect on performance is first evaluated by means of load cell measurements, while the flow field is studied employing a stereoscopic particle image velocimetry (SPIV) setup. Three different configurations are tested: single propeller, co-rotating and counter-rotating cases at varying advance ratios. The results indicate that the performance of the single propeller is decreased due to aerodynamic interaction, leading to an average 3.2% reduction in propulsive efficiency, evaluated across all the tested operating conditions. The effect is stronger at lower advance ratios, owing to a greater interaction between the two streamtubes in such conditions. SPIV measurements indicate a widening of the wake as well as a reduction in the turbulence intensity for the cases with two propellers, with a stochastic fluctuations approximately 15% lower for the twin propeller cases than the single propeller. The data is then sorted and phase-ordered a posteriori via a data-driven approach, effectively reconstructing phase-averaged flow fields. This enables the decomposition of the velocity field into phase-correlated and purely turbulent components. The results show that the stochastic (turbulent) component of the velocity field increases when the propellers operate at advance ratios different from the maximum efficiency condition. © 2026 The Author(s).

Authors: Abbas, N.; Hao, L.-Y.
Title: Intelligent hybrid neural network-reinforcement learning framework for fault-tolerant formation control
Abstract: This study presents an intelligent hybrid control framework that integrates a Neural Network–based Fault and Disturbance Observer (FDO) with a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) controller to enhance autonomy and resilience in aerospace formation systems. The hybrid NN–DRL architecture replaces conventional model-based control with a learning-driven approach capable of real-time fault diagnosis, adaptive recovery, and stability preservation under uncertain flight conditions. The FDO provides high-accuracy fault estimation for actuator and dynamic anomalies, while the DRL agent learns optimal control strategies through Lyapunov-guided reward shaping to ensure Uniform Ultimate Boundedness (UUB) of formation-tracking errors. High-fidelity simulations demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves an average formation-tracking error of 0.17 m , fault-recovery within 1.6 s , and real-time implementation with a control update of only 2.3 ms . These results confirm superior fault tolerance, faster convergence, and improved reliability, offering a scalable solution for next-generation autonomous aerospace missions. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Long, K.; Mei, B.; Fu, Y.; Zhu, W.
Title: AFP-Net: A single-stage approach for defect detection in automated fiber placement of carbon fiber reinforced polymers for aircraft manufacturing
Abstract: The development and application of Automated Fiber Placement (AFP) technology have laid a solid foundation for the large-scale application of composite materials in aircraft manufacturing. However, layup defects are inevitably present during the AFP process, directly affecting the performance of the manufactured composite products. To address this problem, this paper creates an Automated Fiber Placement Defect Detection Network (AFP-Net) for implementation in domestically manufactured gantry-type AFP machines. AFP-Net is an end-to-end object detection framework that integrates three key innovations: a novel multi-scale feature fusion convolutions module, an adaptive feature pyramid network with weighted multi-scale information aggregation, and a lightweight detection head specifically tailored for AFP defect detection. Specifically, this study introduces three innovative designs for AFP defect detection: (1) a Multi-scale Feature Fusion with Convolutions (MFFC) module that employs multi-scale convolution kernels without dilation to extract features at different scales and capture local context; (2) a Gap-foreign Aimed Detection Feature Pyramid Network (GADFPN) that enhances multi-level feature utilization through efficient weighted fusion; and (3) a Gap-Aimed Detection (GAD) Head that uses shared convolutions instead of redundant independent ones, improving gap defect detection accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed AFP-Net exhibits excellent performance in defect detection in the AFP of composite materials. Moreover, comparison experiments on the publicly available surface defect dataset validate that AFP-Net outperforms YOLO and RT-DETR, demonstrating that AFP-Net has outstanding generalization ability. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, X.; Sun, D.; Wu, D.; Liu, T.; Zhang, B.
Title: A CS-PINN-DRL method for predicting the response of the mistuned bladed disk under unsteady loads
Abstract: Mistuning in bladed disks can cause significant amplification and localization of vibration responses, particularly under unsteady loads, thereby posing serious challenges to structural reliability and service life. This study proposes a hybrid prediction framework that integrates a cyclic-shift (CS) equivariance-consistent physics-informed neural network with deep reinforcement learning (CS-PINN-DRL) to overcome the limitations of conventional methods under small-sample and uncertainty conditions. By embedding rotation-equivariance under coherent sector relabeling (cyclic shifts) together with physical constraints into the neural network and adaptively optimizing the loss-function weighting, the model achieves improvements in both accuracy and stability. Numerical validation shows that the surrogate attains a test-set coefficient of determination (R2≈0.997) with typical pointwise mean absolute percentage error near 1–2% and reliably identifies the critical blade. Predictions of sectoral peaks and the global resonant frequency show a tight concentration (most deviations within approximately ±1 Hz). Uncertainty propagation and variance decomposition indicate that the displacement amplification factor (DAF) and displacement localization factor (DLF) are highly sensitive to parameter variations, whereas resonance metrics remain robust. Furthermore, the total uncertainty is found to be dominated by aleatoric components, with only a minor contribution from epistemic sources. The framework offers an efficient and accurate surrogate for mistuned bladed-disk response prediction, supporting vibration safety assessment and health monitoring. © © 2026. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wei, C.; Shao, K.; Gu, D.; Wang, K.
Title: Calibration of spaceborne accelerometer through satellite dynamic precise orbit determination: Enhancements and evaluations
Abstract: Influenced by systematic errors, spaceborne accelerometer measurements are contaminated by unknown scale and bias, and must be calibrated before any scientific applications. This contribution conducted a comprehensive investigation of dynamic precise orbit determination (POD) based accelerometer calibration. A novel scale factor determination strategy is proposed, and the calibration is enhanced by refining the GNSS observation model at the receiving end. Three months of flight data from the GRACE and GRACE-FO missions are processed. Through external and internal comparisons, the effectiveness and reliability of the proposed strategy for determining the accelerometer scale factor are first validated. In view of refinements to the GNSS observation model, calibration results report systematic shifts in the derived accelerometer z-axis (approximately radial) bias factors from both radial phase center offset (PCO) corrections and carrier phase integer ambiguity resolution (IAR) constraints. Statistical analysis indicates that a 1.0 cm radial PCO deviation would induce a 37.0 nm/s2 shift in z-axis bias factor estimation. The implementation of both phase center variation (PCV) corrections and IAR constraints demonstrates simultaneous stability improvement and accuracy enhancement in accelerometer calibration. For the x-axis (approximately along-track), the overlap comparison residuals of the estimated bias factors decrease from 1.37 to 1.17 nm/s2 with PCV corrections and are further reduced to 0.78 nm/s2 with IAR constraints, improving accuracy by 14.6 % and 33.1 %, respectively. Even more notably, for the y-axis (approximately cross-track), the simultaneous application of PCV corrections and IAR constraints improves the overlap consistency of the estimated bias factors by over 84 % and 93 % for GRACE and GRACE-FO, respectively. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhao, B.; Gu, X.; Hu, R.; Wu, S.; Deng, Z.
Title: Nonlinear dynamic modeling and analysis of rigid-flexible coupling spacecrafts with time-varying temperature distribution
Abstract: When establishing the dynamic model of a rigid-coupled spacecraft, the traditional linear method requires the flexible structure to satisfy the small deformation assumption. However, the wide range of motion of the large spacecraft may lead to nonlinear deformation of the flexible structure. In this paper, the nonlinear dynamic model of the rigid-flexible coupling spacecraft is established during in-orbit operation. Firstly, the kinetic energy of the rigid-flexible spacecraft is derived by incorporating the nonlinear deformations with second-order coupling terms. Secondly, the total potential energy of the system is obtained by thermal analysis, including deformation potential energy and gravitational potential energy. Then, the nonlinear time-varying coupled dynamics equations of the rigid-flexible coupling spacecraft are obtained by the Hamilton’s variational principle. The discrete equations are derived by using the assumed modal method (AMM). Finally, the results obtained through numerical calculations indicate that the nonlinear model accurately predicts the dynamical stiffening phenomenon as the attitude rotational velocity of the rigid hub increases. At the same time, the effects of the gravity gradient (GG), the structure size and the temperature on the response of the spacecraft are obtained. © © 2026. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Fu, S.; Zeng, W.; Zhao, Q.; Zhao, W.; Hao, L.; Hu, B.
Title: Soot formation characteristics of the RP-3 aviation kerosene/ oxygen laminar premixed flame under certain fuel-rich combustion
Abstract: As mission requirements continue to advance in the aerospace field, the development of power systems with wide-range operation capabilities has become imperative. To achieve efficient propulsion under wide-range operating conditions, fuel-rich combustion strategies have been extensively adopted in applied engineering systems. However, this approach significantly intensifies soot formation, which negatively affects combustion efficiency and harms the functionality of downstream components through particulate deposition. Consequently, in order to investigate the formation mechanism and microscopic characteristics of soot in RP-3 aviation kerosene/oxygen mixture under fuel-rich conditions, the spatial distribution of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons was measured using the planar laser-induced fluorescence technique. Simultaneously, the temperature, volume fraction, and particle size distribution of soot particles were also obtained using the laser-induced incandescence method. Soot samples were collected using thermophoretic sampling, and their microstructure was analyzed via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. The results indicate that as the equivalence ratio increases from 1.67 to 1.91, the concentration of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons is raised, the volume concentration of soot is increased, and the average particle size is enlarged. Evidently, fuel-rich conditions can significantly promote the formation of soot precursors and the agglomeration/growth of soot particles. Through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy characterization, it was discovered that the fractal dimension of the soot microcrystalline structure shows a decline. Additionally, the curvature was increased while the interlayer spacing was enlarged, leading to the formation of a looser and more complex soot particle structure. Critically, it has been demonstrated that precise control of the equivalence ratio during the design of gas generators and engines can effectively regulate soot morphology. This discovery holds significant implications and offers valuable guidance for optimizing practical combustion systems. © © 2026. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, Y.; Zhang, W.; Wang, Y.; Huang, D.; Li, X.; Wang, L.
Title: Toward high-efficiency design for jet-based flow control in high-pressure turbine vanes
Abstract: Variable geometry turbines offer significant efficiency benefits under off‑design conditions, but conventional adjustable guide vanes face severe challenges in harsh high‑pressure turbine environments. Jet-based flow control (JFC), which operates without moving parts, offers a promising alternative. This study employs numerical simulations to investigate the effects of aerodynamic and geometric parameters on JFC performance, evaluating net benefit and economic feasibility based on outlet mass flow and regulation efficiency. The results show that JFC introduces complex flow structures, nearly doubling the total losses compared to the baseline. The choked primary flow induces a regulation saturation effect, stabilizing outlet flow variation and ensuring operational robustness in real turbine conditions. Increasing the jet pressure ratio expands the regulation range but does not improve efficiency, which remains around 0.55 under the baseline geometry. Consequently, geometric optimization is critical. Counterflow jetting at 60° significantly enhances efficiency by strengthening the upstream flow structure, increasing the average regulation efficiency to 1.06. The optimal jet position is not fixed but varies with jet pressure ratio ( JPR ). At low JPR, jetting at the throat position maintains high efficiency. And at high JPR , the jet position shifts upstream to address the downstream shift of the jet peak penetration point, thus maximizing the regulation range. Wider slots reduce regulation efficiency due to increased jet contribution to the outlet flow, thus the smallest feasible slot width is recommended. In addition, slot‑internal losses are found to rise with JPR and counterflow angle, especially under high‑pressure‑ratio conditions. Based on the above findings, this study not only clarifies the fundamental mechanisms governing JFC performance in high-pressure turbines but also establishes the design-priority for achieving high-efficiency flow control. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Deng, H.; Li, H.; Zhang, S.; Zhu, M.; Qiang, X.; Teng, J.
Author full names: Deng, Hefang (57219602145); Li, Huihui (59068162700); Zhang, Songan (57205544446); Zhu, Mingmin (55607547000); Qiang, Xiaoqing (21743585900); Teng, Jinfang (36899306600)
Title: Aerodynamic performance in a boundary layer ingesting fan with swirl distortion
Abstract: Boundary layer ingestion (BLI) propulsion can reduce fuel burn significantly compared with conventional tube-and-wing aircraft. One of the engineering challenges for BLI propulsion is that the engine fan must safely operate with continuous coupled total pressure and swirl distortions, but most of the previous studies often ignored the impact of swirl distortion. In this paper, full-annulus unsteady Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes simulations are carried out to investigate the aerodynamic performance of a BLI fan under cruise and sideslip conditions. Combined with entropy loss based on the dissipation method, this work focuses on investigating the nonuniform and unsteady loss mechanisms with representative total pressure and swirl distortions. The results show that the presence of the swirl distortion reduces the stall margin by 2.48 % at the cruise distortion, whereas that of the sideslip distortions significantly impacts both overall performance and distortion transfer. BLI inlet distortions cause highly nonuniform loss distributions. Near the blade hub, the negative swirl upstream of the rotor leads to severe corner separation, resulting in the highest entropy loss coefficient at the blade hub. Near the blade tip, the shock structure undergoes significant circumferential changes, with only a few passages maintaining the shock structure at the design condition. The shock waves of non-design conditions cause significant flow losses by interacting with the boundary layer and tip leakage flow. Additionally, due to radial migration flow and flow redistribution, the flow fields between the low span and the blade tip interact with each other, leading to an enhancement of the loss fluctuation at the blade tip by the high loss fluctuation at the low span. These findings not only first and systematically clarify the effect mechanisms of coupled total pressure and swirl distortions on transonic BLI fans, but can also provide useful guidance for many turbine engines that inevitably suffer from coupled total pressure and swirl distortions. © © 2026. Authors: Song, S.; Li, R.
Title: Manifold learning-based reconstruction and prediction of unsteady flow fields
Abstract: The spatiotemporal coupling in unsteady flow fields leads to high nonlinearity and complexity, significantly increasing the computational cost of computational fluid dynamics (CFD). Reduced-order model (ROM) techniques are widely employed to extract intrinsic flow structures and enhance computational efficiency. In order to efficiently and accurately predict unsteady high-dimensional flow fields, the reduced-order framework is proposed by combining manifold learning and long short-term memory (LSTM) network, named as ISOMAP-LSTM. Firstly, a nonlinear ROM is constructed using ISOMAP to project time-varying high-dimensional flow field into a low-dimensional manifold space. Secondly, the LSTM network is employed to learn the temporal dynamics of the low-dimensional flow field. Finally, this integrated framework facilitates rapid prediction of future flow behavior. Unlike traditional linear ROMs such as proper orthogonal decomposition and dynamic mode decomposition, the proposed framework can precisely capture the highly nonlinear flow structures and achieve high-accuracy reconstruction and prediction, even with a much smaller reduced dimension. For cylinder flow cases under both constant and varying Reynolds number, the proposed framework outperforms traditional linear ROMs in modeling and predicting vortex evolution with constant/varying frequency, achieving a two-order magnitude improvement in computational efficiency over CFD. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Gu, X.; Qiu, G.; Fei, W.; Yu, H.; Liu, Y.; Liu, C.
Author full names: Gu, Xiangyu (57190492797); Qiu, Gongwang (56288357500); Fei, Wanghua (57211355883); Yu, Haichuan (57193927494); Liu, Yanchao (60188677400); Liu, Chuanzhen (56106393400)
Title: Efficient bayesian aerodynamic shape optimization informed by the waveriding-dependent area theory
Abstract: Practical waverider configuration design requires a careful balance between achieving high aerodynamic efficiency and satisfying engineering constraints. While aerodynamic shape optimization provides a powerful framework for this purpose, its effectiveness is often limited by the high computational cost of exploring high-dimensional design spaces. This study proposes a physics-informed design method integrating the theoretical waveriding-dependent area concept into a Bayesian optimization framework. The core lies in a physics-informed parameterization strategy that leverages theoretical aerodynamic principles to reduce geometric dimensionality strategically. In addition, a tailored Bayesian optimization engine with a state-driven constraint-handling mechanism was developed to enhance robustness and efficiency. The proposed approach was validated on a double-swept waverider by comparing a 30-variable physics-informed design to a conventional 38-variable design. The analysis showed the physics-informed approach was significantly more efficient, with the conventional method requiring approximately 40% more Computational Fluid Dynamics evaluations to converge. Additionally, high-fidelity Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes validation confirmed that the physics-informed design achieved a higher final lift-to-drag ratio at a comparable internal volume. These results demonstrate that physics-informed dimensionality reduction is a powerful approach for improving the design efficiency of complex shape optimization tasks. © 2026

Authors: Park, J.Y.; Kim, S.; Khemka, S.; Lee, C.Y.; Gao, Z.
Author full names: Park, Jun-young (60329636000); Kim, Subin (60329406100); Khemka, Samiksha (60329170400); Lee, Cheuk Yan (60329636100); Gao, Zhenyu (57193865609)
Title: Integrating urban analytics in low-noise airspace design for urban air mobility
Abstract: Aircraft noise is a significant constraint in the urban integration of urban air mobility (UAM) system. Recent research has explored various approaches for UAM noise control within the context of urban airspace design, which could facilitate efficient noise-aware flight trajectory planning and enable integrated urban airspace management. However, a notable research gap lies in the lack of an urban analytics component in current noise-aware airspace designs. Urban analytics is essential because UAM operates in complex urban environments where the impact of noise is influenced by residents’ responses to UAM noise emissions. Leveraging modern smart city data, we address this gap through an integrated approach that combines geographic information system techniques, high-quality urban geospatial and spatio-temporal datasets, and an aircraft noise model to create 3D noise-aware no-fly zones. We also introduce a tailored optimal path planning algorithm HFPI-A* that considers both no-fly zones and flight performance constraints. A case study applies the proposed approach to a detailed examination in Hong Kong, revealing the sensitivities of various key factors in the design process and demonstrating their impact on the design outcomes. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xiao, Z.; He, B.; Zhang, B.; Chen, Y.; Cai, G.; Weng, H.
Title: A physics-informed neural network model for viscous compressible turbulent flows simulations within a rocket engine nozzle
Abstract: The simulation of rocket exhaust flow is challenging due to its highly compressible, turbulent, and nonlinear nature, as well as the significant computational costs of traditional numerical methods. Physics-informed neural network (PINN), integrated with turbulence models, have emerged as a promising tool for solving partial differential equations in complex flows. In this study, a Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS)-based PINN framework is extended by incorporating the shear stress transport (SST) k - ω model to simulate high-speed compressible turbulent flow in a rocket nozzle, accounting for boundary layer effects. The use of a signed distance function and spatial distribution weight enables accurate resolution of boundary layers and turbulence. To balance computational efficiency and predictive reliability, the model was first trained on coarse-grid data to test the network´s ability to capture complex flows, while transfer learning was applied by incorporating 50% of the boundary-layer data and 5% of the off-boundary-layer data extracted from the high-fidelity grid to enhance the accuracy of the predictions. Results show that embedding the Euler equations significantly improves prediction compared to data-driven methods, while the RANS-based PINN further reduces errors near the wall and throat. Relative errors for all five variables remain below 5.2% across five test sets. The transfer learning strategy minimizes the number of training samples from 533,715 on the full high-fidelity grid to 279,840, substantially lowering Graphics Processing Unit(GPU) memory requirements while maintaining interpolation errors below 5.5% and keeping the extrapolation errors of most cases within about 11%. Moreover, the PINN method greatly improves efficiency, achieving speedups of 565 for a single case and 1037 for five cases with matrix-based inputs. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Rahman, M.A.; Prantik, M.F.R.; Islam, S.M.T.; Rahman, A.; Masum, S.H.; Hossain, M.A.
Author full names: Rahman, Md Atiqur (59140971100); Prantik, Md Fahim Razi (60328341800); Islam, S. M.Tarikul (58728963300); Rahman, Atiqur (59658200200); Masum, Sajjad Hossain (59666412700); Hossain, Md Arman (60328171600)
Title: Hyperdimensional computing and ridge regression for airfoil aerodynamic prediction : A physics-informed machine learning framework
Abstract: Conventional computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations and wind tunnel tests for predicting airfoil aerodynamic coefficients are computationally expensive and time-consuming for rapid design iterations in early-stage aircraft development. While machine learning offers faster alternatives, most models are black-box systems that lack interpretability and often produce physically inconsistent results. This paper presents a framework combining Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) with Ridge Regression for accurate prediction of airfoil aerodynamic coefficients (lift C<inf>L</inf> and drag C<inf>D</inf>). Key innovations include an Adaptive HDC Encoder that dynamically re-weights level hypervectors based on feature variance, a Physics-Informed Penalty enforcing aerodynamic constraints (e.g., dC<inf>L</inf>dα≈2π near zero angle of attack), and 10-fold stratified cross-validation. The framework encodes continuous features (Reynolds number, angle of attack, Mach number) into high-dimensional binary vectors (D=10,000) and employs regularized linear regression with thin-airfoil theory constraints. Validated on 260 high-fidelity CFD simulations of the NACA 0012 airfoil across Reynolds numbers from 5 × 105 to 107 and angles of attack from −4∘ to 20∘, the model achieves R 2 values of 0.9952 for C<inf>L</inf> and 0.9990 for C<inf>D</inf> , with superior post-stall performance compared to FFBPNN, Genetic Algorithm, and Bayesian methods. The proposed HDC-Ridge model offers a lightweight, interpretable, and physically consistent alternative to computationally expensive CFD simulations and opaque deep learning architectures, enabling rapid aerodynamic analysis and optimization in early-stage design. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, S.; Zhang, X.; Xue, H.; Fang, X.; Li, X.
Title: Knowledge-guided graph transformer for gaze-based pilot operation recognition in dynamic flight operations
Abstract: Real-time and accurate recognition of pilots’ operational intent in dynamic flight tasks is critical for enhancing aviation safety, preventing human error, enabling intelligent decision support, and supporting online human-reliability assessment. Eye-tracking data, an objective physiological signal obtainable in real time in the cockpit, provides crucial evidence for inferring intent. However, the volatility of time-series gaze data and its tight, nonlinear coupling with complex operational responses limit the accuracy of approaches that rely on gaze features alone. Methods based on physical parameters capture action outcomes rather than intent, while purely data-driven models often generalize poorly and offer limited interpretability due to the absence of task-logic guidance. This study introduces a Knowledge-enhanced Graph Transformer Network (KGTN) that integrates structured domain knowledge as a computable guidance signal with dynamic eye-movement behavior, and explicitly models their nonlinear interactions. A task knowledge graph grounded in a cognitive-activity taxonomy is constructed and encoded by a Relational Graph Neural Network, alongside a multi-scale Patch-Transformer and a Hierarchical Task-Context Aware Operation Recognition Module (HTCA-ORM) for knowledge-guided fusion and precise operation recognition. On a high-fidelity flight-simulation dataset, KGTN attains 87.63% accuracy and 87.48% weighted F1, outperforming a range of baselines. Ablation and interpretability analyses indicate strong potential for high-precision, reliable aviation human-factors analytics. © 2025

Authors: Kassem, A.; Romeo, S.A.S.; Tiwari, B.; San, O.; Kara, K.
Author full names: Kassem, Ashraf (58862615500); Romeo, Shafi Al Salman (58861869700); Tiwari, Bipin (59666930800); San, Omer (26424808900); Kara, Kursat (22950528200)
Title: Data-driven reduced-order modeling framework for blunt-body aerodynamic characterization
Abstract: Atmospheric entry vehicles experience highly nonlinear, unsteady aerodynamic loads that drive complex dynamic behavior. This study introduces a physics-informed reduced-order modeling framework based on the Energy-Ranked Dynamic Mode Decomposition (ER-DMD) algorithm to link unsteady flow dynamics with the pitching motion of the Genesis sample return capsule. Time-resolved, cell-wise aerodynamic moment distributions are computed directly from surface pressure and shear stress data obtained via Free-Flight CFD simulations in one-degree-of-freedom, free-to-pitch motion. The ER-DMD algorithm is applied to extract energetically ranked modes, from which a minimal predictive model is identified through a combined relative information content and maximum relative error of the angle-of-attack peaks. The proposed model demonstrates strong predictive capability, achieving maximum peak amplitude errors below 1∘ for the stable 20∘ case using only four DMD modes, with root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 0.37∘. For the unstable 2∘ case, accurate reconstruction of the pitching dynamics requires a larger modal basis (31 modes), yielding maximum peak errors below 0.5∘ and an RMSE of 0.12∘. These results highlight the ability of the reduced-order model to capture dominant oscillatory behavior in the stable regime, while emphasizing the role of higher-order modes in representing nonlinear instability mechanisms. Overall, the framework effectively reconstructs the dominant flow-structure interactions and preserves the essential dynamic behavior across both stable and unstable regimes. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Author full names: Wang, Yingjie (57193569415); Zhang, Junhua (60284120800); Zhang, Peng (57221241372); Zheng, Zixuan (57217011004); Zhao, Qijun (8356099600)
Title: Finite-time pursuit-evasion spacecraft game control by fast convergent rate neural network learning
Abstract: In this paper, the finite-time pursuit-evasion (PE) game problem is investigated for spacecraft control under both complete and incomplete information scenarios. The spacecraft PE game is formulated as a zero-sum game in which the pursuer strives to minimize the relative distance, while the evader strives to maximize it. To achieve finite-time capture, a novel nonlinear quadratic cost function is proposed to construct the Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs (HJI) equation. First, the finite-time PE game control policies for the pursuer and evader are derived by solving the Riccati equation and HJI equation under complete information. Furthermore, a critic-based reinforcement learning method is developed to learn the unknown control strategy of the evader with fast convergence. Finally, a numerical example of the spacecraft PE system is presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, F.; Yang, H.; Li, L.; Ren, Y.
Title: Damage mechanism and impact resistance of bionic spirally laminated CFRP laminates under high-speed hail impact
Abstract: This work addresses the challenge of high-velocity hail impact on aerospace composite structures, aiming to improve the impact resistance of CFRP laminates through a bionic spiral design and reveal their damage mechanisms. The research examines these mechanisms using a coupled SPH–FEM numerical model, where the Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) method is applied to simulate hail fragmentation, while the Hashin failure criterion and cohesive elements are employed to capture intralaminar and interlaminar failure. Four helicoidal design schemes, Helicoidal-recursive (HR), Helicoidal-exponential (HE), Helicoidal-semicircular (HS), and Linear-helicoidal (LH), are systematically compared, with twelve layup configurations analyzed under hail impact at 160.16 m/s. Results demonstrate a clear superiority of the HE configuration, which exhibits the lowest damage extent: fiber tensile damage as low as 5.09%, fiber compression damage of 5.63%, and a delamination area of 3.60%. In contrast, the HS configuration exhibits the most severe fiber damage, up to 7.40%. While the HS laminates absorbed the most energy (∼540 mJ), the HE laminates achieved the optimal balance with superior damage control. The results reveal that the exponential-helicoidal stacking effectively delays delamination propagation and reduces fiber breakage by promoting crack deflection and efficient stress redistribution. These findings provide key design strategies for developing high-performance and impact-resistant composite materials. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xiong, C.; Wang, X.; Cheng, M.; Chen, Z.
Author full names: Xiong, Cheng (57653638200); Wang, Xin (56073657100); Cheng, Mengmeng (60310927000); Chen, Zhenqian (16041718700)
Title: Coupled transpiration and film cooling characteristics of injector porous plate in a 45-element rocket thrust chamber
Abstract: This study investigates the coupled active thermal protection of transpiration cooling and film cooling for rocket thrust chambers. A computational fluid dynamics model integrating combustion reactions and a coupled porous-media/film cooling process was established. The performance and enhancement mechanisms were analyzed, focusing on the effects of injector and ignition hole geometries. Parametric studies examined the influence of coolant mass flow rate, supply temperature, and structural thermal conductivity on system temperature, pressure drop, cooling effectiveness, and flow distribution. Key results show that film cooling synergistically reduces porous plate and injector temperatures. Increasing coolant mass flow improves porous plate cooling (up to 154.4 K temperature drop and 6.2 % effectiveness increase) but raises supply pressure demand. A vortex generated by film cooling induces a non-monotonic trend in the minimum temperature of the porous plate and injector with increasing flow rate. Higher structural thermal conductivity reduces porous plate cooling performance (temperature increase of 12.3 %, effectiveness decreases of 5.0 %) but enhances thermal protection for the injector and igniter, lowering their maximum temperature by 112.7 K. The findings provide guidance for the design of coupled cooling systems in high-heat-flux thrust chambers. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, S.; Gao, C.; Shen, Y.; Zhang, J.; Lin, B.
Title: Current prediction of damaged solar array and multi-constraint attitude optimization for the DRO-A satellite
Abstract: The Distant Retrograde Orbit (DRO) - A satellite's solar arrays were damaged in the cislunar space exploration mission due to a launch anomaly, rendering it impossible to obtain sufficient energy via solar array rotation. In this case, the DRO-A satellite cannot obtain sufficient energy in its normal pointing mode, which makes finding an energy-optimal attitude that simultaneously meets the conditions for effective star tracker operation and visibility to ground stations an urgent problem to be solved. To address this issue, a two-step attitude planning strategy is proposed. In the first step, an improved particle swarm optimization-radial basis function (PSO-RBF) algorithm is adopted to predict the solar array current. The core innovation of our method lies in the dual-particle swarm search, wherein each swarm specializes in tracking data from a distinct operating condition. This strategy ensures an effective balance of current prediction accuracy across all operational scenarios. In the second step, under multiple constraints, including operational star trackers and visibility to ground stations, the energy-optimal attitude that satisfies these constraints is obtained using sequential quadratic programming (SQP). On-orbit telemetry data show that the proposed algorithm enables high-precision prediction of solar array current, with a root mean square error of less than 0.1A. Attitude maneuver of the DRO-A satellite is performed using the optimal attitude designed in this paper. The stable in-orbit operation of the DRO-A satellite effectively validates the long-term attitude planning strategy proposed in this paper. This achievement serves as a valuable reference for handling anomalies in future spacecraft missions. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Title: Robotic satellite attitude pointing adjustment method via multibody angular momentum transfer
Abstract: To address the issue of high-precision attitude pointing adjustment for robotic satellites without propellant cost during long-term on-orbit missions, a novel satellite attitude control method is proposed based on multibody angular momentum transfer via space manipulators. The mapping relationship between manipulator joint motion and satellite attitude dynamics is established as a geometric model based on the conservation of angular momentum, enabling monotonic quasilinear control to reduce computational complexity. A coordinated control strategy combining coarse attitude adjustment using reaction wheels and fine-tuning via manipulators is proposed to achieve high-precision pointing control over a wide angular range. The effectiveness and feasibility of the approach are validated through simulation experiments. Compared with conventional reaction wheel-based systems, the proposed method significantly improved the pointing accuracy and robustness without fuel-consumption and angular momentum offloading. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, W.; Wang, Y.; Gu, X.; Tu, S.; Wu, T.; Wang, K.
Title: Shock-wave interference effects on flow and heat transfer mechanisms in combined micro-hole transpiration cooling
Abstract: Transpiration cooling demonstrates substantial potential for thermal protection of hypersonic vehicles under extreme aerodynamic heating. However, the interactions between incident shock waves and boundary layer disturb coolant flow distribution, induce a separation vortex region near the wall, and result in non-uniform surface temperature distribution. In addition, the presence of cooling film modifies the shock structure, adversely affecting thermal protection. In this research, a combined micro-hole transpiration cooling scheme with a non-uniform injection strategy was proposed. The evolution of the separation vortex region under shock waves and its impact on temperature uniformity were analyzed. The effects of shock wave redistribution on cooling performance were investigated, and the micro-hole region was classified based on flow and heat transfer characteristics to optimize coolant allocation. The numerical method was validated using PIV flow field measurements, heat transfer experiments, and oblique shock wave theory. The results indicate that when the gradient micro-hole region accounts for 60%, the upstream shift of the flow separation point is suppressed, and the pressure peak decreases by 11.37% compared with the case without coolant injection. The extent of the separation vortex region is mainly determined by the blowing intensity of the transpiration surface, whereas temperature uniformity depends on the effective thermal protection area. Furthermore, the cooling mechanism under shock wave interference is divided into the coolant accumulation region, separation vortex region, and boundary layer development region. By employing non-uniform injection, allocating 30% of the coolant mass flow rate to the accumulation region enhances hot surface temperature uniformity by 40.11% relative to the baseline structure, while maintaining effective thermal protection. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, P.; Zhao, C.; Wang, Y.; Zheng, Z.
Author full names: Zhang, Peng (57221241372); Zhao, Chenhao (60301333600); Wang, Yingjie (57193569415); Zheng, Zixuan (57217011004)
Title: Inverse reinforcement Q-Learning distributed control for satellite formation
Abstract: In this paper, a data-driven distributed inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) is proposed to achieve relative motion control in satellite formation flying, which is based on imitation control method. The target is to infer the underlying cost function and derive a consistent control policy from expert demonstrations of satellite formation flying. This method does not depend on the system dynamic model information, and only uses the local states and input data of the experts and learners under the communication topology. Firstly, a discrete-time multi-satellite formation system model is established in a leader-follower structure. Then, a distributed optimal controller is given for the multi-satellite expert system by using the discrete-time algebraic Riccati equation. Accordingly, a model-based IRL algorithm is developed to replicate the expert control strategy. Furthermore, a fully data-driven inverse reinforcement Q-learning algorithm is designed by using the trajectory data of experts and learners to realize model-free imitation learning. Theoretical analysis proves the stability and convergence of the proposed algorithm. Finally, through the numerical simulation of the four-satellite formation system, the proposed method is shown to accurately learn the expert control strategy, enabling it to achieve optimal consensus performance. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, X.; Fan, X.; Xiong, B.
Author full names: Wang, Xiaojuan (57203137676); Fan, Xiaoqiang (7403393750); Xiong, Bing (57189594616)
Title: The influence of permeability anisotropy on transpiration cooling under uniform and non-uniform pressure conditions
Abstract: The demand for thermal protection in high-Mach-number flights is becoming increasingly stringent. The integration of ceramic matrix composites with transpiration cooling has emerged as a new and promising direction to meet this challenge. However, the permeability anisotropy of materials may affect the cooling performance. This study investigates the mechanisms by which the permeability anisotropy of material influences transpiration cooling under both uniform and non-uniform outlet pressure conditions. The results show that under a uniform outlet pressure without blockage, permeability anisotropy minimally affects the flow and temperature fields. The blockage will induce local high temperature. Compared with the non-blockage condition, with a decrease in lateral permeability, the peak solid temperature at the hot end increases by 4.97%, 6.07%, 8.61% and 9.37%, respectively. Under non-uniform outlet pressure conditions without blockage, a lower lateral permeability is more beneficial for cooling. When a blockage is present, the effect of lateral permeability depends on whether reverse permeation exists. In the presence of reverse permeation, blockage inhibits reverse permeation and enhances cooling performance, with the peak temperature along the central axis dropping by over 100 K. In the absence of reverse permeation, blockage leads to locally high temperatures, but due to the sufficient flow of coolant, overall cooling remains adequate. © © 2026. Author full names: Zhou, Hang (59279677700); Shen, Qiang (55903899800); Xie, Yongchun (15756562800); Razoumny, Vladimir Yu (25628685500); Wu, Shufan (57190223905)
Title: Safety guaranteed whole-body coordinated control in task space of the redundant Free-Flying space manipulator on SE(3)
Abstract: The redundant free-flying space manipulator (RFFSM) has emerged as a major research focus, providing enhanced operational flexibility and robustness for increasingly complex tasks, while the whole-body coordinated control under unstructured environments and multiple complex safety constraints is still a critical challenge. To this end, this article presents a novel hierarchical safety-critical control framework for the RFFSM, explicitly modelled on the Special Euclidean group SE(3), to perform whole-body coordinated control in the task space. A robust nominal controller is developed to simultaneously control the spacecraft base and the end-effector (EE) in the task space based on the full pose configuration error defined on SE(3), integrated with a disturbance observer (DOB) to actively compensate for external disturbances. In addition, a robust rectified control barrier function (R2eCBF) is presented to enforce safety constraints, including joint angle limits, singularity avoidance, and whole-body collision avoidance constraints, and is further integrated into the safety filter through quadratic programming (QP) to modify the nominal control in a minimal way. The effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method are validated through comparative simulations on a free-flying space robot mounted with a 7-DOF manipulator, demonstrating enhanced safety and robust performance. © © 2025. Authors: Yao, Y.; Wang, C.; Hu, B.
Title: Evaluation of particle motion sensitivity and energy performance to axial gap in aerospace turbine rotor-stator cavity based on CFD-DEM
Abstract: Efficient flow and thermal management within rotor-stator cavities (RSCs) are crucial for the thermal reliability and durability of high-speed aerospace turbines. In environments with particulate contamination, complex particle-gas-wall interactions alter energy transport and surface wear mechanisms, posing new challenges for cavity design. To address this issue, a high-fidelity CFD-DEM coupling framework is developed to quantify the coupled effects of axial gap ratios ( G = 0.075-0.175) and particle volume fractions ( ε = 0-1.07 %) on multiphase flow, heat transfer, and energy performance in RSCs. Results reveal a nonlinear regulation of flow structures and particle dynamics by axial gap: smaller gaps enhance local shear and promote rapid radial migration, whereas larger gaps increase particle residence and accumulation. Particle inclusion disrupts thermal uniformity, forming transient high-temperature clusters, while moderate gaps maximize overall heat transfer efficiency. Wall wear exhibits strong spatial asymmetry and gap dependence, with rotor erosion intensified at large gaps and stator wear dominant at smaller ones. Energy analysis shows that particle loading increases pressure-work losses but mitigates thermal losses; notably, total energy loss rises by up to 16.35 % at small gaps but decreases by 5.90 % under large gaps. The findings provide quantitative insights for optimizing RSC geometry, improving cooling effectiveness, and enhancing the energy efficiency and reliability of high-temperature rotating components. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Rea, S.; Guida, M.
Title: Application of neural networks for real-time erosion monitoring and adaptive attitude/morphology control in low earth orbit/very low earth orbit
Abstract: Satellites operating in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and Very Low Earth Orbit (VLEO) are subject to extreme environmental stressors, including high energy radiation, significant thermal gradients, atomic oxygen (AO) erosion, and frequent impacts from orbital debris. These conditions pose substantial challenges to structural integrity and mission longevity. To increase robustness and extend operational life, smart materials equipped with embedded sensors and self-actuating mechanisms capable of real-time structural adaptability are integrated. This study presents a model that is parameterized using the mission-specific characteristics of a 10U CubeSat configuration, leveraging a Physics Informed Neural Network (PINN). The model incorporates inertial properties, control authority constraints and environmental conditions to predict attitude dynamics feedback and trigger adaptive responses based on micrometroids-induced erosion or penetration. The system monitors key parameters, such as temperature fluctuations, surface erosion, and penetration events transmitting sensor data to an onboard processor. A deep neural network interprets this information to trigger corrective actions: either through dynamic shape morphing or attitude control maneuvers. Coupled quaternion-based kinematics, rigid body rotational dynamics, and AO driven surface degradation are simulated in a custom virtual environment to generate synthetic, noise corrupted datasets. These data pairs are used to train a feedforward PINN, which maps erosion states to discrete rotational deviations and surface morphology adaptation triggers. Unlike traditional black box models, the PINN is grounded in physical laws, enabling real-time, onboard inference of erosion induced anomalies with improved robustness. While achieving robust average performance, capturing the full complexity of thresholded, rare impulse events remain challenging, and reliance on synthetic data alone cannot encompass every in-orbit phenomenon. Nevertheless, grounding the inference in first principles allows the network to generalize more reliably when confronted with novel erosion scenarios. The lightweight surrogate architecture is optimized for flight representative hardware, supporting fully autonomous anomaly detection and response without ground intervention and the integration of sensor driven morphology adaptation with conventional control strategies broadens the design space for resilient small satellites. Moreover, the framework is inherently extensible: it can be applied to more complex geometries and multi-axis erosion effects, and it readily accommodates hybrid training that incorporates on-ground test data or live in-orbit telemetry. © © 2025.
