Authors: Li, Z.; Wang, L.; Zhang, W.; Wang, K.; Ye, X.; Chen, Y.; Jiang, Q.
Author full names: Li, Zhitong (57204556996); Wang, Luqi (60284759700); Zhang, Wanting (59931791300); Wang, Kaixuan (59472538700); Ye, Xiufen (8055047200); Chen, Yunsai (55878617000); Jiang, Qinghua (57208782323)
Title: Experimental research on the water strike phenomenon of the rotor for multi-rotor hybrid aerial underwater vehicles
Abstract: The water strike phenomenon (WSP) significantly compromises rotor performance, posing considerable challenges to the stable control and operational safety of multi-rotor hybrid aerial underwater vehicles (HAUVs). In this paper, through experiments, the influence of the water surface effect (WSE) with both horizontal and inclined attitude on rotor performance is explored, and the flow field evolution process of the WSP is analyzed. During the water strike process, the rotor performance characteristics is compared and analyzed, and the influence of the WSE on rotor performance is revealed. The influence law of various factors on rotor performance is further explored in both still water and wave environments. The experimental results indicate that: compared to operation in free space, rotor performance exhibits more intense fluctuations and instability due to the alternating interaction with different environmental medium and the coupled effects of the second WSP. A substantial increase in rotational resistance leads to increased rotor torque, which in turn results in a loss of rotational speed, as well as an increase in motor current. Concurrently, as a significant portion of energy is dissipated into the water, the force effect of the rotor is severely degraded. Rotor lift is primarily governed by the WSE and rotational speed, and the influence of the WSE on rotor torque, rotational speed and motor current can be negligible. In both still water and wave environments, changes in various influence factors will alter rotor torque and the intensity of the WSE, consequently leading to changes in overall rotor performance. Furthermore, compared to that in still water environments, rotor performance in wave environments exhibits distinct periodic fluctuations and more pronounced instabilities. The results help to promote a deeper understanding in the WSP, and assist in improving the water strike avoidance method from “passive avoidance” to “active avoidance”. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, T.; Sun, X.; Liu, Q.; Du, X.; Bui, T.Q.
Title: Geometrically nonlinear electro-mechanical coupled multi-patch IGA for distributed piezoelectric structures based on Nitsche’s method
Abstract: Numerical simulations play a crucial role in predicting electro-mechanical coupling nonlinear behavior of distributed piezoelectric smart structures, which have been greatly implemented in real-world engineering practices. This paper presents an original investigation into the application of non-conforming multi-patch methodology for analyzing the geometrically nonlinear behavior of distributed electroelastic coupled structures, effectively overcoming the limitations of conventional single-patch NURBS-based isogeometric analysis (IGA). In the framework of the first-order shear deformation theory (FSDT), von Kármán nonlinear theory, and Total Lagrangian (TL) formulations, the geometrically nonlinear multi-patch IGA formulations for piezoelectric smart structures are developed with regard to Nitsche’s method. Specifically, Nitsche’s method enforces field variable continuity across coupling interfaces between adjacent NURBS patches. Based on the verification of isotropic plates, the validated multi-patch geometrically nonlinear IGA is extended to investigate nonlinear static/dynamic behaviors and sensor output characteristics of fully-covered and distributed piezo-laminated plates. For broader applications, both conventional piezoceramics and advanced macro-fiber composites (MFC) are considered. The systematic comparative results demonstrate that the proposed methodology provides accuracy and efficiency in analyzing geometrically nonlinear behavior of distributed piezoelectric smart plates. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Garofano, A.; Riccio, A.
Title: Crashworthiness of a composite fuselage barrel and related certification aspects: Evaluation of the passengers safety under critical drop test conditions by numerical simulations
Abstract: The increasing use of composite materials in aviation has enabled the design of lighter and more performant aircraft, while maintaining passenger safety as a primary design criterion. However, the development of fully composite structures raises the need to thoroughly investigate their crashworthiness performance and the potential they may offer in impact scenarios. In this work, a composite fuselage barrel section has been modelled in Ls-Dyna to simulate different drop test scenarios. Specifically, several impact conditions have been analysed, varying both impact velocity and attitude to investigate the structural response of the composite fuselage and the passengers safety. The use of an Anthropomorphic Test Device enabled the evaluation of the lumbar load perceived by the occupant and its comparison with regulatory thresholds, above which severe spinal injuries may occur. The comparison of simulations results at different impact velocities, with zero pitch and roll angles, highlighted the effective crashworthy features of the composite structure and the ability to ensure occupant protection even under more demanding conditions than those required by certification standards. The comparison of simulations results with nonzero roll angles underlined the ability of the composite fuselage to withstand such impact conditions without compromising either its structural integrity or the safety of the passengers. Overall, results confirmed the capability of composite structures to provide efficient energy absorption during impact and effective occupant protection. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: An, K.; Huang, W.; Liu, S.; Han, T.
Title: Hybrid multi-constraint reentry trajectory planning: Insights from convex optimization and predictor-corrector
Abstract: This paper develops a hybrid multi-constraint reentry trajectory optimization framework to address the challenges of reentry trajectory planning in complex environments. The proposed method is inspired by convex programming and prediction-correction techniques, and inherits the algorithm structure of both the prediction phase and correction phase. In the prediction phase, a 3-DoF non-convex trajectory planning model is transformed into a convex problem through a lossless convexification (LC) methodology. Next, in the correction phase, an improved sequential convex programming (SCP) algorithm is utilized to deal with potential issues of diminishing accuracy and infeasible solutions. Specifically, a high-quality initial guess is first prepared for this phase to accelerate the optimization process. In order to ensure a safe and reliable reentry trajectory, a feasibility-recovery strategy is designed that includes some skills such as trust region optimization, parameter adaptation, and warm starting to guide the optimization process effectively. Finally, simulation results demonstrate the feasibility, superiority, and robustness of the proposed framework, and the final optimized objective cost converges to 1×10−9 for each simulation case, and achieves the notable improvement in optimization accuracy and total optimization time compared to basic case. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhu, G.; Zheng, Z.; Guo, Y.; Sun, P.; Zhang, J.
Author full names: Zhu, Guangtong (59666376100); Zheng, Zixuan (57217011004); Guo, Yufei (57210412962); Sun, Pengyu (58161009500); Zhang, Junhua (60284120800)
Title: A deliverability-aware mission scheduling framework for agile earth observation satellites
Abstract: Agile Earth-observation satellites create many imaging opportunities, but limited downlink and finite onboard storage make sequential “collect-then-transmit” pipelines prone to over-production and missed deadlines. We address this by making deliverability a first-class signal in observation selection. We present a deliverability-aware dynamic programming (DA-DP) framework that forecasts residual downlink capacity and injects a soft penalty on storage-capacity mismatch during label expansion. The planner is coupled with an exact linear program that allocates per-pass flows under deadlines, station contention, and storage bounds, and a small neighborhood refinement that perturbs observations and re-optimizes. The approach enforces agility, duty-cycle, and coverage constraints, and it naturally handles mixed area and point targets. On hour-scale horizons with heterogeneous LEO constellations, DA-DP improves delivered mission value by 23.5% relative to a sequential baseline and reduces over-production from 88.0% to 3.0%, while maintaining operational solve times under 90 seconds. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xiang, Y.; Guo, J.; Zheng, Z.; Wang, Z.; Tang, S.
Author full names: Xiang, Yan (57734578100); Guo, Jie (55709458000); Zheng, Zhongzhong (60006614300); Wang, Zheng (60178317700); Tang, Shengjing (7403432946)
Title: Fixed-time adaptive neural network fault-tolerant control during the Belly Flip maneuver for a starship-like vehicle
Abstract: A fixed-time nonsingular terminal sliding mode fault-tolerant controller (FxNTSMC-FTC) is developed for a starship-like vehicle’s ”Belly Flip” maneuver. Different from the previous work, a holistic control scheme that couples aerodynamics, trajectory, and control is presented, achieving rapid and precise large-angle attitude stabilization under multiple uncertainties and actuator faults. First, the actuator model and trajectory optimization model are proposed to calculate the aerodynamic parameters and the reference trajectory, and the attitude dynamics model is established using quaternions to prevent the singularity phenomenon. Then, a fixed-time adaptive radial basis function neural network (RBFNN) disturbance observer (RBFNN-FxTDO) are introduced to handles uncertainties and disturbance, which effectively estimates internal uncertainties caused by mass and inertia changes and external disturbance such as aerodynamic deviation and unmodeled deviation. Finally, a fixed-time nonsingular terminal sliding mode fault-tolerant controller is developed to address the large-angle attitude maneuvers during the short-duration ”Belly Flip” process, ensuring rapid, precise, and robust attitude tracking, with rigorous fault tolerance and fixed-time stability. Simulation results demonstrate the scheme’s effectiveness and superiority. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Author full names: Su, Wenhao (59971984800); Wang, Haoran (59187736700); Zhao, Jiangbin (59259397200); Zhao, Shengfeng (35217188600); Lu, Xingen (59939737800)
Title: Investigation on the Mechanism of Solidity Influence on Loss Characteristics and Flow Field Structure in Variable Pitch Fan
Abstract: The variable pitch fan (VPF) effectively enhances energy utilization efficiency and operational stability by adjusting the rotor pitch angle. This paper systematically investigates the internal flow field structure, loss mechanisms, and performance evolution of a single-stage VPF with a convergent channel under varying pitch angle adjustments and different rotational speed conditions. Results indicate that an increase in pitch angle leads to a decline in efficiency, though the decline is more gradual at lower rotational speeds. The dominant loss mechanism within the rotor shifts from boundary layer losses to leakage flow and secondary flow losses as operational conditions change. Furthermore, analysis of the influence of solidity (blade number) demonstrates that an increased blade count improves stability, effectively suppresses leakage flow, and mitigates secondary flow losses at high pitch angles. Based on comprehensive performance and stability analyses, an optimal design parameter of blade number N = 20 was determined, establishing a solidity selection methodology that balances performance across multiple operational conditions. © © 2025. Authors: Khan, A.; Dai, H.; Sun, H.; Zhao, Y.
Title: Adaptive sliding mode control scheme for satellite detumbling using flexible rod with improved dynamic model
Abstract: Space debris removal, especially malfunctioning satellites, has become increasingly critical with the rise in space launches. A major challenge is the uncontrollable spin of malfunctioning satellites due to residual momentum, which complicates capture and deorbiting efforts. This study proposes a detumbling approach using a Detumbling Satellite (DS) equipped with a flexible rod, addressing the difficulties associated with large rod deformations, contact dynamics, and control. Although the Absolute Nodal Coordinate Formulation (ANCF) has been effective for large deformations, further studies are needed to achieve more accurate contact dynamics and robust control. Furthermore, severe impulsive disturbances caused by contact processes make it difficult for existing controllers to meet the demand for strong robustness, high accuracy, and quick convergence. Therefore, this work uses modified Leuven friction model, which offers clear advantages over the Coulomb friction model, along with a dissipative contact force model, for improved accuracy in force prediction. To counteract impulsive disturbances arising from contact interactions, an Adaptive Sliding Mode Control (ASMC) strategy is designed to stabilize DS attitude. A boundary layer thickness is incorporated to mitigate chattering effects. The simulation results show that the proposed scheme is capable of detumbling a malfunctioning satellite in 437 s to an acceptable range. Other simulations validate the effectiveness of the improved dynamic model and the proposed ASMC controller. This research advances space debris removal by improving the precision and robustness of detumbling strategies, with significant implications for satellite servicing and sustainable space operations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Drikakis, D.; Kokkinakis, I.W.; Fung, D.; Spottswood, S.M.; Brouwer, K.R.; Riley, Z.B.; Daub, D.; Gülhan, A.
Author full names: Drikakis, Dimitris (56273846200); Kokkinakis, Ioannis W. (56520382300); Fung, Daryl (58862387000); Spottswood, Stephen Michael (6604036778); Brouwer, Kirk R. (56732618000); Riley, Zachary B. (55805518700); Daub, Dennis (57113411400); Gülhan, Ali (6602632355)
Title: Informer deep learning forecasting for high-speed aeroelastics
Abstract: This paper examines the suitability and limitations of the Informer deep-learning architecture for forecasting aeroelastic response in high-speed shock-wave/turbulent boundary-layer interaction (SBLI). Dynamic displacement measurements of a thin, compliant panel were obtained in the H2K hypersonic wind tunnel at Mach 5.3 for several cavity-pressure configurations. The Informer, which employs ProbSparse self-attention for long-sequence time-series forecasting, was trained on displacement time histories from a subset of cases and used to predict unobserved scenarios. We systematically varied the forecast horizon (256–768 samples) and data sparsity (1200–4000) and evaluated performance using the normalised root-mean-square error. The model achieved low error (minimum NRMSE 0.19) only for carefully tuned combinations of step size and sparsity, while many other configurations yielded NRMSE values of O(0.5–1) and exhibited systematic under- or overestimation. In particular, the Informer tended to underestimate displacement following recent sharp decays and to overestimate signals that contained an initial near-steady phase. These behaviours reveal a strong sensitivity to hyperparameters and difficulty in capturing the non-stationary, thermally buckled, shock-induced dynamics of the panel. The results indicate that, in its standard form, the Informer is not robust enough to serve as a stand-alone predictor for aeroelastic design. Still, it provides a valuable diagnostic benchmark for long-sequence attention models in hypersonic fluid-structure interaction. The study highlights the need for adaptive or hybrid architectures and suggests future applications in virtual sensing and safety monitoring for high-cost hypersonic testing. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chang, Y.; Guo, S.; Wu, Y.; Qiu, H.; Kou, Y.; Yang, Z.
Title: Impact of nozzle dynamic enlargement on the propagation of liquid kerosene/air rotating detonation waves
Abstract: Advancing rotating detonation engines (RDEs) toward practical application requires a deep understanding of dynamic nozzle control during critical processes such as ignition and thrust modulation. For the first time, the effects of nozzle dynamic enlargement on the propagation of a kerosene/air rotating detonation wave (RDW) are investigated through direct-connect ground tests. Experimental results indicate that the equivalence ratio (Φ) is the dominant factor influencing detonation capability and stability, while the nozzle area ratio (AR) and its actuation speed (V <inf>N</inf>) play secondary roles. Nozzle dynamic enlargement does not extend the detonation boundary, however, a larger AR combined with an appropriate V <inf>N</inf> helps shorten the ignition delay time and improves RDW propagation stability. Conversely, an excessively high V <inf>N</inf> can disrupt the pressure matching between the nozzle and the combustion chamber, leading to deteriorated RDW propagation characteristics. Furthermore, the RDW velocity follows a Cauchy-like distribution, with most data clustered between 920 and 1020 m/s, and its median velocity decreases as V <inf>N</inf> increases. These findings offer valuable insights for the design and application of RDEs equipped with dynamically adjustable nozzles. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wu, J.; Huang, Z.
Title: Continuously rotated upper wall on the flame dynamics and combustion performance of a model supersonic combustor
Abstract: The aft part of the upper wall in a strut–based, hydrogen–fueled model supersonic combustor is continuously rotated either clockwise or anticlockwise with time. Unsteady numerical simulations with dynamic mesh are performed under constant wall rotational speeds of 100°, 500°, and 2500°/s, respectively. The influences of wall rotation are concerned under fixed air inlet and hydrogen injection conditions. The anticlockwise rotational speed has limited influence on the flow and combustion fields when the upper wall diverges. The engine performances including the total thrust, overall heat release rate, and combustion efficiency deteriorate with increased rotation angle. Meanwhile, the supersonic and premixed flame bases become highly unstable with large lift–off distances and strong oscillations (respectively lie in 62–99 mm and 46–70 mm). However, the clockwise rotational speed has noticeable influence on the overall flow and combustion modes when the upper wall converges. The critical angle that chokes the combustor increases with the rotational speed (-4.30°, -5.75°, and -7.25°, respectively) while the combustion performance increases with the convergence angle. The supersonic and premixed flame bases gradually move toward the strut base when the upper wall converges. The novel numerical approach rapidly estimates the combustor performance under a range of divergence angles, which acts as a powerful tool for optimized combustor design. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Singh, D.B.; Suryanarayana, G.K.; Yanamashetti, G.; Jagadeesh, G.
Author full names: Singh, Dheerendra Bahadur (57188632667); Suryanarayana, Gargeshwari K. (6603283517); Yanamashetti, Gireesh (57194040303); Jagadeesh, Gopalan (7003653771)
Title: Active control of bimodal transonic flow over the payload region of a launch vehicle model using a counterflow jet
Abstract: Bimodal flow involving alternate subsonic separated and supersonic attached flows occurs at small angles of attack over the payload region of a launch vehicle due to severe Shock Wave and Boundary Layer Interactions (SWBLI) when the semi-nose cone angle of the vehicle exceeds 15∘. This non-stationary random phenomena causes extremely high levels of pressure fluctuations especially in the low frequency band, which can excite structural buffeting. A counterflow sonic jet was found to alleviate the problem by replacing the bimodal flow with a stationary flow typical of an attached turbulent boundary layer. Dramatic reductions occurred in the pressure fluctuations in the low-frequency band, indicating the effectiveness of the active control device. The studies clearly bring out the physics of the flow control, which is essentially to energize the boundary layer which can withstand the adverse pressure gradients associated with the transonic shock waves. The counterflow jet penetrates a short length upstream of the freestream and stagnates. The outerflow repulses the jet and the shear layer of the jet flow reattaches on the nose cone, along with the shed eddies. The turbulent kinetic energy associated with the reversed jet and the eddies are absorbed into the boundary layer due to the receptivity in the region of favourable pressure gradient over the nose. As a result, the boundary layer growing over the payload region gets energized and thickened in a manner similar to that in blowing. The increased thickness of the boundary layer pushes the lambda-shock system away from the wall. Consequently, the boundary layer remains attached despite the shock-induced adverse pressure gradients. In the presence of the counterflow jet, the pressure fluctuations in the worst affected region are significantly reduced and comparable to an attached turbulent boundary layer. Also, dramatic reductions of pressure fluctuations in the low-frequency band (Overall Acoustic Sound Pressure Levels dropping from about 172 dB (max) to about 155 dB, demonstrating the efficacy of the active control technique. The findings clearly suggest that the buffet-free payload regions with large semi-nose cone angles can be designed by incorporating passive/active control of SWBLI. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, X.; Liu, Y.; Wang, H.; Liu, T.; Fan, W.; Zhang, R.
Author full names: Wang, Xiaochen (60277849300); Liu, Yongfeng (55742024000); Wang, Hui (59941934700); Liu, Tong (59942086500); Fan, Weijun (9745790400); Zhang, Rongchun (14039715400)
Title: Numerical investigation on flow and combustion characteristics of novel reflux compact combustor under different rotating speeds for gas turbine
Abstract: In the contemporary aerospace industry, particularly in aero-engine applications, combustor design optimization has prioritized compactness and miniaturization as the critical objectives. Key constraints limiting further size reduction include flame length and outlet temperature uniformity; the former has traditionally been dealt with by employing swirl flow. To address these challenges, this study introduces a novel combustor configuration and conducts comprehensive numerical simulations to analyze its flow characteristics, combustion performance, vortex system distribution, and outlet temperature uniformity. Results demonstrated that the proposed high-swirl combustor effectively reduced the extent of the high-temperature zone. Optimized wall rotational speed settings increased the combustion efficiency to 99.27 %, while the outlet temperature distribution factor reduced to 0.10, indicating superior thermal uniformity at the exit plane. Furthermore, precise rotational speed control improved performance and enabled axial shortening of the combustor. This study provides new insights into liquid fuel combustion behavior within rotating combustors, offering practical guidance for advancing next-generation methodologies in compact combustor design. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xu, B.; Sun, Z.
Title: Crashworthiness of Kirigami-inspired hourglass structures under quasi-static and impact loading
Abstract: This study introduces a kirigami-inspired hourglass structure (KHS) as a novel thin-walled energy absorber for aerospace crashworthiness applications, including lightweight protective components and advanced sandwich core systems. The KHS is engineered to achieve high energy absorption efficiency and improved impact resistance through a geometrically induced deformation mechanism. Its performance was evaluated through experiments and numerical simulations and was compared with three reference structures, namely a square tube, a kirigami inspired truncated pyramid (KTP), and a folded kirigami (FK) structure, all having identical wall thicknesses. Quasi static compression tests and validated finite element analyses showed that a single KHS unit achieved a specific energy absorption of 11.97 J/g, which is 2.8, 1.1, and 2.1 times higher than the values obtained from the FK, KTP, and square tube. Under axial compression, the foot like supports stabilized the inner rectangular tube and activated a unique plastic hinge evolution mode in which the hinge lines were parallel to the loading direction. This mechanism significantly improved the crashworthiness of the structure. Parametric studies on wall thickness, polygonal number, and height to width ratio identified an optimal configuration that provided enhanced stability and energy dissipation. Low velocity drop weight tests further confirmed the superior impact resistance and stable dynamic deformation of the KHS. A metal and nonmetal hybrid configuration that combines a metallic mesh with PLA was also investigated. The hybrid layer effectively suppressed fracture and delamination during impact and improved the structural integrity of the KHS. Overall, the KHS provides a lightweight, efficient, and reliable solution for impact mitigation in aerospace structures, offering strong potential for integration into next-generation aircraft and spacecraft protection systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Serrar, A.; El Khlifi, M.E.
Title: Study of sweeping jet flow control over a ramp with experimental validation
Abstract: The goal of this study is to control a separated turbulent flow in order to reduce the recirculation region. A flow over a 25° ramp is studied, and a sweeping jet actuator is used to modify the topology of the separated shear layer and the recirculation zone behind the ramp. This layer is bounded above by the turbulent/non-turbulent interface and below by the interface of the recirculation region. To evaluate actuator’s performances, three cases are examined, differing in the divergent exit length and the throat diameter. To compare their performance, the unsteady Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes equations, coupled with the k−ωshear stress transport model, are solved for a two-dimensional flow. The results show that, by increasing the pressure recovery, eliminating downstream flow separation region, and correspondingly reducing drag, the case with the smallest throat proved to be the most efficient. It is the generation of a jet, remaining tangent to the wall, that increased the mass and momentum entrainment directly in the boundary layer near the wall. These findings are in good agreement with the experimental results. © 2026 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Gao, Y.; Li, D.; Zhao, S.; Xiang, J.; Li, H.
Title: Lift enhancement mechanisms analysis of dual-stage circulation control based on dual synthetic jets
Abstract: The limited energy output and insufficient control authority of synthetic jets have hindered their widespread application in circulation control. To address this issue, this paper proposes a dual-stage circulation control scheme based on dual synthetic jets (DSJs). Multiple configurations were generated by altering the relative positions of the two jet slots. We analyzed the variations in lift characteristics across these configurations through numerical simulations. The study investigates the lift-enhancement mechanism of dual-stage circulation control and summarizes design guidelines for relatively optimal lift-enhancement configurations under different jet parameters. Results demonstrate that a relay effect between the upper and lower slots enhances circulation control performance, significantly improving the lift coefficient. Under identical jet parameters, the dual-stage circulation control achieves lift coefficients up to 4.4 times higher than those of conventional single-stage circulation control. The relatively optimal configuration varies with jet operating conditions. Increased jet frequency reduces the jet separation angle, making closely spaced configurations more effective. Conversely, a higher momentum coefficient enlarges the jet separation angle, but due to relay effect attenuation and strong negative circulation control occurring in the second half-cycle, configurations with wider slot spacing do not deliver optimal lift performance. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hwang, H.; Tai, M.; Jeong, S.; Park, D.
Author full names: Hwang, Hyeonwoo (58843526100); Tai, Myungsik (57204040521); Jeong, Shinkyu (7402425338); Park, Donghun (36673606600)
Title: Development of data-driven wall interference correction method for subsonic wind tunnel
Abstract: A data-driven wall-interference correction framework for subsonic wind tunnel testing was proposed using deep neural networks (DNNs) trained on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) data. To analyze the characteristics and causes of wall interference, CFD simulations were conducted for a wide range of model geometries, angles of attack, and test-section sizes. The analysis revealed that the wall interference generally increases the lift coefficient (C<inf>L</inf>) under pre-stall conditions, whereas the drag (C<inf>D</inf>) and pitching moment (C<inf>M</inf>) behave differently depending on the parameters. Sensitivity analyses revealed strong correlations between wall interference and geometric parameters, such as wingspan (b) and relative tail position. Two DNN-based correction methods were proposed and evaluated. One method predicts the correction factors based on the concept of the classical method. The other method directly predicts the corrected aerodynamic coefficients. Quantitative evaluation showed that both methods achieved appropriate correction performance, achieving R ² values above 0.99 and lower root mean square error across all aerodynamic coefficients (C<inf>L</inf>, C<inf>D</inf> , and C<inf>M</inf>). These results demonstrate the potential of the proposed method as a practical correction framework to replace or augment conventional correction methods. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Liu, W.; Chu, W.; Zhang, H.; Dong, J.
Author full names: Liu, Wenhao (57207250670); Chu, Wuli (8294621500); Zhang, Haoguang (56066147500); Dong, Jiezhong (57561457000)
Title: Experimental and numerical study of recirculation hybrid casing treatments in a high-speed axial flow compressor
Abstract: This study experimentally and numerically investigates the impacts of two recirculation-based hybrid casing treatments, namely recirculation-groove casing treatment (CT) and recirculation-slot CT, on the stability and performance of a high-speed axial compressor rotor. The results show that both hybrid CTs enhance compressor stability at the cost of efficiency loss. The recirculation-groove CT achieves a stall margin improvement (SMI) of 19.19% with only 0.57% peak efficiency loss (PEL), outperforming the recirculation-slot CT, which provides 16.29% SMI with 1.22% PEL. Numerical analysis reveals confirms that the degree of tip blockage mitigation is the primary physical driver for stability enhancement. The recirculation-groove CT’s superior performance is directly correlated with its ability to achieve the lowest overall tip blockage factor across the passage by robust tip leakage flow ingestion and effective downstream flow management. Furthermore, unsteady analysis reveals that the recirculation-groove CT imposes a quasi-steady control over the tip flow field through strong, multi-dimensional momentum transport, whereas the recirculation-slot CT relies on localized, fluctuating exchanges. The recirculation-groove CT is confirmed as the optimal configuration, fundamentally driven by superior blockage reduction and coherent momentum transfer. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Duan, J.; Xue, K.; Ren, X.; Jiang, L.; Song, Y.; Yan, G.; Liu, Y.
Author full names: Duan, Jiateng (57329389600); Xue, Kunpeng (57217144109); Ren, Xiaotong (57195262899); Jiang, Lanlan (57218448341); Song, Yongchen (57221639631); Yan, Guanghan (57907277300); Liu, Y. (57116595600)
Title: Numerical study of a sweeping jet from an embedded fluidic oscillator in co-flow
Abstract: Although co-flow represents a highly relevant operating condition for fluidic oscillators (FOs) in applications such as drag reduction in aerospace and high-speed trains, related research remains limited. In this study, numerical simulations combined with particle image velocimetry (PIV) experiments were conducted to investigate the influence of co-flow on the flow characteristics of the oscillator. Considering the presence of the oscillator casing, the variations in sweeping jet behavior under different velocity ratios (R), Reynolds numbers, and throat widths (d) were analyzed while maintaining the geometric proportion of the FO. The results show that, unlike in cross-flow conditions, the co-flow has little effect on the internal flow structure and self-sustained oscillation behavior of the oscillator, whereas the external flow field exhibits significant changes with varying R . Distinct differences were observed in jet trajectories, with wake lengths of 2.32 d , 3.17 d , and 3.84 d for R = 4, 2, and 1, respectively. The effects of Reynolds number and oscillator size on the overall flow structure were minor, and similar flow patterns were observed at the same velocity ratio. However, higher flow velocity led to stronger vortex cores and regions. This study provides valuable insights into the potential applications of sweeping jets under co-flow conditions in fields such as aerospace and high-speed train drag reduction. © © 2025. Authors: Shan, C.; Ding, J.; Zhu, Z.; Long, Y.
Author full names: Shan, Chengjun (58247410100); Ding, Jialiang (60122427100); Zhu, Zhen (60122309000); Long, Yaosong (7203042918)
Title: An improved rapid method for cyclorotor aerodynamic performance prediction integrating low-Mach Leishman-Beddoes dynamic stall
Abstract: The cyclorotor has regained significant research interest as a vertical take-off and landing propulsion system due to its enhanced maneuverability and acoustic advantages over conventional rotors. However, its development has been limited by the absence of efficient aerodynamic prediction tools during conceptual design phases. Existing approaches present inherent limitations where simplified models overlook dynamic stall effects and the original Leishman-Beddoes model, calibrated for helicopter applications (Ma ' 0.3), proves inaccurate at the lower Mach numbers (Ma ' 0.2) characteristic of cyclorotor operation. This paper proposes an improved rapid prediction framework featuring three key advancements: four-bar mechanism based kinematics capturing realistic blade motion, a state-space reformulated low-Mach modified Leishman-Beddoes model for unsteady blade aerodynamics, and a double-actuator-disk multiple-streamtube inflow model accounting for non-uniform velocity effects. Validation against experimental data and computational fluid dynamics (CFD) results demonstrates the method's superior accuracy in predicting time-averaged vertical force and power across varying blade numbers and pitch angles, while achieving 34–66 × computational speed-up relative to high-fidelity CFD. The proposed method provides an effective tool for rapid design evaluation and parameter optimization in cyclorotor development. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Huang, F.; Zhao, Y.; Lv, Z.; Li, T.; Kong, W.
Title: Ground-based experimental study on liftoff and turbulent transition characteristics of non-premixed jet flames
Abstract: A ground-based study was performed to identify suitable conditions for space-based non-premixed jet flames. This research investigated the effects of nozzle diameter and nitrogen dilution on the liftoff and transition behaviors of propane and ethylene flames. Results showed that ignition method and height significantly influence ethylene flame behaviors but have minimal influence on propane fuel. For ethylene, the transition Reynolds number under single ignition (Re = 2000) was significantly higher than under multiple ignitions (Re = 1312), and a higher ignition position promoted earlier liftoff and transition. For propane, pure propane flames exhibited both liftoff and turbulent transition using a 0.4 mm nozzle. However, with the addition of 10 %-50 % nitrogen diluent, only flame liftoff was observed. For 0.5 mm and 0.6 mm nozzles, pure propane and cases with 10 % and 20 % diluent showed both liftoff and turbulent transition, while other conditions only showed flame liftoff. For ethylene fuel with 0.33 mm nozzle, neither flame liftoff nor transition occurred under any condition. For 0.4 mm nozzle, pure ethylene flames showed both liftoff and transition, while only liftoff was observed in diluted cases. For 0.5 mm nozzle, turbulent transition occurred in pure ethylene and with 10 % diluent, while only liftoff was observed in other cases. Froude number analysis revealed that propane laminar flames were buoyancy-controlled, while ethylene flames are in the buoyancy-controlled regime at low flow rates and in both buoyancy- and momentum-controlled regimes at high flow rates. Turbulent flames of both fuels remain buoyancy-controlled. Suitable fuels for in-orbit experiments were identified as 80 % propane/20 % nitrogen (using a 0.6 mm nozzle) and pure ethylene (0.4 mm nozzle). Under normal gravity, they exhibited maximum flame heights of 103 mm and 107 mm, peak combustion loads of 258 W and 268 W, and optimal ignition heights of 30 mm and 25 mm, respectively, with propane requiring single ignition and ethylene multiple ignitions. This study identified the experimental condition points for 80 % propane/20 % nitrogen and pure ethylene aboard the Tiangong Space Station and predicted the corresponding flame heights. © © 2025. Authors: Appar, A.; Poovathingal, S.J.
Title: Gas-Surface Interaction Model for Very Low Earth Orbit (VLEO) Systems
Abstract: Accurate estimation of the drag force is essential for spacecraft in very low Earth orbit (VLEO), as it significantly impacts orbit prediction, control, and collision avoidance. Accurate gas-surface interaction (GSI) models are crucial for predicting drag, particularly in collisions involving hyperthermal atomic oxygen with spacecraft surfaces operating in VLEO environments. This work examines the scattering of atomic oxygen on amorphous silica, a common satellite material, using reactive molecular dynamics. A comparison of angular scattering distributions and energy transfer between molecular dynamics calculations and experiments reveals excellent agreement. Based on the physical insights from molecular dynamics calculations, a new hybrid GSI model has been developed. The new model, termed the diffuse-Cercignani-Lampis-Lord (DCLL) model, combines velocity contributions from non-drifting diffuse Maxwellian and impulsively scattering drifting distributions. The new model reproduces angular flux distributions observed in molecular dynamics and beam experiments, while preserving a structure suitable for implementation into particle solvers. The DCLL model is both simple and accurate, effectively bridging molecular-level fidelity with practical usability and providing a reliable tool for drag prediction and orbital analysis of VLEO spacecraft. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ali, M.A.; Maqsood, A.; Athar, U.; Sial, S.
Author full names: Ali, Mirza Aqib (59961364500); Maqsood, Adnan (36134796800); Athar, Usama (57445056900); Sial, Sara Baber (57209995060)
Title: Benchmarking control strategies for UAV swarms: Centralized, decentralized, or federated reinforcement learning
Abstract: Autonomous UAV swarms are becoming increasingly important in mission-critical domains where coordination depends on training approaches that enable effective cooperation among agents. This work presents an evaluation of three dominant reinforcement learning paradigms, centralized, decentralized, and federated, within the context of cooperative UAV swarm training. We develop a unified experimental approach and benchmark these strategies across standardized environments and tasks, including target search and coordinated navigation. This evaluation covers eight critical metrics, including total training time, convergence rate, sample efficiency, training stability, final reward achieved, policy generalization, transferability, and scalability. The results show that centralized learning converges fastest (1,800 ± 82 epochs) and achieves the highest final reward (26.31) with the best sample efficiency (2.46 reward/1k interactions), but suffers from poor scalability (75.7 % drop in reward from 8- to 36-agent swarm) and lower transferability (64.9 %). Decentralized learning generalizes better (81.0 % transferability) and scales more effectively (64.3 % drop) but converges slowest (2,400 ± 96 epochs) with the lowest sample efficiency (1.78). Federated learning provides a strong middle ground, combining a good convergence speed (1,950 ± 89 epochs) and high transferability (88.6 %) with the best scalability (55.6 % drop) while maintaining comparable final reward (25.48). These findings provide practical guidelines for selecting appropriate learning architectures in real-world swarm deployment scenarios and set the groundwork for future research on hybrid and adaptive reinforcement learning strategies. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Yuan, H.; Yu, G.; Cai, C.; Zhu, Y.; Lee, C.
Title: Novel thermoelectric-generators-based design for aerodynamic heat management: A preliminary study
Abstract: Hypersonic vehicles suffer extreme aerothermal loads, posing a critical threat to flight safety. In this study, a thermoelectric generators (TEGs) based aerothermal protection structure was developed to harvest heat from compression corner flow. The experimental results showed that, by employing the flow separation region as the cold source and the reattachment region as the hot source, the TEG system simultaneously reduce the reattachment region temperature and convert aerodynamic heat into electricity. With increasing Re<inf>unit</inf> , the reattachment region exhibits a more significant temperature rise than other areas, thereby enlarging the temperature gradient across the TEG and enhancing its output power. Specifically, the TEG system achieved a maximum output power of 4.89 mW/cm2, while simultaneously reduced the peak temperature of reattachment region by 8 K. These findings demonstrate a novel design of TEGs in hypersonic vehicles, achieving both aerothermal protection and onboard power generation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhu, Z.; Cheng, H.; Duan, P.
Author full names: Zhu, Zihao (59412983500); Cheng, Hongzhi (57324982900); Duan, Penghao (57195584110)
Title: Flow separation control over a low-pressure turbine blade using a DBD plasma actuator
Abstract: Flow separation over the newly developed highly curved Institute of Engineering Thermodynamics-F (IET-F) turbine blade significantly contributes to the total pressure loss over the cascade, which in turn reduces the overall efficiency of gas turbines. One promising approach to reduce this loss involves the use of plasma actuators to re-energize the boundary layer and delay separation. Compared to passive flow control methods, plasma actuators offer several unique advantages, including light weight, rapid response and the absence of moving mechanical components. However, the fundamental physics governing plasma-based flow control over the low-pressure turbine blade remain understudied. In addition, the optimal parameters for plasma actuation under different inlet conditions require further investigation. Therefore, this study employs Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulation to investigate the effects of plasma flow control over the IET-F turbine blade under different plasma actuation signals, where the plasma flow control effect is treated as a body force and calculated by a well-established mathematical model. First, the flow fields of the baseline design are simulated and validated under various inlet conditions. Second, the interactions between the plasma-induced wall jet and the separation bubble on the suction surface are examined. Finally, the quantitative effects of plasma flow control under different actuation signals are assessed, and the optimal installation locations and signals for each inlet condition are identified. This paper provides guidance for the plasma flow control over the newly developed IET-F turbine blade as well as insight into the fundamental physics underlying plasma flow control. © © 2025. Authors: Geng, Y.; Wang, L.; Hu, J.; Luo, S.; Zhou, Q.
Title: A dual category-channel attention convolutional network fault diagnosis method for imbalanced multi-sensor data of spacecraft
Abstract: Fault diagnosis is a key technology for ensuring the safety and stable operation of spacecraft systems. However, available fault data are extremely scarce and exhibit severe category imbalance, particularly with the presence of extremely rare fault samples. In addition, the complexity of integrating heterogeneous features from multiple onboard sensors poses significant challenges, undermining the accuracy and reliability of fault diagnosis. Therefore, a dual category-channel attention convolutional network (DCCAN) is proposed to address the aforementioned shortcomings. First, a multi-sensor adaptive feature re-weighting (MSRW) module is proposed. This module dynamically models the importance of each sensor channel, enabling the extraction of latent fault-related features from multi-source data and enhancing the representation capability of fault information. Second, a category-specific feature adaptive enhancement (CFAE) module is proposed, which leverages category labels to explicitly guide the model's learning process. This allows the model to focus more precisely on extremely rare fault categories, thereby alleviating the challenges posed by imbalanced sample distributions. Ultimately, a DCCAN fusion network is designed to perform feature-level integration of the two aforementioned modules, thereby facilitating the comprehensive fusion of multidimensional fault information and enabling accurate identification of diverse spacecraft fault conditions. The proposed method was evaluated on a spacecraft fault dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that DCCAN achieves an 41.67% improvement in recall compared to other baseline models in fault diagnosis tasks. Even under high-noise conditions, it maintains an 85.54% increase in recall, highlighting its robustness and generalization capability. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yu, Y.; Guo, Y.; Wang, P.; Zhang, H.
Title: Intention recognition for space non-cooperative targets using Kolmogorov-Arnold transformer
Abstract: Intention recognition for space non-cooperative targets strongly impacts the safety and reliability of spacecraft’s decision-making. To address this issue, this paper presents an intention recognition method for space non-cooperative targets based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold Transformer (KAT) model. Specifically, a formal description of the intention recognition problem for space non-cooperative targets and its corresponding mathematical model are first introduced. By incorporating relative motion configurations, relative attitude, and lighting conditions, we define a comprehensive intention space set of non-cooperative targets. Subsequently, we propose an intention recognition model based on KAT which integrates Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) into Transformer to enhance the expressiveness and feature extraction capability of the intention recognition model. Numerical simulations are conducted and the results show that the proposed model has an accuracy of up to 99.15% for intention recognition at optimal parameters. Moreover, comparative simulations with other intention recognition models are conducted to validate the effectiveness, accuracy, and noise resistance of the proposed method. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, A.; Gu, D.; Huang, Z.; Zhang, H.; Jiang, T.
Title: Evaluation of real-time orbit determination performance for LEO satellites using different empirical acceleration models
Abstract: Real-time orbit determination (RTOD) is essential for the autonomous operation of low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites supporting Earth observation and communication missions. Within the reduced-dynamic orbit determination framework, empirical acceleration modeling plays a key role in compensating for unmodeled orbital perturbations, particularly under the computational limitations of onboard processing that preclude the use of high-fidelity force models. This study introduces three novel empirical acceleration modeling strategies and assesses their performance against three conventional models using two months of onboard Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) observations from the Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment Follow-On (GRACE-FO, 492 km) and Sentinel-6A (1336 km) satellites. In the framework of dynamical model compensation, the proposed approach of estimating three independently inverse correlation times yields the best orbit accuracy, with a maximum improvement of up to 4.2 cm in three-dimensional (3D) root mean square (RMS) error. Under the empirical acceleration compensation framework, the combined model incorporating piecewise constant and 1-cycle-per-revolution (1CPR) components demonstrates superior performance, enhancing 3D orbit accuracy by up to 3.1 cm. Among the six strategies evaluated, the optimal 3D RMS orbit accuracy achieved was 27.9 cm for GRACE-FO and 9.0 cm for Sentinel-6A. Overall, the dynamical model compensation with three independently estimated inverse correlation times offers the best trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency, making it well-suited for real-time, onboard implementation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tsung Chang, T.; Kontis, K.
Title: Experimental study on the effect of installations in the supersonic cavity flow
Abstract: The systematical effect of installations in the supersonic flow of M<inf>∞</inf>=1.6 over a cavity of L/D=4 was investigated experimentally aiming to correlate the volume effect of installation, which are the scaled models of weapon ejection system, in the cavity and important fluid dynamic characteristics. The pressure contour of the pressure sensitive paint (PSP) shows that the static pressure decreases proportionally with the occupied volume of the installations, rather than the geometry. The lower pressure caused by these installation indicates that the ejection system in the cavity is beneficial for weapon releasing. The percentage of reduction in OASPL is double as the volume is twice larger and the maximum reduction can be achieved by approximately 8 dB. The fundamental Rossiter modes remain unchanged, but the tonal amplitude can be decreased by 10 dB. The double-volume installation significantly and exclusively suppresses the tonal amplitude of the third Rossiter mode by 20 dB, which is associated with shear layer shedding, while the tonal amplitude of the second mode is unchanged. However, from the scalogram in the wavelet space, it suggests that the energy in the third Rossiter mode is reduced by 50 % but it increases by 20,% in the second mode with the double-volume installation. This strongly suggests that the perturbation caused by the installation to the shear layer effectively excites the second mode. © 2025 .

Authors: Yang, W.; Cui, T.
Title: A novel LPV-MPC of turbofan engines based on off-equilibrium linearization
Abstract: Turbofan engines impose stringent requirements on controllers, such as performance, stability and so on. Model predictive control (MPC) is a promising approach to satisfy these requirements, yet its effectiveness depends on models that are simultaneously accurate and computationally efficient. To address this trade-off, this paper proposes an MPC framework based on off-equilibrium linearization for turbofan engine acceleration and deceleration control. First, a quasi-LPV discretization method based on off-equilibrium linearization is introduced, and an MPC scheme is subsequently developed. The method extends operating points beyond the traditional equilibrium manifold to the entire feasible region, thereby exploiting MPC’s online optimization to generate efficient trajectories. Subsequently, the recursive feasibility and exponential stability of the resulting closed-loop system is formally established under reasonable assumptions. Application to a turbofan engine model further shows that the proposed approach achieves superior dynamic tracking compared with robust tracking control based on off-equilibrium linearization. This study integrates off-equilibrium linearization with MPC, combining their respective advantages and providing a foundation for future incorporation of robustness in practical engine control. © © 2025. Authors: Liu, X.; Tang, Z.; Wang, J.; Chen, S.
Author full names: Liu, Xiaowei (57322905800); Tang, Zhixuan (59397611600); Wang, Jinli (59397567600); Chen, Shuaibing (60264997400)
Title: A method of oblique detonation wave initiation control with cavity-jet combination
Abstract: The realization of initiation and standing of oblique detonation wave (ODW) is pivotal to the design of the combustor of an oblique detonation engine (ODE). Various active and passive methodologies have been proposed for the initiation control of ODW. In this paper, the initiation characteristics of conventional wedge and cavity wedge are firstly investigated by solving the two-dimensional RANS equations with chemical reactions. The results demonstrate that the cavity wedge enhances the initiation of ODW. However, as the flight Mach number decreases further to Ma <inf>0</inf> = 8, the detonation is no longer successful. This paper proposes a cavity-jet combination method to achieve the initiation and standing of ODW under low flight Mach number conditions. The jet is located behind the trailing edge of the cavity. The ODW is induced by opening the jet for a brief period. Subsequently, the ODW moves to the trailing edge and reaches a quasi-steady state after the jet is closed. Further research has revealed that the ratio of peak pressures calculated through numerical simulations and semi-theoretical methods for the Mach stem structure is crucial for the movement of the wave structure. The working mechanism of this method has been elucidated. This method circumvents the respective drawbacks of both active and passive control, and is able to successfully induce ODW and maintain the position fixed near the trailing edge of the cavity. This work constitutes a significant reference point for the design of combustor of ODEs. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, J.; Hao, M.; Chen, S.; Pan, N.; Guo, N.; Meng, W.; Fan, D.; Zhang, H.; Xie, Y.; Liu, K.; Li, D.
Title: Investigation into the performance of a miniaturized ionic liquid electrospray thruster
Abstract: The miniaturization of electrospray thrusters for micro-nanosatellites is hampered by a limited understanding of how thruster geometry and operational parameters influence performance in high-conductivity ionic liquid systems. To address this, our study introduces an integrated approach combining computational fluid dynamics with time-of-flight experiments to systematically decouple and optimize the key parameters—emission voltage, propellant flow rate, and emitter-extractor spacing—for a miniaturized thruster design. Our results quantitatively demonstrate that an optimized configuration (8000 V, 2.0 nL/s) achieves a high-efficiency regime, producing 450 μN of thrust, a specific impulse of 424.7 s, and a remarkable efficiency of 88%. This work provides a validated design framework and performance benchmarks, offering a clear pathway for engineering high-performance, miniaturized electrospray thrusters. © © 2025. Authors: Mandal, S.; Divvela, R.; Karmakar, S.; Roy, A.
Author full names: Mandal, Saugata (57223109498); Divvela, Rakesh (57215005083); Karmakar, Srinibas (55236230800); Roy, Arnab (57216386920)
Title: Ballistic performance of boron-HTPB fuelled hybrid gas generator-based ducted rockets (HGDR)
Abstract: This study may be the first experimental demonstration for reporting the proof of concept for hybrid gas generator-based ducted rockets (HGDRs) using boron-loaded hydroxyl‑terminated polybutadiene (HTPB) fuel. Unlike conventional solid-fuel ducted rockets, HGDRs allow active oxidiser modulation and two-stage combustion, creating a favourable environment for high-energy additives such as boron. Submicron boron 10% and 20% by weight were incorporated with HTPB, along with gaseous oxygen as the primary oxidiser in the gas generator. Preheated high-enthalpy air was supplied as the secondary oxidiser into the ram combustor to replicate Mach 2.5 flight conditions at an altitude of 6 km. Results suggest that boron addition enhanced the specific impulse by up to 7.2% (844.2 s with 20% boron), but had a lower regression rate, lower specific thrust (nearly 4.65%), and higher char formation relative to neat HTPB. Combustion efficiencies up to 75% for boron-laden fuel are achieved, suggesting the need for optimising the fuel formulation and/or combustor geometry. These findings indicate that the HGDR concept shows promise, leveraging hybrid rocket technology and higher energy density additives to achieve superior performance, which is essential for volume-constrained advanced supersonic propulsion systems. © © 2025. Authors: Chen, X.; Zhao, R.; Zhang, L.; Sun, X.; Qian, Y.; Zhang, Y.
Author full names: Chen, Xianqiang (60263300800); Zhao, Rongchao (55798606400); Zhang, Lei (60263300900); Sun, Xiaolei (60263492800); Qian, Yuping (35216135500); Zhang, Yangjun (59867476900)
Title: Numerical study on aerodynamic characteristic of eVTOL ducted fan at hovering condition under crosswinds
Abstract: Ducted-fan electric Vertical Take-off and Landing aircrafts (eVTOL) show significant potential in emergency response, logistics, and urban air mobility. However, crosswinds critically impact their stability and efficiency. Few studies systematically analyze crosswind effects on hovering ducted-fan aerodynamics, particularly drag coefficients. This paper investigates the crosswind effects based on a validated 3D CFD model. Results indicates that the hovering efficiency (figure of merit) increases as the crosswind velocity increases. However, crosswind generates significantly large lateral force even at low wind velocities. The drag coefficient of ducted fans is over 17 under specific conditions. Then, further analysis shows that the large lateral force is mainly contributed by the duct inner wall. Under crosswind conditions, the combined effect of negative attack angle at the duct inlet and fan suction effect induces large-scale flow separation and low-pressure zones, which dominates the lateral force generation. Finally, a correlation between drag coefficient, Reynolds number, crosswind velocity ratio and aspect ratio (H/D) was established and analyzed. It is shown that the crosswind velocity ratio is the dominant factor for the lateral force while Reynolds number has negligible impacts. These findings provide critical insights for the optimized design and control of ducted fan eVTOL aircraft. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yang, J.; Ge, J.; Liang, J.
Title: A mesoscopic thermochemical ablation-erosion coupling model of 3D woven C/C composites under high-speed airflow shear
Abstract: Aircrafts suffer from severe aerodynamic heating and aerodynamic force in the hypersonic environment, leading to the thermochemical ablation and aerodynamic erosion of thermal protection materials. Characterizing the ablation and erosion performances of thermal protection materials is of great significance for the engineering design of thermal protection systems. In the paper, the ablation tests of 3D woven C/C composites were firstly conducted using an oxygen-acetylene ablation test rig, and the surface ablation morphology and recession were characterized and analyzed. Then, the analysis method of mesoscopic ablation morphology is established, considering the component ablation rate difference and the reaction/diffusion competition mechanism. Based on the mesoscopic structure characteristics and surface ablation behavior, an aerodynamic erosion model is developed to analyze material failure under supersonic airflow shear, and an aerodynamic erosion criterion for 3D woven C/C composites considering aerodynamic force and surface ablation roughness is proposed. A mesoscopic ablation-erosion coupling method and the corresponding numerical program are developed. Finally, the effects of different aerodynamic shear pressures on surface recession, roughness, and mass loss are investigated, and the mechanical erosion factor is calculated via numerical methods. Combined with the classical macroscopic thermochemical ablation model, the total surface recession caused by ablation and erosion is calculated. The accuracy of the total surface recession calculated by the numerical model is verified by comparison with experimental results. This study can provide guidance for the analysis of surface ablation-erosion performance of thermal protective materials, as well as the engineering design of thermal protection systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Shao, M.; Chen, S.; Jiang, H.
Title: Numerical investigation of the interaction between turbulence and a small-scale unmanned aerial vehicle propeller
Abstract: The ingestion of turbulent inflow by propellers generates broadband noise, which constitutes one of the primary aeroacoustic sources in propeller systems. This study employs a synthetic turbulence method to generate homogeneous isotropic turbulence with controllable parameters, featuring varying length scales and turbulence intensities. Numerical simulations are conducted to investigate the interaction between the turbulence and the propeller. Impact of turbulence length scales and intensities are evaluated separately. Turbulence ingestion noise emerges as the dominant noise source across all operational conditions. When ingesting turbulent inflow, the aeroacoustic directivity pattern become rotational axis dominant, while the noise spectra features a “haystack” pattern. Then, the aeroacoustic sources and blade-to-blade correlation are further examined. Key findings indicate that while larger turbulence scales induce stronger blade correlation, they generate reduced high-frequency noise components, consequently yielding lower far-field acoustic radiation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, X.; Zhang, R.; Liu, W.
Author full names: Wang, Xiaozhen (58561693100); Zhang, Ruihao (60226872400); Liu, Weihua (57207219099)
Title: Research on the method of acquiring fuel tank thermal parameters for airworthiness certification
Abstract: Fuel tank thermal parameters are crucial to the quantitative assessment of fuel tank airworthiness flammability. To address the challenge of obtaining thermal parameters that meet airworthiness requirements, a method comprising experiments, thermal modeling, and parameter optimization inversion is proposed. This method enables the cost-effective acquisition of fuel tank thermal parameters that meet the airworthiness accuracy and reliability requirements and are applicable to various flight conditions. The data such as fuel temperature and heat sources in fuel tank are measured under specific flight profiles. The fuel temperature variations are acquired and analyzed by establishing a thermal model of the fuel tank. Combined with the improved whale optimization algorithm (IWOA), a rapid and accurate solution for fuel thermal parameters inversion is achieved. The results show that: (1) Under the two flight profiles specified by airworthiness, the fuel temperature simulated based on thermal parameters is higher than the test data by an average of 2.23 K. This meets the accuracy and reliability requirements for airworthiness verification, confirming the effectiveness of this fuel tank thermal parameters extraction method. (2) Across nine different flight conditions, the average error between the fuel temperature simulated based on the thermal parameters and the fuel tank thermal model outputs is 1.28 K. This indicates that the fuel tank thermal parameters accurately represent fuel temperature variations across varying flight conditions. (3) In a complete flight profile, the fuel temperature generally follows a pattern of increase-decrease-increase, with the rate of decrease slowing as cruise time increases and the overall fuel temperature shifting upward with higher ambient temperature. The results of this study can provide useful guidance on the method for assessing aircraft fuel tank flammability for airworthiness compliance. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, Y.; Li, D.; Zhao, S.; Xiang, J.; Lu, Y.
Title: Aerodynamic analysis of a small-scale propeller under ground-sidewall coupling interference
Abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are extensively utilized in low-altitude environments for military and civilian applications owing to their high flexibility, low price, and high maneuverability. However, drones occasionally encounter confined environments while cruising or performing missions at low altitudes. In such spaces, the proximity effect causes severe aerodynamic perturbations, resulting in drone instability. This study utilizes a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) approach to assess the ground-sidewall coupling effect for small-scale propellers. The time-averaged results indicate that propeller heights of 0.8R and 2R serve as threshold points for variations in the lift and lateral forces, respectively. The data demonstrate that when the height exceeds the threshold point, the magnitude of aerodynamic force is primarily influenced by the single wall. In the event of the drone operating in this condition, with consideration for wall collisions, it is imperative to take into account the acceleration changes that are brought about by variations in additional forces. The transient results identified the regions where propeller thrust and lateral force fluctuations were most pronounced under ground and sidewall coupling interference. Within these regions, the intense interference from ground vortices and sidewall vortices leads to heightened aerodynamic oscillations. Notably, near a height of 1R, the ground and the sidewall vortices are fully developed and interact, generating the strongest coupling perturbations. Whenever possible, drones operating in restricted environments should either avoid areas with ground and sidewall coupling interference or enhance the capabilities of their controllers. These results are crucial for enhancing flight controllers and optimizing path planning in complex and confined environments. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Dong, L.; Tao, C.; Zhang, Z.; He, G.; Xu, Y.; Liu, D.
Author full names: Dong, Liheng (58140420600); Tao, Chengyang (58139995000); Zhang, Zhaoxiang (57191228269); He, Guiqing (54797338600); Xu, Yuelei (34878145700); Liu, Dong (58421064500)
Title: An attitude-centric and cross-band infrared framework for aerial target intention recognition
Abstract: In terminal attack-defence scenarios, aerial target intention can be accurately recognized based on trajectory and attitude information. Currently, radar-based intention recognition methods hold a dominant position. However, radar is not adept at capturing target attitude information (e.g., pitch, roll, and yaw) and can only perform intention recognition based on trajectory information. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes an attitude-centric and cross-band infrared aerial target intention recognition framework that simultaneously extracts trajectory and attitude representations from multi-band infrared images. The framework comprises two steps: pose estimation for predicting keypoints from infrared images, followed by intention recognition based on these keypoints. For pose estimation, a cross-band invariant representation learning method is proposed to reduce the impact of band-bias, thereby improving the model’s generalization on multi-band infrared images. For intention recognition, regularized adaptive adjacency matrix and parameter fusion mechanisms are designed to effectively capture aerial target attitude representations, forming an attitude-centric approach. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances the generalization of various pose estimation models on multi-band infrared images. Additionally, with the introduction of attitude representations, the intention recognition accuracy increases from 90.12 % to 96.64 %. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Gui, K.; Lu, W.; Lu, Y.
Title: Mode switching in fluidic oscillators from feedback channel length variation
Abstract: This study conducts an investigation with two primary objectives: first, to examine the impact of feedback channel length on the operational mechanism of a fluidic oscillator, and second, to explore the mechanism underlying the observed mode switching phenomenon. Using numerical simulations, we analyzed internal flow losses, jet frequency, and sweeping angle. Results show that increasing the feedback channel length to 1.5 times the prototype's length induces a mode-switching phenomenon, where the jet intermittently alternates between low-frequency, high-amplitude (LF–HA) and high-frequency, low-amplitude (HF–LA) oscillations. This switching results from changes in the feedback channel pressure gradient, caused by unsteady flow conditions in the mixing chamber. Moreover, a coupling between the inlet velocity and feedback channel length also influences mode transition. By adjusting the inlet velocity, the influence of the extended feedback channel can be controlled—either enhancing or suppressing mode switching to stabilize a single oscillation mode. A key advantage is the ability to generate two distinct jet modes using one oscillator simply by varying the inlet velocity. This offers significant potential for flow separation control: the LF–HA mode suits large-scale separation, while the HF–LA mode is ideal for wake or mild separation, enabling effective control with reduced energy loss across various conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Park, K.T.; Lee, B.S.; Park, N.; Myong, R.S.; Lee, H.
Author full names: Park, Kyutae (59453003200); Lee, Byoungsoo (59763730800); Park, Nameun (15077059300); Myong, Rhoshin (6602079264); Lee, Hakjin (55849200700)
Title: Effects of hub wake and engine exhaust on pusher propeller aerodynamics in a compound coaxial helicopter using high-fidelity LBM simulation
Abstract: Coaxial compound helicopters with aft-mounted pusher propellers offer extended range and high-speed capability but are highly sensitive to complex wake interactions. During high-speed forward flight, unsteady and asymmetric wakes generated by the upper and lower hub fairings, mast fairing, and engine cowling are convected along the tail boom and impinge on the pusher propeller. The inflow entering the propeller disk is decelerated and distorted, which increases the effective blade angle of attack and causes variations in thrust, torque, and overall propulsive efficiency. Herein, the unsteady aerodynamics of a full-configuration coaxial compound helicopter based on the Sikorsky–Boeing SB>1 Defiant, modeled without main rotor blades, are analyzed using PowerFLOW, a high-fidelity solver based on the lattice Boltzmann method. Simulations capture the evolution of fuselage–hub wake structures, their interaction with the pusher propeller, and resulting changes in aerodynamic performance. Results indicate that wake impingement reduces propeller thrust and efficiency compared with the isolated propeller and induces azimuthal thrust asymmetry owing to local inflow distortion and partial blade stall. The findings provide a detailed understanding of how decelerated and distorted inflow affects pusher propeller performance, offering guidance for aerodynamic optimization and vibration mitigation in next-generation high-speed coaxial compound helicopters. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Title: Design and verification of an intelligent variable-stiffness vibration isolator for a high-resolution optical satellite based on machine learning
Abstract: To mitigate micro-vibration disturbances in high-resolution optical satellites arising from the simultaneous operation of multiple reaction wheels, this study develops a novel intelligent variable-stiffness vibration isolator (IVSVI), successfully verified in orbit for the first time. The key innovation lies in employing machine learning to infer the optimal control temperature of the IVSVI under cooperative multi-wheel operation, thereby enabling adaptively enhanced vibration isolation performance across varying working conditions. The proposed vibration suppression strategy integrates a surrogate model trained via machine learning with data-driven utilization of polymer material characteristics and a lightweight isolator design, forming a comprehensive intelligent semi-active isolation system that minimizes pixel offset in optical payloads. Compared with conventional passive or active systems, the method offers simpler implementation while significantly reducing spatial and power requirements. Both ground and on-orbit validation demonstrate that the IVSVI increases isolation efficiency by >25 % in most scenarios, leading to noticeable improvements in imaging quality. These results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed methodology. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, Z.; Wang, L.; Li, F.
Title: Model-and-data-driven robust concurrent optimization for structural topology and device layout integrating load location and non-design domain effects
Abstract: With the continuous improvement of the lightweight level of advanced equipment, structural design is also developing towards refinement. An important trend is multifunctional integration, which means taking into account multiple function or performance requirements in structural design. This paper proposed a model and data-driven robust concurrent design method for structural topology and device layout integrating load location and non-design domain effects. For the optimization of device layout, the load effect caused by it and the limitations it imposes on the design domain have been effectively considered, and the best load location is obtained through the radial basis function neural network and data-driven intelligent algorithm. For structural topology optimization, the influence of multi-source uncertainty and the best load location of the device are considered, and the problem is solved via the gradient-based optimization algorithm. By decoupling two coupled optimization problems in the robust concurrent integrated optimization model into two sequential optimization problems, efficient design of device layout and structural topology can be achieved. Multiple numerical examples demonstrate that the proposed method has good applicability in multiple typical problems, including multi-scale problems, multi-load problems, three-dimensional problems, etc. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kim, H.; Lee, S.
Title: Integrated guidance-filter design for semi-active laser homing missiles under lock-on and lock-off maneuvers
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a homing guidance strategy for intercepting maneuvering targets under measurement saturation conditions. The strategy introduces intermittent lock-on/off sequences during impact angle control to improve missile capturability. The proposed guidance framework integrates a two-stage filtering system: a Tobit Kalman filter to process saturated look-angle measurements and an extended Kalman filter for precise target state estimation. Furthermore, the guidance law incorporates a dynamic, time-varying field-of-view bound, allowing the missile to execute controlled lock-on/off maneuvers. Numerical simulations confirm that this approach expands the capture region and enhances interception accuracy. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, C.; Wang, Z.; Bai, W.; Lei, R.; Li, J.; Hu, C.; Yang, Y.
Title: Study on boron-hydrocarbon combustion interactions in a scramjet combustor at mach 5.5
Abstract: This study investigated the combustion processes of boron powder combined with ethylene fuel within a cavity-based combustor under an incoming flow Mach number of 5.5, employing both direct-connect experiments and numerical simulations. The results indicate that the combustion efficiency of boron powder within the combustor reaches 81.97% at a low equivalence ratio. At a high equivalence ratio, the combustion efficiency decreases to 66.56% due to reduced mixing quality between the boron powder and the incoming air. The cavity demonstrates a significant enhancement effect on boron combustion efficiency, contributing 40–50% to the particle consumption rate. Particle combustion primarily occurs in the region adjacent to the fuel injection ports, leading to the formation of a pronounced high-temperature zone on the injection side. Under low equivalence ratio case, the static temperature in this high-temperature zone located in the combustor's expansion section reaches 1800–1900 K, whereas it exceeds 3000 K under high equivalence ratio cases. The combustion of boron particles can be divided into three distinct stages: (1) the ignition and heating stage, driven by the high-temperature ethylene-rich boundary layer and surface reactions; (2) the intensive combustion stage within the cavity region; and (3) the combustion stage within the expansion section. Furthermore, the combustion zones of boron powder and ethylene within the combustor exhibit distinct spatial distributions. The intermediate boron combustion product B₂O₂ is predominantly concentrated within the cavity region on the side opposite to the injection location, while H₂O is mainly distributed near the ethylene fuel injectors. © © 2025.
