Authors: Li, X.; Zhang, H.; Liu, C.; Gao, J.; Li, P.
Title: Hierarchical real-time energy management strategy of eVTOL hybrid power system based on finite-set
Abstract: This study focuses on battery/supercapacitor hybrid power systems of electric vertical take-off and landing aircraft. In this paper, a real-time energy management strategy with two-layer hierarchical architecture is designed to optimize power allocation, extend battery lifespan, and maintain bus voltage stability under dynamic operating conditions. The upper layer employs a discretized optimization approach based on a finite control set to dynamically generate the battery reference current, using a multi-objective cost function that considers bus voltage and battery current change rate. The lower layer uses finite control set model predictive control to realize high-precision current tracking. To verify the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, a rapid control prototype experimental platform is built. Experiments have shown that the proposed energy management strategy does not immediately follow load power changes under drop and surge conditions, but achieves a smooth transition through the current rate of change constraints of the upper-level strategy, which also maintains the bus voltage fluctuation within ± 0.6 V, and the transition time is less than 30 ms. Finally, compared with the rule-based and fuzzy logic-based energy management strategies, the proposed method reduces the slope of battery current drop during transient load changes by 82.14% and 92.86% respectively through the hierarchical optimization mechanism, effectively mitigating the impact of abrupt current variations on the battery. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, M.; Liu, J.; Liu, J.; Ju, W.; Wang, P.; Zhang, Y.; Lu, X.
Title: Endwall flow and loss mechanisms in an aero-engine low-pressure turbine cascade with local variable-thickness guide vane adjustment
Abstract: A locally variable thickness adjustment scheme is proposed for the variable geometry turbine (VGT) to improve off-design performance by limiting incidence excursions and mitigating clearance-driven endwall losses. Instead of rotating the entire guide vane, throat-area modulation is achieved by rotating only a suction-surface leading-edge segment, which reduces incidence variation and driving torque requirements. The adjustable-segment rotation angle θ and the normalized axial pivot position a / C<inf>x</inf> are treated as key design parameters. Using experimentally validated numerical simulations (validated against surface static pressure and downstream five-hole-probe data), the endwall flow mechanisms and loss characteristics of a high-load low-pressure turbine cascade are investigated for a matrix of θ –a/ C<inf>x</inf> combinations. Results show that increasing θ primarily redistributes the chordwise leakage-flow discharge, whereas the overall leakage mass flow changes only marginally. However, larger θ induces earlier and stronger suction-surface boundary-layer separation and significantly intensifies the endwall vortex system, increasing the core strength of both the passage vortex and the leakage vortex and expanding high-loss regions in radial height and circumferential width. In contrast, a rearward pivot position dominates the loss magnitude by extending the effective leakage path, raising leakage flow rates, and transforming concentrated high-loss cores into streamwise-penetrating “loss corridors”. Strong coupling effects are identified: rearward pivots amplify the sensitivity of losses to θ , producing accelerated loss growth. Overall, the clarified leakage–vortex–loss linkage provides quantitative guidance for selecting θ and a / C<inf>x</inf> to achieve precise throat-area control with reduced leakage penalties in a high-performance VGT. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Capel-Jorquera, J.; González, L.M.; Valero, E.; Torres-Ruiz, L.; Lacruz-Alcaraz, R.; Gómez-Blanco, R.
Author full names: Capel-Jorquera, J. (59715077800); González, L. M. (60243011500); Valero, Eusebio (25230720800); Torres-Ruiz, Luis (60189228900); Lacruz-Alcaraz, Ramón (60189344900); Gómez-Blanco, Rafael (6508230454)
Title: Deployable fins effect on store separation dynamics
Abstract: We present a Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) time-accurate Unsteady Reynolds-Averaged-Navier-Stokes (URANS) framework using a six-DoF Dynamic Fluid-Body Interaction (DFBI) solver with overset meshes and adaptive refinement to simulate the strongly coupled, nonlinear fluid-rigid-body interaction that governs store separation with moving appendages. In addition to the release itself, four deployable fins, which remain stowed during the first stage of the simulation, swing out after ejection, adding unsteady aerodynamics and further nonlinear coupling between loads and motion. To capture the multiple relative motions, the simulation employs six overset regions: one background, one store, and four fin regions. The approach is first validated on the canonical Wing-Pylon-Store (WPS) benchmark by reproducing steady C<inf>p</inf> distributions and measured free-release kinematics. It is then extended to analyze deployable fins with prescribed opening rates, assessing their effects on stability, loads, and trajectory. Results show that fins act primarily on attitude rather than on early center-of-mass motion: deployment increases static margin and pitch/yaw damping, reduces peak angles, and accelerates alignment with the freestream. The coupled response is distinctly nonlinear, with small changes in deployment law leading to noticeable differences in attitude histories. A small steady rolling moment appears, producing a gradual roll-up that must be considered in guidance and control. Increasing deployment rate advances the onset of these stabilizing effects, but benefits saturate above 210.6 deg/s. We conclude that deployable fins enhance separation safety by improving attitude stability with minimal trajectory penalty. A key contribution is the detailed meshing methodology, where adaptive refinement around moving interfaces ensures accurate, stable overset coupling without global over-refinement. The workflow is general and applicable to a wide range of moving-appendage problems. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Cheng, X.; Wang, Y.; Zhao, J.; Liu, N.; Cai, Z.
Author full names: Cheng, Xiaomei (58162396600); Wang, Yingxun (7601519334); Zhao, Jiang (55543714900); Liu, Ningjun (55802749600); Cai, Zhihao (8293047900)
Title: Data-driven adaptive hybrid-based control for some tiltrotor UAVs in the low-speed flight
Abstract: This paper proposes a data-driven adaptive hybrid strategy for modeling and control of some tiltrotor UAVs in the nonlinear low-speed flight. A physics-constrained Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) framework is developed to accurately capture the nonlinear and unsteady dynamics by incorporating the multirotor and fixed-wing aerodynamic characteristics. To enhance model adaptability, an online recursive least squares (RLS)-based mechanism refines identification results in real time. Leveraging this, a predictive compensation control algorithm is designed to improve robustness against sensor noise and modeling uncertainties. Furthermore, an adaptive hybrid control strategy fuses model-based predictions with sensor-based observations and incorporates them into the control system, significantly enhancing disturbance rejection and adaptability. Simulation and flight experiments confirm the superior control performance of the proposed method under the various flight conditions, providing a robust solution for the low-speed flight of some tiltrotor UAVs. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, R.; Guo, T.; Li, Y.; Meng, D.; Liang, B.
Title: Generalized Jacobian operator-based full-arm trajectory planning for multi-arm continuum space manipulators
Abstract: While multi-arm continuum space manipulators (M-C-SMs) offer new prospects for on-orbit servicing through their full-arm collaborative capabilities, their infinite degrees of freedom (DOFs) and non-rigid nature create a technical bottleneck for traditional Jacobian-based kinematic modeling. To address this challenge, this paper introduces the Multi-arm Generalized Jacobian Operator (MGJO), a novel concept established by incorporating the law of momentum conservation into a multi-arm operator framework. The MGJO extends the conventional Generalized Jacobian Matrix (GJM) from describing the end-effector motion of rigid manipulators to an infinite-dimensional operator space capable of describing the full-arm motion of M-C-SMs. Based on this operator, we establish a complete kinematic framework that includes the differential forward kinematics, along with its adjoint and pseudo-inverse operators. This framework provides a systematic solution to the full-arm differential inverse kinematics problem. The framework is then applied to trajectory planning for collaborative on-orbit tasks, integrating systematic solutions for handling constraints such as base disturbance minimization, collision avoidance, and deformation limits, which enhances its practicality and reliability. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model and method. This research provides the first unified, operator-based theoretical framework for the kinematic modeling and trajectory planning of M-C-SMs, establishing a key enabling technology for future autonomous on-orbit servicing, maintenance, and assembly. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Mowla, M.N.; Durhasan, T.; Asadi, D.; Kesilmis, Z.; Rashid Jafari, J.
Author full names: Mowla, Md Najmul (57419993700); Durhasan, T. (56237207500); Asadi, Davood (55835174400); Kesilmis, Zehan (56545820200); Rashid Jafari, Javad (60259908900)
Title: Aerodynamic coefficient prediction of bio-inspired camber morphing wings with flexible surfaces using an explainable transformer
Abstract: Bio-inspired morphing wings with flexible surfaces can enhance aerodynamic efficiency at low Reynolds numbers ( Re ), yet predicting their fluid-structure interaction remains challenging. We present PhysAero-MHANet, a physics-aware, interpretable deep learning framework coupled with controlled wind tunnel experiments for aerodynamic prediction of camber-morphing finite wings. The campaign yielded 911 samples spanning Re ∈ [3 × 104, 1 × 105], camber deflections up to 10∘, and angles of attack from −18∘ to 18∘. Experiments showed up to 34% drag reduction at small angles of attack, a stall delay of ≈ 6∘, a maximum lift coefficient C <inf> L ,max</inf> ≈ 1.44, and a peak lift-to-drag ratio C<inf>L</inf> / C<inf>D</inf> ≈ 8.84. The proposed model is a transformer-based multi-task surrogate with physics-informed attention, hierarchical cross-feature fusion, and shapley additive explanations (SHAP) for interpretability. Against 11 machine-learning, deep-learning, and attention baselines, PhysAero-MHANet achieved R 2 ≈ 0.985 and MAPE < 12% across lift ( C<inf>L</inf> ), drag ( C<inf>D</inf> ), and rolling moment ( C <inf> M,R </inf>) predictions. These results provide new insight into morphing-wing aerodynamics and support real-time control, performance optimization, and integration into unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and micro aerial vehicles (MAVs). © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, C.; Lu, H.; Liu, J.; Guo, S.; Peng, B.; Tian, Z.; Wang, L.
Title: Experimental study on flow separation in a s-shaped intake with variable cross-section controlled by sweeping jets based on fluidic oscillators
Abstract: As a critical component for conveying airflow to the compressor, the intake duct not only performs compression and rectification functions but also directly affects the overall engine performance. For the widely used S-shaped intake with variable cross-section, its three-dimensional curvature characteristics cause drastic flow deflection, thereby inducing severe flow separation. Based on a fluidic oscillator as the excitation source, experiments were conducted to investigate the effects of sweeping jet intensity and layout on flow separation in the S-shaped intake at various Mach numbers. The results indicate that sweeping jets significantly improve the uniform distribution of the total pressure at the S-shaped intake outlet. The loss reduction capability of the sweeping jet gradually enhances with increasing jet intensity. For different conditions, the sweeping jet scheme with an intensity of 30 kPa reduced the total pressure distortion coefficient by 50 %, 30 %, and 18 %, respectively. Furthermore, jet layouts affect distinct loss regions. The overall layout scheme combines the advantages of both local jets, effectively reducing center loss and streamwise vortex loss to achieve greater aerodynamic benefits. Considering the cross-impact between jet layout and intensity, the overall layout scheme significantly controls flow separation at low-speed conditions. However, as the Mach number increases, the jet with higher intensity demonstrates more advantages in reducing total pressure distortion. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Muhammed, M.; Harvey, D.; Khawaja, H.A.; Virk, M.S.; Momen, G.
Author full names: Muhammed, Manaf (57573484400); Harvey, Derek (57218293556); Khawaja, Hassan Abbas (57193408129); Virk, Muhammad Shakeel (25722460400); Momen, Gelareh (16425691500)
Title: Investigating the role of propeller geometry and surface characteristics in UAV ice accretion: An experimental study
Abstract: A lab based experimental study of atmospheric ice accretion on UAV propellers with different geometric and surface characteristics was conducted to study the ice accretion physics and resultant changes in propeller thrust and electrical power consumption. These experiments were conducted at the Anti-icing Materials International Laboratory (AMIL) Icing Wind Tunnel (IWT) at the Université du Québec à Chicoutimi (UQAC), Canada. The experimental icing conditions are determined in accordance with the 14 CFR Part 29 Appendix C for rotorcraft operating at altitudes below 10,000 feet. In this study the influence of following four geometric parameters and two surface characteristics of UAV propeller on ice accretion is analysed: 1) propeller diameter, 2) propeller pitch, 3) propeller chord length, 4) propeller winglets, 4) propeller surface finish and 6) Icephobic coatings. The analysis of results shows that the change in these features does not significantly impact the nature and shape of ice accretion but mainly influence the surface area affected by ice accretion. The thrust coefficient and electrical power coefficients vary considerably with change in propeller geometric features. The variation in propeller blade surface characteristics has a significant impact on the ice shedding characteristics of UAV propeller blade. Considering the high-power requirements of active ice mitigation techniques for UAV propellers, the results obtained from this study can be employed to develop a passive/hybrid ice mitigation approach and further optimize the geometric parameters of UAV propeller blades for efficient operations in icing conditions. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Deng, W.; Hou, B.; Zhou, X.; Wang, J.; Zhao, X.; Zhou, H.
Author full names: Deng, Wangqian (57894533300); Hou, Bowen (57193674435); Zhou, Xuanying (56895326400); Wang, Jiongqi (14030757500); Zhao, Xinyu (60075235800); Zhou, Haiyin (12752598800)
Title: Robust factor graph-based state estimation for Mars rovers under sparse and low-quality observations
Abstract: In the complex and variable Martian environment, uncertainties increase the variability of observations and reduce the quality of observations, while sparse observations limit the information redundancy while increasing the difficulty of identifying anomalies. Under sparse and high-variability observations, Huber loss-based factor graph optimization method suffers from robustness degradation. To address this limitation, this paper proposes an enhanced factor graph method featuring a novel Huber loss mechanism, which is reformulated through a full combinatorial enumeration and the multi-state coupled model. Specifically, the high-variability anomalous datasets within a sliding window are fully combinatorially enumerated to generate multiple observation combinations, which are then weighted based on the constraints. The optimal estimation for each observation combination is realized by exploiting the convexity of the weighted Huber loss function. The ambiguity of the solution space induced by exhaustive enumeration is then resolved by the multi-state coupled model. Simulations were carried out with the Mars rover integrated navigation system. The results demonstrate that, under complex random disturbance scenarios, the proposed method achieves an average 58.02 % reduction in the root mean square error for state estimation compared to the Kalman filter, standard factor graph optimization method, Huber loss-based factor graph optimization method, and dynamic covariance scaling-based factor graph optimization method. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Fu, H.; Ke, P.; Xu, R.; Guo, H.; Yang, H.
Title: Investigation of the cushioning characteristics and structural optimization of multi-connected airbags
Abstract: With the increasing payload mass and growing environmental complexity in airdrop missions, traditional airbag systems face significant challenges in controlling overload, impact velocity, and rebound. However, research on the cushioning mechanism of the newly emerging multi-connected airbags remains limited. In this study, both theoretical and numerical approaches were developed to analyze the cushioning behavior of multi-connected airbags. The mechanisms of different components—including the main bag, auxiliary bag, and internal bag—were systematically investigated. The effects of operating conditions such as altitude and approach velocity on cushioning performance were explored. Furthermore, two optimization strategies for vent area design were proposed based on genetic algorithms: dynamic adjustment of vent areas under multiple conditions and global optimization of vent areas across all conditions. Results demonstrate that incorporating auxiliary bags can reduce impact velocity by up to 15.19% at an altitude of 4000 m and an approach velocity of 12 m/s. The internal bag reduces the impact velocity by increasing the maximum overload. Under the same conditions, it decreases the impact velocity by 5.93% and reduces the main-bag internal pressure by 9.27%, thereby alleviating the material load. Compared with the baseline model, the multi-condition dynamic vent optimization reduces the maximum overload of the platform by 63.95%, while the global optimal vent area design achieves a 50.88% reduction. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xue, Y.; Yu, Q.; Wang, T.; Huang, Y.; Yang, B.; Wang, H.
Title: Collaborative control strategy for low-cost fixed-wing UAV swarms based on deep Q network
Abstract: Efficient information detection accomplished by unmanned systems in an area is a key prerequisite for achieving adversarial advantage in complex environments. Currently, most of the methods are targeted at high-cost rotary-wing UAVs and lack research on small fixed-wing UAVs, and the collaborative mission is usually split into path planning and target assignment problems, which also cannot realize integrated decision-making. This paper proposes a low-cost UAV swarm collaborative decision-making method based on UAV kinematic model and target spatial correlation model, which reduces the control error of algorithmic decision-making results and flight guidance law, and improves the detection effectiveness. Based on the Markov decision process, a sequential mission control strategy is proposed, which is combined with deep reinforcement learning technology to enhance the autonomous adjustment ability of UAVs facing complex dynamic environments, and the generalizability is enhanced by the target swarm gray decay model. This paper finally realizes the integrated decision-making of multiple member target assignment and attitude control by an improved dual periodic strategy DQN (DPS-DQN) algorithm. Through simulation experiments, it is verified that the DPS-DQN algorithm improves the convergence speed by 57.1 % and the mission effectiveness by about two times compared to the DQN algorithm, and improves the mission effectiveness by 65.42 % compared to the PSO method, and the standard deviation decreases by about five times. It provides a new scheme for efficient autonomous decision-making of low-cost fixed-wing UAV swarms. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Cheng, X.; Ma, P.; Chen, B.; Ai, C.; Xiong, Y.; Li, Y.; Zhou, M.; Liu, K.; Zhang, H.
Title: Thermal design and on-orbit validation of deployable synthetic aperture radar antennas for satellite constellations with emphasis on temperature uniformity
Abstract: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) is increasingly being deployed on small satellites in Low Earth Orbit constellations. However, ensuring full-cycle thermal control of SAR antennas under stringent cost constraints, particularly for maintaining high temperature uniformity, remains a major challenge. This study proposes an integrated structural–thermal strategy that avoids the use of embedded heat pipes and relies primarily on passive thermal-control measures. Thermal simulations and ground thermal-balance testing under representative orbital scenarios demonstrated that all antenna components remained within allowable limits, while the overall design required only 214 W of peak heater power. Comparison of on-orbit telemetry and simulation results indicated that insufficient deployment mechanism insulation and multilayer insulation (MLI) edge leakage induced limited on-orbit temperature uniformity (ΔT up to 9.7 °C) and substantial simulation/telemetry temperature deviations (maximum ΔT<inf>err</inf> up to 8.2 °C). Scenario analyses further revealed that increasing the thermal contact conductance between the hinge and antenna (h<inf>c</inf>) from 20 to 200 W·m⁻2·K⁻1 elevated the ΔT value from 2.7 °C to 8.7 °C, with the well-insulated regime exhibiting strong sensitivity to h<inf>c</inf>. By contrast, applying side MLI reduced the ΔT value from 7.6 °C to 2.7 °C at h<inf>c</inf>=20 W·m⁻2·K⁻1, while additional side MLI yielded a further 0.9–1.5 °C improvement. After optimizing deployment mechanism insulation and side MLI coverage, the ΔT and maximum ΔT<inf>err</inf> values decreased to 4.5 °C and 5.9 °C, respectively. The findings of this study provide a practical reference for thermal designs of future large-scale SAR constellation systems. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, X.; Jiang, Z.; Cheng, P.; Peng, J.; Bai, X.; Li, Q.; Chen, L.
Author full names: Liu, Xinlin (60259970000); Jiang, Zhuohang (58863744200); Cheng, Peng (56435206400); Peng, Jingfeng (60204260000); Bai, Xiao (57202813913); Li, Qinglian (56042836800); Chen, Lanwei (57215501026)
Title: Heat transfer characteristics analysis of spiral regenerative cooling channel with variable helix angle in LOX/CH4 engine
Abstract: To address the insufficient thermal protection capacity of LOX/CH4 (liquid oxygen/methane) variable thrust rocket engines operating under low conditions, a fast design method for spiral cooling channels with varying helix angle was proposed based on the nonlinear local linearization principle. Heat transfer characteristics analysis at 45 % RPL(Rated Power Level) was conducted. The results showed that the solving model for the variable-helix-angle spiral is simple, easy to understand, and suitable for different types of nozzles. Secondly, increasing the helix angle of the cooling channel results in a larger pressure drop and lower maximum wall temperature. The pressure drop increases from 0.77 MPa to 2.10 MPa as the helix angle increasing from 0° to 35°. However, due to the increase in cooling area, the maximum wall temperature decreases from 1504 K to 1437 K as the helix angle increases from 0° to 35°. Thirdly, the variable helix spiral groove achieves a reduction in the maximum wall temperature by increasing the helix angle at the throat and decreasing it at other positions, while ensuring a lower coolant pressure drop. Compared to 35° constant-helix-angle channels, the 50° variable-helix-angle scheme can lower the maximum wall temperature by approximately 60 K while decreasing the pressure drop by 0.50 MPa. This research provides important guidance for the design of spiral cooling channels and offers new insights for wide range reliable thermal protection in variable thrust rocket engines. © © 2025. Authors: Yang, C.; He, J.; Zhang, C.; Duan, L.; Chen, M.; Kang, Q.
Title: Measurement methods and experimental study of micro-thrust response time
Abstract: To address the need for measuring the response time of micro-thrusters in space gravitational wave detection missions, a novel method based on torsional pendulum acceleration extremum detection is proposed. This method resolves the inherent design conflict between measurement sensitivity and high-frequency bandwidth in the test platform, while avoiding the complexities associated with dynamic modeling and parameter identification. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that when the thrust response time is below a critical value, t<inf>c</inf> , and the system is initially at rest, the time interval for the derived acceleration to rise from zero to its maximum (or fall to its minimum) accurately corresponds to​ the thrust rise (or fall) time. Numerical simulations verify that the method exhibits strong adaptability to nonlinear thrust waveforms. Experimental validation was performed using a Roberval-type torsion pendulum within a vacuum chamber. A response time of 22 ms was measured for an electromagnetic force, closely approaching the system’s detection limit. For a cold gas micro-thruster, a response time of 140 ms was obtained, which agrees well with the 0–90% response time independently derived from the thruster’s flow signal. The proposed method requires only the second-order differentiation of displacement data to obtain acceleration, eliminating the need for system parameter identification. It thereby provides a universal and efficient approach for characterizing the thrust response time of micro-thrusters used in space gravitational wave detection. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, L.; Hu, X.; Xin, L.; Wang, J.; Ma, Y.; Li, Y.; Zhao, J.; Guo, P.; Kang, Z.
Title: Aerodynamic response and vortex structures evolution in an isolated rotor during gust encounters
Abstract: This study presents a numerical simulations investigation of the aerodynamic response and vortical structure evolution of an isolated rotor in hover and forward flight subjected to constant crosswinds and sharp-edged gusts. Analysis compares constant crosswind intensities (Tip-speed Ratios TSR=4.2–16.9) to quantify the effects on aerodynamic load oscillations, wake characteristics and vortex structure variations in both hover and forward flight. An overset mesh methodology is established to resolve gust encounter transient process during forward flight, studying Gust Ratios (GR=1–5) on aerodynamic response and vortex evolution, particularly focusing on gust-induced tip vortex breakdown and blade-vortex interactions (BVI). It is found that wind intensity demonstrates positive correlations with both the mean values and amplitudes of rotor torque and thrust under constant crosswind conditions, while concurrently inducing phase shifts in peak load occurrences. High crosswind intensity exacerbates BVI effects, identified as the primary reason for aerodynamic fluctuations. During rotor forward flight encountering sharp-edged gusts, a Reverse Abrupt Effect (RAE) manifests in transition periods, characterized by temporarily change direction in opposite tendency in torque and thrust. This phenomenon intensifies with increasing GR and seems to be correlated with destabilization and fragmentation of rotor tip vortex structures. The emergence of the RAE poses critical flight safety risks for rotorcraft UAVs. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, L.; Zheng, Q.; Zhang, H.
Author full names: Li, Liangliang (58298043100); Zheng, Qiangang (59695479200); Zhang, Haibo (57029454800)
Title: High-dimensional onboard steady-state surrogate modeling method for an aeroengine
Abstract: The increasing number of adjustable components in the aeroengine raises the input dimension of the onboard model. This results in an exponential increase in the required sample size for onboard modeling, leading to the "curse of dimensionality." To construct an onboard model with sufficient accuracy at a lower cost across a broad flight envelope and operating conditions, a high-dimensional modeling method is proposed. This method is based on the High Dimensional Model Representation (HDMR) approach to decompose the high-dimensional function into multiple low-order component functions, combined with the Maximum Dispersion and Gradient Adaptive Sequence Sampling (MDGASS) strategy to optimize sample distribution, and uses Basic Spline (BS) to construct component functions with a small size of samples. Simulation results show that, in benchmark function tests, MDGASS-BS-HDMR achieves an R square close to 1 and demonstrates better overall performance in fitting accuracy and sample efficiency compared to existing HDMR methods. For steady-state onboard modeling of the turbofan engine, the maximum prediction error of performance parameters is 1.4 % - 3.1 % for the four-variable model and 1.9 % - 4.6 % for the ten-variable model. Compared to the Neural Network-Propulsion System Matrix (NN-PSM) method, the required sample size is reduced by '70 % and 90 % for four-variable and ten-variable models, respectively. The proposed method enables engine performance parameter estimation at a lower modeling cost, providing a practical solution for constructing the high-dimensional onboard model of the aeroengine. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, Z.-Y.; Liu, H.-T.; Xiong, J.
Title: Digitally coded T-slot-connected gradient metastructures for morphing wings with programmable mechanical properties
Abstract: Most metastructures retain fixed properties after fabrication, limiting adaptability. This work develops a T-slot–connected gradient metastructure (TCGMM) whose mechanical response is programmed by assembly using a two-dimensional layout matrix with entries {–1,0,1} to specify the orientation (±1), presence (0), and position of female platelets. Four series (A, B, C, D) of TCGMMs are designed and evaluated in three-point bending and torsion via finite element analysis (FEA) and experiments (EXP). Results show that both the equivalent bending modulus and torsional stiffness can be tuned by the assembly pattern. In torsion, symmetric layouts yield equivalent torque–rotation responses; positioning the platelet near mid-height maximizes torsional resistance. In bending, multi-column layouts of female platelets (Series C) achieve a higher mean equivalent modulus than single-column layouts (Series B) by ≈39 % (EXP), demonstrating the benefit of increasing lateral constraint and load-transfer efficiency. The results of FEA and EXP are in agreement, which proves the accuracy of the mechanical performance analysis. The matrix-based parametric strategy enables rapid, reconfigurable design of TCGMMs and suggests strong potential for multi-degree-of-freedom morphing wings and related aerospace structures. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Mohammadi, H.; Nematollahi, M.S.
Title: Asymptotic full-body vibration suppression of flexible structures via adaptive MIMO boundary actuators
Abstract: This paper presents a novel integrated rotary–linear actuator for asymptotic and full-body vibration suppression in flexible beams modeled as infinite degree of freedom systems. Unlike conventional boundary controllers that primarily stabilize endpoint vibrations, the proposed system achieves complete convergence to equilibrium across the entire beam body—even under sustained external distributed loads. Implemented at a single boundary point, the actuator simultaneously provides displacement and rotation control, forming an effective multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) boundary control strategy. The control design integrates active and adaptive mechanisms, enabling robust compensation of nonlinear backlash that arises from the actuator’s inherent mechanical return effect. To ensure realistic modeling, this nonlinearity was explicitly incorporated into the system equations, allowing the controller to effectively neutralize its influence on vibration dynamics. The adaptive structure simultaneously identifies the unknown parameters associated with both the input backlash and the external disturbances. For numerical simulation and temporal response analysis, the finite difference method was employed to discretize the governing equations, enabling precise evaluation of beam-wide control performance. Three-dimensional numerical results validate the method, demonstrating its superior performance in suppressing both free and forced vibrations throughout the beam. The proposed approach offers significant practical advantages through its single-point boundary implementation, fast response, and robust full-body stabilization. Owing to these features, it holds strong potential for aerospace applications such as aircraft wings, deployable booms, and lightweight space structures, where effective vibration suppression is critical for operational reliability. This work marks a substantial advancement beyond existing boundary control techniques. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhao, F.; Chen, J.; Zheng, X.; Lv, P.; Yao, W.; Li, S.
Title: CoGAN: A novel correlation-integrated generative adversarial network for enhanced satellite anomaly detection
Abstract: Satellite anomaly detection is essential for ensuring mission safety; however, existing algorithms often suffer from high false alarm rates and limited accuracy, particularly in label-scarce environments. To address these challenges, we propose CoGAN, a novel unsupervised framework that integrates Deep Convolutional Generative Adversarial Networks (DCGAN) with correlation analysis. CoGAN effectively captures both the temporal dynamics of satellite telemetry data and the complex inter-variable dependencies through a maximum correlation coefficient model. A key innovation of CoGAN is the introduction of a dynamic anomaly metric that automatically adjusts detection thresholds during training, thereby enhancing adaptability to diverse operational conditions. Experiments on three on-orbit telemetry datasets show that CoGAN significantly reduces false alarms and improves detection accuracy, confirming its effectiveness and robustness for practical aerospace applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhou, Z.; Jia, Y.; Gao, P.; Wei, D.; Wang, J.; Jin, J.; Chen, Y.
Title: Mistuning parameter identification for rotating bladed disk based on temporal-spatial global surrogate model
Abstract: To address the problem of identifying mistuning parameter and predicting vibration responses of rotating bladed disk (blisk), this study proposes an efficient mistuning parameter identification method based on a temporal-spatial global surrogate model. The surrogate model is constructed using a two-layer proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) combined with radial basis function (RBF) interpolation, which enables accurate prediction of vibration responses, effectively replaces the complicated finite element model (FEM), and significantly improves computational efficiency. First, numerical analysis demonstrates that the surrogate model provides high prediction accuracy in both the time and frequency domains, with errors remaining below 10%. Secondly, the vibration response data of the blisk are obtained by the static frequency sweep experiment and blade tip timing (BTT) test. The mistuning parameters are then identified based on the surrogate model and the trust region optimization algorithm. Finally, the identified mistuning parameters are used to calculate the natural frequency of the blisk, and the results show that the computed natural frequencies closely match the strain gauge measurements, with average errors below 1.3%. The proposed method ensures computational efficiency while achieving high-accuracy prediction of the dynamic characteristics of rotating blisk systems and reliable identification of mistuning parameters, demonstrating strong engineering applicability. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wu, Z.; Li, Z.; Li, Y.; Li, J.
Title: Optimization of sounding rocket Tailfin aerodynamics: A surrogate-based machine learning approach
Abstract: This study tackles the optimization design of the aerodynamic tailfin of sounding rocket, addressing the critical need for high performance in near-space exploration. Conventional design methods, which separate the aerodynamic and structural optimization processes, are time-consuming and costly, often yielding suboptimal solutions. This paper leverages the high efficiency of machine learning techniques. More precisely, it adopts an Electric Eel Foraging Optimization (EEFO) algorithm combined with an optimized Kernel Extreme Learning Machine model to establish a surrogate-based optimization framework. This framework aims at minimizing the computational load caused by high-fidelity and high-accuracy Computational Fluid Dynamics simulations. A multi-objective EEFO is introduced to balance the drag coefficient, tailfin volume, and lift coefficient. A comprehensive analysis is then conducted, demonstrating that the optimized tailfin design significantly reduces the drag, slightly decreases the lift, and increases the lift-to-drag ratio. The obtained results demonstrate the high effectiveness of the proposed method in balancing conflicting objectives and constraints, leading to a higher aerodynamically efficient and a better structurally optimized design. This study contributes to the field by providing a robust optimization strategy that can be applied to other aerospace design problems, providing theoretical insights and practical guidance. © © 2025. Authors: Wang, Y.; Jin, K.; Yang, L.; Xiao, Z.; Luo, J.
Title: Numerical study of flow and particle erosion co-evolution in a high-loading compressor cascade
Abstract: Erosion is an inevitable degradation mechanism in turbomachinery, yet most studies neglect its temporal evolution and feedback on the flow. How progressive erosion influences flow behaviors, particularly leakage flow and boundary layer, remains insufficiently understood. To clarify these interactions, this work quantifies the time evolution of aerodynamic performance, loss sources, and flow behavior. The Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes equations and Lagrangian particle equations are solved to obtain the flow field and particle trajectories, respectively. The coupled simulation framework incorporating dynamic mesh method and a validated empirical erosion model is employed to capture the co-evolution of flow and erosion with continuously evolving wall boundaries. Results show that the decoupled treatment with a constant erosion rate underestimates the overall loss since the actual erosion rate increases with time as the effective impact area decreases and impact intensity increases. For instance, the leading edge of the eroded blade exhibits a 21.1 % increase in erosion rate while compared to the uneroded blade. In terms of fundamental mechanisms, erosion reshapes the relative contributions of loss sources. Boundary layer loss increases by 7.5 % and becomes the primary driver of performance deterioration, while a trade-off emerges between a weakened leakage vortex core and an intensified vortex sheet. These findings emphasize the necessity of coupled simulation approach and reveal fundamental mechanisms of erosion-induced aerodynamic degradation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhao, Y.; Yang, Q.; Li, D.; Fu, X.
Title: Flow topology and entropy generation mechanisms of twisted wings in a dust-laden Martian environment
Abstract: While fixed-wing flight in the rarefied Martian atmosphere offers unprecedented opportunities for large-scale aerial exploration, its feasibility is critically challenged by low-Reynolds-number boundary-layer instabilities and the pervasive dust-laden environment. To investigate these challenges, this study employs a high-fidelity numerical framework to resolve unsteady, three-dimensional flow structures under Martian-relevant conditions. The methodology integrates a Delayed Detached-Eddy Simulation (DDES) with the SST turbulence model, the γ–Reθ transition model, and low-Reynolds-number corrections. This fluid dynamics solver is two-way-coupled with a Discrete Phase Model (DPM) to capture particle–flow interactions across various mass loadings. The coupled aerodynamic and thermodynamic effects of suspended particles are examined on finite wings with both wash-out and wash-in twist. Results reveal that the particulate phase exerts a dual influence on the flow topology: it damps large-scale unsteady vortices and stabilizes span-wise structures while simultaneously triggering bypass transition within the boundary layer. This dichotomy leads to a strongly twist-dependent aerodynamic outcome. Wings with wash-out benefit from particle-induced flow regularization, which reduces mid-span thermodynamic irreversibility. Conversely, wings with wash-in twist experience a span-wise amplification of turbulent dissipation—by up to 250 %—despite a partial alleviation of tip stall. Furthermore, increasing particle mass loading induces a non-linear regime shift from transport-limited to dissipation-dominated behavior, where high concentrations lead to a contracted particle cloud and more uniform surface deposition. Ultimately, entropy generation analysis confirms that the observed aerodynamic degradation is driven primarily by this enhanced turbulent dissipation, establishing a quantitative framework to predict and mitigate dust-induced performance penalties for future Martian fixed-wing platforms. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: van den Broek, S.; Hunns, T.; Simpson, E.; Coggon, S.; Cooper, J.
Author full names: van den Broek, Sander (57204612668); Hunns, Tom (59710953700); Simpson, Edwin (56018158000); Coggon, Simon (56034936000); Cooper, Jonathan E. (7406078835)
Title: Surrogate modeling for realistic early-stage landing gear design load estimates
Abstract: The work presented in this paper focuses on surrogate modeling for early-stage landing gear design to enable rapid load prediction and optimization under uncertainty. We propose an approach that integrates Gaussian-Process regression with Principal Component Analysis to approximate the dynamic behavior of a simplified landing gear model. The work presented in this paper focuses on surrogate modeling for early-stage landing gear design to enable rapid load prediction and optimization under uncertainty. We propose an approach that integrates Gaussian-Process regression with Principal Component Analysis to approximate the dynamic behavior of a simplified landing gear model featuring nonlinear damping and stiffness characteristics. A key feature of the methodology is the management of design uncertainty combined with global sensitivity analysis using Sobol indices. These indices assess the contribution of key parameters, such as aircraft mass, and tire stiffness, to the peak landing loads. These analyses not only identify the most influential factors, but also guide targeted subsystem optimization. The optimization process minimizes the reaction factor, defined as the ratio of the peak ground-load to the static-load by adjusting the landing gear’s stiffness and damping properties. Comparisons between design-only and per-sample uncertainty optimization highlight the advantages of directly incorporating uncertainty within the optimization loop. Results show that the surrogate model provides accurate load estimates that closely align with higher-fidelity simulations, while the optimization reduces peak loads and enhances the dynamic response of the landing gear. The framework offers practical insights into the effects of varying design parameters on system performance, thereby aiding robust design decision-making. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: He, H.; Deng, H.; Zuo, X.; Zhang, S.; Teng, J.; Zhu, M.
Title: Comparative study of neural structure paradigms for compressor cascade flow field modeling based on the condition-encoder
Abstract: Conventional numerical simulation methods are computationally intensive and inefficient when dealing with complex multi-physics parameters coupled with compressor internal flow fields, making it difficult to meet the demand for efficient modeling in engineering applications. To address this issue, the Condition-Encoder U-Net (CEU-Net) and Condition-Encoder FNO (CEFNO) are proposed in this paper, which enable rapid and precise modeling of flow fields in scenarios characterized by sparse data. Specifically, the Learning-based Condition-Encoder (LCE) based on the trainable network and the Non-Learning-based Condition-Encoder (NLCE) based on the fixed broadcasting mechanism were constructed separately. Meanwhile, Mach number and incidence were utilized as physical parameters, which were embedded into the network through the Condition-Encoder (CE) to contribute to flow field modeling. The research results indicate that both CEU-Net and CEFNO models can achieve high-precision modeling performance; however, notable differences remain in their predictive characteristics and generalization capabilities. Among these, CEFNO demonstrates superior performance in terms of global-modeling accuracy, while CEU-Net exhibits enhanced predictive capability within local boundary layer and separation regions. Further comparison revealed that for CEFNO, the LCE exhibits higher prediction accuracy. In contrast, NLCE is more advantageous in CEU-Net, indicating that the selection of the CE must be aligned with neural structures. Additionally, LCE provides higher accuracy in boundary layer modeling, while NLCE combined with CEFNO exhibits a certain capacity for super-resolution prediction. This study has significant guidance for the collaborative design and selection of model structure and physical parameter embedding strategies in the modeling of compressor internal flow fields. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Shi, X.; Gu, C.; Ren, X.; Li, X.
Author full names: Shi, Xinhuan (57445631500); Gu, Chunwei (7202186503); Ren, Xiaodong (36024283000); Li, Xuesong (57192394504)
Title: Two-dimensional film cooling effectiveness prediction on curvature surface using ResNet-based deep learning
Abstract: For currently in-service gas turbines, both fan-shaped and cylindrical holes are extensively used for turbine blade film cooling. In light of the escalating demands imposed by advanced turbine design, there is an increasing necessity for the development of prediction models that can accurately and rapidly predict two-dimensional film cooling at the initial design stage. This paper proposed a Residual Network (ResNet) based deep learning model for rapidly predicting the two-dimensional film cooling effectiveness, taking into account the effect of blade curvature, working conditions and geometric parameters of film cooling holes. Specifically, the curvature of the protected surface (I), working conditions, such as Mach number (Ma) of the mainstream and blowing ratio (M), as well as geometric parameters of the film cooling hole, such as lateral expansion angle of the film hole (β <inf>lat</inf>), forward inclination angle of the film hole (β <inf>fwd</inf>), inclination angle (α) and compound angle (θ) were considered in this study to fit a prediction model with broader applicability. Moreover, through comparative experiments, it was found that modifications to up-sampling methods, convolution styles, and loss functions could significantly enhance prediction accuracy. The final model achieved an average MAE of 0.0103 as confirmed by testing and validation, with the upper limit of prediction accuracy determined by the fidelity of the training dataset. The proposed model exhibits predictive accuracy comparable to CFD simulations but with significantly faster computational speed, making it suitable for rapid prediction and optimization of cooling strategies during the initial stages of turbine cooling design. The SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method, used to analyze the importance of the input features, indicated the necessity to consider curvature of the protected surface when predicting the adiabatic film cooling effectiveness. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Manabe, A.; Irie, K.; Nakatani, T.; Komurasaki, K.; Shinya, T.; Ikeda, R.; Kajiwara, K.; Koizumi, H.
Author full names: Manabe, Ayuto (57468308500); Irie, Kosuke (59800066700); Nakatani, Tomonori (59801220600); Komurasaki, Kimiya (7003642232); Shinya, Takahiro (55370504700); Ikeda, Ryosuke (15822568400); Kajiwara, Ken (57203174765); Koizumi, Hiroyuki (59999717800)
Title: Observation of abnormal discharge formation in microwave rocket and its quantitative impact on thrust performance
Abstract: Microwave Rocket is a beamed energy propulsion (BEP) concept in which thrust is generated by a millimeter-wave (MMW) beam transmitted from a remote power station. The thruster operates on a pulse detonation engine (PDE) cycle driven by millimeter-wave-supported detonation (MSD), where an ionization wave propagates supersonically inside the thrust tube and drives a shockwave. However, abnormal discharges that ignite away from the thrust wall during the PDE cycle can disrupt MSD formation, leading to severe thrust reduction. The initiation mechanisms of such abnormal discharges, as well as their quantitative impact on propulsion performance, remain poorly understood. In this study, we investigate four abnormal discharge events (occurrence rate ∼ 13 %) that were randomly observed during double-pulse operation with a 550 kW MMW beam in dielectric-structured thrusters. Ionization-front dynamics were optically measured, and gauge-pressure histories at the thrust wall were recorded. Compared with normal operation, the occurrence of abnormal discharges reduced the propagation velocity of the primary ionization front by approximately 65 %, indicating a comparably large loss of MMW energy due to scattering and/or absorption in the abnormal discharge region. The associated loss of plateau pressure at the thrust wall and the resulting thrust degradation were quantitatively confirmed. Furthermore, orange emission was observed near the abnormal discharge initiation region, suggesting that soot contamination in the exhaust plume may trigger abnormal discharge by locally enhancing electron density and/or lowering the breakdown threshold. These findings provide valuable insights not only for the design of high-performance, reliable Microwave Rocket systems, but also for the broader field of BEP systems employing high-intensity electromagnetic beams. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Chen, C.; Sun, J.; Sun, B.; Zhang, T.; Li, Y.
Title: A novel Bayesian model validation method based on bootstrap resampling and distance remapping
Abstract: A Bayesian model validation method based on Bootstrap resampling and distance remapping techniques is proposed. Building upon a rigorous likelihood formulation and a judicious choice of priors, the proposed method performs posterior inference using a novel hybrid algorithm. This algorithm couples Transitional Markov Chain Monte Carlo (TMCMC) with Simulated Annealing (SA) and is further augmented by Bootstrap resampling and distance remapping strategies to effectively overcome the limitations imposed by strong hyperparameter dependence. The research develops a concentration index to systematically classify and assess the effectiveness of model calibration and the quality of samples participating in the calibration process. The effectiveness of the method is validated through the NASA Langley UQ challenge problem and an engineering wing model. This research provides a theoretical and methodological framework for model updating and validation in the engineering field. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, Y.; Du, M.; Wang, L.
Title: Application study of discontinuous transpiration cooling technology in aerospace vehicle thermal protection
Abstract: Transpiration cooling, as an efficient thermal protection method, is of great significance for addressing the extreme thermal load challenges faced by aerospace vehicles. This study employs numerical simulation methods to conduct a comparative analysis of the thermal protection performance between continuous transpiration cooling and discontinuous transpiration cooling, and systematically investigates the influence of discontinuous length (DL) and the proportion of porous media ( Q ) on transpiration cooling efficiency. The results demonstrate that, without increasing the cooling intensity and system complexity, discontinuous transpiration cooling can effectively reduce the stagnation point temperature and overall wall temperature of the leading edge by introducing solid walls (SW) to reconstruct the cooling mode, and its comprehensive thermal protection effect is superior to that of continuous transpiration cooling. As DL increases, the pressure and density within the cooling chamber rise, and the boundary layer effect intensifies, leading to uneven distribution of coolant and the emergence of local protective vacuum zones, which is unfavorable for improving transpiration cooling efficiency; whereas reducing DL helps increase the thickness of the gas film and enhance the thermal blocking effect. Under different DL conditions, the influence of Q remains essentially consistent: a larger Q contributes to the formation of a thicker gas film, reducing the heat transfer from the mainstream to the wall. Moreover, the discontinuous layout, through discontinuous discharge of coolant, can significantly reduce the entrainment of coolant by the mainstream, thereby enhancing the coverage capability and stability of the gas film on the hot end wall. This study provides a theoretical reference for the practical application of discontinuous transpiration cooling technology. By reasonably adjusting the matching relationship between DL and Q , the flow and distribution of the coolant can be effectively regulated to improve thermal protection performance. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yingkai, C.; Pu, H.; Wang, W.
Title: Multi-agent reinforcement learning for constrained cooperative 6-DOF pose control in on-orbit proximity operations
Abstract: The precision and robustness of multi-spacecraft systems in close-proximity operations are pivotal for next-generation on-orbit services. A central challenge lies in achieving synchronized six-degree-of-freedom (6-DOF) pose coordination under stringent constraints, including dynamic limitations, actuator saturation, and stringent inter-agent collision avoidance. Traditional model-based controllers often lack the scalability and adaptability to manage this high-dimensional, complex-constrained problem efficiently. This paper introduces a novel Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework to synthesize control policies for this problem. Key innovations include: (1) a dual-channel deep network architecture that extracts and fuses heterogeneous positional and attitudinal features for stable policy learning; and (2) a two-phase training methodology with policy transfer to mitigate reward sparsity and learning instability. This methodology first develops foundational maneuvering skills in a simplified single-agent setting, then transfers and optimizes these policies in a full-scale, constrained multi-agent environment via a decentralized training paradigm. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the learned decentralized policies enable a team of spacecraft to establish and maintain a precise formation, accomplishing the 6-DOF pose coordination task while rigorously satisfying all safety and operational constraints. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Han, S.; Ouni, M.H.E.; Ghazouani, N.; Yaylacı, M.
Author full names: Han, Shaoyong (58597516000); Ouni, Mohamed Hechmi El (58719046300); Ghazouani, Nejib (36458421400); Yaylacı, Murat (55906015300)
Title: Aerodynamic and control response of sandwich smart plates in aircraft wings subjected to lightning-induced hybrid electric shock: Advanced modeling and mitigation techniques
Abstract: This research, for the first time and as a novel study, presents the aerodynamic and control response of sandwich smart plates embedded in aircraft wings under a combined electrical shock of both direct current (DC) and alternating current (AC) voltage surge, which is similar to a lightning strike. Unlike prior studies focusing solely on DC or AC components, this work establishes a coupled analytical framework for hybrid electrical shock, better representing real lightning physics. The proposed sandwich configuration comprises sensor and actuator face sheets and a hybrid nanocomposite reinforced core to provide multifunctional durability. Halpin–Tsai micromechanical model to estimate effective material properties for the core component. Structural modeling is performed using Carrera unified formulation (CUF) with equivalent single-layer (ESL) models expressed through Taylor series expansion. Refined finite element approximation uses Legendre polynomials and Lagrange-type shape functions, with the geometric relations developed through the mixed interpolation of tensorial components (MITC) method. The use of a single-layer substrate lossless structure and a three-layered lossless electromechanical active sensing and actuating configuration is examined. By embedding the constitutive and geometric relations into a unified modeling framework for the governing equations, the corresponding governing equations requiring consideration of coupled electromechanical–aerodynamic responses were developed. Control strategies are evaluated to mitigate destabilizing effects on the electromechanical boundary value problem caused by hybrid electric shocks, and the time delay feedback (TDF) with derivative action (DA) controller consistently enabled the best reduction in the response's vibration and stabilization. Overall, results can be used for the modeling and mitigation of lightning-induced hybrid electric shocks to aerospace structures while also providing valuable decision support information for the implementation of advanced smart wing technologies in the future. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhu, B.; Li, H.; Lu, L.; Du, Z.; Yang, L.
Title: Data and knowledge dual-driven design method for Earth–Moon free-return orbit
Abstract: For manned spacecraft between the Earth and the Moon, the preferred orbit scheme is the free-return orbit (FRO), a data and knowledge dual-driven method is proposed for designing FROs. First, multi-category FRO database is generated based on prior knowledge of dynamics and orbital phase constraints. Subsequently, the database is classified into distinct datasets corresponding to different types of orbital phase to support regression learning. Second, regression learning is conducted based on merged datasets of different types to train network models, which are built for calculating the perilune orbital paraments of FRO. To solve the multi-solution problem of FRO design, abductive reasoning is conducted based on evaluation metrics of network models and the distribution of reachable set at perilune. It is concluded that constraint of orbital phase at perilune is redundant. Third, an optimization algorithm assisted by regression learning is proposed. The configuration of optimization parameters is facilitated using deviation obtained in testing network models. Finally, the conclusions from knowledge reasoning and information extracted from data learning are used to assist in FRO design. Compared with the traditional design method, the proposed dual-driven design method reduces the iterations of optimization by approximately 20 % and avoids redundant constraint settings for the multi-solution problem. The overall computational efficiency is improved by 166.30 %. The present investigation offers an important reference for expanding FRO design ideas for crewed lunar missions. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, C.; Zhu, Y.; Chen, Y.; Yang, L.
Author full names: Zhang, Chengming (57465885600); Zhu, Yanwei (15764036000); Chen, Yujie (59667895400); Yang, Leping (35761825300)
Title: A learning and communication-based coordination framework for multi-spacecraft orbital games with impulsive maneuvers
Abstract: Research on the safe operation of on-orbit spacecraft is increasingly shifting from single-spacecraft scenarios to those involving multiple spacecraft. Based on the Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient algorithm, this paper addresses the problem of cooperative pursuit of a target spacecraft by multiple interacting spacecraft with communication-based coordination. The orbital pursuit-evasion games is formulated as a partially observable Markov games, with carefully designed observation and action spaces. To leverage cooperative advantages among pursuers, a communication target allocation mechanism is designed, enabling the formation of a dynamic ring topology during action execution. A hybrid reward structure combining global and local rewards is proposed. Local rewards incorporate dynamic characteristics to differentiate contributions of individual spacecraft, while global rewards are designed based on an ideal encirclement configuration with its associated constraints. For network architecture, an Encoder-Head structure is adopted: the Encoder, implemented with Long Short-Term Memory networks, effectively captures temporal patterns in the observation space, while the Head, composed of Multi-Layer Perceptron networks, maps encoded latent features into actions. Simulations first investigate a scenario with six pursuers under observational noise, and comparisons against three communication mechanisms and two distinct evader strategies demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively enhances pursuit performance and exhibits generalization capability. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, C.; Luo, Y.; Yue, X.; Li, S.
Title: Hierarchical cooperative adaptive model predictive control for swarm self-assembly of large-scale spacecraft
Abstract: A hierarchical cooperative model predictive control approach is proposed considering multi-source complex disturbances, restricted control inputs from swarm spacecraft, mutual interference among swarm members, and low control accuracy during the self-assembly process aiming at the on-orbit swarm self-assembly mission with modularized powered intelligent components. The swarm self-assembly module is divided into the platform docking module located in the leader layer and the functional load module in the follower layer. The adaptive model predictive controller based on extended state observer is performed to transform the swarm self-assembly module from initial relative positions to the assembly docking point positions, and at the same time, realize the relative speed synergy, so as to realize the autonomous docking and assembly with a large-scale space platform. The algorithm can simplify the computational process and reduce the amount of on-board equipment computation as well as the mutual interference between modules through spacecraft modularization processing and hierarchical cooperative control. For the swarm control of on-orbit self-assembly, simulation results show that the algorithm can effectively improve the control accuracy compared with the traditional MPC approach, achieving high-precision platform assembly and payload docking. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Mi, Y.; Zhou, Z.; Cao, T.; Liang, R.
Title: Cooling effect of multi-stage water injection on the rocket thermal environment
Abstract: During rocket launching, the high-temperature and high-pressure exhaust gases generated by engine combustion impose significant thermo-mechanical loads on launch platforms. To ensure launch safety, the launch platforms are typically equipped with water cooling systems to mitigate the thermal impact of the exhaust gases. In this study, the turbulent combustion process of the gas was simulated using a RANS/LES turbulence model coupled with a finite-rate chemical kinetics model, while the gas-liquid two-phase interactions were calculated through the Eulerian Dispersed Phase (EDP) model. Validation against experimental data confirmed the validity and accuracy of the numerical model. Furthermore, this study compared simulations of multi-stage water injection configurations and investigated the influence of the total water flow rate on the thermal characteristics of the flow field. The results showed that afterburning primarily occurs within the mixing layer, and water injection effectively suppresses the temperature rise associated with the afterburning effect. Compared with single-stage injection, multi-stage water injection significantly reduces the kinetic energy and average temperature of the mixing layer, thereby alleviating thermal impact on the impingement plane and improving the overall rocket thermal environment. Under identical total water flow rates, multi-stage injection achieves a notably smaller high-temperature area than single-stage operation, proving that multi-stage water cooling system offers a more pronounced improvement on the thermal conditions. The findings provide important theoretical guidance for the optimized design of water cooling systems in rocket launch platforms. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: That, V.; Muy, S.; Lee, J.-R.
Author full names: That, Vitou (57959748200); Muy, Sengly (57209180602); Lee, Jungryun (7601482859)
Title: Beamforming center control in LEO satellite communications for maximizing minimum throughput of ground users
Abstract: The growing demand for high-speed internet access has made low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite networks a promising solution for serving geographically distributed ground users (GUs). However, determining the optimal beamforming center to ensure fairness among GUs remains a challenging task, especially in dynamic or large-scale scenarios. This paper introduces an optimization framework that maximizes the minimum data rate received by any GU in the satellite coverage area. For the two-GUs and three-GUs cases, we derive closed-form solutions based on geometric principles. For the general case with more than three GUs, we extend these geometric insights to develop an iterative algorithm that progressively refines the beamforming center location using boundary information extracted via the Jarvis algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method closely approximates the global optimum achieved by exhaustive search (ES), significantly outperforms both the centroid-based approach and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) in terms of fairness, minimum throughput, and average per-GU data rate, and remains both low-complexity and scalable. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Moghoufe, A.; Saffaripour, M.; Houshfar, E.
Author full names: Moghoufe, Ashkan (60248845000); Saffaripour, Meghdad (59994779900); Houshfar, Ehsan (41261574500)
Title: Numerical simulation and analysis of thermoacoustic instabilities in a planar trapped-vortex combustor using compressible turbulence models
Abstract: The Trapped Vortex Combustor (TVC) is developed to mitigate flame instability issues in lean-burning gas turbine combustors. This study examines thermoacoustic instabilities in lean, non-premixed methane combustion in a planar TVC at limit-cycle operation. Two complementary approaches are employed: (1) unsteady simulations using compressible Detached Eddy Simulation (DES) and Large Eddy Simulation (LES), and (2) eigenfrequency analysis of the linearized acoustic wave equation. Three operating conditions are investigated with constant mass flow rate and input power (310 kW per unit width), but with a cavity-to-main air momentum ratio varied between 0.55 and 12.9. Results show that increasing the cavity air momentum enhances turbulence intensity and mixing, thereby improving combustion efficiency from 89% to nearly 100%. However, higher momentum ratios amplify thermoacoustic instabilities, as reflected in increased maximum pressure fluctuations, from 3.1% to 14.8% of the mean, and RMS heat release rate, from 0.7% to 15.8% of the mean. Rayleigh index analysis confirms that flame-acoustic coupling strengthens with increasing cavity air momentum. Temperature analysis shows improved outlet uniformity but larger RMS fluctuations, suggesting higher thermal fatigue risk. A comparison between 2D DES and 3D LES simulations shows that while 2D simulations capture dominant frequencies within 2% accuracy and reproduce the main acoustic characteristics, they overpredict oscillation amplitudes, consistent with Squire’s theorem. Eigenfrequency analysis provides a fast and reasonably accurate estimate of frequencies and mode shapes. Across all cases, the first and third acoustic modes dominate, with frequencies of nearly 530 and 2200 Hz, largely insensitive to the cavity-to-main air momentum ratio. Sensitivity analyses indicate weak coupling between thermoacoustic instability and chemical kinetics. A complementary growth-phase analysis reveals an initial exponential amplification of pressure oscillations, followed by slower nonlinear growth, with all phases sharing the limit-cycle frequency. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ye, J.; Liu, H.; Nie, S.; Lin, X.; Yu, X.; Zhang, D.; Qin, F.
Title: Experimental and numerical investigation of a methane-fueled variable geometry RBCC combustor
Abstract: Variable-geometry combustors play a key role in enabling high-performance operation and mode transitions in RBCC engines. In this study, the combustion characteristics of methane in an RBCC combustor are investigated by ground direct-connect experiments. Three methane reaction mechanisms are further assessed through numerical simulations and validated against the experimental data to evaluate their predictive accuracy. The best-performing mechanism is then adopted to analyze the effects of throat height on the coupled flow and combustion behavior. The main conclusions are as follows: (1) The methane-fueled RBCC combustor can achieve auto-ignition and maintain stable combustion, with the maximum combustor pressure reaching 0.27 MPa. As the equivalence ratio increases, the pressures in both the combustor and the isolator rise, and the shock train becomes stronger and moves further upstream. (2) The results obtained with the three methane reaction mechanisms are generally consistent with the experimental data and can reasonably capture the pressure-variation trends in the combustor. However, discrepancies remain in predicting the exact shock-train location. Comparative analysis shows that the 7-step mechanism provides the closest agreement with the experimental pressure distribution, with shock-train position errors within 10% and an overall deviation within 7%. Overall, the 7-step mechanism accurately captures the key features of the supersonic combustion flow and exhibits good potential for engineering applications. (3) The engine performance is strongly influenced by the geometric configuration of the throat. As the throat height decreases, the combustor pressure increases, and the shock train moves further upstream with higher intensity. At the same time, reducing the throat height leads to a decrease in heat release and combustion efficiency. In particular, reducing the throat height from 2.64 H to 2.36 H results in a 34% reduction in heat release and an 8.5% decrease in combustion efficiency. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xu, T.; Zhang, D.; Xie, Y.; Chen, Z.
Title: Multi-fidelity network framework for field and performance parameters prediction of turbomachinery based on deep graph learning
Abstract: To tackle the intrinsic conflict between prediction accuracy and data cost in surrogate models within the turbomachinery field, this work introduces a multi-fidelity (MF) framework based on deep graph learning, which enables the comprehensive utilization of data with different fidelities. This framework enables simultaneous prediction of fields and performance parameters. Taking SCO₂ turbines as the research object, this paper has verified the superiority of the model. The results demonstrate that the MF model constructed based on the SAGE operator achieves the highest prediction accuracy. The maximum errors of the pressure, temperature, and velocity field predicted by the MF model are 0.15MPa, 3K, and 2m/s respectively, corresponding to relative errors of 1.43 %, 0.41 %, and 1.03 %. And the error of each physical field is lower than that of the single-fidelity model. Most of the predicted results for performance parameters fall within the 0.5 % error band, and the MF model is more capable of achieving unbiased estimation of parameters compared with the single-fidelity model. The MF network architecture effectively addresses the challenge of high-precision prediction in small-sample scenarios. It provides technical support for optimization in industrial scenarios, helps accelerate the intelligent upgrading of the turbomachinery manufacturing industry, and can be extended to other rotating machinery fields to broaden its engineering application scope. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tang, J.; Li, H.; Li, K.; Liang, Y.; Chen, L.
Title: Pulsed guidance method for Exo-atmospheric interceptor via reinforcement learning
Abstract: This paper presents a reinforcement learning based fixed pulse thrust guidance method to address the terminal interception problem in exoatmospheric environments. Markov decision process is established to model the interception for RL, where the gain term in the true proportional navigation guidance considering pulse thrust characteristics serves as the learning parameter, effectively addressing challenges of low interception accuracy and excessive energy consumption against maneuvering targets under uncertain environments. Originally, for interceptors that cannot provide continuous thrust, a guidance and control principle is derived, inspiring by the concept of suppressing line-of-sight rate. A dead-zone hysteresis nonlinear module is designed within the control framework to effectively reduce energy consumption during interception. Subsequently, the missile-target distance and zero-effort-miss are integrated into the reward function design to optimize both interception performance and fuel consumption. The proximal policy optimization is employed to train a variable-gain reinforcement learning agent that demonstrates superior performance. Finally, the proposed method is validated through simulations on a high-precision six-degree-of-freedom interceptor model under random uncertain scenarios. The results show that the proposed approach autonomously handles various uncertainties while ensuring high interception accuracy and success rates. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Dai, W.; Jia, T.; Liu, C.; Yue, L.
Title: Full-spectrum order based modal analysis for forward–backward whirl identification during run-ups
Abstract: Operational Modal Analysis (OMA) is widely used to extract modal parameters under operating conditions, however, its application to rotating machinery such as turbomachinery is challenging because non-stationarity, speed variability, and gyroscopic dynamics introduce harmonics, end-of-order effect, and the forward and backward whirl modes, thereby complicating precise identification of modal frequencies and damping ratios. To address these challenges, a Full-Spectrum Order Based Modal Analysis (FS-OBMA) method was proposed for forward and backward whirl identification during run-ups. The novelty of this method lies in the simultaneous measurement of tachometer pulses and orthogonal vibration responses at the bearing housing. By constructing a complex angle-domain signal and computing the Pseudo-FRFs, the forward and backward whirl components can be effectively separated and identified. To validate its feasibility, the method is applied to simple structures with known modal parameters a four-disk rotor test rig. Across all cases, FS-OBMA closely follows the theoretical whirl curves, maintains small frequency errors ('0.02 Hz) and limits damping-ratio scatter to about 10–12 %, whereas conventional OMA/OBMA methods may miss the whirl split, suffer biased damping. The results demonstrate that FS-OBMA provides accurate and noise-robust whirl-mode identification under run-up conditions. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yuan, X.; Jin, T.
Title: Dynamic response of the unstable oblique detonation wave in confined space via different wedge rotation
Abstract: The oblique detonation engine exhibits a high thermal efficiency in hypersonic propulsion for its special combustion model. However, at low flight Mach number, the oblique detonation wave is prone to reflecting on the upper wall, forming an unstable Mach stem structure, which leads to wave system instability. By solving the two-dimensional multi-species Euler equations, this study provides the first systematic investigation of adjusting the unstable Mach stem structure in confined space by rotating wedge. The results demonstrate that wedge rotation reduces the pressure behind the Mach stem and suppresses its upstream propagation. The effectiveness of the adjustment is influenced by the degree of Mach stem development, the angular velocity of the wedge rotation, and the rotation angle. In the study of the critical wedge-rotation angular velocity, it was found that the Mach stem is critically stable at a fixed critical location. The success of the adjustment depends on whether the Mach stem can exceed this critical position. Finally, by analyzing variations in the average total pressure recovery coefficient and mean temperature at the exit of the combustor under different adjustment strategies, guidance is provided for selecting adjustment parameters. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.
