Authors: Xiang, X.; Liu, Y.; Zhao, W.; Ma, Y.; Luo, W.; Zhao, Q.
Title: Study on flow resistance characteristics and equivalent simplification methods of micro-tube array in precooler under fluid-thermal coupling
Abstract: As the core component of the air-breathing hyper-sonic engine, the intake precooler's flow resistance characteristics under intense fluid-thermal coupling and its equivalent simplified simulation methodology hold critical significance for the precooled intake system design and the scaled experimental research on integrated matching characteristics between intake ducts and precoolers.This study initiates with an investigation of the “unit-cell” model for the precooler's four-row microtube array, elucidating the influence laws of inflow Reynolds number and relative tube spacing on total pressure loss under intense fluid-thermal coupling effects. By correlating the total pressure loss coefficient with intake mass flow rate across varying operational parameters (total pressure/ total temperature) and geometric parameters (tube diameter/row count/tube spacing) of periodic precooler modules, a mathematical model for precooler flow resistance characteristics has been established.Subsequently, for the double-layer cylindrical orifice plate model, this study systematically examines the effects of equivalent diameter ratio, relative plate spacing and staggered-hole coefficient on the total pressure loss at different Reynolds numbers. An equivalent simplified simulation method is developed based on regulated staggered-hole ratios in dual orifice plates, with experimental validation under characteristic operating conditions. Results demonstrate that when applying the staggered-hole coefficient predicted by this method, the maximum relative deviation between the measured orifice plate total pressure loss coefficients and the mathematical model predictions remains below 10 %, fulfilling engineering requirements for large flow-resistance unit simulation in integrated intake-precooler scaled testing. © © 2025. Authors: Li, J.; Feng, Y.; Zeng, B.; Wang, G.; Liu, K.; Wu, Z.
Title: Mechanism model and protective control for an air-breathing hypersonic vehicle considering the combustion mode transition
Abstract: The mode transition process in a dual-mode scramjet exhibits strong nonlinear characteristics, manifested as thrust transients and hysteresis phenomena, which pose significant threats to the stability of air-breathing hypersonic vehicles (AHVs). This paper proposes a control-oriented mechanism modeling framework that comprehensively captures both the transient dynamics and hysteresis effects during transitions. A model refinement method is developed, targeting empirical parameters governing the transition dynamics. By integrating reliable sensor feedback, the approach enhances the modeling accuracy of nonlinear transition characteristics through neural networks. To mitigate attitude instability induced by nonlinear pitching moment transients, a resilient controller is designed with transient amplitude prediction. Furthermore, a hysteresis-based auxiliary control system with actuator delay compensation is introduced to prevent excessive fuel adjustments, thereby avoiding combustion mode reversal. The key contribution of this work lies in the integration of a nonlinear mechanism model and a resilient control framework under state/input constraints, effectively safeguarding AHVs from instability caused by nonlinear mode transition phenomena. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yang, X.; Sun, G.; Jin, Y.; Ju, A.
Title: Sensitivity analysis and ANN-based surrogate optimization for transonic fan rotor: Multi-condition aerodynamic design incorporating fan-intake interaction
Abstract: Intake flow separation, especially under crosswind conditions in compact nacelle designs, is a primary cause of distortion and significantly degrades the aerodynamic performance of transonic fan rotors. This study developed a comprehensive aerodynamic optimization framework integrating fan–intake coupled simulations, global sensitivity analysis (Sobol method), surrogate modeling (Artificial Neural Networks), and multi-condition aerodynamic optimization (NSGA-II) to enhance rotor efficiency across crosswind and uniform inflow conditions. A full-annulus fan-intake coupled CFD setup was adopted to accurately capture flow distortion under a critical 30-kts crosswind condition meeting airworthiness standards. Sobol sensitivity analysis preliminarily quantified the significant impacts of blade sweep and lean angles on aerodynamic performance, providing valuable priori design knowledge. To reduce computational cost, two ANN surrogate models were constructed for both inflow conditions. The NSGA-II algorithm was then applied to identify Pareto optimal geometries under the two design points while satisfying mass-flow rate and fan pressure ratio constraints. The optimized designs achieve efficiency improvements of up to 2.33 % and 1.61 % for the intake-distortion-prioritized and uniform-inflow-prioritized solutions, respectively, while also increasing the surge margin and significantly enhancing aerodynamic performance under crosswind. This work demonstrates a robust and computationally efficient multi-condition optimization framework for transonic fan design under distortion. This work offers valuable insights into parameter prioritization and highlights the advantage of this optimization routine. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhao, Y.; Li, Z.; Li, S.; Song, X.; Yan, M.; Qi, L.
Title: Small-sample simulation of circumferential total pressure distortion uncertainty parameters based on the maximum entropy criterion
Abstract: This study addresses the challenging problem of modeling the small-sample probability distribution of the total pressure distortion parameter by introducing a practical framework through the fusion of the maximum entropy principle and physical features. In response to the problem of estimation bias caused by the traditional method relying on empirical assumptions such as normal distribution due to insufficient samples under working conditions, the method can determine the truncated probability density function parameters corresponding to the distortion intensity and range with only 1–2 constraints through the maximum entropy criterion and physical assumption, which achieves a more reliable estimation of the corresponding distribution of the total pressure distortion. Taking a certain type of turboshaft engine as the research object, combined with the helicopter's special operating envelope, indicated that the method can effectively support the input modelling of the failure factors of the compressor aerodynamic system, provide a theoretical solution for system safety analysis, and contribute to the optimization of the reserved stability margin range and the improvement of engine performance. © © 2025. Authors: Zhou, J.; Wang, K.; Wang, J.
Author full names: Zhou, Junjie (60016152500); Wang, Kedong (55387596600); Wang, Jinling (7701322564)
Title: Terrain matching with effective 3D Zernike moments: Spectrum analysis and algorithm
Abstract: Terrain-Aided Navigation (TAN) systems have been investigated as an effective alternative to the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in the GNSS-denied environment. The core of a TAN system is a terrain matching algorithm to enable the positioning parameter determination using both the pre-existing digital elevation model and the real-time sensor data. Compared with the other terrain features, the 3D Zernike Moments (3D-ZMs) are not only orthogonal but also non-sensitive to noise, which is good for improving the performance of the terrain matching algorithm. However, there are still the 3D-ZMs insensitive to the terrain elevation fluctuation which are no good for the terrain matching and yet addressed sufficiently. Hence, a terrain matching algorithm based on the 3D-ZMs is proposed for the TAN systems. The 3D-ZMs are derived in the frequency domain for the spectrum analysis of the 3D-ZMs for the first time. The performance of the 3D-ZMs including the discrimination power and the sensitivity to noise is analyzed as the foundation for the proposed matching algorithm. With the revised 3D-ZM descriptors and feature vector by removing the 3D-ZMs vulnerable to noise and those insensitive to the terrain elevation fluctuation, the proposed terrain matching algorithm has revealed the superior matching performance to the existing ones, especially when there are noise and rotations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, M.; Han, Y.; Zhong, Z.; Yang, Y.; Tang, J.; Li, Y.
Title: Vibration characteristics of multi-span multi-layer lattice sandwich beams under general boundary conditions: A unified higher-order shear deformation model
Abstract: Currently, existing research on lattice sandwich structures is focused on single-layer ones, and the analysis of lattice core layers is mainly based on the first-order shear deformation theory, with low precision and complex calculations. In this paper, a higher-order shear deformation model is proposed for the first time for multi-span and multi-layer lattice core structures under arbitrary boundary conditions, and the vibration characteristics are analyzed using the improved Fourier series method and the Rayleigh-Ritz method. Linear springs and torsion springs are introduced at the boundaries and supports to simulate the boundary and support forces of the multi-span and multi-layer lattice sandwich beams. The accuracy of the current method is verified by comparison with the finite element results. In addition, the convergence issue is considered, and the influence of different boundary conditions on the vibration characteristics of multi-span and multi-layer lattice sandwich beams is analyzed. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Cv, S.B.; Aj, L.; Lijo, V.; Suryan, A.; Kim, H.D.
Author full names: Cv, Sumesh Babu (60244678900); Aj, Leo (60244523200); Lijo, Vincent (35272957900); Suryan, Abhilash (36487403100); Kim, Heuy Dong (57203008510)
Title: Numerical investigation of a supersonic ejector under varying backpressure conditions: Insights into flow dynamics during Fabri and compound choking phenomena
Abstract: In the context of supersonic ejectors, developing a comprehensive understanding of the evolution of choking Fabri-choke ( FC ) or compound choking ( CC ) is crucial since choked flow defines the upper limit of its performance. Severe degradation in ejector performance is observed when the backpressure deviates from the design value, attributed to the absence of secondary flow choking. Until now, many works have been performed to design and evaluate the ejector systems; however, in majority of these studies, it is assumed that the ejector is working with a fixed backpressure. This work takes a new look at the transient operation of a supersonic ejector with varying backpressure. Unsteady numerical simulations have been conducted employing the Menter’s updated SST k−ω turbulence model. The onset of both FC and CC exhibits a rate-dependent and hysteresis-like response, influenced not only by the instantaneous backpressure but also by its temporal evolution. This study explores the simultaneous occurrence of FC and CC in supersonic ejectors, offering new insights into their combined choking mechanisms. Considerable insight has been gained with regard to the evolution of compound and Fabri choking, formation of multiple instances of FC and its impact on the flow and shock structures inside the ejector. An extended low-pressure shockless region emerges near the entrance of the mixing duct, coinciding with FC formation and resulting in the abrupt vanishing of the recirculation zones. Moreover, it has been shown that the chances of forming multiple instances of FC depend on the strength of wave reflections, aspects that have not received exhaustive exploration in prior research. © © 2025. Authors: Li, L.; Qiao, L.; Xu, J.; Bai, J.
Title: Improved aerodynamic-propulsion interaction model for distributed-electric-propulsion system and its integration in sizing for hybrid-electric distributed-propulsion aircraft
Abstract: Currently, there is a growing global demand for electric propulsion aircraft, driven by the objectives of carbon peaking and carbon neutrality. Distributed-electric-propulsion (DEP) aircraft, notable for improved aerodynamic and propulsive efficiency through aero-propulsive coupling, have attracted much attention. A key research direction of this configuration is to develop DEP aero-propulsive integration models applicable to the sizing design of DEP aircraft. However, the predictive accuracy of current models is limited, as they neglect the effects of flap deflection and variations in the wing’s effective aspect ratio. To address these issues, an improved model is proposed that incorporates these factors for a more accurate prediction. Validation is performed by comparing the aerodynamic coefficient augmentation predicted by the proposed method with those from other methods, and experimental and high-fidelity CFD data based on different configurations. The results show that, compared to the baseline model, the improved model provides predictions that achieves closer agreement with CFD and wind tunnel data. Subsequently, the improved model is integrated into a previously developed sizing framework for electric propulsion aircraft, to facilitate hybrid-electric distributed-propulsion (HEDP) aircraft sizing design. Specifically, the updated terms are incorporated into the sizing matrix plot (SMP) module and flight performance module, highlighting their interconnection with sizing design system. The X-57 case study indicates that, compared to the design with baseline model—which underestimated the actual configuration’s maximum takeoff mass (MTOM) by 3.31 %—the design using improved model achieves a reduction of the relative deviation from the actual MTOM to 2.06 % by simulating aerodynamic force coefficient increments more precisely and assessing the aircraft’s power requirement and energy consumption more reasonably, thereby enhancing the accuracy of design parameter evaluations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Shen, Y.; Li, Z.-L.; Huang-Fu, R.-S.; Zuo, F.-Y.
Author full names: Shen, Yu (58981681500); Li, Zhonglong (57211553280); Huang-Fu, Ruoshi (60244658700); Zuo, Fengyuan (56940179500)
Title: Aerodynamic effect of mode transition time on dual-flow axisymmetric inlet
Abstract: To investigate the influence of mode transition time on the axisymmetric turbine-based combined-cycle (TBCC) propulsion system, the unsteady Reynolds-averaged Navier-stokes (URANS) simulations are performed using overset mesh method. Three different mode transition times from turbojet to ramjet mode are studied, namely, T=0.2s, T=0.5s and T=0.8s. The characteristics of flow unsteadiness are analyzed in detail and aerodynamic performance parameters are also obtained. Numerical simulation results reveal that the rotation of the plates can restrain flow separation to a certain extent. Within the range of T=0.2s∼0.8s, the faster the transition from turbojet to ramjet mode, the smaller the separation bubbles near the plates. The comparison of aerodynamic performance parameters further shows that, as the mode transition time decreases, the total pressure recovery coefficient increases, while the outlet distortion coefficient decreases. Conclusion can be drawn that a reduced transition time is helpful for improving the aerodynamic performance of TBCC propulsion system during mode transition. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, S.; Liu, D.; Tan, H.; Pan, J.; Zhu, Y.
Author full names: Zhang, Shunli (59483638600); Liu, Dongyang (59560996700); Tan, Hongwei (60244467300); Pan, Jianfeng (8931276800); Zhu, Yuejin (55552814000)
Title: Axial dual jet flame for detonation initiation in hydrogen-fueled rotating detonation engines
Abstract: To address the limitations of conventional detonation initiation methods, an innovative initiation strategy using axial dual jet flame is proposed. In this approach, high-temperature combustion products are injected axially into the combustor as high-speed thermal jets. By adjusting the jet flame angle and intensity, localized high-temperature and high-pressure zones are generated, promoting the formation of a stable, self-sustaining rotating detonation wave (RDW). The results show that the detonation initiation process is systematically analyzed and can be divided into three key stages: the initial jet stage, the periodic compression oscillation stage, and the self-adjustment stage of RDWs. During the periodic compression oscillation stage, detonation initiation primarily relies on the coupled interaction between the flame front and localized high-pressure zones. During this process, the periodic interaction between the compression waves and the dual jet flames effectively promotes the formation of localized high-pressure zones. In the early phase of the periodic compression oscillation stage, the dual jet flames merge to form a deflagration flame accompanied by compression waves. As the deflagration flame expands, collisions between flame fronts, as well as between the flame front and the wall, lead to the development of localized high-pressure zones. In addition, the convergence of compression waves toward the jet center also contributes to the formation of these high-pressure zones. The initiation and stable propagation of RDWs are highly sensitive to both the jet injection angle and flame velocity. The jet flame angles influence the propagation direction of the detonation wave, whereas an increase in jet flame velocity generally reduces the detonation initiation time and accelerates the transition to stable RDW propagation. Introducing asymmetry in the jet flame intensity allows for the directional control of RDWs, as the detonation tends to initiate preferentially on the side with the weaker jet intensity. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tang, N.; Fang, Z.; Yang, Z.; Cai, Z.; Hu, H.; Li, H.
Author full names: Tang, Ningbiao (57222416358); Fang, Ziruo (58609077200); Yang, Zhongguang (57214724915); Cai, Zhiming (57205074654); Hu, Haiying (56514367400); Li, Huawang (57196366117)
Title: Neural network-based self-gravity modeling via mechanism decomposition in space gravitational wave detection
Abstract: In space gravitational wave detection, the gravitational effect of the spacecraft on the test mass (TM), known as self-gravity, is a key source of residual acceleration noise. The variation in self-gravity effect induced by changes in mass distribution poses a challenge to the application of conventional finite element method (FEM) due to its limited computational efficiency. To address this issue, we propose a novel neural network-based method for self-gravity modeling via mechanism decomposition to achieve efficient and reliable calculations. Multi-source disturbances factors of self-gravity are analyzed to develop a mechanism model, which decomposes the self-gravity into self-gravity bias and stiffness coupling term. The cascade forward neural network (CFNN) and convolutional neural network (CNN) are designed for data-driven modeling of the decomposed parameters. Based on the data set generated by the FEM, a comprehensive self-gravity model combining mechanism and data-driven model is successfully constructed. Experimental results show that the designed model achieves root mean square error (RMSE) below 2 × 10−16 m·s−2 on all axes while significantly improving the computational efficiency. This research introduces a new method for calculating the self-gravity effect and analyzing its evolution under complex coupling, with promising applications in gravitational wave detection missions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, J.; Zhou, Y.; Dong, Z.
Title: TSAE-UAV: A time-sense autoencoder for unsupervised anomaly detection of UAV based on flight data
Abstract: With the widespread application of Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV), ensuring their safety and reliability has become an urgent challenge. However, UAV flight data are highly complex, exhibiting multiperiodicity and intricate correlations with intra- and inter-periods. Existing methods fail to adequately address these challenges. This paper introduces TSAE-UAV, a novel self-supervised learning framework for detecting anomalies in UAV flight data. The framework encompasses a complete process from data pre-processing and feature extraction to anomaly score calculation and final detection result generation, providing a systematic solution for UAV anomaly detection. It realizes the explicit and joint representation of intra- and intra-period variations in time series by reshaping one-dimensional time series into different two-dimensional structures according to different main periods, and it uses multi-scale two-dimensional convolutions to realize multi-scale modeling of multi-periodic coupled data, which enhances the extraction of potential features. Extensive experiments on a real-world UAV dataset with 46 flight sequences, over 300 sensor features and more than 17,200 time steps after resampling, demonstrate that TSAE-UAV significantly outperforms existing methods. On five different splits of the dataset, TSAE-UAV achieves average values of 0.9653 for accuracy, 0.9434 for recall, 0.8771 for F1-score, and 0.9826 for AUC. Compared with the best-performing baseline, these metrics are improved by 0.65 %, 9.53 %, 5.04 %, and 0.76 % on average, respectively. Meanwhile, the standard deviations are reduced by 90.59 %, 92.17 %, 89.17 %, and 85.14 %, respectively. These results validate the effectiveness and reliability of TSAE-UAV in detecting anomalies in UAV flight data. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Qamar, M.S.; Gururaj, A.; Morris, S.; Raghav, V.
Author full names: Qamar, Muhammad Shahan (60244578800); Gururaj, Abbishek (57191571070); Morris, Sarah E. (57198428268); Raghav, Vrishank S. (36149311800)
Title: Effect of pitch-rate and pitch pivot location on aerodynamic loads and flow structures of a rotating flat plate
Abstract: Simultaneous pitching and rotation occurs in many natural and engineered systems, including insect wings, helicopter rotors, and wind turbines. The aerodynamic loading and flowfield dynamics in such motions depend strongly on the pitch rate and pitch–pivot location. This study systematically examines these effects by varying the reduced pitch rate (K) and pivot location (x<inf>p</inf> / c) while performing load and flowfield measurements in the rotating frame of reference. Results show that the combined influence of K and x<inf>p</inf> / c governs the aerodynamic response of the pitching–rotating wing: increasing K amplifies aerodynamic loads most prominently for a leading–edge pivot, while aft movement of the pivot reduces these differences, vanishing at the trailing edge. At fixed K=0.2, loads decrease with increasing x<inf>p</inf> / c , but this dependence weakens as K decreases. Flowfield measurements reveal that lower K or aft pivot positions delay leading–edge vortex formation and yield higher peak circulation. Based on the induced camber effect of pitching, a new scaling based on trailing-edge velocity is introduced, which collapses lift and drag coefficients across all K and x<inf>p</inf> / c , demonstrating that the combined rotational and pitch-induced velocities dictate the unsteady aerodynamic loading on simultaneously pitching and rotating wings. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xiong, M.; Yang, J.; Ding, X.; Zhang, H.; Kong, S.
Title: A concurrent dual-scale topology optimization approach of integrated thermal protection system considering macro and micro structure volume competition
Abstract: Due to the multifunctionality requirements of integrated thermal protection systems (ITPS) for hypersonic vehicles, this study proposes a concurrent dual-scale topology optimization approach to design sandwich composite structures achieving thermal insulation, high stiffness, and low mass. The optimization mathematical model is established by defining three sets of design variables: pseudo-densities for the macro layout and the micro configurations of both the web (Ti-6Al-4 V) and the insulation layer (aerogel). The optimization objectives are minimization of structural compliance and maximization of thermal compliance, subject to a total mass constraint encompassing both scales. Moreover, macro and micro structure volume competition is considered by constraints in the optimization mathematical model. Also a shell generation strategy is adopted to address the connection problems arising from the shape differences among micro structures. Numerical examples by programming reveal that optimal performance depends critically on the volume fraction allocation between macro and micro scales. Specifically, a macro-structure volume fraction with 0.3 yields the best overall performance, demonstrating 26.4 % higher stiffness and 50.5 % improved thermal resistance compared to a benchmark design. Furthermore, analysis shows that the weight coefficient for structural stiffness in the multi-objective function must exceed 0.5 to ensure adequate load-bearing capacity. Verification with commercial software confirms the effectiveness of the proposed methodology. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, Q.; Su, H.; Liu, S.; Zeng, S.; Gong, C.
Title: A multidisciplinary design optimization method for cross-domain vehicles based on distributed-centralized augmented Lagrangian coordination
Abstract: A nested multidisciplinary design optimization method based on augmented Lagrangian coordination and joint geometric/aerodynamic/structural disciplines modeling is proposed to address the problem of inefficient solving in high time-consuming disciplines such as aerodynamic, structural, and trajectory, brought by the complexity of the cross-domain vehicle configurations. Firstly, integrated geometric-aerodynamic-structural parametric modeling based on the 3D CST method is proposed to realize joint geometric/aerodynamic/structural modeling. Second, a distributed-centralized augmented Lagrangian coordination method that balances efficiency and parallel computation is proposed with an adaptive asynchronous multiplier update strategy to update the penalty weights. Finally, the X37-like vehicle in the cross-domain vehicle is taken as an object, and its nested multidisciplinary design optimization process is established to carry out the optimization design with the goal of optimal mass. The optimization results show that during the whole mission, the optimized design shape increases the payload mass by 70.2 % based on the initial shape, and the performance is significantly improved, which verifies the effectiveness of the distributed-centralized augmented Lagrangian coordination multidisciplinary design optimization method proposed in this paper. © © 2025. Authors: Wang, Y.; Liu, P.; Deng, T.
Title: An energy management strategy for fuel cell hybrid electric UAVs based on physically-aware soft reinforcement learning
Abstract: For fuel cell hybrid Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), conventional energy management strategies struggle to balance economy and device lifespan, while data-driven methods suffer from low efficiency due to a lack of physical priors. To address this, this study proposes a Physics-Aware Soft Actor-Critic (PA-SAC) algorithm that deeply integrates physical constraints and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for multi-objective co-optimization. The energy management problem is formulated as a Markov decision process incorporating physical constraints, and an adaptive weight management mechanism harmonizes data-driven learning with physical laws. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm achieves significantly higher convergence speed and a more stable training process compared to reinforcement learning methods like Deep Q-Network (DQN) and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Against online benchmarks such as the Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS), this strategy reduces flight cost by 12.6 % and extends the power source lifespan by over 3 %, effectively balancing system economy and durability. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: García-Tíscar, J.; Quintero, P.; Varela Martínez, P.; Ramírez, F.N.; Cremades, A.
Author full names: García-Tíscar, Jorge (56419902400); Quintero, Pedro M. (57285116200); Varela Martínez, Pau (57225924707); Ramírez, Federico N. (57449481300); Cremades, Andrés (57222653442)
Title: On the effect of popular additive manufacturing technologies on the performance and noise emission of UAV propellers
Abstract: Noise remains a major concern for the widespread employment of Unmanned Air Vehicles (UAVs) operations, especially in urban environments. Moreover, not only must overall noise levels be considered when analyzing the acoustic impact of UAVs, but noise quality based on the spectral content of the acoustic signal must also be considered due to the psycho-acoustic effect on the listeners. Additionally, as UAVs become widespread, the ability to perform field replacement of propellers using additive manufacturing (AM) is of increased interest to many operators. However, as different AM techniques become popular, their impact on performance and noise must be assessed. In this investigation, the effect of manufacturing characteristics, such as surface roughness and flexibility, on the propeller thrust, torque, and acoustic signature is explored through an experimental campaign, revealing differences depending on the selected technique. A numerical experiment is then performed to isolate the effect of flexibility and roughness on these characteristics. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Zheng, H.; Wu, B.; Yuan, X.; Gao, C.; Yan, R.; Ye, K.; Ding, T.
Title: Aeroelastic characteristics of the Aft deck in the convergent-divergent nozzle
Abstract: The thin-walled aft deck of advanced aircraft tail nozzles is highly susceptible to excitation by the supersonic exhaust jets, making it prone to aeroelastic instabilities. To investigate the aeroelastic behaviors of the aft deck under complex aerodynamic loads, a quasi-two-dimensional convergent-divergent(C-D) nozzle model with an aft deck is developed. Additionally, a two-way fluid-structural interaction(FSI) framework is constructed. A comprehensive study is conducted on the aeroelastic characteristics of the aft deck subjected to different nozzle pressure ratio (NPR) and pressure differentials(Δ P ) across the aft deck. The results reveal that the aft deck exhibits three types of aeroelastic responses: stable deformation(SD), multi-frequency irregular oscillation (MIO), and limit-cycle oscillation (LCO). At NPR = 8, as Δ P gradually increases, the aeroelastic response transitions through a “SD-LCO-SD” sequence, reaching the maximum number of flutter extrema at Δ P = 6 kPa. The positive extrema are predominantly concentrated in the midsection of the aft deck. In terms of aerodynamic performance, the effect of aft deck flutter on the nozzle thrust vector angle is more pronounced than on the thrust coefficient. At NPR = 8 and Δ P = 6 kPa, the transition of the shock wave pattern near the trailing edge of the deck, combined with the alternating appearance of expansion waves in the wake, results in the largest oscillation amplitude of the thrust vector angle among all cases, reaching approximately 47% of the initial value. This study provides valuable guidance for the refined design and optimization of advanced aircraft propulsion systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, H.; Xu, C.; Huang, G.; Mao, Z.; Yu, X.
Title: Tip leakage-flow control in a high-subsonic compressor cascade by distributed pulsed suction
Abstract: To mitigate the adverse effects of tip leakage flow and vortices on the internal flow field, numerical simulations are conducted using the delayed detached eddy simulation framework. For a high-subsonic (Ma =0.67) compressor cascade, this study examines tip leakage flow control by endwall distributed pulsed suction under various excitation parameters. Dynamic mode decomposition is employed to examine the mechanism whereby distributed pulsed suction affects the flow. The results indicate that distributed pulsed suction provides secondary control of the leakage vortices that redevelop downstream, further reducing the associated losses. At the same axial position, distributed pulsed suction holes positioned along the leakage vortex trajectory are more effective than those located along the blade camber line, particularly the upstream holes along the vortex trajectory. Distributed pulsed suction requires a certain suction flowrate threshold, when the suction flowrate of the second suction hole is m<inf>s</inf> =0.25 %, it leads to adverse effects. Based on dynamic mode decomposition (DMD) of the 1 F+ excitation frequency control scheme, the intensity of the leakage vortex increases during the development stage in the 1 F+ mode, with the control effect mainly manifested during the vortex breakdown phase. In the 0.5 F+ and 2 F+ modes, the intensity of the leakage vortex is effectively controlled throughout all stages. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yin, S.; Guo, Y.; Li, C.; Ran, G.; Park, J.H.
Title: A clustering-reconstruction-based multi-objective refueling mission scheduling framework for large-scale geosynchronous orbit spacecraft
Abstract: In the contemporary landscape of burgeoning space exploration activities, on orbit refueling (OOR) missions play a pivotal role in the long-term sustainability and expansion of space operations. However, as the scale of the demand for on orbit service (OOS) missions grows in tandem with the increasing number of spacecraft launched into space, the existing mission scheduling strategies struggle to meet the requirements of large-scale OOR. To cope with this challenge, this paper presents a Clustering-Reconstruction-Based multi-objective refueling mission scheduling framework for large-scale spacecraft. In this proposed framework, an Exact Euclidean Locality Sensitive Hashing based on Cosine Similarity Selecting (E2LSH-CSS) clustering method is employed to implement grouping target spacecraft, which effectively reduces the complexity of the large-scale scheduling problem by decomposing it into multiple sub-problems. This method applies E2LSH to construct a hash table containing a wide array of target spacecraft groupings, then selects the optimal target grouping according to silhouette analysis based on cosine similarity. Within each sub-problem, the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) is introduced to generate optimal Pareto fronts in each sub-problem. Furthermore, a Multi-Objective Reconstruction Algorithm (MORA) is used to reorganize the sub-problems to obtain the global optimal Pareto front. Finally, extensive numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed scheduling framework, showcaseing its flexibility and scalability in obtaining optimal mission scheduling schemes. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wall, I.P.; Amoozgar, M.R.; Popov, A.A.
Author full names: Wall, I. P. (57987386700); Amoozgar, Mohammadreza Reza (44560921400); Popov, Atanas A. (7402985778)
Title: Effect of a Nonlinear Energy Sink on the Post-flutter Behaviour of High Aspect Ratio Wings
Abstract: In this paper, the efficacy of using a Nonlinear Energy Sink (NES) to achieve a reduction in the post-flutter response of high aspect ratio wings has been investigated. The wing has two degrees of freedom in bending and torsion and is modelled as a 3rd order beam with cubic stiffness nonlinearities, accounting for the bending curvature and inertia effects of high aspect ratio wings. The unsteady incompressible aerodynamic loading is simulated using Wagner’s indicial lift model and a NES is attached to the wing spar via a linear viscous damper and a nonlinear spring with cubic stiffness. The coupled equations of motion are discretised using Galerkin’s method and converted into a state space model that is integrated numerically. Following this, the structural equations are validated against experimental results, whilst the aerodynamic model is verified against similar computational models. The effect of the NES mass, stiffness and placement in both chordwise and spanwise directions on the wing post-flutter is investigated. The efficiency of the device is improved for higher mass ratios, however, the stiffness coefficient is sensitive to the flow speed and therefore the capability for an active NES device to have tuneable stiffness is one that shows promise. The findings also demonstrate that the optimum placement for the NES in the spanwise coordinate is a function of the flow speed and the stiffness ratio of the wing model. From the initial findings a device is tuned with the target to reduce the limit cycle oscillation (LCO) amplitude in the immediate post-flutter region. The tuned NES achieved a 3.5% delay in the bifurcation and a reduction in the bending and torsion tip deflections of 84.9% and 89.5% respectively. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Ye, Y.-H.; Qian, Y.-J.; Liu, Y.; Chen, W.-X.
Title: IMDBO-based extreme learning machine for orbit prediction in earth-moon missions
Abstract: Accurate orbit prediction in the Earth-Moon space is a critical technology for space missions. This paper proposes a method that employs an improved dung beetle optimizer to optimize the extreme learning machine (IMDBO-ELM) for enhancing the precision of satellite orbit prediction. The ELM model is first applied to orbit prediction for Earth-Moon space, which enhances the computational effectiveness and prediction accuracy. And then, to obtain the optimal weights and basis of the ELM model, the IMDBO algorithm is presented, which integrates the DBO, adaptive weight adjustment strategy and nonlinear convergence factor to enhance the balance between global exploration and local exploitation. Furthermore, the verification framework is introduced to validate the proposed algorithm, which including the NRHO, DRO, and GRAIL mission observation data. Simulation results demonstrate that, under various environmental conditions, IMDBO-ELM achieves higher prediction accuracy and faster convergence compared to other algorithms, such as ELM and SABO-LSTM. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hu, H.; Guan, T.; Meng, Y.
Title: SABLE-DNN learning-based parameter identification of multi-rotor UAVs with a cross-trajectory validation and DTW evaluation
Abstract: Accurate identification of multirotor UAV parameters during closed-loop flight is challenging because of strong nonlinear couplings, environmental disturbances, and the limited excitation present in routine trajectories. This study addresses the problem using experimental flight data and deep neural networks (DNNs) for parameter identification. Initially, the closed-loop system dynamics model of the UAV is formulated with the position and attitude control. The dynamic model with randomly sampled system parameters is employed to generate feature data for tracking navigation points along experimental flight trajectories-including position, velocity, and attitude angles. To remedy the slow and highly oscillatory convergence exhibited by optimisers such as SGD, Momentum and Adam when confronted with the high-dimensional, sparse and noisy flight-data features produced by multi-sensor payloads, a Self-Adaptive Bounded-Learning Explorer for DNNs (SABLE-DNN) is proposed and a SABLE-DNN training and testing framework is constructed to establish the mapping between feature data and system parameters. The network learns implicit relationships from data to estimate parameters with high accuracy. Then, two cross-trajectories verification protocol and a Dynamic Time Warping (DTW)-based trajectory-level fidelity metric are designed to assess generalization across distinct flights. Experiments demonstrate that the system parameter identification error percentage for Trajectory 1 in SABLE-DNN is less than 5 %, and the DTW trajectory error percentage in cross-trajectory validation is under 18 %, which demonstrate accurate predictions on held-out data and consistent generalization in cross-trajectory validation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yuan, Y.; Lu, Y.; Sun, J.; Yang, Y.; Liu, Y.
Title: Low-order multi-frequency adaptive internal model control for helicopter cabin noise using MFC piezoelectric actuators
Abstract: The gear meshing noise from the helicopter main gearbox exhibits typical multi-line spectral characteristics, harming ride comfort and safety. Active structural acoustic control provides an effective solution. To address limitations of conventional control algorithms, including reliance on reference sensors, poor multi-frequency control performance, and high computational complexity, this paper proposes a low-order multi-frequency adaptive internal model control algorithm. It reconstructs the reference signal online using the internal model principle, eliminating reference sensors, improving disturbance signal tracking while simplifying the hardware system. A bandpass filter array and subband adaptive controller module are employed to achieve multi-frequency control. Additionally, an autoregressive moving average model is introduced to characterize the secondary path, with a low-order model describing system dynamics within the target frequency band to substantially reduce computational complexity. To validate the algorithm’s feasibility and effectiveness, simulations are first conducted using a scaled helicopter cabin model, demonstrating its multi-frequency noise reduction capability. Subsequently, an experimental platform is developed to simulate real helicopter gear meshing noise characteristics. For the first time, macro-fiber composite actuators are integrated into the thin-walled structure for helicopter interior noise reduction. Steady-state and time-varying multi-frequency active control experiments are then conducted. Results show that under steady-state disturbances, the algorithm achieves superior noise reduction and faster convergence than traditional feedforward algorithms, with sound pressure level reductions of 46.6 dB and 34.4 dB at 360 Hz and 900 Hz. Under time-varying disturbances, it rapidly tracks dynamic noise variations while maintaining excellent steady-state convergence, demonstrating strong adaptability and robustness. © © 2025. Authors: Chan, Y.Y.; Shu, Y.; Chen, W.-H.; Ng, K.K.H.
Title: Time-optimal motion planning of cable-suspended payload quadcopters using spatial reformulation
Abstract: Cable-suspended payload quadcopters (CSPQ) have gained significant attention due to their mechanical simplicity and agility for various applications. However, the presence of a slung payload introduces complex dynamics, making the system highly underactuated and challenging to control. An accurate and intuitive representation of the safety region is also critical for collision avoidance as the operating environment is always complex and cluttered. This paper proposes a unified hierarchical framework for time-optimal motion planning and trajectory tracking of CSPQ systems, built upon a novel spatial reformulation method leveraging the Gravity-Normal (GN) frame. As an alternative to conventional reformulation approaches, this method transforms the system positions into path-parametric coordinates with clear physical interpretation and no singularities, thereby simplifying the representation of spatial constraints. The hierarchical framework comprises an offline module for global time-optimal trajectory generation using the direct multiple shooting method and an online module for real-time trajectory tracking through nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC). Simulation experiments demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed framework in achieving time-optimal motion while suppressing payload oscillations within acceptable limits. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xu, Y.-Y.; Sun, S.; Huang, H.-X.; Tan, H.; Qin, Y.; Xie, J.-B.; Liu, Z.-J.
Title: Effects of localized low-velocity particle injection on supersonic boundary layer
Abstract: In this paper, the effects of localized low-velocity particle injection on the supersonic boundary layer are studied through numerical simulation. Results indicate that particle injection induces velocity deficit in the boundary layer. Based on the magnitude and origin of the velocity deficit, near-wall velocity profiles can be partitioned into the following regions: undisturbed region, shock influence region, recovery region, and particle influence region. With increasing streamwise distance, the velocity deficit in particle influence region gradually recovers, while the shock influence region simultaneously shifts upward and finally cannot be observed. The process of gas-particle momentum exchange near the wall is explored through analysis of the slip velocity. Additionally, the parametric study shows the influence and underlying mechanisms of particle properties on boundary layer thickness δ , displacement thickness δ *, and momentum thickness θ . The results reveal that both particle density and mass flow rate correlate with the total magnitude of momentum exchange, inducing monotonic variation in δ * and θ . Particle diameter simultaneously governs the magnitude and rate of gas-particle momentum exchange, causing the δ * and θ to exhibit non-monotonic behavior with increasing particle diameter. Using the Buckingham method and multivariate fitting, a dimensionless parameter is derived to determine the existence of local maximum in the particle diffusion distance. Finally, the implications of the findings for research on novel flow control methods are discussed. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Lee, J.; Kim, K.H.; Kim, J.G.
Author full names: Lee, Jaesung (60189112100); Kim, Kyuhong (27170064800); Kim, Jaegang (23492450600)
Title: A drag coefficient model for spinning and tumbling motion using modified free-molecular aerodynamics
Abstract: This article presents a modified drag coefficient model that accurately accounts for the drag coefficients of objects undergoing spinning and tumbling motions in the transitional regime. For the calculation in transitional regime, the aerodynamic computation method for the free-molecular regime, which is computationally efficient at high altitudes, is enhanced by incorporating multi-species collisional terms. To ensure the reliability and accuracy of this approach, surface pressure, heat flux, total force and drag acting on the object are computed and validated by comparing with DSMC results. Using this framework, drag coefficients are determined for various rotational motions, leading to the development of a modified model capable of accurately representing drag coefficients across the free-molecular, transitional, and continuum regimes. The proposed model is then implemented in a re-entry survivability analysis program to assess its impact on total range predictions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tang, Z.; Li, D.; Li, X.; Ren, J.
Title: Aerodynamic heat controlled by microjets in hypersonic shock wave turbulent boundary layer interaction
Abstract: In hypersonic shock wave turbulent boundary layer interaction (STBLI), microjet is an effective flow control technique and also shows the thermal protection potential. Based on the verified DNS method, this paper investigates cooling microjet of total pressure ratio of 0.3 for controlling STBLI generated by a ramp flow of Mach number of 5. The results show that the flow field can be divided into BL area characterized by boundary layer, MJ area characterized by microjet and counter-rotating vortex pair (CVP) and SI area characterized by shock/CVP interaction. In SI area, the CVP passes through the ramp shock and the separation area around the corner is reduced by 45% due to the downwash flow of the CVP. As for the thermal protection performance, the effective heat reduction length in STBLI is 58 the diameter of the microjet hole with the maximum heat reduction rate of 51% and the average heat reduction rate is 23.4% on the ramp wall. Further analysis reveals that the heat reduction mechanisms can be attributed to the protection of low-total-temperature jet, the heat transport to the mainstream and the reduction of the aerodynamic heat production, which indicates that the microjet changes the flow field and hence effectively controls the aerodynamic heat transport and production. The above conclusions provide an important theoretical support for developing the microjet as an active thermal protection technique. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wei, L.; Yajun, W.; Kehong, C.; Yuchen, Y.; Yingwen, Y.
Title: An afterburner configuration for improving combustion efficiency via enhanced fuel–gas mixing
Abstract: This study introduces an integrated afterburner combustion configuration that combines a ridge flameholder with fan nozzles (RFFAN). The performance of this configuration is evaluated through both experimental measurements and Large Eddy Simulation with the Flamelet Generated Manifold approach, and the results are compared with those of a traditional flameholder coupled with a direct injection nozzle (TFDIN0.8). The findings indicate that: (1) at a fuel–air ratio of 0.030, the RFFAN configuration achieves a combustion efficiency 3 % higher than that of TFDIN0.8, with a minimum efficiency of 92.9 %; (2) The ridge flameholder promotes the circumferential expansion of the fuel, leading to a corresponding circumferential expansion of the CH* signal and the formation of a broader reaction zone. (3) Analysis of the spatial distribution of the combustion state equivalence ratio processed based on the numerical simulations indicates that in the TFDIN0.8 configuration, a pronounced fuel-rich zone exists between 500 and 800 mm positions. (4) Both the uniformity and mean combustion state equivalence ratio along the flow path are greater for the RFFAN configuration than for the TFDIN0.8 configuration. This result demonstrates that the ridge flameholder enhances fuel–air mixing and evaporation rates, thereby improving the combustion efficiency of the integrated afterburner. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Che, D.; Zheng, Z.; Guo, Y.; Sun, P.; Li, C.; Yuan, J.
Title: Degeneracy-resilient registration and loop closure detection for a tumbling non-cooperative space target
Abstract: Accurate relative pose estimation is essential for on-orbit servicing of non-cooperative space targets. However, uncontrolled tumbling and sparse, noisy LiDAR point clouds degrade conventional odometry pipelines and lead to drift accumulation. To address this issue, we propose a LiDAR odometry tailored for tumbling non-cooperative targets, integrating two newly developed core modules. First, a degeneracy-resilient registration method is proposed to mitigate sensitivity to numerical degeneracy in state-of-the-art algorithms, improving registration accuracy with negligible computational overhead. Second, leveraging the attitude-overlap correlation, an overlap-driven loop-closure detection tailored to tumbling targets that effectively suppresses accumulated registration drift is provided. The LiDAR odometry employs the degeneracy-resilient registration for continuous registration and the overlap-driven loop-closure detection for loop-closure registration. Comparative simulations with conventional methods verify that the new odometry achieves higher accuracy and efficiency. Tests on both experimental and synthetic point-cloud sequences demonstrate that it maintains attitude errors consistently below 0.06 rad under constrained computational resources, confirming its suitability for autonomous on-orbit servicing tasks. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ao, C.; Xu, B.; Chen, Z.
Title: A novel 3D integrated circuit microchannel heat sink with coupled phase change cooling for thermal management of aerospace electronic devices
Abstract: With the development of electronic systems in aircraft toward higher frequency and greater integration, heat dissipation has become a critical constraint on their performance and reliability. To address this challenge, a novel three-dimensional integrated circuit (3D-IC) microchannel heat sink(MHS) was designed in this study, utilizing cascaded phase change materials (PCM) as a thermal buffer layer to effectively control the hotspot temperature of stacked chips. Numerical simulations were conducted to investigate the effects of inlet temperature and heat flux distribution on cooling performance. The conventional stacked MHS filled with PCM have weak thermal buffering capabilities, whereas the optimized MHS, with a PCM layer of only 1 mm thickness, can effectively mitigate temperature fluctuations caused by load variations. It is further demonstrated that thermal conductivity and flow disturbance intensity are the fundamental reasons for the differences in PCMs melting rates. The optimized 3D-IC embedded MHS reduced the total thermal resistance to 0.02 W/K. The viscosity change induced by inlet temperature resulted in a 0.8 % decrease in pressure drop and a 24.1 % increase in average heat transfer coefficient. Notably, the embedded 3D-IC MHS demonstrated an ultra-high average heat transfer coefficient (189.8 W/m²·K) while maintaining a relatively low pressure drop (34.8 kPa). Compared with conventional stacked MHS designs, the optimized 3D-IC MHS significantly reduced the chip hotspot temperature by 55.6 %, providing an efficient thermal management solution for high power density electronic systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Pu, J.; Lian, F.; Fan, Z.-E.
Title: HA-PointAE: A novel hybrid attention PointNet autoencoder for high-fidelity airfoil generation and aerodynamic shape optimization
Abstract: Effective and accurate airfoil generation is essential for aerodynamic shape optimization. However, conventional parametric methods rely heavily on predefined functions and parameters, which limits their flexibility for generating diverse airfoil shapes. Furthermore, existing deep generative models (DGMs) often suffer from training instability or produce imprecise results, leading to undesirable airfoil designs. To address these limitations, we propose a novel hybrid attention PointNet autoencoder (HA-PointAE), which achieves geometrically accurate airfoil generation while maintaining strong training stability. Our model consists of two components: an encoder and a decoder. The encoder utilizes spatial and channel attention to capture comprehensive geometric features, while the decoder employs multi-head attention to model long-range geometric dependencies. To further enhance generation quality and ensure airfoil smoothness, we introduce a novel loss function that integrates essential physical geometric information. Compared to other airfoil generation methods, our model not only exhibits superior reconstruction accuracy and generation quality but also demonstrates high efficiency and performance in aerodynamic shape optimization tasks. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, C.; Huang, H.; Feng, Z.; Zhang, Q.; Ge, J.
Author full names: Zhang, Chuyao (60240079500); Huang, Hao (60240125200); Feng, Zhiwei (35751748700); Zhang, Qingbin (16445028700); Ge, Jianquan (27172195800)
Title: The FFD parametric method based on streamline for three-dimensional inward-turning inlet
Abstract: The three-dimensional inward-turning inlet has broad application prospects in the design of inlet due to its advantages of high internal space utilization, strong compression capacity, and high total pressure recovery coefficient. In this paper, a streamline based FFD (SLBFFD) parametric method is proposed to address the problems of high variable dimensions and difficulty in controlling the inlet profile in the traditional Free Form Deformation (FFD) method for three-dimensional inward-turning inlet parametric modeling. This method constructs a mapping relationship between the two-dimensional rectangle and the inward-turning inlet profile by leveraging the streamlines that define the inlet profile. Specifically, the arc length along the flow capture tube (FCT) and the streamlines themselves are normalized, facilitating the transformation of grid points on the wall of the three-dimensional inlet into grid points on the two-dimensional rectangle. Subsequently, the application of a deformation in the normal direction of the rectangular control box can be directly mapped into a corresponding deformation of the inlet profile, thereby enabling precise control the deformation of the inlet profile. The viscous optimization design is conducted utilizing an inward-turning inlet after viscosity correction as the baseline inlet, by employing the proposed SLBFFD parametric method. The deformation of 50 control points is generated through the linear combination of 10 design variables, and the kriging-assisted bi-objective constrained global optimization (BOCGO) algorithm is employed to solve two optimization problems with the objectives of the total pressure recovery coefficient and the comprehensive indicator combining the total pressure recovery coefficient, the pressure ratio, and the internal drag coefficient. The constraints considered in the above cases are the Mach number at the throat (and the pressure ratio for the first case). The results demonstrate that: the total pressure recovery coefficient increase 6.06 % for the first case, while the second one exhibits an 8.29 % improvement in comprehensive performance to the baseline inlet with the total pressure recovery coefficient at the exit of the isolator section is increased by 5.73 %. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ganchaudhuri, P.; Bhawal, C.
Title: Mission specific PID tuning for energy-efficient quadrotor flights
Abstract: Quadrotors suffer from limited flight time due to fixed-capacity batteries and the absence of in-flight recharging, driving the need for more energy-efficient flight strategies. In this work, we address mission-specific energy minimization by integrating a physics-based Energy Consumption Model (ECM) into an Optimal Control Problem (OCP). Unlike existing methods relying solely on thrust and torque constraints, our formulation imposes realistic bounds on rotor speeds and accelerations, resulting in a more accurate and feasible least-energy trajectory. We further propose a comprehensive controller-tuning framework for cascaded PID controllers to closely track this optimal trajectory, achieving energy savings of up to 70% compared to conventional tuning techniques. We demonstrate using extensive numerical simulations the significant benefits of tailoring both trajectory design and controller parameters for practical energy-efficient quadrotor operations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tuo, Z.; Wang, Z.; Wang, N.; Xia, H.; Lyu, D.; Tang, J.; Zheng, Z.; Zhang, B.
Author full names: Tuo, Zhonglan (58882570600); Wang, Zhaohui (55818805800); Wang, Nianxian (35745323000); Xia, Haishan (59713793200); Lyu, Dingkang (60240110800); Tang, Jinshan (60240017100); Zheng, Zhengding (57437084400); Zhang, Bowen (58998170100)
Title: Internal structural design of liquid hydrogen tanks in microgravity: Thermal management and boil-off mitigation
Abstract: In aerospace hydrogen systems, ensuring the effective and dependable storage of liquid hydrogen (LH₂) is essential for both the success of deep-space missions and the long-term stability and sustainability of the entire system. However, under microgravity conditions, LH₂ tanks frequently encounter issues of non-uniform temperature distribution and boil-off losses, which reduce storage efficiency and increase the risk of material failure. To address these challenges, this study employed computational fluid dynamics (CFD) to systematically evaluate eight internal structural configurations and their effects on thermal management and boil-off behavior. The results indicate that the combined structural configuration of a bottom disk nozzle and dual conical nozzles exhibits the best performance among all structural configurations, reducing the cooling time by 36.49 %–54.05 % and reaching a cooling rate that is 3.77–18.18 times that of the other structural configurations within the 120–165 s interval. Compared with the baseline structural configuration, this structure increased the Nusselt number by 42.24 %, while reducing LH₂ boil-off mass and loss rate by 73.62 % and 72.00 %, respectively. The performance enhancement arises from the formation of a stable high-velocity wall-attached flow near the tank wall, which mitigates local overheating, strengthens overall heat transfer, improves temperature uniformity, and effectively suppresses boil-off. This structural configuration offers a feasible solution for long-term LH₂ storage in microgravity and guides cryogenic propellant management in future deep-space missions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: He, K.-L.; Zhang, T.-Y.; Zhang, X.-G.; Chen, Q.
Title: Model predictive control for aircraft engine thermal management under nonlinear heat transfer and time delay
Abstract: Growing thermal loads and frequent engine shifts in advanced aircraft pose critical thermal management challenges. Fuel thermal management systems (FTMS) enable heat dissipation from distributed sources but exhibit nonlinearity and strong coupling that challenge conventional control methods. Although model predictive control (MPC) has been applied, its dependence on linearized models limits effectiveness across wide operating conditions. This paper applies the heat current method to derive a linearized equivalent model from the original nonlinear heat exchange constraints, explicitly incorporating time-delay effects. This model enables the design of a constrained MPC strategy, which seamlessly integrates real-world system characteristics to achieve robust and fully automated thermal management across wide operating conditions. Experimental validation on a full-scale three-medium (fuel/water/air) ground-test rig with an intermediate circuit demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed strategy under realistic flight conditions. The system maintains air outlet temperature within strict operational limits while ensuring complete prevention of water boiling, achieving a maximum temperature deviation of 3.43 K and a time-averaged deviation of 0.96 K. These quantitative results confirm the controller's robust performance in managing complex thermal dynamics under strong time-varying operating conditions. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Han, Y.; Kong, C.; Lv, C.; Wang, Z.; Chang, J.
Title: Multi-modal data-driven neural network for isolator velocity field reconstruction
Abstract: In supersonic inlet research, the isolator’s flow field characteristics are critical to aircraft performance. Experimental measurements, limited by sensors, cannot fully obtain full-field parameters, while CFD simulations, though providing full-field information, suffer high computational costs and deviations from experiments. This study constructed a dataset via variable-condition wind tunnel experiments (measuring wall pressure signals and flow schlieren images of the isolator separation zone) and numerical simulations (generating corresponding velocity fields), then proposed a dual-module neural network for isolator velocity field reconstruction. The Pressure-to-Schlieren Module takes measured upper/lower wall pressure signals as input to output schlieren images; the Multi-Modal Velocity Field Reconstruction Module fuses these signals and images to learn mappings between experimental data and CFD-derived x- and y-direction velocity fields. Post-training, only real-time wall pressure enables rapid velocity field output. Quantitative validation shows: the P-S Module achieves a median Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) of 0.72 and pixel-level Mean Absolute Error (MAE) ≤3.2; the Multi-Modal Module has x/y-velocity Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) 7.2/5.8 m/s and Average Relative Error (ARE) ≈0.015, reducing errors by 8.2 %–11.5 % versus the ablation model (without schlieren fusion). Compared with traditional CFD (≈9.2 h per working condition), it realizes real-time output, improving computational efficiency by over three orders of magnitude. The innovation lies in a dual-module architecture breaking single data-driven limits, achieving cross-dimensional reconstruction from local measurements to full-field parameters via pressure-schlieren-velocity field multi-modal fusion. © © 2025. Authors: Hai, H.; Zhang, R.; Luo, J.; Li, B.; Yan, Y.; Fang, S.
Title: Operational stability control of a linear actuator in the telescope tracking mechanism based on a robust disturbance observer
Abstract: Before the TianQin mission is implemented in 2035, it still faces many scientific and technological challenges. One of these is the breathing angle changes between the TianQin constellation. Adjusting optical components using the telescope tracking mechanism to actively compensate for angle changes is a key factor for normal gravitational wave detection. As the driving device of the telescope tracking system, the linear actuator has its tracking error and operational stability directly determining whether the telescope tracking mechanism’s pointing jitter meets the TianQin mission’s requirements. However, factors such as nonlinear friction force, sensor measurement errors, and environmental noise significantly degrade the actuator's tracking accuracy and operational stability, necessitating the development of high-precision control strategies to effectively suppress these disturbances. In this article, we propose a composite control method to improve the linear actuator’s operational stability. Firstly, modeling and identification were conducted based on the experimental setup of the linear actuator drive system, and the main disturbance noise was analyzed and measured. Secondly, to suppress the linear actuator, a high-performance robust disturbance observer (DOB) was introduced into the feedback control system. Finally, the proposed method was validated using the linear actuator drive system experimental setup. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances the transient performance and trajectory tracking performance of the linear actuator. When ω is 0.00001, the linear actuator achieves an operational stability of 1.2 nm/Hz1/2 @ 10 mHz. This meets the stability requirements for the linear actuator in the gravitational wave telescope tracking mechanism. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Zhou, C.; Zhong, Q.; Mo, Y.; Liao, M.; Yao, X.; Li, J.
Title: Aircraft tire-flooded runway fluid-structure interaction simulation: Hydroplaning characterization using the material point method
Abstract: When an aircraft takes off on a water-contaminated runway, the skid resistance of the pavement decreases, leading to an increased risk of runway excursions. Meanwhile, water spray stirred up by the tires may enter the engine inlet, affecting engine performance. To address the issues of tire skidding and water spray during aircraft landing gear operation, this paper proposes a wheel–water–runway fluid–structure interaction (FSI) model based on the material point method (MPM) to simulate the rolling process of a flexible tire on a water-covered pavement. The model is validated against existing experimental data from both static and dynamic perspectives. For the first time, the influence of the tire slip angle on hydrodynamic pressure and spray behavior is quantitatively revealed, while the effects of multiple factors—including water film thickness, tire speed, and tread characteristics—on tire hydroplaning behavior are comprehensively investigated. The results show that when the water film thickness is much smaller than the groove depth, tire grooves can effectively drain water, though their drainage capacity decreases with increasing tire speed. When the water film thickness exceeds the groove depth, the difference in drainage performance between new and worn tires becomes significantly smaller. Severely worn tires, due to insufficient drainage capacity, are more prone to water accumulation and sudden spray. Furthermore, an increase in the tire slip angle enhances asymmetric tire–pavement contact, further reducing drainage efficiency. This study demonstrates the applicability of MPM in handling large-scale FSI problems in airport pavement systems and provides insights for the anti-skid design of tires and runways. © © 2025. Authors: Wang, Z.; Quarta, A.A.; Wang, W.
Author full names: Wang, Zhicong (58979086000); Quarta, Alessandro A. (8324418800); Wang, Wei (57192615321)
Title: Optimal solar system escape trajectories for gradient-index solar sails via saturation functions
Abstract: Solar System escape is a necessary step for interstellar missions and requires long-term, advanced spacecraft propulsion capabilities when the heliocentric trajectory does not include gravity assist maneuvers. In this context, solar sails are becoming an attractive option due to their increasing technological maturity, which enables their effective use in interplanetary missions. Among these propellantless propulsion systems, the gradient-index solar sail is a promising concept capable of providing a transverse component of the thrust vector even when the sail is in a (passively stable) Sun-facing attitude. The presence of this transverse thrust component enhances escape performance compared to a classical reflective solar sail with a fixed Sun-facing orientation or a piecewise-constant attitude profile. This paper investigates the capabilities of a Sun-facing gradient-index solar sail for escaping the Solar System. The problem is formulated within the framework of indirect optimal control to determine the time-optimal escape trajectory. Additionally, a path inequality constraint limiting the minimum solar distance is handled using a saturation function and incorporated into the mathematical model. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhao, Z.; Wu, F.; Chen, J.
Title: Chance-constrained visual servoing MPC for non-cooperative spacecraft rendezvous
Abstract: This paper proposes a model predictive control framework for autonomous rendezvous with non-cooperative spacecraft, explicitly accounting for uncertainties in a chance-constrained manner to ensure that state constraints are satisfied with a user-specified probability. The approach combines image-based visual servoing with a coupled attitude-orbit dynamic model formulated directly in the image space, enabling precise feedback control using visual feature coordinates. To address uncertainties such as target tumbling, modeling errors, and sensor noise, a recursive disturbance estimation and prediction scheme is developed. The resulting disturbance statistics are embedded into a probabilistic constraint-tightening mechanism, guaranteeing mission safety at the desired confidence level. Numerical simulations and Monte Carlo analyses demonstrate that the proposed method improves tracking accuracy and energy efficiency while maintaining high constraint satisfaction under stochastic disturbances and uncertain initial conditions. These results highlight its robustness and reliability for vision-based autonomous rendezvous in complex space environments. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Raj, K.; Lawrence Raj, P.R.
Title: Gravity effect on flow and heat transfer characteristics of liquid methane in vertical feed pipe
Abstract: The multiphase flow of cryogenic propellants in the rocket feed pipe critically influences the engine performance during deep space missions. The cooling process is highly unsteady and difficult to regulate due to flow instabilities and fluctuating cooling rates. The behaviour of two-phase flow in microgravity differs from that observed under the influence of hyper-gravity. This study employs a computational model to investigate the movement of liquid methane in a vertical, three-dimensional feed pipe under various gravity conditions. A three-dimensional Volume of Fluid (VOF) model coupled with the energy equation is employed to model the multiphase flow of liquid methane. The VOF-based computational model enables the prediction of hydrodynamic and thermodynamic characteristics under various velocity and gravity conditions. The volume fraction field and the probability density function (PDF) are analyzed to identify the flow pattern and the interaction between the two phases ( i.e. , liquid and vapour). The velocity profiles and phase distribution are utilized to analyse the structure of the two-phase flow phenomenon. Furthermore, the bulk and wall-adjacent temperatures are analyzed to gain insight into the temperature fluctuations within the pipe. The volume fraction result shows the presence of bubble, annular, and slug flow patterns in the flow regime. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, B.; Song, T.; Ye, J.; Wu, Z.; Jia, K.
Title: Design, performance optimization, and experiments of a tail-propeller layout multi-rotor UAV
Abstract: As a new type of aerial transportation, eVTOLs are regarded as a highly promising aircraft category for future urban mobility, owing to their structural simplicity and environmental benefits. However, improving the aerodynamic performance including flight time and flight range at high speeds is one of the key issues in the practical application of eVTOLs. This paper proposes a new aerodynamic layout based on a tail-propeller configuration. Different from multi-rotor configurations, the tail-propeller UAV utilizes a propeller at the rear to provide the thrust required for forward movement, thereby enhancing the efficiency of the main propeller and consequently improving the performance of rotor UAVs. Firstly, this paper introduces the layout and integrated design method of the tail-propeller UAV, and establishes the dynamic model. Secondly, wind tunnel tests are designed to fit and validate the model, and the mechanism for improving the efficiency of the main propeller is derived. Finally, an optimization design method for the tail-propelled UAV is proposed and validated through actual flight tests. The results show that compared with multi-rotors, tail-propeller UAVs demonstrate superior flight performance and can fly at a speed of 108 km/h for 55 minutes. The problem of short flight time at low speeds caused by the introduction of tail propeller has also been effectively solved through the α -tail-propeller aerodynamic layout. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kang, Y.; Fang, G.; Yin, C.; Zhao, Y.; Xing, F.
Title: Experimental study on liquid-fueled low swirl combustion with inlet of high velocity and temperature
Abstract: This study conducted atmospheric-pressure laboratory component-level experiments to investigate the performance of the low swirl combustion with liquid kerosene under high bulk flow velocities (above 45m/s) and high inlet temperatures (above 650 K) for gas-turbine applications. Effects of the inlet temperature, the temperature rise, the bulk flow velocity and the blockage ratio of the perforated plate on combustion performance are focused. Based on experimental results, the flame stability limits were reported to narrow under the high velocity inlet, the prevaporization and premixing of liquid fuel are key factors that can be facilitated by increasing the inlet temperature and the bulk flow velocity. With the premise of appropriate evaporation and mixing, an increase in the temperature rise would not impair the outlet temperature distribution or pollutant emissions. Indeed, it made the combustion approach the designed ideal states, and reached more uniform distributions of dynamic pressure and temperature and lower emissions of CO and NOx. The potential of liquid-fueled low swirl combustion for gas-turbine conditions was proved by an ultra-low dry basis NOx emission of 4.5 ppm at the inlet temperature of 750 K and the temperature rise of 400 K. Additionally, recommended ranges of four key designed parameters for the combustion are proposed, such as the swirl number above 0.45, the inlet temperature above 750 K, and so on. © © 2025. Authors: Saini, S.; Shankar Sarvankar, S.; Vadlamani, N.
Author full names: Saini, Shivam (59914001400); Shankar Sarvankar, Sumit (60232392100); Vadlamani, N. R. (58023497900)
Title: Epistemic uncertainty due to turbulence closure on intakes under strong crosswinds
Abstract: Modern aircraft tend towards ultra-high bypass ratio engines (with large nacelle diameters) that offer significant fuel burn benefits, reducing emissions and noise. When subjected to crosswinds, the flow over these intake lips experiences severe acceleration, strong streamline curvature, separation, transition, and turbulence reattachment. The flow over the intake lip also experiences severe hysteresis at low mass flow rates. It is challenging to capture these flow characteristics using conventional Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) models. Our initial studies also showed that the results in the hysteresis regime are also sensitive to the initial condition in the RANS simulations. In this paper, we quantify the uncertainty due to turbulence closures in the predictions over a nacelle subjected to severe crosswinds. A series of numerical simulations (using the k−ω shear stress transport (SST) closure model) is carried out on a Quasi 3D duct (Q3D) extracted from a 3D axisymmetric nacelle using the SU2 solver. After carrying out baseline simulations over a wide range of operating conditions, ranging from low-speed separation to high-speed shock-induced separations, we exploited the ”Enabling Quantification of Uncertainty in Physics Simulations” (EQUiPS) module in the SU2 solver for uncertainty quantification due to turbulence closure. This module relies on the eigenspace perturbation of Reynolds stresses, where both the eigenvalues and the eigenvectors of the Reynolds stress tensor are perturbed to extreme states within the barycentric triangle. For every baseline simulation, five additional simulations are carried out to quantify its impact on the quantities of interest, which include the radial variation of normalised pressure ( P<inf>norm</inf> ) and isentropic Mach number (ISM) distribution over the intake lip. Our results show that the eigenspace perturbations can encompass the experimental predictions of the Q3D operating map. Interestingly, for a range of intake operating conditions across which the hysteresis is prominent, we also observe that the eigenspace perturbations prediction are independent of the initial flow conditions and predict similar uncertainty envelopes. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.
