Authors: Chai, Y.; Yang, Z.; Liang, X.; Han, J.
Title: Time-optimal gap-traversal trajectory planning scheme for collaborative aerial transportation systems: An experimental research
Abstract: Efficient delivery of heavy payloads is crucial for collaborative aerial transportation systems, especially in logistics transportation and disaster relief. Without the need for detours, directly passing through such narrow gaps as windows and buildings can effectively shorten the delivery time. With the deployment of multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), an increased payload capacity has been achieved. However, this enhancement comes at the cost of a substantial rise in the complexity of formation coordination and adjustment. Additionally, heavy payloads delivery through multiple UAVs connected by suspended ropes may result in significant coupling between the UAVs and the payload, presenting further challenges to the gap-traversal task in collaborative aerial transportation systems. To address these challenges, a two-stage trajectory planning scheme is developed for the first time for collaborative aerial transportation systems, enabling safe and efficient completion of the gap-traversal task. Specifically, the first stage focuses on obtaining the gap-traversal formation by optimizing the system volume and energy. The second stage generates the time-optimal gap-traversal trajectory by considering dynamics, actuation, and waypoint constraints. A series of simulation and hardware experiments with NMPC and MPCC methods are conducted to validate the effectiveness of the proposed planning scheme. The comparison results indicate that, compared to the baseline methods, the proposed approach achieves at least a 5 % reduction in trajectory length and a 9.4 % decrease in transportation time, clearly demonstrating its effectiveness in improving transportation efficiency. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Han, M.-H.; Li, X.; Zhuang, Y.-G.; Song, X.; Li, M.-Q.
Title: Reliable system design under mixed uncertainties using analytical target cascading
Abstract: A method for multi-disciplinary design optimization (MDO) of reliable systems is proposed, which simultaneously considers reliability allocation (RA) and the combined effects of both stochastic and epistemic uncertainties. By treating reliability as a fuzzy variable, insufficient data can be used to approximate reliability properties and search for optimal reliability values within the prescribed system target. This approach interacts with other design variables, facilitating simultaneous execution of reliability allocation and reliable system design. Analytical target cascading (ATC) serves as the framework for implementing MDO, translating both reliability and design targets into specifications for the elements constituting a decomposed system to emulate the reliability allocation process. The uncertainty quantification and propagation of both stochastic random design variables and fuzzy reliability variables in a multi- level system are evaluated based on the reliability-based design optimization (RBDO) and possibility-based design optimization (PBDO). A mixed uncertainty analysis is proposed to quantify the interactions between aleatory and epistemic uncertainties, and sequential optimization and reliability assessment (SORA) is used to improve the efficiency of nested optimization process. The effectiveness of the proposed method, termed mixed uncertainties-based analytical target cascading with reliability allocation (MUATC with RA), is demonstrated through three illustrative examples. This approach successfully achieves the coordinated advancement of reliability design and system performance optimization. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, F.; Zhang, R.; Zheng, R.; Sun, Q.; Qin, M.
Author full names: Liu, Fangbin (57203385556); Zhang, Rongchun (14039715400); Zheng, Riheng (54419208700); Sun, Qiang (59737379600); Qin, Mengze (58943596600)
Title: Effects of inflow total pressure on combustion performance of kerosene-fueled multi-cavity scramjet combustor
Abstract: The kerosene-fueled scramjet with multi-cavity combustor is a promising concept for hypersonic flight. However, the influence of inflow total pressure on its combustion performance has not been sufficiently investigated, which constrains the preliminary design and optimization under ground-based test conditions. To address this issue, the present investigation utilizes numerical simulations to analyze combustion performance at combustor inflow Mach number of 2.8. The combustor features six cavities arranged in three groups, each containing two transverse cavities. The results show that, firstly, variations in inflow total pressure affect combustion efficiency, combustion zone length, total pressure loss coefficient, mainly through interacting with equivalence ratio to alter mainstream flow characteristics, rather than through direct effects. Secondly, under similar flow conditions, the direct influence of both the equivalence ratio and inflow total pressure on the combustion zone length is relatively limited. Thirdly, higher inflow total pressure enhances combustion efficiency while reducing its sensitivity to changes in the equivalence ratio. Fourthly, the studied scramjet configuration achieves a peak combustion efficiency of 87.6 % at 0.8 MPa inflow total pressure, accompanied by a 5.2 % reduction in total pressure loss coefficient compared with the baseline. These findings can provide valuable insights for the preliminary design and optimization of kerosene-fueled multi-cavity scramjet combustors operating at high Mach numbers. © © 2025. Authors: Wang, T.; Xuan, Y.
Title: Aerothermal features from interplays between multi-dome combustors and turbine airfoils of an aero-engine
Abstract: The elevation of turbine inlet temperature in high-performance aero-engines intensifies the combustor turbine interaction (CTI), which readily leads to airfoil localized ablation. Current research on CTI predominantly employs sector models (single or multi-dome) with periodic boundary conditions. However, the influence mechanisms of circumferential pulsation characteristics in full-annular model and the effects of periodic boundary conditions in sector models on CTI remain unclear, which seriously hinders the extension of findings from sector models to full-annular configurations. To elucidate the effects of periodic boundary conditions in sector models on CTI, this study benchmarks integrated combustor-turbine full-annular simulation results, conducting a comparative analysis of single- and triple-dome configurations to assess sector model boundary condition impacts. Results indicate that circumferentially rotating pulsation modes revealed by full-annular simulations remain uncaptured by both single-dome and three-dome models. For the single-dome configuration, the circumferential span falls below the characteristic scale of the precessing vortex core of the combustor dome, thereby suppressing precessing vortex core development. Furthermore, artificial continuity constraints imposed by periodic boundary conditions in single- and three-dome models inhibit secondary flow vortex structures within their respective domains, resulting in underpredicted circumferential temperature distortion factor at the combustor outlet. Within turbine passages, relative to full-annular and three-dome configurations, attenuated radial mixing in the single-dome model elevates radial thermal gradients and consequently overpredicts the detrimental impacts of hot streaks on turbine blades. This study informs the methodology development for extrapolating sector-model-based CTI findings to actual full-annulus configurations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhou, C.; Qiao, Y.; Huang, X.; Li, S.; Ma, L.
Title: Safe and robust satellite formation control via dynamic constraint following with input saturation
Abstract: This paper tackles the interconnected issues of cooperative tracking, collision avoidance, and input saturation for satellite formation flying in the presence of model uncertainties. To this end, a novel, constraint-following-based safe and robust cooperative formation control scheme is proposed. First, a new collision avoidance activation condition with approach velocity (CAAC-AV) is proposed to provide a less conservative trigger for avoidance maneuvers. Second, a two-level weight arbitration strategy is designed: at the inter-constraint level, the CAAC-AV governs a dynamic weighting scheme to manage the priority between tracking and safety; at the intra-constraint level, static weighting parameters are selected to balance control smoothness and performance. Finally, a diffeomorphism-based transformation is integrated to handle actuator saturation, converting the constrained control problem into an unconstrained one. The proposed framework can suppress model uncertainties, and its effectiveness is demonstrated through comparative simulations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yi, C.; Tang, D.; Li, Y.; Song, H.; Yan, C.
Title: Explicit improvement of SST model for separation flows under pressure gradient based on field inversion
Abstract: In this paper, an explicit eddy viscosity correction model is developed for pressure-gradient-induced turbulent boundary layer separation flow. Based on the shear-stress-transport (SST) model, the corrections are obtained by field inversion by adding a multiplicative correction to the linear eddy viscosity as well as an additional nonlinear term, taking turbulent separation bubble (TSB) as the inversion case. It is shown that the inaccurate prediction of the separation flow originates from the linear eddy-viscosity deviation in the near-wall region, and a correction formula based on the pressure gradient is developed. To evaluate the applicability of the correction model, several test cases are performed, including other cases of Coleman’s TSBs, Abe’s TSBs, axisymmetric separated boundary layers, and different heights of bump flows. According to numerical results, the correction model can accurately characterize the separation flow and enhance the skin friction coefficient prediction for flows with a pressure gradient. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ait Chikh, M.A.; Ragueb, H.; Ravelet, F.; Khelladi, S.; Hachemi, M.; Belaidi, I.
Author full names: Ait Chikh, Mohamed Abdessamed (57200088710); Ragueb, Haroun (55587958200); Ravelet, Florent (6505926632); Khelladi, Sofiane (9841703400); Hachemi, Madjid (57217869494); Belaidi, Idir (16303170800)
Title: Design optimization of contra-rotating axial flow fan using a SHADE-based approach
Abstract: Contra-rotating fans are known to offer an efficient solution for applications requiring high pressure and low energy consumption. This study introduces a novel approach utilizing a metaheuristic algorithm for the optimal design of a contra-rotating axial flow fan. The geometry of the two rotors is obtained using Success-History based Adaptive Differential Evolution (SHADE) optimization algorithm and a 1D inverse design model, aiming to maximize overall hydraulic efficiency. For the optimal design, the blade cross-section between the hub and tip of the two rotors must comply with geometric constraints defined by the limitations of NACA65-type airfoil cascade. To achieve the desired nominal pressure rise, a sequential optimization strategy is proposed. First, the front rotor is optimized, and then the rear rotor is optimized based on the results from the front rotor, including parameters such as hub and tip radius, velocity triangles, and others. The optimal geometry is analyzed over a range of flow rates using loss models and computational fluid dynamics (CFD) software. The results show a high performance for the contra-rotating geometry obtained by the proposed approach, with a wide operating range. At design point, relative to the existing fan in literature, the proposed fan achieves a 5 % efficiency improvement and a 4 % higher pressure rise than the specification, whereas the reference fan exhibits a 12 % deficit. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yang, S.; Zhou, W.; Qu, S.; Chu, W.; Yang, Q.; Srigrarom, S.; Khoo, B.C.
Author full names: Yang, Shangru (57878613700); Zhou, Wentao (58695668300); Qu, Shenwei (59653618800); Chu, Weimeng (57202389098); Yang, Qingjun (7404076917); Srigrarom, Sutthiphong (15137193900); Khoo, Boo Cheong C. (7006437595)
Title: Fast and high-Accuracy state estimator for some unknown dynamic objects with a stereo camera in aerial tracking
Abstract: Using cameras to estimate the state of targets whose data cannot be directly measured or transmitted by internal sensors has become a new focus in recent years. This technique is often applied in important fields such as counter-drone systems and localization of interested targets. Existing approaches that combined object detection with Kalman filter offered fast estimation, but performed poor accuracy resulting from mismatched motion models and large fluctuations in manually calibrated bounding boxes. Moreover, the depth estimated by stereo cameras can only rely on single-frame stereo triangulation rather than multi-frame optimization because targets are moving. The depth error is large, and most algorithms cannot balance accuracy and real-time performance well. In this paper, we proposed a novel target state estimator that achieved both high accuracy and real-time performance by tightly integrating filtering and optimization. An adaptive Kalman filter with chi-squared test was integrated to object bounding boxes, enabling robust tracking while preserving sensitivity to visual angle changes. Then, the model of stereo depth error was derived, and the closed-form least-squares depth cost function was constructed using an expanded set of optimized static features. Finally, dynamic features and target states were jointly optimized. The experiments on public datasets and real-world testing showed that our method had competitive accuracy and almost doubled real-time performance compared to existing state-of-the-art approaches, improving accuracy by up to 42% improvement over the original bearing-angle method while maintaining high computational efficiency. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Cumelles-Céspedes, J.; Grau-Álvarez, A.; Casas, O.; Ortega, E.
Author full names: Cumelles-Céspedes, Joel (60017903700); Grau-Álvarez, Adrià (60017886300); Casas, Oscar R. (6602883423); Ortega, E. (19639015000)
Title: An eight-degree-of-freedom coupled aerodynamic model for high performance paraglider-harness/pilot systems
Abstract: Parametric aerodynamics is commonly used to estimate forces and moments in parafoil-payload dynamic models, and extensive data exist in the literature to support these models. However, high-performance paraglider-harness systems have significant differences in geometry and behaviour, and aerodynamic data for these configurations remain limited. Conventional models of parafoil-payload systems sometimes fail to accurately reproduce the geometric features of paragliders, and achieving proper coupling between longitudinal and lateral aerodynamics, which is relevant in many flight scenarios. These limitations restrict designers’ ability to predict paraglider flight dynamics during the early design phase and, therefore, more accurate and dedicated modelling approaches are required. This work couples an eight-degree-of-freedom dynamic model with a Horseshoe Vortex Method (to solve Prandtl's lifting-line theory) enhanced with a viscous drag model to estimate the canopy aerodynamics within the simulation loop and specifically tailored for paraglider-harness/pilot systems. The method naturally accounts for the actual canopy geometry, longitudinal/lateral coupling, and aerodynamic damping effects. Additional improvements include a nonlinear aerodynamic model for the harness/pilot body and a method for estimating line drag based on real setlines data from high-performance paragliders. The model’s capabilities are demonstrated through numerical simulations, which show good agreement with available experimental data. The proposed simulation framework offers a more accurate approach for analysing high-performance paragliders, supporting design optimisation and sensitivity studies prior to prototyping. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yuan, J.; Song, Q.; Sun, J.
Author full names: Yuan, Jia (57612231900); Song, Chengcheng (54883147700); Sun, Jianhong (55716288400)
Title: Safety assessment and operational boundary modeling for rescue aircraft in forest fire environments via spatiotemporal graph learning
Abstract: Forest fire rescue operations require aircraft to perform low-altitude flights in extreme environments with high temperature, strong convection, and poor visibility. This creates an urgent need for reliable flight safety assessment and operational boundary prediction methods to ensure mission safety and stability. This paper proposes a spatiotemporal graph learning-based framework for aircraft safety assessment and operational boundary modeling. The proposed framework comprehensively accounts for multidimensional environmental factors, including flame heat flux, smoke dispersion, and meteorological disturbances. It maps the fireground threat information into a spatiotemporal graph structure and incorporates aircraft dynamics and safety boundary models to achieve deep coupling between environmental evolution and flight dynamics. Furthermore, a spatiotemporal risk prediction mechanism combining graph neural networks and uncertainty modeling is developed to realize dynamic threat evaluation and adaptive safety boundary adjustment. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high prediction accuracy and boundary stability in complex fireground conditions, reducing prediction error by approximately 29 %, increasing regional overlap by >10 %, and lowering boundary deviation by about 22 % compared with baseline methods. This study provides a novel methodology for aircraft safety assessment and operational boundary prediction in complex and dynamic environments. It establishes both theoretical foundations and engineering implementation pathways for risk monitoring, adaptive safety control, and intelligent decision-making in low-altitude flight missions, showing strong potential for broad practical applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, T.; Ding, K.; Wang, Y.; Wang, Z.
Author full names: Zhang, Tao (59351198600); Ding, Kunying (24464793700); Wang, Yuanhang (59481461800); Wang, Zhiping (8220543800)
Title: Flow and heat transfer characteristics of turbine blades with thermal barrier coatings under multi-physics coupling and identification of failure-sensitive areas
Abstract: To accurately characterize the multiphysics behavior of turbine blades integrating thermal barrier coatings (TBCs) and cooling structures under actual operating conditions, this study systematically analyzes the blade's flow characteristics, heat transfer behavior, macro-damage, and micro-stress response laws using a fluid-thermal-solid coupled numerical analysis method based on real flight data from an aircraft engine. By comparing the results with in-service blade damage data, the failure-prone regions were precisely identified. The results show that the aerodynamic flow characteristics of the turbine blade are the primary factor determining the temperature distribution. The flow stagnation effect at the leading edge is the primary cause of the high-temperature zone in this region, resulting in peak temperatures reaching 1278 K. The reverse pressure gradient at the trailing edge of the suction surface induces localized thermal load concentration by reducing film cooling efficiency. Concurrently, tip leakage vortices cause cooling failure at the suction-side blade tip by entrainment of the cooling airflow. TBCs play a crucial thermal protection role by reducing the substrate's average temperature by 90–170 K. However, coating failure occurs in the leading edge and suction-side tip regions due to extreme thermal loads and insufficient cold gas coverage. Furthermore, significant stress concentration zones were identified at the tip, around the film holes, and at the trailing edge root, which are highly consistent with the measured crack locations in the substrate. Meanwhile, the high-temperature zone at the leading edge tip corresponds to coating spalling and ablation failure. This study provides an important theoretical and engineering basis for turbine blade life prediction and reliability design. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, C.; Li, H.; Chen, Y.; Cao, H.; Zhang, Y.; Zhao, X.
Author full names: Wang, Chuang (57272641100); Li, Hongkun (27169008900); Chen, Yugang (36668293200); Cao, Hongwei (57221978698); Zhang, Yuhang (58407668000); Zhao, Xinwei (57202984612)
Title: A novel blade tip timing data generation method based on the enhanced reduced-order model
Abstract: Blade Tip Timing (BTT) is the most effective technique for monitoring blade vibration. Efficient generation of accurate BTT data is crucial for evaluating the performance of newly developed BTT algorithms. To this end, an enhanced reduced-order model (EROM) under variable-speed conditions is derived, which integrates the stiffness mistuning, damping mistuning, and rotation effect. Based on this, a new BTT simulator is developed for rotating bladed disks, enabling efficient generation of more reliable BTT simulation data. The novelty of this simulator lies in its ability to incorporate the rotation effect, stiffness mistuning, damping mistuning, and speed fluctuation into the BTT data. In addition, additional masses are used to detune all blades except the testing one, and impact testing is carried out blade by blade to collect the free decay responses. Stiffness and damping mistuning are identified from the free decay responses, and the identified mistuning parameters are then used to update the EROM and construct the BTT simulator. Furthermore, the effect of various uncertainties on blade tip vibration and BTT data is analyzed in detail. Finally, the classical single-parameter algorithm is used to identify vibration parameters from the simulated BTT data to validate the BTT simulator, and the performance of the algorithm is further evaluated. This work provides a more realistic and effective method for generating BTT data, which can be used to verify the performance of the BTT algorithm in damping identification. © © 2025. Authors: Chang, Y.; Niu, X.; Li, Y.; Jinyu; Yao, S.; Sun, Z.; Song, Y.
Title: A dual-characteristics program spectrum compilation method and applied to aero-engine fan disk
Abstract: To address the challenge of replicating actual flight profiles of aero-engines in laboratory settings, this study proposes a novel approach to compiling a program spectrum that fully considers the non-stationary random or operation-related characteristics of aero-engine loads and maintains consistency in both structural and fatigue characteristics. By incorporating clustering concepts, the Clustering by Fast Search and Find of Density Peaks (CFSFDP) algorithm is employed to identify Typical Characteristic Cycles (TCC), which are then used to define the load levels in the program spectrum. The similarity of the cumulative frequency curve serves as the criterion for determining the size of the load-blocks by minimizing the Relative Integrated Absolute Error (RIAE). For the first time, the concept of "hierarchical fatigue load" is introduced, leading to the development of a load-block sequencing strategy that compensates for the limitations of purely statistical fatigue methods in load sequence influence. The proposed method is applied to 3-generation X-type aeroengine disk random spectrum and validated through load spectrum tests. The approach not only enriches the classification of program spectrum but also provides a useful reference for other forms of program spectrum. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tang, X.; Xiong, B.; Liang, J.; Fan, X.
Author full names: Tang, Xiao (57218454053); Xiong, Bing (57189594616); Liang, Jianhan (59964526000); Fan, Xiaoqiang (7403393750)
Title: Self-starting improvement of hypersonic inlets using lip-side bleed in multiple solution phenomena
Abstract: Under complex shock wave/boundary layer interactions and three-dimensional effects, hypersonic inlets exhibit multiple solution phenomena, manifested as diverse unstart flow modes that significantly complicate self-starting characteristics. Among these, the unstart modes of large separation on the body (LSB) and dual-separation on both the body and lip (DSBL) are identified as critical factors affecting the self-starting performance. Building upon the mechanistic understanding of these two unstart modes, this study proposes lip-side bleed as an effective approach to improve inlet self-starting performance, with bleed schemes designed for critical flow regions. The studied model is a two-dimensional mixed-compression inlet with sideboards (three-dimensional effects are present at the sideboards), and the design condition is set at Ma <inf>0</inf> = 5/ α = 6° ( Ma <inf>0</inf>: inflow Mach number, α : the angle of attack). Systematic wind tunnel experiments were conducted to comprehensively evaluate the efficacy and underlying mechanisms of lip-side bleed. Results demonstrate that the lip-side bleed method not only effectively suppresses both the initiation and persistence of DSBL unstart modes, but also significantly optimizes the flowfield structure of LSB unstart modes to promote separation zone elimination, consequently improving the inlet self-starting performance. By comparing the experimental results before and after lip-side bleed, it is revealed that the bleed configuration achieves either a 1.0-Mach reduction in the inlet’s self-starting Mach number or a 10° increase in its self-starting attack angle. This study significantly advances the theoretical understanding of hypersonic inlet flow processes while providing effective guidance for engineering practice. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Qin, F.; Ge, B.; Yi, Z.; Xu, D.; Yuan, M.; Bing, B.; Xi, M.; Zhu, S.
Title: Mixing and heat-release modulation of liquid kerosene by high-enthalpy jets in RBCC at Mach 3
Abstract: At low Mach numbers, the low total temperature of airflow reduces the evaporation of liquid kerosene and limits chemical reactions. Thus, reliable ignition and stable combustion are challenging for RBCC. High-enthalpy jets are often used as pilot flames to assist ignition and stabilize flames. In this paper, ground direct-connect experiment and numerical methods were used to investigate the influence mechanism of high-enthalpy jets on the mixing and heat release characteristics of liquid kerosene in RBCC at Mach 3. The results show that the injection equivalence ratio ( ER ), rocket flow rate ( Q ), and rocket oxygen-to-fuel ratio ( O/F ) significantly affect the mixing and heat release processes. The development of the fuel-air mixing layer exhibits typical nonlinear characteristics. The growth process can be categorized into four distinct stages. The enhancement of the mixing by high-enthalpy jets is primarily attributable to the synergy of baroclinic vorticity and stretching vorticity, with dilatational vorticity exhibiting a non-essential role. The findings indicate that the three factors exert distinct effects on the flame stability zone, and the combustor exhibits a state of multifaceted combustion modes coexistence. However, a notable disparity exists in the contribution of these combustion modes to heat release. Lean premixed combustion emerges as the predominant mode, accounting for over 50 %, while diffusion combustion contributes the least, not exceeding 15 %. Additionally, the heat release proportion of rich premixed combustion is moderately higher than that of diffusion combustion. The coupling effect analysis of ER - Q, ER - O/F , and Q - O/F clarified the parameter-performance relationship. Within the range ( ER =0.3–0.4, Q = 0.2–0.25 kg/s, O/F = 1.4–1.6), both high combustion efficiency and mixing efficiency can be maintained simultaneously. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhu, W.; Liu, Z.; Zhang, Q.; Pang, Z.; Zhu, Z.H.
Author full names: Zhu, Weiliang (57221221326); Liu, Zhengze (60230853300); Zhang, Qi (58478964900); Pang, Zhaojun (55303110100); Zhu, Zhenghong (59897448400)
Title: Development of pseudo-celestial navigation and tracking system for planar air-bearing satellite simulators
Abstract: This paper presents the development of a planar air-bearing microgravity testbed integrated with a pseudo-celestial navigation and tracking system onboard the satellite simulators. The developed navigation system utilizes a low-cost onboard camera as star tracker and a pre-calibrated pseudo-celestial field positioned above the testbed. This approach eliminates the need for expensive, external, and centralized observation and tracking systems. By processing star constellation images onboard with a Kalman filter, each satellite simulator can accurately and stably estimate its pose and velocity in real time. To address challenges associated with discrete gas thrust with on/off binary output and the low update rate of pseudo-celestial navigation, the tracking system integrates an anti-saturation control algorithm with a composite closed-loop control strategy to enhance pose control robustness and improve system responsiveness. The accuracy and stability of the navigation system are validated experimentally with static pose errors of 0.05 mm and 0.005° and dynamic pose errors of 1.2 mm and 0.5°. The efficacy of the control algorithm is also validated by trajectory tracking experiments on the air-bearing table. Furthermore, a cooperative formation flying experiment with two simulators demonstrates that the pseudo-celestial navigation system can be easily scaled for multi-simulator formations. Compared to existing solutions that rely on external and centralized observation systems, this approach proves to be more efficient and effective for synchronized multi-simulator formation flight experiments. © 2025 The Authors.

Authors: Ke, Y.; Liu, C.; He, J.; Zhang, F.
Title: Self-organizing fencing formation control of quadrotor swarms under actuator saturation
Abstract: Cooperative fencing control has attracted increasing attention due to its potential applications in area denial and surveillance missions. However, most existing studies rarely address cooperative fencing under practical constraints such as actuator saturation and limited sensing. This article investigates the self-organizing fencing formation control problem of the quadrotor swarm subject to actuator saturation. We propose a fully distributed hierarchical control scheme composed of three complementary components that collectively enable the swarm to achieve self-organizing coordination and fence a maneuver target. The target maneuver can be arbitrarily time-varying and unknown, although it is reasonably assumed to be bounded. Each quadrotor estimates the target’s motion primarily from its own local sensing. In the absence of complete direct measurements, the estimate is supplemented with information shared by neighboring quadrotors. In order to overcome the actuator saturation problem, a proxy-based position controller is developed to guide these quadrotors to form a fencing formation through only local interactions. Concurrently, a finite-time attitude controller provides fast and accurate tracking of the desired thrust and orientation for the position regulation. The proposed strategy enables the quadrotors to fence the target in a self-organizing way. The effectiveness of our design is demonstrated through rigorous Lyapunov analysis and numerical simulations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Fu, W.; Zuo, R.; Yu, J.; Sun, P.
Title: A multidimensional non-axisymmetric stator coupling regulation strategy for boundary layer ingestion fans
Abstract: Boundary layer ingestion (BLI) propulsion represents a potential solution for improving aircraft efficiency and reducing fuel consumption in next-generation designs. However, the intake of non-uniform boundary layer flow introduces severe inlet distortion at a fan face, leading to flow separation, performance degradation, and even premature stall. This poses significant safety risks. To address this issue, in this study, a BLI inlet coupled with a single-stage fan was investigated, and a non-axisymmetric stator design strategy was proposed. First, a non-axisymmetric pitch arrangement was introduced, which suppressed low-energy fluid accumulation near the hub and reduced the stator total pressure loss by 7.5 %. Building on this, non-axisymmetric three-dimensional modifications were applied, for which stacking redesign alleviated the hub separation and inlet angle adjustment mitigated the tip separation, further reducing the total pressure loss by 3.15 %. Unsteady simulations across multiple operating conditions showed that the non-axisymmetric stator enhanced the fan performance, with a 0.57 % increase in efficiency and a slight rise in the pressure ratio at the design point. At near-stall conditions, the efficiency improved by 2.9 %, the pressure ratio increased by 0.58 %, and the stability margin was extended by approximately 45 %. Additional investigations for varying inlet boundary layer thicknesses demonstrated that the beneficial effects of the non-axisymmetric stator became more pronounced as the distortion severity increased. These findings confirmed the effectiveness of non-axisymmetric stator design in enhancing the aerodynamic performance and stability of BLI fans, providing valuable insights for the development of future green aviation propulsion systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Long, Q.; Qi, M.; Wei, J.
Title: Investigation of aerodynamic excitation and forced vibration induced by rotor-stator interaction on the radial turbine with double-row staggered tubular nozzles in aircraft environment control system
Abstract: Under complex flight conditions, the aircraft environmental control system (ECS) plays an important role in ensuring the comfortable cabin environment and reliability of the electronic system. Radial turbines in ECS are susceptible to high-cycle fatigue (HCF) failures caused by rotor-stator interaction (RSI). Given the HCF failures frequently identified in radial turbines with double-row staggered tubular nozzles, this study investigated the behavior and formation mechanism of aerodynamic excitation by the nonlinear harmonic (NLH) method. The forced vibration of the turbine wheel was investigated using the fluid–structure interaction (FSI) approach, which was validated by the vibration experiment. The influence of the inlet and outlet pressure on the aerodynamic excitation was discussed. The results demonstrate that the staggered double-row tubular nozzles induce a partial conversion of first-order aerodynamic excitation into second-order aerodynamic excitation. Consequently, the overall second-order aerodynamic excitation is comparable to the first-order aerodynamic excitation, with the maximum difference being only 13 % in the paper. The two full resonance points with high stress are both induced by second-order aerodynamic excitation, which causes the HCF failure at the leading edge of the splitter blade. A substantial risk of damage to the leading edge of the main blade is also observed. Furthermore, variations in inlet and outlet pressures induce proportional changes in aerodynamic excitation, which accounts for the non-monotonic relationship between excitation amplitude and expansion ratio in ECS radial turbines. This finding provides practical guidance for ground-based HCF testing. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, X.; Liu, Z.; Li, H.; Ren, J.; Ge, X.; Gao, H.; Li, K.; Wu, K.
Title: Three-dimensional oscillation characteristics in the bypass shock-induced thrust vector control
Abstract: Among the increasingly developing multitude of fluidic thrust vector control technologies, bypass shock-induced thrust vector control technology has emerged as a promising aerodynamic solution for advanced propulsion vector systems. A bypass is utilized to transport the upstream gas to inject the supersonic section of the converging-diverging nozzle as a secondary flow, ultimately causing the vector deflection of the primary flow. The resulting bypass shock-induced thrust vector control system induces considerable thrust vectoring performance while maintaining reduced structural complexity. However, there is a phenomenon of self-excited oscillation that affects the control accuracy of the vectoring jet. So far, it has been seldom studied, and its mechanism is unclear. Therefore, based on the large eddy simulation method, the present work designed an arc-shaped bypass to study the jet self-excited oscillation phenomenon of a bypass shock-induced thrust vector nozzle in detail. The influence of different values of the nozzle pressure ratio on the self-excited oscillation characteristics of vector jets was illustrated. The results indicate that all thrust vector performance parameters exhibit oscillation phenomena. As the nozzle pressure ratio increases, the self-excited oscillation phenomenon weakens, and the bottom wall separation shock reflection point moves downstream. Self-excited oscillation in the spanwise direction is observed in the three-dimensional flow field, where the non-uniformity of the pressure distribution in the spanwise direction occurs as the nozzle pressure ratio increases. The disturbance source of the self-excited oscillations comes from the secondary induced vortex. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhou, Y.; Xu, Y.; Bian, K.; Zhou, J.; Geng, L.; Han, H.; Zhang, J.
Title: Fractal intelligence self-organized mission planning for multi-UAV cooperative search-attack in complex boundaries
Abstract: To address the adaptability challenges of multi-Unmanned Aerial Vehicle(UAV) systems in executing search-attack mission across complex boundary mission areas and the instability caused by grid size variations in grid-based partitioning methods. This paper proposes a fractal intelligence self-organized mission planning (FISOMP) algorithm for multi-UAV cooperative search-attack in complex boundaries. The algorithm integrates centralized mission allocation, coverage path generation, and distributed attack coordination. Leveraging fractal decomposition, it adapts to complex boundary mission areas of arbitrary sizes and introduces an optimal fractal granularity function to calculate fractal granularity that satisfies UAVs’ sector-shaped detection areas and continuous waypoint flight requirements, eliminating manual grid size configuration. We propose a distributed attack coordination objective function. It is designed to maximize target destruction under limited UAV attack payloads. Additionally, based on the fractal mechanism, we studied the joining and withdrawal mechanisms of mult-UAV. Numerical experiments validate the efficiency and stability of the proposed method, while simulation tests demonstrate robust mission execution across four distinct operational scenarios. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Rajapaksha, T.; Khodadadian, A.; Bab-Hadiashar, A.; Ishtiaq, N.; Hoseinnezhad, R.
Author full names: Rajapaksha, Tharani Thathsara (59539926800); Khodadadian, Amirali (60230492700); Bab-Hadiashar, Alireza (6603333330); Ishtiaq, Nida (57188859955); Hoseinnezhad, Reza (6602707666)
Title: Swarm-enhanced labeled multi-bernoulli filter for dynamic swarm tracking
Abstract: Tracking individual agents within dense and dynamically maneuvering Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) swarms poses a significant challenge, particularly when agents operate in close proximity and measurements are often missed and prone to high false-alarm rates. Traditional multi-target filters frequently struggle under these conditions due to ambiguous data association leading to reduced tracking accuracy. To address this, we propose a swarm information incorporated tracking framework that jointly leverages swarm level dynamic and formation related information to enhance tracking accuracy of individual agent within the swarm. The key idea is to estimate swarm-level motion and formation parameters using the Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) swarm tracking approach by modeling the swarm as a single entity and integrate this information into the Labeled Multi-Bernoulli (LMB) filter to improve individual trajectory estimation of UAVs. This joint formulation improves measurement association, reduces identity ambiguity among closely spaced UAVs, and increases robustness to clutter and missed detection. The performance of the proposed method is evaluated on common swarm formations, such as the elliptical shape, V-shape, and swarm with multiple-varying formation patterns under conditions of imperfect detections and high clutter. Tracking performance is quantified using the Optimal Sub-Pattern Assignment distance for a set of tracks (OSPA(2)) metric, demonstrating up to an 28.1 % reduction in tracking error and an 33.8 % improvement in cardinality estimation compared to the standard LMB filter. These results highlight the effectiveness of integrating the swarm information to strengthen individual UAV tracking within the swarm, providing a robust framework for real-time monitoring. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Wang, J.; Bennett, G.J.; Humbert, T.; Aurégan, Y.; Åbom, M.; Bodèn, H.
Author full names: Wang, Jiayu (57215508110); Bennett, Gareth J. (24474353600); Humbert, Thomas (55752830900); Aurégan, Yves (6602092789); Åbom, Mats (6603965344); Bodèn, Hans (7003812989)
Title: Broadband sound transmission loss of multi-chamber micro-perforated panel absorbers in grazing flow
Abstract: A multi-chamber micro-perforated panel absorber is designed and optimized for broadband attenuation in grazing flow acoustic incidence conditions. Mach number dependent analytical equations for impedance are input into a graph-theory-based two-point impedance method (TpIM) so that the total impedance of these highly complex multi-degree of freedom absorbers can be modeled and calculated. A novel Cremer impedance-based cost-function is presented which attempts to optimize the impedance of the absorber within a broadband target frequency range of interest. The optimization routine converges on an optimal impedance and also provides the geometric parameters of the absorber, such as individual sub-chamber depths, hole diameters and porosities per uniquely perforated chamber top, or internal side-wall. This allows the absorber to be 3D printed and numerically modeled using COMSOL. Liner samples 200 mm in length were 3D printed and tested in a grazing flow facility as a function of Mach number - up to M=0.25, sound pressure level - up to 140 dB, and where the acoustic source can be located upstream or downstream of the liner. Experimental results compare extremely well with those predicted validating the analytical design and optimization methodology. TL levels of 25 dB were achieved at 140 dB at M=0.25. © © 2025. Authors: Bai, X.; Yuan, J.; Xu, M.
Title: Density evolution control in large-scale spacecraft swarms via physics-informed deep learning
Abstract: This paper focuses on the autonomous migration and intelligent regulation of large-scale spacecraft swarm density. A swarm evolution dynamics model is proposed based on the mapping between state variables and control inputs in the Gauss variational equations, which intuitively reveals the global diffusion and migration behavior of the swarm under control actions. In line with the characteristics of density evolution, tangential and normal control strategies are designed to modulate the velocity distribution within the swarm and drive its evolution toward a desired density profile. To address the computational challenges associated with density-based regulation methods, a physics-informed deep learning framework is developed for intelligent density control. In this framework, control parameters are treated as optimization variables, and a composite loss function is constructed by incorporating constraints from the target distribution, initial conditions, boundary conditions, and governing physical equations. This data-physics hybrid approach enables the efficient inversion and optimization of control parameters with minimal data requirements. Finally, numerical simulations under both tangential and normal control scenarios are conducted to validate the effectiveness and computational advantages of the proposed approach in solving swarm density regulation problems. © © 2025. Authors: Li, F.; Liu, H.; Duan, F.; Guo, G.; Teng, G.; Zhou, X.; Zhou, Q.; Fu, X.
Title: Capacitive sensing-based method for blade tip clearance and timing measurement through symmetric inverse function fitting in aero-engines
Abstract: Blade Tip Clearance (BTC) and Blade Tip Timing (BTT) are critical parameters for condition monitoring of aero-engines. Existing capacitive methods for simultaneous measurement often suffer from low peak-timing accuracy and high computational complexity, primarily due to the strongly coupled and nonlinear nature of the multi-parameter estimation problem. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an efficient method based on Symmetric Inverse Function Fitting (SIFF). The method decomposes the complex nonlinear multi-parameter fitting problem into three stages: adaptive signal segmentation, symmetric inverse function fitting, and least-squares linear fitting, enabling rapid and high-precision decoupled estimation of the peak time and signal amplitude. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that, compared to the traditional nonlinear Gaussian fitting method, the proposed SIFF method improves the time of arrival (TOA) extraction accuracy to a best case of 0.292 μs, limits the BTC measurement error to within 8.2 μm, and significantly reduces the single signal processing time from 29.5 ms to 1.8 ms. This study validates the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method across a wide range of rotation speeds and tip clearances, offering a promising solution for real-time monitoring of both BTC and TOA using a single capacitive sensor. © © 2025. Authors: Luo, Q.; Xue, W.; Luo, L.; Du, W.; Yan, H.
Title: Two loss decomposition methods for high-load turbine cascades
Abstract: This study addresses the flow loss decomposition problem in high-load turbine cascades and proposes two loss decomposition methods based on different principles. The influence of cascade geometry parameters on the loss distribution is analyzed. Method I is based on flow structure characteristics and decomposes friction, shock wave, wake, and mainstream loss through flow field discretization and shock wave identification technology. Method II is based on flow partitioning, dividing the cascade passage into different loss-dominated regions and calculating the loss in each region through the mass-averaged parameters. An orthogonal experimental design is adopted, with the trailing edge radius, outlet Mach number, and trailing edge wedge angle as variables. The results of loss decomposition by the two methods are compared. The results show that the total loss trends calculated by the two methods are consistent, but the total loss calculated by Method II is generally lower than that of Method I. The main difference comes from the friction loss. The range analysis shows that the trailing edge radius has the greatest influence on the total loss, followed by the outlet Mach number, and the trailing edge wedge angle has the least influence. In addition, Method I is more suitable for refined analysis of shock wave position sensitivity, while Method II has higher calculation efficiency, but the accuracy of extracting boundary layer parameters needs to be optimized. This study provides theoretical support for the loss mechanism analysis and optimal design of high-load turbine cascades. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jin, Y.; Li, H.; Lu, S.; Jiang, H.; Teng, J.
Title: Tip leakage flow behavior and loss characteristics of turbine blade squealer tip with inclined shelf and partition rib
Abstract: This investigation examines the influence of inclined shelf and partition rib on tip leakage flow (TLF) pattern, comparing these designs with a conventional squealer tip. The underlying mechanisms driving variations flow behavior and loss characteristics are analyzed by examining the evolution of squealer cavity vortices and the interactions between squealer cavity vortices and tip leakage vortices, combined with the entropy generation model. For the squealer tip featuring an inclined shelf, both the TLF driving force and TLF jet resistance are reduced within the shelf-influenced region. The combined effect of the two reduce both the TLF rate and the TLF angle, thereby mitigating the mixing loss. The combined use of an inclined shelf and a partition rib result in effects on TLF rate and mixing loss that exhibit variations along the streamwise direction. In the region influenced mainly by the partition ribs, the reduction in mixing losses is primarily due to two factors: increased jet resistance, which lowers the TLF rate, and a notable decrease in the TLF angle. Within the region affected by the combined action of inclined shelf and partition rib, the reduction in driving force and the increase in jet resistance both help to decrease the TLF rate. A lower mixing Mach number in this region additionally contributes to a suppression of mixing losses. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, S.; Chao, T.; Wang, S.; Yang, M.
Title: Morphing-aerodynamic collaborative control algorithm for hypersonic vehicles
Abstract: Hypersonic morphing vehicles enhance aerodynamic performance through dynamic external configuration adjustments. By integrating morphing commands as dynamic control inputs rather than predefined ones, the vehicle achieves improved adaptability to varying flight conditions. However, this introduces significant coupling between morphing and attitude control loops. This paper proposes the Morphing-Aerodynamic Collaborative Control Algorithm (MACC), which incorporates morphing parameters into the control input vector, enabling autonomous adaptation to environmental changes.The MACC framework leverages dynamics and aerodynamic models for hypersonic vehicles with variable wingspan and sweep angle. The control layer uses a nonsingular fast terminal sliding mode controller to generate robust virtual commands. A third-order tracking differentiator with fixed-time convergence ensures precise guidance command tracking, offering rapid responses and maintaining fixed-time convergence within predefined bounds. Additionally, a fixed-time convergent extended state observer (ESO) estimates system disturbances, providing real-time compensation for unmodeled dynamics and external perturbations. The observer parameters are analytically derived to simplify tuning and reduce implementation complexity.Control allocation is optimized using a modified sequential quadratic programming algorithm. Numerical simulations validate MACC’s effectiveness in attitude control, and Monte Carlo analysis confirms its robustness under severe aerodynamic disturbances. The fixed-time convergence properties of the tracking differentiator and ESO improve transient performance, ensuring rapid disturbance attenuation even with abrupt configuration changes. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: He, L.; CHEN, F.; Qin, Y.
Title: A novel data-driven method for predicting heat flux of hypersonic aircraft based on Fourier neural operator
Abstract: Aerodynamic heating occurs during high-speed cruising and atmospheric reentry of hypersonic vehicles, which brings significant challenges to space transport missions. One of the essentials for efficient design of thermal protection systems is the prediction of aerothermal loads on hypersonic vehicles. This paper introduces a data-driven and physics-constrained method based on Fourier neural operator with localized kernels (L-FNO) for quickly and precisely predicting heat flux on surfaces under different conditions. An architecture based on pre-training and fine-tuning is designed to reduce the reliance on a large number of samples. During the pre-training, L-FNO is trained with a small dataset comprising samples of various types to acquire the capability for heat flux prediction. The physics-constrained term inspired by laminar heat flux theory is incorporated into the loss function of fine-tuning. We fine-tune the pre-trained model for accurate heat flux predictions on new shapes. The results of comparative experiments show that the mean relative error of the proposed model in predicting heat flux is 1.47 % in the pre-training stage. The heat flux prediction at stagnation point exhibits remarkable accuracy. Furthermore, fine-tuning can enhance the model’s geometric generalization capability. With a tiny dataset comprising only 10 new samples, the heat flux prediction error for the new shape is decreased from above 10 % to 2.16 % through the fine-tuning method, while the number of required iterations for fine-tuning is 1 % of the number of iterations for pre-training. Compared to numerical simulations, the proposed method achieves a speedup of several thousand times, demonstrating its high efficiency. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, G.; Liu, X.
Title: Switching anti-windup control of gas turbine engines with average dwell-time
Abstract: Actuator saturation, resulting from physical and mechanical constraints, can degrade performance and even destabilize gas turbine engines. To address these challenges, anti-windup compensators are commonly incorporated into the control loop. Conventional approaches typically activate compensators immediately upon actuator saturation, whereas alternative activation mechanisms—such as delayed-activation or anticipatory-activation—offer distinct advantages. However, no single activation mechanism consistently delivers optimal performance. This paper proposes a switching anti-windup control method with Average Dwell-Time (ADT) rule to exploit the complementary strengths of immediate-activation, delayed-activation, and anticipatory-activation anti-windup compensators. The effects of virtual saturation thresholds and related parameters on compensation performance are first analyzed, and anti-windup compensators for each activation mechanism are designed via Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI)-based design methods with parameter tuning using the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO). A switching logic based on the optimal L <inf>2</inf>-norm of the controlled performance output is then developed for efficient online selection among compensators. Stability and guaranteed L <inf>2</inf>-gain performance of the overall closed-loop system are established using multiple Lyapunov functions in combination with ADT analysis. To balance the trade-offs between tracking performance and overshoot, a Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO) index function is formulated and solved via the Multi-Objective Grey Wolf Optimizer (MOGWO), yielding a Pareto front of candidate ADT sets. Hardware-in-loop (HIL) experiments confirm that the proposed method achieves the shortest settling time and the smallest overshoot compared to benchmark methods, thereby demonstrating its effectiveness and superiority. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Karthikeyan, K.V.; Harish, R.
Title: CFD-ML analysis of hybrid flow control on NACA4412 airfoil using triangular gurney flap and dual ns-DBD actuators
Abstract: Improving airfoil performance through advanced flow control is essential for enhancing aerodynamic efficiency in automotive and aerospace applications. This study explores a hybrid flow control strategy on a NACA4412 airfoil by integrating a passive triangular gurney flap (TGF) with active dual nanosecond dielectric barrier discharge (ns-DBD) plasma actuators. The aim is to evaluate aerodynamic improvements through both geometric and plasma based modifications and to develop predictive machine learning models for aerodynamic estimation. The investigations were carried out at a Reynolds number of 3.1 × 106, considering an angle of attack (AOA) interval between 0∘ to 20∘. Three TGF configurations equal, left and right triangles were tested individually and in combination with ns-DBD actuators. Key aerodynamic parameters including lift coefficient ( C<inf>L</inf> ), drag coefficient ( C<inf>D</inf> ), lift-to-drag ratio ( C<inf>L</inf> / C<inf>D</inf> ) and vorticity magnitude were analyzed. While TGF alone improved lift, they also increased drag due to flow recirculation. The addition of ns-DBD actuators mitigated these effects by delaying flow separation, suppressing vortex shedding and enhancing boundary layer stability. The left triangle configuration yielded the highest lift improvement of 135.56 %, while the equal triangle setup with ns-DBD showed a 61.96 % increase in C<inf>L</inf> / C<inf>D</inf> . To enable rapid aerodynamic predictions, artificial neural networks trained with Levenberg-Marquardt and Scaled Conjugate Gradient algorithms were employed, showing strong accuracy with high correlation and low mean squared error. Overall, the hybrid method significantly enhances airfoil performance with reliable machine learning based prediction. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Simhony, Y.; Segal, A.; Amrani, O.; Etzion, E.
Author full names: Simhony, Yoav (58127875500); Segal, Alex (58246889600); Amrani, Ofer (6602126331); Etzion, Erez (7007013278)
Title: High-end space electronics: Active shielding to mitigate catastrophic single-event effects
Abstract: Operating electronic systems in space environments presents significant challenges due to continuous exposure to cosmic, solar, and trapped radiation, which can induce catastrophic single-event effects. This paper introduces a novel nonintrusive mitigation apparatus designed to protect high-end commercial off-the-shelf electronics in space. The apparatus incorporates an array of real-time particle detectors coupled with a mitigation algorithm. Upon identifying potentially harmful particles, the system power cycles affected electronics, preempting permanent damage. The apparatus was evaluated using GEANT4 simulations, which were compared with empirical data from the “COTS-Capsule” experiment aboard the International Space Station, demonstrating strong agreement. Key results indicate that the system achieves a 95 % detection accuracy with a power cycle rate of once every seven hours per square centimeter of sensitive electronics. The COTS-Capsule represents a cost-effective, flexible solution for integrating modern, high-end, non-space-qualified electronics into a variety of space missions, addressing critical challenges in the new-space era. © 2025 The Author(s).

Author full names: Zhao, Wandong (57203354806); Wei, Zhehao (60004776400); Luo, Dejiao (60005245300); Deiterding, Ralf (60283623100); Yang, Xiong (56442817500); Huang, Minchao (7404260462); Li, Xiaokang (23005423300)
Title: Influence mechanism of ignition energy on flame acceleration and detonation transition in supersonic flow
Abstract: Deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT) represents a critical process for achieving efficient operation in detonation-based aerospace propulsion, particularly under challenging supersonic flow conditions. This study employed high-fidelity numerical simulations to investigate the influence of varying ignition energy on flame acceleration and DDT dynamics within supersonic combustible mixtures. Utilizing adaptive mesh refinement coupled with the reactive Navier-Stokes equations and an 11-species, 27-step detailed hydrogen-air chemical kinetic mechanism, simulations were conducted for three distinct ignition energy levels in a slightly supersonic flow. Key findings demonstrate that: (i) a curved leading shock wave (LSW) evolves into a Mach stem structure, and its subsequent reflection from solid boundaries initiates reactive hotspots that rapidly develop into detonation waves; (ii) counterintuitively, elevated ignition energy, despite inducing stronger initial flame acceleration, prolongs both the run-up time to detonation (TDDT) and run-up distance to detonation (LDDT); (iii) this delay is likely attributed to ignition-induced blockage: high energy causes excessive early flame expansion and generates intense shocks that impede coherent LSW formation and amplification; (iv) conversely, moderate ignition energy facilitates rapid LSW formation, significantly accelerating detonation onset. Quantitatively, the moderate energy case achieved reductions of 21 % in TDDT and 31.25 % in LDDT compared to the high-energy scenario. This study elucidates the critical, non-monotonic role of ignition energy in supersonic DDT, providing essential insights for optimizing ignition strategies in air-breathing propulsion systems. © © 2025. Authors: Sun, H.; Li, P.; Liu, Q.; Zhang, D.; Ji, B.; Yang, L.; Fu, Q.
Title: Efficient atomization of gel propellants using an impinging-jet injector with self-excited oscillation chambers
Abstract: Efficient atomization of gel propellants is crucial for enhancing the performance of propulsion systems in missiles and spacecraft. Given the limitations of conventional injectors for gel atomization, novel injector designs and comprehensive experimental measurements are urgently required. In this study, the atomization characteristics of four gel propellant simulants with different gelling agent concentrations were experimentally investigated using a novel impinging-jet injector equipped with self-excited oscillation (SEO) chambers. To evaluate atomization performance, the spray pattern, droplet size distribution, and injection pressure drop were measured under various conditions. Furthermore, the proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) and power spectral density (PSD) methods were employed to analyze the role of self-excited oscillations in improving atomization. Results show that the SEO injector introduces dominant high-frequency perturbations that amplify surface instabilities and accelerate the breakup of the impinging sheet. Compared with conventional injectors, the SEO injector achieves a fully developed spray at lower flow rates and produces smaller, more uniform droplets, with the Sauter mean diameter (SMD) reduced to less than half that of conventional designs. In addition, the atomization quality of the SEO injector shows low sensitivity to gel viscoelasticity, demonstrating better adaptability across propellant formulations. However, achieving enhanced atomization requires a higher pressure drop due to the conversion of pressure potential energy into surface energy. This study reveals the underlying mechanism of SEO-enhanced atomization and confirms the superior performance of the SEO injector, providing a valuable reference for improving the efficiency and applicability of gel propellant injection systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Author full names: Zeng, Xinxi (57804533200); Yuan, Ronggang (58303615200); Yang, Lifeng (59346149200); Huang, Xuxing (57201373216); Li, Shuang (56288781000)
Title: Long-term multi-region observation scheduling for large-scale constellations via two-stage hybrid planning
Abstract: Recent rapid development of large-scale constellations for regional observation has been accompanied by an exponential growth in the mission planning search space and a sharp increase in computational complexity. In this study, the long-term multi-region observation scheduling problem for large-scale constellations is investigated. Under multiple constraints, the problem is formulated as a nonlinear integer programming model with the objectives of maximizing regional multi-coverage and minimizing coverage imbalance. To address the resulting large-scale combinatorial optimization, a two-stage hybrid planning approach is constructed. First, a lexicographic maximum-minimum allocation–based multi-sampling initialization (LAMSI) method is proposed to generate high-quality initial solutions. Then, a thinking-innovation-strategy genetic algorithm (TISGA) is developed to enhance global search capability and mitigate premature convergence. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed LAMSI method significantly enhances optimization solution quality, with GA and PSO experiencing performance increases of 40.59% and 30.86%, respectively. Furthermore, the two-stage hybrid planning approach is shown to outperform several state-of-the-art methods in large-scale cooperative scheduling by exhibiting superior search capability and yielding higher fitness. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Title: Influence of hydrogen fuel on the thermodynamic cycle and propulsive performance of aeroengines
Abstract: Hydrogen aeroengines are expected to play a pivotal role in advancing green aviation, owing to their substantial potential for reducing carbon emissions. Compared with conventional kerosene, hydrogen fuel possesses distinct characteristics such as high heating value, high specific heat capacity, and demanding storage requirements, which profoundly reshape the thermodynamic cycle behavior and propulsion performance of aeroengines. Despite growing interest, a systematic and mission-oriented theoretical framework for understanding these impacts has been lacking, limiting the advancement of hydrogen propulsion systems. This study presents a comprehensive and mechanistic analysis of how hydrogen fuel affects aeroengine performance, with particular focus on three critical dimensions: thermodynamic cycle transformation, hydrogen tank mass efficiency, and heat sink utilization through advanced thermal management strategies—including hydrogen recuperation, precooling, and intercooling. Both civil and military application scenarios are considered, using hydrogen recuperated turbofan engines and hydrogen precooled/intercooled mixed turbofan engines as representative case studies for numerical validation. Simulation results indicate that hydrogen recuperation can reduce mission fuel consumption by 4.89% and increase payload capacity by 16.4%, while precooling and intercooling cycles extend the upper Mach limit of turbine engines to 3.4 and 4.0, respectively, under optimal heat sink matching. Moreover, the study identifies a critical threshold of 45% tank mass efficiency required for hydrogen aircraft to surpass kerosene counterparts in range. The findings establish an analytical foundation and design reference for hydrogen aeroengines, and offer original insights into how hydrogen’s thermal and storage properties can be effectively harnessed across diverse aviation mission profiles. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yıldız, A.; Tanürün, H.E.; Es, H.A.; Seyhan, M.
Author full names: Yıldız, Abdussamed (59412950500); Tanürün, Himmet Erdi (57223221461); Es, Huseyin Avni (56113371300); Seyhan, Mehmet (57189715633)
Title: Multi-response optimization of slotted airfoil for total aerodynamic performance: A Taguchi methodology with fuzzy decision support
Abstract: This study presents a novel multi-response optimization framework for enhancing the total aerodynamic performance (TAP) of a NACA 63(4)-421 airfoil via an advanced slot design. By integrating a Taguchi methodology with a fuzzy decision support system comprising a Rank Ordering Method (ROM) and Fuzzy Grey Relational Analysis (GRA) the investigation simultaneously addresses multiple key aerodynamic responses including lift coefficient (C<inf>L</inf>), drag coefficient (C<inf>D</inf>), and the lift to drag ratio (C<inf>L</inf>/C<inf>D</inf>). Comprehensive wind tunnel experiments, conducted over pre-stall, near-stall, and post-stall regimes, are coupled with Surface Oil Flow Visualization (SOFV) to capture detailed flow topology. Notably, Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) reveals that the slot angle and slot inlet position are the most influential parameters, contributing 48.59% and 39.35% respectively to TAP, while the slot width ratio and coanda radius contribute 2.32% and 9.75%. The optimized design achieves a striking 120.3% improvement in the maximum lift coefficient (C<inf>L,max</inf>) and a 1.3-fold enhancement in aerodynamic efficiency relative to the baseline (B1) configuration, while also delaying stall by 2°. This integrated experimental and fuzzy MCDM approach not only validates the effectiveness of the optimized slot configuration in suppressing undesirable vortex formation and enhancing flow reattachment but also sets a new paradigm for passive flow control (PFC) strategies in airfoil design. The methodology and findings have broad implications for aerospace and renewable energy applications where aerodynamic efficiency is crucial. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Title: A framework for flight vehicle aerodynamic data fusion based on generative models and interpretability methods
Abstract: The acquisition of high-fidelity aerodynamic data for flight vehicles through wind tunnel experiments often incurs substantial costs and resource demands. To enable the effective integration of low-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation data with limited experimental measurements, we propose a guided diffusion model for aerodynamic data fusion (GDFuse) in this work. This model integrates a guided mechanism with a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM), where constraints applied to both the forward and backward diffusion processes enhance generation stability and correction accuracy. Using an axisymmetric flight vehicle configuration from a NASA report as a case study, GDFuse learns the noise distribution during the forward diffusion process and gradually removes noise in the backward process to approximate wind tunnel results. To overcome the challenge of insufficient experimental coverage across diverse geometries, transfer learning is incorporated to extend the learned fusion knowledge to new axisymmetric flight vehicle configurations. This strategy improves prediction accuracy in small-scale datasets while significantly reducing training costs. GDFuse supports both direct error correction and residual-based correction strategies. Experimental results show relative errors of 5.11 %, 1.52 %, and 7.69 % for three aerodynamic coefficients, outperforming conventional fusion approaches that include guided models, random forests, and multi-fidelity neural networks. To address the limited interpretability of GDFuse, we further incorporate symbolic regression and sensitivity analysis to derive physically interpretable expressions and to assess the influence of flow conditions and fin deflection on aerodynamic responses. These methods enhance the interpretability of the model, making GDFuse a high-accuracy and generalizable solution for aerodynamic data fusion. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Title: Analytical investigation of thermal-fluid iterative coupling and parametric influences in oil-jet lubricated double-helical planetary gear transmissions
Abstract: This study addresses thermal management challenges in oil-jet lubricated double-helical planetary gear transmissions for GTF engines. A thermal-flow iterative-coupling framework is established by integrating the Volume of Fluid (VOF) multiphase model, RNG k - ε turbulence model, and mixed friction theory, incorporating a temperature-dependent iterative update mechanism for lubricant properties (viscosity, density, specific heat, etc.). This framework dynamically resolves temperature feedback effects on flow features and heat transfer characteristics, enabling high-fidelity simulation of gas-liquid two-phase flow and temperature field interactions. Through transient multiphase flow simulations, the influence of input speeds, tooth surface roughness, oil injection locations, oil injection velocities, and oil injection angles on convective heat transfer coefficients is systematically quantified. Time-varying tooth surface heat flux, obtained from mixed lubrication friction coefficients, is coupled with spatially mapped thermal boundaries to construct and solve a gear temperature field finite element (FE) model. Model validity is rigorously confirmed by mesh independence verification and experimental benchmarking. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Cao, M.; Liu, Z.; Zhang, C.; Jiang, B.; Sheng, J.; Liu, J.; Li, Y.
Title: Test and numerical simulation study on the containment characteristic of an aeroengine turbine casing
Abstract: Blade off events have always been a considerable risk to aircraft flight safety. To study the containment characteristics of aeroengine turbine casing, a series of containment tests and corresponding numerical simulation analyses are carried out in present paper. The containment tests on turbine casing specimens are performed using a high-speed rotor test rig under room and high temperature. Test results show that the turbine casing successfully contains the released blade. At high temperature, the pronounced curling deformation of the blade is observed, due to material thermal softening. A finite element model is performed using LS-DYNA software and its predictions exhibit good agreement with test results. The validated numerical simulation method is further applied to real aeroengine low-pressure turbine component to study the containment process between the released blade and casing. Parametric analyses are performed to study the effects of multiple blade interactions, numbers of released blades, and rotate speed on casing containment capability. It is shown that multiple-blade interactions can alter the trajectory of released blade compared to single-blade. During the impact process, the blade root has the most serve impact on the casing, which will affect the final deformation and failure of the casing. This effect becomes more serious with increasing release speed. Simultaneous release of three blades produces more debris and material failure, subjecting higher impact loads to the turbine containment structure. These findings can offer critical insights for the structural design of aeroengine turbine casing containment. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, C.; Wu, K.; Cui, N.; Yuan, Y.; Zhang, T.; Fan, X.
Title: Flame stabilization mode transitions in supersonic combustion of liquid kerosene with gas-assisted atomization
Abstract: This study utilizes a twin-fluid injection method to demonstrate the feasibility of gas-assisted atomization in modulating the flame stabilization modes in supersonic combustion by varying gas-to-liquid mass flow ratios (GLR). Experiments were performed in an incoming supersonic flow with a Mach number of 2.5, a total temperature of 1600 K, and a total pressure of 0.93 MPa, with a constant equivalence ratio of 0.6 during the experiments. The combustion reaction zone was characterized by CH* chemiluminescence. The results indicate that as GLR increases, the atomization performance improves. The penetration height increases modestly with GLR, following the relationship h<inf>s</inf>/d∝GLR0.13. As the GLR increases, the flame stabilization mode transitions from cavity stabilization mode to jet wake stabilization mode. At a GLR of 10 %, the flame resides in the cavity shear layer and dominated by the acoustic feedback within the cavity, with a distinct low-frequency oscillation at 264 Hz. When the GLR increases to 14 %, as the flame stabilization is still assisted by the cavity, the low-frequency oscillation persists with the dominant frequency shifting to 276 Hz. At the largest GLR of 20 %, the flame stabilizes in jet wake, eliminating cavity-related oscillations and exhibiting a wideband oscillation behavior. This work demonstrated for the first time that gas-assisted atomization is a flexible approach to extend the ignition margin, improve the flame stability, and enhance the combustion efficiency of liquid fuel in supersonic flows, which may be an enabling technique for wide range scramjets. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Author full names: Chen, Yuting (59567456000); Liu, Shijie (35201819700); Peng, Haoyang (57202612589); Zhong, Shenghui (57196009081); Zhang, Hailong (56578000600); Yuan, Xueqiang (57191658496); Fan, Weijie (57208681948); Jiang, Luxin (56609538100); Liu, Weidong (56287527500)
Title: Characteristics and mixing-controlled mechanism of rotating detonation wave under high Mach number flight conditions
Abstract: This study performs three-dimensional numerical simulations with detailed chemical kinetics to examine rotating detonation wave (RDW) under Mach number 6 flight conditions, employing dual fuel injection at upstream and downstream locations within the isolator. Theoretical and numerical analyses reveal that upstream oblique shock waves (OSWs) generated by RDWs, which are deflected downstream, have limited upstream propagation under Mach number 6 flight conditions and tend to induce trailing RDWs near the primary RDWs. We propose a parameter-extraction method focusing on combustible gases near the RDW to establish local mixing quality as important controlling factors for RDW propagation structure. The results reveal that inadequate upstream mixing causes RDWs to degenerate into upstream OSWs, where the theoretical Chapman‒Jouguet velocity of the combustible gas is lower than its synthetic velocity. Fuel distribution, controlled by the ratio of upstream to downstream injection orifices, considerably modulates mixing quality. An increased upstream fuel injection proportion directly intensifies RDWs through improved mixing efficiency. Overmixed conditions lead to upstream propagation of RDW, perturbing inlet stability, whereas undermixed conditions cause RDW destabilization or extinction. Within an appropriate range, better-mixed conditions generate stronger RDW intensity, with a broader high-heat-release zone and accelerated propagation velocity. Furthermore, mixing quality exerts indirect control on RDWs: intensified interactions between counterclockwise-rotating waves and the RDWs under poorer-mixed conditions elevate local temperatures and pressures axially upstream of the RDWs. These results advance understanding of RDW stabilization mechanisms under high Mach number conditions, offering critical insights for extending the operational envelope of rotating detonation ramjet engines. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.
