Authors: Cui, P.; Chen, Z.; Zhu, S.; Long, J.
Author full names: Cui, Pingyuan (7004791540); Chen, Zelong (58548648800); Zhu, Shengying (35212852100); Long, Jiateng (56609614600)
Title: Residual learning method for Mars aerobraking autonomous predictive guidance
Abstract: Due to the extended duration and highly nonlinear Mars aerobraking dynamics, state prediction via numerical integration is computationally prohibitive for predictive guidance. In this paper, a residual learning method for autonomous predictive guidance is proposed. The orbital state of the spacecraft governed by high-fidelity Mars aerobraking dynamics is demonstrated to consist of a simplified two-body component and a residual component. The solution for the simplified two-body component is first analytically derived, while the residual component arising from the nonspherical gravitational perturbations and aerodynamic accelerations is compensated by a neural network. With its specially designed structure, the neural network achieves better physical consistency with the integration process. Compared to directly learning the orbital states, it is proved that learning the residual mitigates the vanishing gradient problem, thereby improving prediction accuracy. Leveraging the residual learning method, a predictive guidance method is developed. By predicting the spacecraft’s orbital states over future revolutions, the velocity increments are optimized to maximize aerodynamic deceleration while ensuring compliance with the heat flux constraint. The guidance method is also proved to be asymptotically stable. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through Mars aerobraking numerical simulations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: He, B.; Zhou, Z.; Zhang, B.; Weng, H.; Wang, W.; Cai, G.
Author full names: He, Bijiao (9232746100); Zhou, Ziheng (59208249100); Zhang, Baiyi (57219609369); Weng, Huiyan (57203388391); Wang, Weizong (36072139300); Cai, Guobiao (55656628500)
Title: Development of the fast prediction surrogate model for vacuum plume field in variable-thrust engines based on multi-decoder ConvNeXt-form network
Abstract: Evaluating vacuum rocket plumes with DSMC is accurate but too slow for engineering design iteration. To address this limitation, we develop a deep learning surrogate model based on multi-decoder ConvNeXt-form network that delivers real-time plume predictions for variable-thrust engines. Trained on DSMC data, the model achieves high fidelity and consistently lower errors than a vanilla U-Net. This high-fidelity model reduces simulation times from hours to seconds and requires two orders of magnitude less floating-point operations. Compared to the vanilla U-Net model, the global prediction error decreases by 18.54 %. By ingesting a free initial field (FIF), a signed distance function, and an identifier matrix, the network generalizes across various thrust settings. Additionally, two training methods are proposed to improve accuracy, and transfer learning is performed with a small dataset under a different thrust, thus broadening the model’s applicability. The transferred model accurately predicts plume fields at the new engine thrust, demonstrating practical value for missions involving variable-thrust engines. These results demonstrate the potential of this approach for developing diverse, large-scale, rapid prediction models for plume fields, highlighting its strong application value in aerospace. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kan, X.; Lei, H.; Yuan, M.; Suo, L.; Wu, W.; Zhong, J.
Author full names: Kan, Xiaoxu (55624588700); Lei, Haodong (59233214100); Yuan, Minghao (59912367900); Suo, Licheng (59379805300); Wu, Wanyang (57015792600); Zhong, Jingjun (23981701500)
Title: Local blade profiling design for stall-stage rotor in a three-stage compressor: a numerical investigation using dissipation weighting
Abstract: In multistage compressor, t he flow field near stall is highly complex, necessitating a thorough investigation of blade optimization methods for stall stages. In conventional studies, optimization approaches for compressor blades usually fail to enhance compressor performance comprehensively. To overcome this limitation, numerical methods are used to analyze a self-designed subsonic three-stage axial compressor, beginning with identifying the first stage as the stall-initiating component. Dissipation weighting is applied to the near-stall rotor flow, from which two distinct optimization approaches are proposed. The first approach is focused solely on attenuating the peak dissipation term and yields a marked increase in stable operating margin at the expense of reduced efficiency and pressure ratio. Conversely, the second scheme implements coupled optimization targeting several predominant dissipation terms, achieving favorable tri-parameter enhancement (stable operating margin, adiabatic efficiency, and total pressure ratio), designated here as local blade profiling. This design scheme increases the peak efficiency by 0.35% compared to the original stage while maintaining pressure-ratio improvement across all operating conditions at the design speed, with a 0.25% enhancement near the stall point and 0.34% at peak efficiency. The enhancement in stability margin reaches 5.47%. Finally, a detailed comparative analysis is conducted between the local profiling scheme and individual optimization approaches, establishing a design criterion for tri-parameter enhancement. Progressive enhancement of aerodynamic performance metrics is thereby enabled by implementing co-optimization strategies targeting multiple primary dissipation components, each governed by distinct dissipation mechanisms, and maximized impact is exerted on different performance metrics. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ma, Y.; Du, H.; Sun, K.
Title: Envelope parameter sensitivity analysis on fluid-thermal-structural coupling characteristics of stratospheric non-rigid airship
Abstract: The research on fluid-thermal-structural coupling effect of stratospheric non-rigid airships is crucial for ensuring long-endurance performance. However, there remains a lack of comprehensive analytical studies on the quantitative parameter sensitivity related to this coupling effect. In this study, an iterative analysis method based on buoyancy-gravity re-equilibrium was developed to investigate the fluid-thermal-structural coupling characteristics of non-rigid airships. By integrating both single-parameter and multi-parameter sensitivity analysis methods, the sensitivity of envelope thermal and structural parameters—such as absorptivity, emissivity, elastic modulus, and thickness—on the coupling effect was systematically evaluated. Both the independent and synergistic effects of these envelope parameters were examined. Results indicate that the elastic modulus and thickness of the envelope have a significantly greater influence on structural deformation compared to absorptivity and emissivity. Conversely, the impact of elastic modulus on the maximum equivalent stress is less pronounced than that of absorptivity and emissivity. Additionally, the synergistic interaction between structural and thermal parameters was found to significantly affect the diurnal volume variation and structural deformation. These sensitivity analysis results provide valuable guidance for the design and flight testing of long-endurance stratospheric non-rigid airships. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ding, S.; Zhang, M.; Liu, D.; Liang, J.
Title: DCR-YOLO: An enhanced anti-UAV detection method based on triple collaborative optimization strategy
Abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have been widely used in various industries due to their convenience. However, misuse may pose threats to society. Therefore, anti-UAV detection in low-altitude security scenarios is important. The existing detection methods have a core contradiction of being difficult to balance detection accuracy and detection speed, and the detection effectiveness will be obviously reduced in scenarios with complex backgrounds and extremely small UAV targets. To address these issues, this paper proposes an enhanced anti-UAV detection method DCR-YOLO, which improves the YOLO11 model by introducing a triple collaborative optimization strategy. First, the DySample upsampling module is integrated to retain more effective information in the shallow features and make up for the defect of sparse features of small targets. Second, the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) is incorporated into the detection head to suppress complex backgrounds and enhance the ability to extract features of small targets. Finally, the lightweight ReGhostConv convolution module with channel distillation effect is introduced, which not only can effectively accelerate detection speed, but also can reduce computational complexity and minimize the model size. Experiments on two open-source detection datasets and tracking datasets show that, compared with mainstream detection methods, the proposed method significantly improves the detection accuracy and speed of small target UAVs. Meanwhile, the parameter has been reduced, achieving lightweight. Detection accuracy and speed have been effectively balanced. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yu, H.; Wang, C.; Li, G.
Title: Vortex dynamics and propulsion efficiency of pulsed Laval nozzles investigated by large eddy simulation
Abstract: Laval nozzles are extensively used in aerospace propulsion and jet applications, yet the unsteady mechanisms of pulsed operation and their impact on propulsion efficiency remain insufficiently understood. This study clarifies how geometric parameters of pulsed Laval nozzles regulate flow structures and thrust performance, and identifies optimization strategies for nozzle design. A three-dimensional Large Eddy Simulation (LES) approach was applied to investigate the effects of contraction ratio (0.5 < D <inf>1</inf>/ D < 1), expansion ratio (1 < D <inf>2</inf>/ D <inf>1</inf> < 1.5), contraction section length (0.5 < L <inf>1</inf>/ D < 2.5), and expansion section length (0.5 < L <inf>2</inf>/ D < 2.5) on vortex evolution, unsteady thrust, and propulsion efficiency. Results indicate that reducing contraction ratio enhances momentum injection and peak thrust but intensifies oscillations, whereas larger expansion ratios attenuate outlet momentum and thrust, with D <inf>2</inf>/ D <inf>1</inf> ≥ 1.4 inducing multi-peak fluctuations. Contraction length has little impact in full nozzles but affects vorticity transition in contraction-only cases. Short expansion sections promote strong vortex detachment, while moderate extension delays interactions and reduces oscillations. Single-section nozzles may yield higher propulsion efficiency, though with lower wall thrust ratio K<inf>W</inf> . Overall, moderate reduction of contraction and expansion ratios raises power demand but improves thrust. The findings provide physical insight into nozzle geometry–vortex coupling and offer design recommendations to enhance propulsion efficiency in pulsed nozzle systems. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Nakagawa, E.; Yokozeki, T.; Tsushima, N.
Author full names: Nakagawa, Eisuke (58242236300); Yokozeki, Tomohiro (6603926331); Tsushima, Natsuki (56736013800)
Title: Topology optimization framework for designing elastic wind tunnel models with additive manufacturing
Abstract: Wind tunnel testing remains essential for aeroelastic analysis in aircraft development. However, creating scaled models that accurately replicate both the static and dynamic structural properties of full-scale aircraft is a significant challenge. Traditional fabrication methods are often time-consuming and expensive, highlighting the need for more efficient design and manufacturing processes. This study proposes a methodology for designing an elastic wind tunnel model with an internal structure optimized to mimic the static (compliance) and dynamic (eigenfrequencies) characteristics of a full-scale wing, while ensuring manufacturability through Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) 3D printing. A level-set-based topology optimization framework was used to design the model's internal structure systematically. The optimization process, combining finite element method (FEM) analysis and automatic differentiation, aimed to match the target wing's eigenvalue ratios and compliance matrix. Penalty functions were added to prevent overhangs and sharp edges, ensuring the design's suitability for 3D printing. The numerically optimized model closely matched the scaled full-scale wing, with a maximum error of 1.7 % in frequency ratios and a cosine similarity above 0.999 for the compliance matrices. Further aeroelastic analyses confirmed similar static deformation, lift distribution, and flutter characteristics. Experimental validation of a 3D-printed model showed good correlation in eigenfrequencies, accounting for material property differences inherent to additive manufacturing. The proposed methodology effectively combines topology optimization with additive manufacturing to create structurally equivalent aeroelastic models. Validated through both numerical and experimental results, this approach demonstrates its effectiveness and potential to advance aircraft development. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Qi, W.; Zhang, Z.; Li, Y.; Tian, S.
Title: Stability boundaries and nonlinear aeroelastic behavior of a supersonic panel under thermal-degradation and damping effects
Abstract: During the service period, the thermal and mechanical properties of a supersonic panel continuously evolve with the accumulation of aerodynamic heat, and coupled effects may occur. The study quantifies the stability boundaries and nonlinear aeroelastic response of three-dimensional supersonic panels in thermal environments based on a linear thermal degradation model and Kelvin damping model. First, the von Kármán large deformation theory and piston theory are introduced to establish the aerothermoelastic model of the panel, which is then discretized using the Galerkin method. Subsequently, based on Lyapunov's indirect method and the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, the stability boundaries of the panel under different thermal degradation and structural damping parameters are examined, systematically analyzing the influence of factors such as modal truncation number, degree of thermal degradation, structural damping coefficient, and aspect ratio on the panel's stability region. This work distinguishes itself by providing the first systematic investigation of the coupled evolution of progressive thermal degradation and structural damping over a representative duration, uncovering a previously underexplored competition that dictates the system's stability. Then, the fourth-order Runge-Kutta numerical integration method is employed to solve the nonlinear dynamic response of the panel, to identify its LCOs, chaotic response, and bifurcation behavior. The results demonstrate that thermal degradation significantly reduces the critical flutter dynamic pressure of the panel system and increases vibration amplitudes, making the system more prone to chaotic motion. In contrast, structural damping exhibits a notable suppression effect on chaotic vibrations, particularly in high dynamic pressure regimes. It is noteworthy that the system may exhibit "low-dynamic-pressure flutter" phenomena under specific conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, Y.; Xu, G.; Qin, F.; Jing, T.; Sun, X.; Zhang, Z.; Zhu, S.; Wang, Y.; Zhang, D.; Gascoin, N.; Fu, Y.
Title: An experimental study on the cracking heat sink behavior of n-decane and RP-3 under supercritical pressures
Abstract: The intensifying heat load on hypersonic aircraft has promoted the advancement of active cooling technologies utilizing endothermic hydrocarbon fuels. The wide range heat release characteristics of fuel are the key factors for further expanding the application scenarios and Mach numbers of regenerative cooling technology. This study performed experimental measurements of the heat sink characteristics of supercritical n-decane and RP-3 in a 1000 mm long GH3128 microtube with an outer diameter of 3 mm and an inner diameter of 2 mm, covering a range from room temperature to beyond a 50 % conversion rate at 3 to 6 MPa. The initial temperature of the cracking reaction for n-decane and RP-3 is respectively about 850 and 900 K, and the heat sink augment rate in the cracking reaction region is significantly greater than that in the uncracking region. The fuel undergoes a secondary deep cracking reaction when the conversion rate exceeding about 30 %, accompanying with the reduction of the product alkene/alkane ratio and fuel heat sink increase rate. The importance of residence time and reaction path to the heat sink release accounts for various proportions in different temperature ranges. The definition method of physical and chemical heat sink is established, and the differences in heat sink characteristics of the two fuels are compared. The results of this work will deepen the understanding of the wide-range heat sink characteristics of fuel and provide strong data support for the design of regenerative cooling channels for hypersonic aircraft. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zheng, R.; Li, J.; Gong, E.; Jin, W.; Yao, Q.; Zhang, T.
Title: Design optimization and regulation mechanism of channel configuration for a radial wave rotor combustor
Abstract: The radial wave rotor combustor (RWRC) represents a promising pressure-gain combustion device capable of achieving simultaneous pre-compression and self-expansion, thereby significantly enhancing energy conversion efficiency. To improve the RWRC’s energy conversion efficiency and power output, this study proposes a general design method grounded in the turboshaft engine cycle. Multiobjective optimization is conducted by integrating a parametric channel configuration, designed via Bézier curves, with response surface methodology. Additionally, the influence mechanism of the optimized structure on the wave rotor’s transient performance is investigated relative to a baseline structure. The results demonstrate that the expansion efficiency of the optimized model transitions from negative to positive, reaching 1.39 %, while the hot-state average output power increases to 13.61 kW. Flow-field analysis reveals a nonlinear regulation mechanism regarding the effect of channel expansion angle ( β ) on performance characteristics: when β ranges from 31° to 55°, a balance between efficiency and total pressure loss is achieved. Conversely, in the high-torque region where β < 31°, shaft power output is significantly improved through a "trading loss for power" strategy. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to establish a quantitative regulation relationship for the channel expansion angle, offering theoretical support for the compact design and efficient operation of radial-flow wave rotors. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chan, S.; Li, S.; Xu, L.; Xing, F.
Title: Failure phenomena and mechanisms in the wave rotor-combustor closed loop system
Abstract: A wave rotor and a combustor constitute a closed loop system as the topping cycle of a gas turbine, which is known for performance enhancement. Development of such a new technology shall take continuous optimization based on observable failures, but it has been trapped for decades by a lack of knowledge on how or why the closed loop system may fail. This work proposes a control model for coupled numerical simulation of the closed loop system to calculate the flow field data in failing processes. Results show that the system exhibits a self-regulation ability within certain limits, but excessive adjustment of the exhaust pressure leads to a failure. A transient failing process is demonstrated with the variation of critical fluid dynamic parameters, and critical phenomena of failure was identified as flame out in the combustor and collapse of the unsteady pressure wave system in the wave rotor. It revealed that the adverse interaction loop between the wave rotor and the combustor caused the failure of the closed loop system. This work shall contribute to understanding how a wave rotor-topped gas turbine may fail, so that similar failures can be avoided with optimized control or design in future. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jia, S.; Qi, N.; Li, Z.; Zhou, G.; Liu, Y.
Title: Mission planning for UAV swarm air-launched by single carrier aircraft under multiple constraints
Abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) swarm air-launched by single carrier aircraft can significantly expand the operational range and maximize the collaborative potential of the unmanned swarm system. However, the air-launch paradigm introduces additional constraints and subproblem, which greatly increase the complexity of mission planning and lead to strong input–output coupling among subproblems. To address these challenges, a mission planning model is developed in this study, and the coupling relationships among four interdependent subproblems-release point information determination, task allocation, scheduling, and path planning-are thoroughly analyzed. A self-search differential evolution (SSDE) algorithm is proposed to solve the mission planning problem under multiple constraints and strong coupling. The algorithm incorporates a constraint-guided clustering initialization method to improve the potential feasibility and diversity of the initial population. In addition, a mutation operator embedded with a self-search mechanism is introduced to enhance the ability to escape local optima. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed SSDE algorithm effectively handles the multi-constraint, strongly-coupled mission planning problem of the air-launched UAV swarm, outperforming conventional algorithms in solution quality. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wei, X.; Nan, P.; Xin, G.; Zhang, Y.; Yang, H.
Author full names: Wei, Xiangyu (59735010300); Nan, Pengyu (57198350349); Xin, Guoguo (36698566900); Zhang, Yupeng (57991452700); Yang, Hangzhou (35270633900)
Title: Efficient real-time reconstruction of transient temperature fields in some hypersonic thermal protection systems with a conditioned adaptive physics network
Abstract: To address the challenge of real-time surface temperature monitoring in thermal protection systems (TPS) of hypersonic vehicles, this study proposes a Conditioned Adaptive Physics Network (CAP-Net) framework for efficient reconstruction of three-dimensional transient temperature fields. The method employs Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) to extract dominant modes of the thermal source surface, while Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks encode sparse sensor signals into dynamic features that serve as conditional inputs to drive the physics-constrained neural network. An uncertainty-driven adaptive weighting mechanism is further incorporated to dynamically balance data fitting and physical consistency, thereby improving prediction accuracy and generalization capability for this ill-posed and highly nonlinear inverse heat conduction problem. In addition, a Temperature-Gradient-Driven Condition Number Optimization Method (TGD-CNOM) is introduced to enhance sensor placement stability and robustness. A three-layer composite TPS model consisting of an LI-900 insulation layer, an strain isolation pads sealing layer, and an underlying structural layer is constructed using finite element simulations. The results show that CAP-Net achieves an average normalized root mean square error (NRMSE) of 1.35 % with a mean computational cost of only 0.0111 s per reconstruction, demonstrating both high accuracy and efficiency in reconstructing transient fields in anisotropic materials. Moreover, the method maintains strong generalization performance when accounting for LI-900 insulation degradation. Experimental validation under complex thermal environments further indicates that the NRMSE at most measurement points remains below 2.5 %, confirming the robustness and reliability of the proposed approach. Overall, CAP-Net provides a high-precision and efficient solution for real-time thermal safety monitoring of hypersonic vehicle TPS under extreme thermal conditions. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Author full names: Karatzas, Eleftherios (58561545600); Nikolaou, Eleftherios (58950065600); Pitsis, Antonis (59967725300); Alexopoulos, Spiridon (59967344800); Roussos, Savvas (59964466500); Georgantakis, Emmanouil (60113999500); Lappas, Vaios J. (10638900500); Kostopoulos, V. E. (7003891255)
Title: Conceptual design and analysis of a tube-launched fixed-Wing UAV with integrated folding wing systems
Abstract: This study presents the design and analysis of a foldable fixed-wing mini unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) developed for intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) missions in harsh environments. The work addresses the gap in small electric UAV research by adapting conceptual and preliminary design methodologies typically used for manned aircraft to small-scale platforms. Aerodynamic performance and subsystem selections were analyzed to identify the implications of downscaling conventional design approaches. Rapid-prototype models incorporating 3D-printed components were fabricated to evaluate structural feasibility within compact size constraints. The resulting configuration combines the endurance and range advantages of fixed-wing UAVs with the portability and ease of deployment commonly associated with rotary-wing systems. The findings demonstrate that foldable-wing integration can enhance field deployability without significant aerodynamic penalties, supporting future development of compact fixed-wing UAVs for extended ISR operations. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Ai, X.; Bai, Y.; Chen, X.; Liu, J.; Chen, Z.; Yu, Q.; Qian, W.
Title: Nonlinear aeroelastic analysis of an all-movable horizontal tail with bending free-play
Abstract: All-movable horizontal tails are widely used in modern supersonic fighters. In this paper, theoretical and experimental researches are presented to investigate the influence of bending free-play on the aeroelastic behavior of all-moving horizontal tail. The mechanism by which the bending free-play of all-moving horizontal tail induces limit cycle oscillation (LCO) has been proposed, and the LCO and flutter characteristics caused by bending free-play have been summarized. Additionally, an experimental all-movable horizontal tail model with bending free-play was designed, and a series of wind tunnel tests were conducted to verify the accuracy of the proposed mechanism and characteristics. Computational and experimental results consistently indicate that the mechanism and characteristics of LCOs induced by bending free-play and torsion free-play exhibit significant differences. In addition, the bending free-play in all-moving horizontal tail can cause subcritical LCO, reducing the flight envelope of the aircraft. Therefore, bending free-play is critical issues that cannot be overlooked in the design of all-moving horizontal tail. It is of great practical significance to study the mechanism and characteristics of LCO induced by bending free-play in all-movable horizontal tail and to formulate corresponding design specifications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, G.; Lv, Q.; Liu, L.; Yang, R.; Bie, B.; Wu, Y.; Quan, Y.
Title: An end-to-end deep learning framework for separation and parameter measurement of composite intermittent sampling repeater jamming
Abstract: To address the severe challenge posed by composite intermittent sampling repeater jamming (ISRJ) to aerospace radar, we propose a novel end-to-end deep learning framework for simultaneous signal separation and parameter estimation. At the core of this framework is a custom deep separation network (CISRJ-SN), which features a unique hybrid attention architecture. This architecture synergistically fuses one-dimensional convolution for local feature extraction with a gated attention unit for global dependency modeling, thereby achieving high-fidelity jammer signal separation even at a low Jammer-to-Noise Ratio (JNR). Results from both simulations and real-world hardware-in-the-loop experiments collectively validate the superior performance of our framework. In two-component and multi-component scenarios, it achieves accuracy rates of 99.5 % and 94.4 % respectively, marking a significant improvement over the next-best methods. In addition to high accuracy, the framework demonstrates exceptional estimation precision, reducing the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of key parameters by over 60 %. This proves the high stability and reliability of its estimation results, offering a promising solution for future intelligent sense-and-countermeasure closed-loop systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Gao, Y.; Liu, T.; Ren, K.; Chen, Q.
Title: Semantic-aware visual localization for robust aerial perception
Abstract: Intelligent visual navigation systems often struggle to maintain long-term stable localization performance when facing extreme viewpoint variations, altitude changes, and complex environmental disturbances.To address this, we propose a semantic-aware feature matching framework that uses pixel-level semantic labels to guide the matching process toward temporally stable and structurally consistent regions, while suppressing less reliable areas like vegetation and dynamic objects. Furthermore, we incorporate region-level semantic supervision during training by integrating semantic features from the Segment Anything Model (SAM) encoder, enhancing consistency and generalization without requiring semantic segmentation at inference time. Experiments on the CrossLoc aerial dataset demonstrate that our method achieves good performance under challenging conditions such as large viewpoint changes, seasonal and weather variations, and significant temporal shifts. This work presents a practical and generalizable semantic-aware matching solution tailored for intelligent remote sensing and aerial perception systems, offering new insights into building robust localization capabilities for next-generation aerial platforms. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, F.; Xu, J.
Title: Dynamic modeling, flight control design, and simulation verification of a Ring-Wing Tail-sitter UAV with rudder and propeller redundancy actuator
Abstract: This paper presents a novel ring-wing tail-sitter unmanned aerial vehicle (RWT-UAV) configuration featuring redundant actuation mechanisms composed of propellers and rudders. The ring-wing tail design is introduced to enhance static stability in horizontal flight, while the redundant actuators improve control effectiveness and fault tolerance in both vertical and horizontal flight modes. A nominal dynamic model is developed, and a hierarchical control allocation strategy is proposed to coordinate the redundant actuators. A unified controller combining a nominal control term and an adaptive compensation term is designed based on dynamic inversion and a nonlinear reference model with a proportional–integral structure. The inner-loop adaptive compensator suppresses parameter uncertainties and external disturbances, while the outer-loop compensation employs specific-force feedback to correct control force errors and compute the desired attitude and total thrust. Comprehensive full-envelope flight simulations, including tilt-transition and coordinated turning maneuvers, are conducted on both nominal and uncertain nonlinear models. The results demonstrate that the proposed controller achieves high tracking accuracy, robustness, and disturbance rejection capability. Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulations further verify its real-time feasibility and practical applicability, confirming that the proposed control system can be effectively implemented on embedded flight hardware. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yuan, Z.; Zhao, Y.; Li, M.; Han, B.; Han, M.; Yao, J.
Title: Design and analysis of a bistable capture mechanism based on ring deployable scissor linkages
Abstract: With the development of space technology, the capture of non-cooperative space targets has become particularly important. In response to this challenge, this paper proposes a novel capture mechanism that integrates a ring deployable base (RDB) with bistable capture branches. Based on the RDB and bistable capture branches, the mechanism achieves high stability and adaptability during both deployment and capture processes. The key structural parameters are constrained within specific ranges to ensure that the mechanism maintains a single degree of freedom (DOF), avoids singular configurations, and exhibits bistable characteristics. The DOF of the mechanism is analyzed using screw theory, and results indicate that the entire system maintains a single DOF throughout its motion, which simplifies the control strategy. A finite element model is established to verify the bistability of the flexible components. Furthermore, a prototype was fabricated and tested on targets with different shapes, demonstrating the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed mechanism. This study presents a practical and scalable technical solution for on-orbit capture of non-cooperative targets by space robots, offering strong potential for engineering applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Du, W.; Pan, Y.; Li, X.; He, N.; Wang, N.; Yang, K.
Title: Flow instability analysis of interactions between regenerative cooling channels with lateral non-uniform heating
Abstract: Flow instability within regenerative cooling channels is a critical issue for scramjet reliability, while the role of channel-to-channel interactions, a key factor in multi-channel systems, remains poorly understood. To address this knowledge gap, a one-dimensional time-domain method is developed and validated for simulating temporal behaviors of supercritical fluids. Using supercritical n-Decane as the coolant, the quantitative analysis of a single-channel system reveals low-frequency density wave oscillations (2–13 Hz), driven by the out-of-phase oscillation between pseudo-fluid and pseudo-gas regions. The disparate locations of maximum oscillation amplitude for flow rate, density, pressure drop, and velocity are found, which arise from nonlinear density variation. This study then directly analyzes dual- and multi-channel systems under lateral non-uniform heating. In the dual-channel system, the temporal evolution of the flow rate and pressure drop reveals significant interactions between channels, leading to two distinct modes of divergent oscillation. For multi-channel systems, the channel number is extended to 3, 4, and 5. As the number of channels increases, the stability boundary of the hottest channel approaches that of a single channel, indicating reduced interaction. However, significant interactions between flow maldistribution and density wave oscillation persist under a minor lateral heat load deviation. Furthermore, a novel dimensionless parameter, related to the pressure-drop-versus-flow-rate slope, is proposed to characterize stability. A linear stability boundary is defined for the single-channel system using the dimensionless parameter and inlet temperature, while further research is needed to comprehensively assess channel-to-channel interactions in multi-channel systems. This work provides theoretical insights for suppressing flow instability in regenerative cooling channels. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liang, S.; Chen, E.; Tian, Y.; Yang, M.; Deng, X.; Wang, H.
Title: Hybrid slime mould algorithm for efficient optimization of chemical kinetic mechanisms in scramjet turbulent combustion simulations
Abstract: Enhancing the computational efficiency and accuracy of turbulent combustion simulations remains a critical challenge in both basic and applied chemistry research. This study presents an innovative methodology that integrates reaction mechanism simplification with parameter optimization to address these challenges. A detailed hydrogen reaction mechanism involving 12 species and 30 reactions was systematically reduced to a simplified mechanism comprising 8 species and 6 reactions, denoted as H<inf>2</inf>-Simple. To optimize the reaction parameters, a novel hybrid Slime Mould Algorithm (SMA) combined with Sobol sequences and Lévy Flight (SMA-LS) was developed, the optimization process focused on key parameters such as pre-exponential factors, temperature coefficients, and activation energy, leveraging ignition delay data across a broad range of operating conditions as targets. Through systematic validation, SMA‑LS reduces the median best‑fitness value by 13.43% relative to SMA and by 25.45% relative to WOA. The median iterations to reach this optimum are 75, 76, and 78 for SMA‑LS, SMA, and WOA, respectively, indicating that the improvement introduces no additional iteration overhead. For multi‑condition ignition delay time prediction, the H<inf>2</inf>‑SMA‑LS mechanism improves prediction stability (IQR) by 8.95% over H<inf>2</inf>‑SMA and by 20.30% over H<inf>2</inf>‑WOA. Comparisons of turbulent‑flow simulations from the three optimized mechanisms with the detailed mechanism and ground wind‑tunnel experiments show that H<inf>2</inf>‑SMA‑LS exhibits overall greater robustness and stability, further confirming the effectiveness of SMA‑LS. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, P.; Song, J.; Cai, J.; Liu, Y.; Wen, X.
Author full names: Wang, Panle (60221302900); Song, Jinsheng (57384849100); Cai, Jiangtao (60221246100); Liu, Yingzheng (26643397400); Wen, Xin (56102097400)
Title: Transient evolution of flow structures induced by sweeping jets on a circular cylinder via multi-scale proper orthogonal decomposition (mPOD)
Abstract: The transient evolution of flow structures induced by sweeping jets (SWJs) at the stagnation point of cylinders has been numerically studied through the multi-scale proper orthogonal decomposition (mPOD). The momentum coefficient (C<inf>μ</inf>) of the jet is continuously varied from 0.10 to 2.24. Analysis of steady/transient flow fields demonstrates that the flow transforms sequentially through single-peak (0.10≤C<inf>μ</inf><0.11), single-vortex (0.11≤C<inf>μ</inf><0.92), double-peak (0.92≤C<inf>μ</inf><2.01), and double-vortex (2.01≤C<inf>μ</inf>≤2.24) modes. Ultimately, the wake dynamics lock onto a new low-frequency mode controlled by the 10th subharmonic of the SWJs. The mPOD analysis reveals nonlinear influences of the SWJs on the flow field with novel modal structures representing the spatiotemporal evolution of the virtual aerodynamic shape for both upstream and downstream flow fields. In the single-peak mode, the modal structures remain stable, with the virtual aerodynamic shape predominantly controlled by the main flow. In the single-vortex mode, the modal structures gradually shrink, and their frequencies decrease, although the main flow still dominates the virtual aerodynamic shape. Upon entering the double-peak mode, the modal structures shift significantly; the original frequency disappears, and a new frequency emerges, indicating that the SWJs are more influential than the main flow, fundamentally altering the virtual aerodynamic shape. Finally, in the double-vortex mode, the modal evolution stabilizes, marking the final stable state of the virtual aerodynamic shape. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Sun, B.; Zhang, Y.; Wang, H.; Chu, H.

Title: Twin Parallel Mechanisms for Aerospace Deployable Structures
Abstract: Traditional parallel mechanisms usually have limited workspace, failing to meet the requirements of aerospace deployable structures for large workspace and high folding ratio. This paper proposes an innovative mechanism design method: stacking multiple identical parallel mechanisms via shared kinematic joints to form twin parallel mechanisms with significantly expanded workspace. First, the design methodology and motion superposition principles of twin parallel mechanisms are systematically expounded. Subsequently, two typical twin parallel mechanisms were developed based on 3-RSP and 3-RSR parallel mechanisms, and the practical feasibility of twin parallel mechanisms was verified through kinematic analysis, numerical simulation, and experimental testing. Moreover, exploratory studies were carried out to investigate the applications of twin parallel mechanisms in space equipment—specifically foldable antennas and deployable space capture systems. This work provides a new practical approach for deployable structure design, which is of positive significance for promoting innovation in aerospace deployable equipment. © © 2025. Authors: Shen, J.; Guo, Q.; Lin, L.; Zhong, Y.; Deng, L.
Title: Taxiway path planning at airports based on dynamic directed graphs and DQN
Abstract: This study aims to address the inefficiency of traditional airport taxi scheduling by proposing an intelligent planning method that combines dynamic directed graphs and deep reinforcement learning. A dual-node state-directed graph model is constructed using Multilateration (MLAT) technology to dynamically update spatiotemporal node resources, which enables a multi-aircraft collaborative environment perception. An enhanced deep Q-network (DQN) with prioritized experience replay and a dueling architecture is designed to improve the algorithm's stability and responsiveness in dynamic scenarios. The proposed method’s effectiveness is verified using extensive simulation experiments. Experimental results demonstrate that, when compared to conventional methods, our approach reduces per-aircraft waiting time by 124 s on average in same-direction departure taxiing and 116 s in node-overlap scenarios. In the 10-aircraft mixed-operation test, it has a total waiting time of 31 s and a makespan of 8 min 31 s, whereas comparison algorithms have makespans that exceed 13 min. The results of this study validate the synergistic effectiveness of dynamic representation and deep reinforcement learning, providing an innovative solution for intelligent airport scheduling. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, B.; Tan, J.; Lyu, H.; Zhang, Z.; Liu, Z.; Huang, H.
Title: Surrogate-assisted evolutionary optimization and microgravity experimental validation of a planar deployable mechanism
Abstract: This paper proposes a surrogate-assisted evolutionary framework to optimize the design parameters of a novel planar deployable mechanism, with experimental validation conducted through microgravity tests. The mechanism features an elastic outer frame integrated with an inner rope net, utilizing stored strain energy for passive deployment to overcome the transient operational time limitations of traditional designs. The highly nonlinear deployment dynamics and computationally expensive simulations pose significant challenges for conventional optimization methods. To address this, a Genetic Algorithm (GA) is integrated with Response Surface Methodology (RSM) and Kriging surrogate models to efficiently minimize the maximum backward penetration length during deployment. A parallel evaluation strategy is implemented to significantly reduce the optimization time compared to conventional approaches. Comparative analysis demonstrates the superior performance of the Kriging model, which shows a substantial reduction in maximum residual error compared to the polynomial RSM. Experimental validation under microgravity conditions confirmed the accuracy of the mechanism simulation, and the optimal design successfully deployed with intrusion depths within required limits. The proposed methodology provides an efficient and reliable approach for optimizing complex deployable structures involving computationally expensive simulations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Niu, H.; Yi, S.; Zheng, W.; Lu, X.
Title: Experimental and CFD investigation of the heat flux distribution and flow field structure on the leeward side of a 75∘ delta flat plate
Abstract: This study investigates the flow phenomenon on the leeward side of a 75∘ delta flat plate through wind tunnel testing and numerical simulations. In a Mach 6 free jet wind tunnel, the heat flux distribution and the flow field structure on the leeward side was measured using temperature-sensitive paint (TSP) and nano-tracer-based planar laser scattering (NPLS) techniques. The results reveal two pairs of high heat flux streaks: primary streaks and secondary streaks. Downstream, the spacing between the two pairs of the high heat flux streaks increases linearly. In the flow field on the leeward side, a system of separated vortices causes the uplift of the boundary layer along the symmetrical plane and a depression near the plane of the delta flat plate. The downwash of near-wall flow, induced by the primary separation vortex, is responsible for the primary high heat flux streak located near the symmetrical plane. © © 2025. Authors: Sun, L.; Cui, B.; Gong, Y.; Guo, Y.; Mei, J.; Wu, W.
Title: Deep reinforcement learning-based fast hybrid planning for multi-type on-orbit service under mass variation dynamics
Abstract: This study proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based fast planning method for the hybrid planning of multi-type tasks of GEO spacecraft under ”service station-service satellite” on-orbit service mode, considering mass variation caused by service. The hierarchical architecture achieves joint fast estimation of transfer duration and service sequence: the lower layer solves orbital transfer planning via supervised learning, introducing residual modules for high-precision prediction of Lambert optimal transfer duration to ensure computational efficiency for upper-layer planning; the upper layer handles service sequence planning with deep reinforcement learning, proposing a categorical-encoded Transformer attention model that ensures target position order invariance while satisfying start-end position constraints. To mitigate the impacts of hard constraints on model training, this study simultaneously proposes a bidirectional calculation of fuel consumption. Finally, simulations show that the method reduces the feasible solution planning time from the order of hours to the order of seconds for 10-target missions, significantly lowering computational resource consumption. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Aghaee, F.; Shaker, H.R.
Title: RB-LLM Control: an Intelligent Control Framework with Rule-Based Large Language Model Decision-Making
Abstract: In this study, the long-term logical reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs) is explored to enhance the decision-making of autonomous control systems operating in the presence of environmental disturbances and model uncertainties. Given that LLMs may exhibit limitations in mathematical and logical reasoning, a structured prompting strategy based on logical rules is designed in this paper to enhance reliability and improve response quality. In this paper, Rule-Based LLM (RB-LLM) is incorporated into the architecture of a control system to strengthen resilience in challenging scenarios that the system wasn’t specifically designed for, it interprets natural language and other log/error information for guidance and adaptation of control inputs using structured user-provided prior knowledge. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of our approach, emphasizing the potential of RB-LLM decision-making to advance the autonomy of control systems in complex and dynamic environments. The effectiveness of the proposed RB-LLM controller in terms of tracking accuracy, robustness to uncertainty, safety in harsh disturbances, and computational efficiency is evaluated. In this study, a comparative performance analysis of the proposed RB-LLM control framework, classical, and state-of-the-art approaches is conducted. Simulation results validate the advantages and limitations of each method, offering insights into the trade-offs between classical, optimization-based, and RB-LLM control techniques. For example, the RB-LLM framework achieves a reduction in maximum error ranging from 36% to 89% compared to existing approaches. RB-LLM control framework shows superior adaptability in the presence of modeling uncertainties, nonlinear dynamics, and emergency scenarios. The proposed framework is readily extendable to a broad range of control applications; in this study, its implementation is demonstrated using simulations for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Yang, S.; Zhong, C.; Ding, N.; Cao, J.; Zhang, H.; Zhuo, C.; Liu, S.
Title: A simplified unified wave-particle method for diatomic gases with rotational and vibrational non-equilibrium
Abstract: The hypersonic flow around near-space vehicles constitutes a multi-scale flow problem. Due to insufficient molecular collisions to achieve equilibrium, rarefied gas effects are present in the flow field. Thus, numerical methods capable of accurately resolving multi-scale flows are required. Furthermore, high-temperature gas effects in hypersonic flows mean vibrational excitation of polyatomic molecules. Consequently, numerical methods accounting for non-equilibrium in rotational and vibrational internal energy modes are required. This study derives a quantified model-competition (QMC) mechanism for diatomic gases with rotational and vibrational non-equilibrium, starting from integral solutions of kinetic model equations with rotational and vibrational energy. The QMC mechanism categorize collisional and free-transport particles in cell, applying computational weighting based on their local scale regimes. We developed a simplified unified wave-particle (SUWP) method for diatomic gases based on QMC mechanism. For the macroscopic of the method, a three-temperature model accounting for rotational and vibrational energy is incorporated into both the kinetic inviscid flux scheme and Navier-Stokes solvers. For the microscopic of the method, a collisionless DSMC solver is employed to resolve non-equilibrium flow physics. This work validates the proposed SUWP method with rotational and vibrational non-equilibrium through benchmark cases, including shock tube, shock structures, flow past a cylinder, Apollo 6 command module and space station Mir. Compared to the DSMC and deterministic methods, the SUWP method exhibits favorable computational efficiency while maintaining accuracy. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Gharezi, S.; Doustdar, M.M.
Title: Design and optimization of a tubular combustor for supersonic propulsion using genetic algorithms
Abstract: Genetic algorithms are interdisciplinary tools that have recently found wide application across multiple scientific and engineering fields. This study utilizes the rationale behind genetic algorithm to design and simulate the combustion process in a turbofan engine combustor, with its optimized configuration subsequently applied to a ramjet simulator engine. The study begins by outlining the types of combustors and the methodologies employed in their design. The combustor's design process and geometric configuration are described based on established design correlations from the literature. After calculating the relevant dimensions, input parameters obtained from GasTurb are used in computational models to determine the combustor geometry. Once the geometry is validated, combustion is simulated in ANSYS Fluent using the non-premixed liquid fuel combustion model. A genetic algorithm is then employed to identify the combustor design with optimal combustion performance for use in ramjet simulator engines. The results indicate that a flameholder is necessary for the combustor, with a 30 mm base yielding superior performance. Modifying the positions of the air holes and flameholder had no significant effect on efficiency, whereas implementing central fuel injection at the inlet enhanced combustion performance. Additionally, maintaining the original positions and mass flow rates of the secondary and dilution air holes was found to be essential for optimal performance. © © 2025. Authors: Yan, K.; Wang, X.; Qu, T.; Zhou, J.; Zhang, X.; Che, C.; Li, H.; Zhang, J.; Liu, M.; Lu, Q.
Title: Forgetting filter-based real-time identification and compensation of whole-angle hemispherical resonator gyroscope errors for extensive aerospace applications
Abstract: Whole-Angle hemispherical resonator gyroscope (WA HRG) has a large dynamic range and stable scale factor (SF) to match extensive aerospace applications. However, the detection, drive, phase, and resonator errors (DDPREs) affect the performance of the WA HRG and are affected by temperature. This paper pioneers the identification and compensation (IC) of the detection, drive, and phase errors (DDPEs) from room temperature and offline to full-temperature and real-time. The complete form of the slow variable equations of the WA HRG is constructed considering the DDPREs for the first time, and the decoupled characterizations of the DDPREs are determined. The RTIC of the DDPEs is accomplished based on the forgetting filter with excellent consistency and repeatability, and the temperature-dependent characteristics of the DDPEs are determined. The WA HRG finally reaches the inertial-level at 25 ∘C with an SF nonlinearity (SFN) of 0.9474 ppm and a bias stability (BS) of 0.0904 ∘/ h . During the temperature cycling in the range of - 40 ∘ C to 60 ∘ C , the SF stability (SFS) under the 18 ∘/ s external angular velocity input (EAVI) is improved by 98.10 % from 818.4427 ppm to 15.5011 ppm, and the virtual rotation SFS (VRSFS) under the 7.5 V virtual Coriolis voltage input (VCVI) is improved by 99.46 % from 7113.69 ppm to 37.92 ppm. The RTIC of the DDPEs significantly enhances the stability and observability of the WA HRG under varying temperatures, which paves the way for the full-temperature performance improvement of the WA HRG and its extensive aerospace applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zou, X.; Zhang, L.; Zhong, P.; Meng, D.; Sun, H.-J.; Liu, Y.; Liu, W.
Author full names: Zou, Xiangxiang (59249182500); Zhang, Luming (60217650900); Zhong, Peineng (59229336100); Meng, Deshan (55827505800); Sun, Huijie (57191202492); Liu, Yu (56119454500); Liu, Wanquan (7407343628)
Title: Predefined-time trajectory tracking control of a rigid-flexible cable-driven space manipulator using neural network
Abstract: Trajectory tracking control of a rigid-flexible cable-driven space manipulator is a challenging task due to the structural flexibility and cable elasticity during long-distance operations. In this paper, the trajectory tracking control of a rigid-flexible cable-driven space manipulator is investigated. First, a two-link rigid-flexible coupled dynamic model is established, considering the structural flexibility of the manipulator links and cable elasticity. Subsequently, a predefined-time trajectory tracking controller is designed using radial basis function neural networks, which also integrates an adaptive parameter adjustment mechanism. The developed controller guarantees trajectory tracking precision while reducing end-effector vibration. Then the asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system is proven using Lyapunov stability theory and numerical simulations are conducted to validate the theoretical analysis. Finally, comparative experiments are validated on a physical prototype platform, the designed control strategy has decreased by 13.81% and 43.66% respectively in terms of Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) indicators compared with existing controller. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, C.; Wang, Q.; Zhang, M.; Zhang, J.
Author full names: Wang, Chunhua (59091624800); Wang, Qian (59957163200); Zhang, Minhui (59227820000); Zhang, Jingzhou (10043147000)
Title: Lightweight machine learning approaches for modeling a rotating cavity with vortex-reducing tubes
Abstract: Accurate assessment of rotating disk cavity performance is critical to the effective design of secondary air systems. The traditional fluid network method offers the advantage of fast computation but fails to meet the increasingly stringent requirements for high precision. While computational fluid dynamics (CFD) enables fine-grained modeling, its complex modeling and calculation processes hinder its application in rapid design iterations. To address this gap, this study realizes refined and rapid modeling of flow and heat transfer inside rotating cavities with vortex-reducing tubes, utilizing a lightweight radial basis function neural network (RBFNN). To overcome the challenge of lightweight neural networks in handling large-scale datasets, an innovative one-to-one grid prediction strategy based on independent machine learning grids was proposed, which allows RBFNN to achieve higher modeling accuracy than a deep-learning deconvolutional neural network (Deconv NN). Furthermore, the RBFNN features a simpler topological structure and fewer hyperparameters. The test results show that the mean absolute percentage errors of the total pressure, total temperature, and swirl ratio predicted by the RBFNN are 0.87%, 0.52%, and 1.09%, respectively, and the root-mean-square errors are 3730 Pa, 1.85 K, and 0.0077. Additionally, the parametric influences on RBFNN outputs were examined in detail, and compared with the results from CFD simulations. For small datasets, the proposed lightweight neural network modeling method outperforms deep learning models in both efficiency and accuracy. This approach can also be extended to other components within the secondary air system of gas turbines. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Mi, Q.; Ling, S.; Gang, D.; Yi, S.; Feng, Y.
Title: Planar laser-induced fluorescence visualization of microporous-wall seeping gas films in supersonic boundary layers
Abstract: Microporous wall seepage is an effective method for reducing aircraft heat and drag. This study uses planar laser-induced fluorescence (PLIF) to visualize the flow field of a microporous wall seeping gas film within a Mach 3 boundary layer. A laminar gas film forms as the cooling gas seeps from the leading edge of the microporous wall. At low injection rates, the gas film grows slowly, maintaining a longer laminar phase. As the injection rate increases, the film thickens, but instability arises when the film thickness reaches 0.8–0.9 mm, causing intense mixing with the mainstream boundary layer and forming large-scale structures. This instability shifts upstream with increasing injection rates, advancing the transition point to x/L = 0.28 along the microporous wall at a 1 % injection rate. By correlating the fluorescence signal strength with the acetone molar fraction, a relative concentration field of the seeping gas film is constructed. At low injection rates ( F < 0.2 %), the diffusion into the outer boundary layer is minimal. As the injection rate increases, the film diffuses further, with stable concentrations within the 0 < z < 0.5 mm range. Beyond a height of 2 mm, the concentration fluctuates, indicating full mixing with the mainstream boundary layer. This study concludes that the gas film cooling efficiency improves with the film thickness in the laminar state, and higher injection rates cause instability and mixing of the film layer, reducing cooling efficiency in the downstream region. © © 2025. Authors: Jiang, Z.; Chen, Z.; Shi, L.; Zhang, Z.; Hao, J.; Wen, C.-Y.
Author full names: Jiang, Ziqi (59390202100); Chen, Zong Nan (57201980311); Shi, Lisong (57192941850); Zhang, Zijian (57195399882); Hao, Jiaao (56278092500); Wen, Chihyung (7201366945)
Title: Numerical study of oblique detonation initiation assisted by local energy deposition
Abstract: Reliable initiation of oblique detonation waves (ODWs) is crucial for the efficiency and stable operation of oblique detonation engines (ODEs), especially under flight conditions of low Mach numbers and/or high altitudes. In this case, conventional initiation approaches may fail, necessitating extra initiation assistance measures. In this study, ODW initiation over a finite wedge with a novel energy deposition zone (EDZ) is numerically investigated to assess the thermal effects of plasma-based initiation techniques. Particular emphasis is put on the effects of forms and magnitudes of energy deposition on initiation modes and flow field structures of ODWs, along with the aims of maintaining sustainable on-wedge detonations with less power consumption. The results showed that both continuous and pulsatile energy depositions can effectively initiate ODWs in scenarios where initiation fails without such assistance. Two main detonation initiation modes emerge in sequence, namely delayed modes with combustion occurring downstream of the EDZ and a direct mode with combustion arising from the EDZ, depending on the applied continuous energy deposition power. Pulsatile energy deposition showed comparable modes as the pulse energy increases. Spatiotemporal analysis under single-pulse energy deposition identifies the minimum pulse repetition frequency for sustainable on-wedge detonation, later validated through multi-pulse simulations. Notably, pulsatile energy depositions achieve sustainable on-wedge detonation with an average power consumption of less than 10% of that required for the continuous ones while maintaining the same initiation length, suggesting that the pulsatile schemes are highly efficient for initiation assistance in ODEs under extreme flight conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Shao, Z.; Chen, Y.; Gao, J.; Shen, Z.; Feng, J.; Sareh, P.
Author full names: Shao, Zerui (58962143900); Chen, Yao (55914060400); Gao, Jiangjun (59326538100); Shen, Zhengliang (59326707700); Feng, Jian (7403883802); Sareh, Pooya (55161625900)
Title: Designing complex re-entrant honeycombs with optimal auxeticity
Abstract: Due to their distinctive mechanical properties, re-entrant honeycomb structures have attracted significant attention across various engineering fields, including aerospace applications. In this work, we propose a complex multi-node re-entrant honeycomb metastructure along with a corresponding design methodology. The mechanical behavior of these metastructures is accurately characterized using the stiffness matrix method. A genetic algorithm is employed to optimize the coordinates of internal nodes, minimizing the Poisson’s ratio under small deformations within a large design space. The results demonstrate that the proposed theoretical approach and optimization framework achieve high accuracy. Moreover, the optimized metastructure exhibits a significantly more negative Poisson’s ratio than conventional designs. The framework is also extensible to three-dimensional (3D) structures, with the 3D stiffness matrix method enabling the successful optimization of 3D multi-node re-entrant metastructures. Additionally, by integrating the Updated Lagrangian method with the genetic algorithm, the desired Poisson’s ratio can be maintained under large strains. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Xiao, Y.; Ghazouani, N.; Yaylacı, M.; Or, S.W.
Author full names: Xiao, Yongchun (60214875900); Ghazouani, Nejib (36458421400); Yaylacı, Murat (55906015300); Or, Siu Wing (7006594254)
Title: Nonlinear group and phase velocity analysis and architectural design of functionally graded tapered plates under supersonic airflow
Abstract: In this research, we study the nonlinear group of phase velocity and the architectural design of functionally graded plates with variable thickness in the presence of supersonic airflow. The plates are modeled as three-directional functionally graded materials (TD-FGMs) using a quasi-3D refined theory (Q3D-RT) to capture a full representation of the interactions between the materials' properties, geometry, and loading conditions. The nonlinearities of the system are described via Von-Karman's geometric nonlinearity, subject to the constitutive equations that are modified for viscoelastic solids, where the time derivative of strain is explicitly included. The analysis encompasses several forms of aerodynamic pressure to model the supersonic flow field, including dynamic unsteady Bernoulli-type pressure, nonlinear dynamic pressure including strain coupling, krumhaar’s modified supersonic piston theory, and the viscoelastic dynamic lag model. The neural network-nerodynamic hybrid pressure model is evaluated as the most accurate model for supersonic airflow conditions. The research utilizes Hamilton’s principle to derive the governing equations before they are solved using the harmonic balance technique, along with a numerical iterative method to achieve good accuracy of the results. As part of the examination of the accuracy of the current work, the results will also be compared with the results from finite element simulations. The results analyze the effect of different material gradation, plate thickness, and supersonic airflow conditions on the dynamic response and stability of the structure. The results add new knowledge in the design and functional performance of functionally graded plates in aerospace applications, where considerations of both material nonlinearity and complex aerodynamic interactions must be taken into account. The method also provides a practically applicable framework for both analysis and design of advanced structural components, experienced with unique multi-physics situations, subject to supersonic environments. The method has potential applications in advanced aerospace vehicle development. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Du, Z.-B.; Shen, C.-B.; Huang, W.; Xu, X.-P.; Tian, S.-S.; Ma, L.; Zhao, Y.
Title: Numerical and experimental exploration on the strut-cavity flow characteristics induced by the oblique shock wave in supersonic crossflow
Abstract: The innovation in mixing strategies of fuel-air is an eternal topic for scramjet engines. In the current study, the strut-cavity flow characteristics without injection under the oblique shock wave in supersonic crossflow is explored numerically and experimentally with three types of cavities, namely rectangular cavity, trapezoidal cavity and virtual cavity. The obtained results predicted show that the primary vortex and secondary vortex are generated in the rectangular cavity and trapezoidal cavity. There is only a primary vortex in the virtual cavity. The oblique shock wave induces changes in the flow field, promotes the early transition of the boundary layer, and the development of the shear layer and vortex structure in the normal direction. The local turbulent pulsation and static pressure amplitude of the cavity are increased, which is beneficial for ignition and flame stability. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Lu, W.; Zhuang, H.; Geng, Z.; Shen, Q.; Mu, Z.; Wu, S.; Razoumny, V.Y.; Razoumny, Y.N.
Author full names: Lu, Wenlong (59443824800); Zhuang, Hongji (57713040600); Geng, Ziyao (60042402500); Shen, Qiang (55903899800); Mu, Zhongcheng (55415384700); Wu, Shufan (57190223905); Razoumny, Vladimir Yu (25628685500); Razoumny, Yury N. (16647281300)
Title: Safe reinforcement learning based agile satellite attitude control for non-cooperative target tracking with operation constraints and actuator faults
Abstract: Persistent tracking of space-based non-cooperative targets is a fundamental capability for achieving space situational awareness and enabling numerous downstream space applications. However, it presents significant challenges, particularly during agile attitude maneuvers, due to the need to respect operational constraints and handle potential actuator faults. In this work, we divide the agile satellite tracking task into three distinct stages and formulate it as a constrained spacecraft attitude control problem. To systematically address the complex practical challenges in agile satellite control–including attitude constraints from forbidden zones, angular velocity limitations, torque saturation, and potential actuator faults–we formulate the problem as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP). To enhance the adaptability and fault-tolerance of the controller, we integrate a data-driven fault identification module capable of estimating both actuator effectiveness loss and additive torque faults. A safe reinforcement learning framework is further employed to learn fault-tolerant control policies that ensure continuous and stable tracking performance while minimizing the risk of constraint violations. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach enables the agile imaging satellite to achieve accurate, persistent tracking under strict operational constraints, while maintaining safe and resilient attitude control in the presence of actuator anomalies. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xu, W.; Wang, S.; Cheng, Y.; Sun, D.; Zhao, H.; Zou, S.
Title: Study on the mechanism of inhibiting the deterioration of compressor performance caused by interstage leakage using root slots
Abstract: This study designs a slot structure connecting the suction and pressure surfaces of the compressor stator to improve the performance and stability of the compressor. The high-energy airflow is then used to improve the corner separation flow state. It investigates the influence of the slot structure on the flow field structure and the compressor passage's aerodynamic performance under different positions and operating conditions. This study finds that the improvement effect first weakens and then increases as the slot position moves toward TE, and the effect of the case near LE and TE is better. The slot at the 25 % chord position has the most obvious effect on improving compressor performance and reduces total pressure loss by 13.7 %. Then, the slot exhibits a good ability to respond to changes in incidence angle. As the angle increases, the inhibitory effect of the slot on loss first strengthens, then weakens. The corner separation of the prototype increases when the condition exceeds the minimal loss angle. The slot flow enters the corner region directly, thereby diminishing the capacity of the slot to suppress corner separation. The flow control effect is most significant under the P0 condition, reducing total pressure loss by 13.7 %, enhancing diffusion capacity. In addition, the slot structure has good Mach number adaptability and can decrease total pressure loss at different Mach numbers. The separation range gradually expands as Ma increases, weakening the inhibitory impact of the slot. The most significant effect is at 0.5 Ma, reducing the total pressure loss by 17.7 %. © © 2025. Authors: Jin, F.; Wang, Y.; Pang, D.
Title: Numerical investigations on aerodynamic characteristics in a longitudinal V-shaped hypersonic vehicle formation
Abstract: A numerical study is conducted to investigate the aerodynamic augmentation characteristics within a longitudinal V-shaped formation of hypersonic vehicles, using X-43A as the baseline geometry. Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) simulations, employing the Shear Stress Transport (SST) k−ω turbulence model, are performed at Mach 6. The credibility of the numerical methodology is established through validation against available flight data and comprehensive grid-independence studies for both single-vehicle and multi-body configurations. The results reveal a dualistic aerodynamic interference mechanism. At low angles of attack (0°-4°), oblique shock waves from the lead vehicle impinge on the upper-echelon trailing vehicles, creating a high-pressure region that significantly enhances their lift-to-drag (L/D) ratios. However, this beneficial effect diminishes as the angle of attack surpasses 4° due to the fixed formation geometry. Concurrently, the lower-echelon vehicles are persistently subjected to the lead vehicle’s downwash field, resulting in a consistent degradation of aerodynamic performance across all tested angles of attack. These findings delineate the narrow performance-gain window for the fixed formation, highlighting its inherent limitations. The study concludes that while properly configured formations can yield substantial aerodynamic benefits, future research could focus on adaptive formation strategies to dynamically exploit favorable interference while mitigating adverse effects across a wider flight envelope. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Cilliers, T.; Herman Steyn, W.; Willem Jordaan, H.
Author full names: Cilliers, Tian (58895970100); Herman Steyn, Willem (60214415200); Willem Jordaan, Hendrik (60214972000)
Title: Robust thruster-only constrained optimal slew control strategies for nanosatellites
Abstract: A common satellite attitude control task is performing large-angle slew maneuvers. When using thrusters to do this, it is necessary to minimize propellant consumption to maximize the operational lifespan, consequently. Classical methods for performing slew maneuvers generally do not optimally utilize thruster placement and do not inherently support attitude constraints, which may be necessary for proper functioning of certain sensors. Existing optimal control methods are often not sufficiently robust to model mismatch and measurement uncertainty. This work proposes a novel modification to significantly improve robustness over the underlying successive convexification-based model predictive control approach. Numerical simulations are performed to demonstrate the efficiency, constraint handling, and robustness of the proposed method as compared to existing methods. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Li, Z.; Zhao, Y.; Ren, X.; Gu, R.
Title: Air route network planning for urban logistics with vertihub: An airspace model
Abstract: Urban logistics unmanned aerial vehicle transportation has rapidly evolved into hub-and-spoke route networks formed with vertihubs as hubs and unidirectional round-trip routes as basic components. Route network planning faces challenges such as dense traffic flow over the vertihub and multiple disturbances from wind fields and other factors. This study proposes a route network planning framework considering energy consumption. First, an airspace model incorporating the fundamental configurations and key parameters of various airspace types is established. Based on constraints such as wind fields, ground obstacles, and cross-track tolerance, a more adaptable Concentric Ring and Improved Visibility Graph (CR-IVG) airspace network modeling method is developed. Second, focusing on the unidirectional round-trip route structure, a wind-aware A* algorithm aimed at minimizing energy consumption is proposed, achieving highly adaptive and efficient path planning under various wind fields. Third, the selection of the Touchdown and Lift-Off Areas (TLOFs) at the vertihub is integrated with the unidirectional round-trip route planning method. Based on this integration, an alignment point allocation method for the TLOFs and a distance-priority-based route planning strategy are introduced, effectively reducing the total energy consumption of the route network. A case study was conducted in the 40–120 m airspace over a central business district to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The method not only develops a logistics route network with greater flexibility, diversity, and enhanced adaptiveness, but also reduces energy consumption by 6.8% compared with the traditional A* method and by 8.0% relative to other route network planning strategies. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, W.; Lu, K.; Wang, H.; Li, D.; Li, R.; Zhang, H.; Fu, C.
Title: Multi-Unit hybrid modeling method and nonlinear dynamics analysis of the whole aero-engine system
Abstract: This study introduces an innovative hybrid multi-unit modeling strategy for the dual rotor-bearing-casing-bracket-accessory assembly, where the finite element method is coupled with order reduction approaches. The rotor-bearing subsystem is formulated using Timoshenko beam elements, rigid discs, and Hertzian contact bearings. In contrast, the casing-bracket-accessory structure is represented through a combined scheme that incorporates shell, beam, and solid elements. The proposed hybrid modeling method and dimensionality reduction method reduce the model’s degrees of freedom from 33,477 to 30, while maintaining a relative error of less than 5 %. Compared with the full-order model, the computational efficiency is improved by more than 99 %. By adopting a specific assembly methodology, a complete aero-engine model is constructed. The inherent dynamics of this model are investigated via Campbell diagrams and amplitude-frequency responses. The study demonstrates that the critical speeds of the complete engine system decrease markedly relative to those predicted for the rotor-only model without casing. Incorporating nonlinear bearing effects introduces various harmonic and coupled frequency components in the spectrum, resulting in complex subharmonic and combination frequency behaviors. Moreover, when bearing seat looseness occurs between the rotor and casing, the vertical component at 0 Hz becomes the dominant spectral feature, serving as a diagnostic indicator of looseness faults. The influence of this fault changes with rotational speed, propagates outward from the defect location to the casing and accessory supports, and gradually diminishes along the path. Experimental validation using a rotor test bench confirms the reliability of the proposed methodology. Overall, the presented approach offers practical insights for modeling and fault identification in complete aero-engine systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.
