Authors: Yuan, S.; Zhao, J.; Li, Z.; Han, G.; Zhao, S.; Lu, X.; Zhang, Y.
Author full names: Yuan, Shilong (59141954800); Zhao, Jiangbin (59259397200); Li, Ziliang (57199059469); Han, Ge (56193479600); Zhao, Shengfeng (35217188600); Lu, Xingen (59939737800); Zhang, Yanfeng (57214254845)
Title: Investigation of loss mechanisms inside ultra-highly loaded transonic tandem compressors with different critical design parameters
Abstract: The pursuit of higher aircraft service ceilings and improved engine thrust-to-weight ratio is continually elevating compressor loading to increasingly demanding levels. Tandem blades have shown the potential to further enhance the performance of highly-loaded compressors. However, the energy loss mechanisms in highly-loaded tandem rotors remain inadequately understood. This study employed numerical simulations to investigate the effects of critical design parameters on the internal flow behavior and energy loss mechanisms in ultra-highly loaded tandem rotors. The types and sources of energy losses in tandem rotors were identified by using a sub-regional loss analysis method. Furthermore, the correlations between various flow structures and energy loss mechanisms in tandem rotors were established. An increase in boundary layer loss is commonly observed in all tandem rotor configurations owing to large regions with a high velocity gradient. The ability of the gap jet to improve the flow behavior and evolution of secondary flow structures downstream are decisive factors affecting the secondary flow loss. The main flow loss is not only strongly correlated with the secondary flow loss, but is also influenced by multiple factors such as passage blockage, flow redistribution, and behavior of the gap jet. The mechanisms underlying the effects of the axial overlap and percent pitch on the tandem rotor performance were clarified. The results indicate that improved aerodynamic performance can be achieved with a zero or slightly negative axial overlap and a relatively smaller percent pitch. The findings of this study provide valuable theoretical guidance for the design and application of ultra-highly loaded tandem rotors. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ullah, R.; Bibi, S.; Asghar, Z.; Shah, A.
Author full names: Ullah, Rizwan (60212310700); Bibi, Samavia (60212271500); Asghar, Zaheer (16311844200); Shah, Ajmal (36460292900)
Title: Multi-objective optimization of parametric airfoil shape using NSGA-II and PSO metaheuristic algorithms
Abstract: This paper presents a multi-objective aerodynamic optimization framework for airfoil shapes using Non-Dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). The design variables comprise eight Class-Shape Transformation (CST) parametric coefficients, ensuring smooth, physically realistic airfoil profiles. The optimization simultaneously maximizes lift-to-drag ratio (C<inf>l</inf> /C<inf>d</inf>) while constraining pitching moment coefficient (C<inf>m</inf>) to approximately -0.10 for longitudinal stability. Aerodynamic evaluation employs XFOIL via AeroSandbox at Reynolds number 1 × 10^6 and 5° angle of attack. The framework introduces three innovations: (1) generation-dependent perturbation strategy with graduated variance reduction balancing exploration and exploitation, (2) intelligent seeding near baseline NACA 4412 for accelerated convergence, and (3) real-time geometric validity checks enforcing manufacturability constraints including minimum thickness (≥6% chord) and leading-edge radius (≥0.01 chord). Both algorithms converged to similar optimal designs, achieving C<inf>l</inf> /C<inf>d</inf> ratios of 123–124, representing 38% improvement over baseline NACA 4412 (C<inf>l</inf> /C<inf>d</inf>≈ 85–90). CFD validation using ANSYS Fluent with Transition SST (4-equation) and SST k-ω turbulence model on structured C-grid mesh (171,000 elements) confirmed results, showing C<inf>l</inf> /C<inf>d</inf>=118 for PSO-optimized airfoil, only 4% lower than XFOIL predictions. PSO demonstrated faster convergence within 200–300 iterations, while NSGA-II provided diverse Pareto fronts after 1000–1200 evaluations, offering design flexibility. The developed Python framework features modular architecture and real-time visualization, providing an extensible platform for aerodynamic shape optimization applicable to wind turbines, UAVs, and aircraft wings. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Felgueiras Luís, D.; Viladegut, A.; Chazot, O.; Camps, A.
Author full names: Felgueiras Luís, Diana (60175385900); Viladegut, Alan (57148222800); Chazot, Olivier (15520728600); Camps, Adriano J. (7005018006)
Title: Radio wave propagation through a characterized CO2 plasma flow
Abstract: The high levels of ionization of the plasma layer created around a spacecraft during the reentry in planetary atmospheres can cause disruption of the communications, leading to a radio blackout phenomenon. The entry, descending and landing phase of a Mars mission includes degradation and complete loss of the signal for a few minutes. This work, as part of the Horizon 2020 Magnetohydrodynamics Enhanced Entry System for Space Transportation (MEESST) project, presents experimental measurements of radio signal propagation for the first time through a stagnant flow of a CO<inf>2</inf> plasma, representative of Mars entry flows. The measurements are conducted at the VKI plasma wind tunnel, the Plasmatron facility, using conical horn antennas at the K<inf>a</inf>-band, transmitting inside an optimally designed probe. The probe designed at VKI and its characterization at the UPC anechoic chamber are detailed. The temperature of the plasma flow is measured by means of optical emission spectroscopy, to estimate the plasma frequency and to correlate it with the experimental signal propagation results. The measurements at the plasma wind tunnel show that the signal propagates almost undisturbed for low electric powers (and plasma frequencies), being its magnitude attenuated and its polarization rotated at higher electric powers, when the electron number densities are higher. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhou, Y.; Wang, Y.; Wang, J.; Pan, Y.; Xie, Z.; Guo, L.
Title: Optimization of scavenge performance in aero-engine bearing chamber structures
Abstract: The scavenge performance of the bearing chamber has emerged as a critical factor in assessing the reliability of the lubrication system and the aero-engine. This study focuses on the issue of oil residence within the bearing chamber, investigating the optimization of the scavenge structure. Compared to the optimized structure characterized by ramp, groove, and cover, the ramp deep-groove equipped with a cover scavenge structure integrates the advantages of each configuration, thereby enhancing the scavenge performance of bearing chamber. The response surface method has been employed to design the structure parameters, achieving a multi-objective optimization design. Taking scavenge efficiency and oil residence volume fraction as the objective functions, an optimization model for the scavenge structure parameters is constructed and its reliability is verified. It is found that the scavenge performance of the optimized structure is significantly improved under maximum continuous, cruise, and idle working conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wu, Q.; Quan, Q.; Tang, D.; Deng, Z.
Title: Modeling and experimental study of a directly coil-actuatedswashplate-less rotorcraft
Abstract: The coaxial rotorcraft typically achieves attitude adjustments through the swashplate’s cyclic pitch motion. However, the complex linkage system in the swashplate, along with the parasitic resistance it introduces, imposes limitations on the lightweight design and reliability of the current coaxial rotorcraft. To address these challenges, this paper introduces a simplified and compact swashplate-less approach: the directly coil-actuated rotorcraft. This rotorcraft employs coils to drive the magnet-embedded blades to deflect, thus changing the blade pitch angle. Leveraging its unique magnetic-driven characteristics, we propose a novel solution method for the blade pitch angle. This method utilizes Hall-effect sensors to sense the magnetic field intensity generated by the coil and magnet, and experimental results demonstrate that it enables high-precision real-time detection of the blade pitch angle. The core challenge of the swashplate-less rotor lies in replicating the cyclic pitch motion. This paper proposes a state feedback control method that successfully achieves the cyclic pitch motion at high rotational speed. Mechanical experiments are conducted to analyze the directly coil-actuated rotor’s characteristics of thrust-torque and tilting moment. Finally, the attitude control experiments demonstrate that the control accuracy of the roll and pitch motion can reach 0.215∘. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jang, S.; Kim, C.; Nam, H.; Kim, D.; Kim, D.; Lee, K.; Kim, K.H.
Author full names: Jang, Sangsoo (59003031200); Kim, Chanho (56028368100); Nam, Hansol (59002903800); Kim, Dohoon (58099979700); Kim, Deokgeon (60084546200); Lee, Kyungtaek (60211345800); Kim, Kyuhong (27170064800)
Title: Demand-driven standardization for multirotor UAVs
Abstract: This study proposes a novel approach to standardizing multirotor Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) by aligning technical specifications with real-world market demands using an optimization framework. Multirotor UAVs are widely utilized across various sectors due to their aerial capabilities, affordability, and superior flight stability. However, despite their extensive adoption, efforts toward standardization remain limited. Existing standards based on Maximum TakeOff Weight (MTOW) primarily define classification boundaries, making them empirical and insufficient for selecting a standard UAV. To address these limitations, this research analyzes market data, particularly bid announcements from non-military procurement in South Korea over a five-year period (2019 to 2023). During this time, demand for multirotor UAVs was highest in agricultural applications, followed by educational, industrial, filming, research, emergency, and other sectors. The multi-objective optimization framework, Conceptual Layout Optimization for Universal Drone Systems (CLOUDS), was employed to extract precise requirements from Requests For Proposals (RFPs), while the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) clustering algorithm was used to categorize UAVs. The process identified potential enhancements in UAV specifications that surpass RFP requirements. A representative UAV was selected for each cluster, demonstrating efficient and effective mission performance. Furthermore, the influence of incorporating an optimal design stage in the standardization process was evaluated by comparing results with and without this stage. This study represents a pioneering effort in UAV standardization, offering objective and consistent outcomes. As the first study to address technical standardization in this context, it holds significant potential to enhance interoperability and efficiency within the UAV industrial ecosystem. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yue, H.; Gao, Z.; Liu, P.; Wang, Z.; Li, J.; Shao, X.; Li, S.; Zhao, W.
Title: Transition planning and control methods for a tilt-wing VTOL UAV: From hardware design to flight validation
Abstract: Tilt-wing vertical takeoff and landing (VTOL) unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) present significant modeling and control challenges due to their complex, multi-body dynamics and severe variations in control authority during the transition phase. This paper presents a comprehensive framework—spanning hardware design, modeling, trajectory optimization, and control—to enable safe and efficient altitude-hold transitions. First, the design of a novel tilt-wing VTOL platform is detailed, upon which a high-fidelity, multi-body dynamic model is developed. Based on this model, a Dynamic Transition Corridor (DTC) is numerically generated to fully characterize the dynamically feasible flight envelope. An optimal control problem is then formulated to generate multi-objective transition trajectories guaranteed to remain within the safe confines of this pre-computed DTC. To robustly track these trajectories, a hierarchical flight control system is designed, featuring a novel decoupled scheduling strategy for smooth handover between multi-copter and fixed-wing logics. The entire integrated framework was successfully validated through high-fidelity simulations and real-world flight tests, which demonstrated a satisfactory altitude-hold capability with a maximum one-sided deviation of <3.6 m during the complete forward and backward transitions. This research provides a systematic methodology for designing and implementing complex transition maneuvers, significantly bridging the sim-to-real gap for this challenging class of aircraft. Flight videos can be found at: https://youtu.be/YTU5gD94G6g . © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wu, X.; Zhang, Z.; Chen, Z.; Guo, M.; Liu, D.; Xie, Y.
Title: Intelligent task planning method for distributed satellite system based on reinforcement learning
Abstract: A Distributed Satellite System comprises multiple satellites collaborating to accomplish space missions. This paper proposes a novel intelligent task planning method that transforms the task planning problem into a graph coloring problem, and the graph coloring strategy is trained using the DQN algorithm. Initially, a DSS task planning mathematical model is formulated, incorporating task reward, execution quality, and load balance into the design of the objective function. Subsequently, a task planning graph is constructed using satellite assets and the planning horizon, where visible window color blocks, execution window color blocks, and maneuver window color blocks are defined to transform the planning problem into a graph coloring formulation. Then, graph coloring behaviors are trained using Deep Q-Network reinforcement learning to derive an optimized planning policy. Finally, simulations confirm convergence during policy training and validate algorithm effectiveness across 10 distinct task scales. Further analysis of executable task capacity impacts on planning results concurrently demonstrates the algorithm’s robustness. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, Y.; Cao, K.; Zhou, Q.; Cui, B.; Liu, D.; Zhang, B.
Title: Economic efficiency and airport compatibility analysis of blended wing body civil aircraft configurations
Abstract: In the pursuit of sustainable development and green aviation, the Blended Wing Body (BWB) configuration has emerged as a focal point in aviation technology innovation, distinguished by its superior aerodynamic characteristics, fuel efficiency, and enhanced passenger capacity. This study systematically reviews the technical progress of both domestic and international civil aircraft projects featuring BWB configurations, with a special emphasis on the NWPU-BWB-300 scheme developed by Northwestern Polytechnical University. The study examines its economic performance and adaptability to airports, revealing that the NWPU-BWB-300 offers advantages in terms of development and manufacturing costs, exhibits superior fuel economy compared to similar models, and aligns well with market competitiveness and eco-friendly aviation trends. Further analysis confirms that this model can integrate seamlessly with existing 4E class airport infrastructure and services, including jet bridges and tanker refueling vehicles, thereby demonstrating robust airport compatibility. These findings not only validate the technical feasibility of the NWPU-BWB-300 scheme but also serve as valuable references for its potential market application. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zheng, Q.; Shi, Z.; Liu, Y.; Xie, Y.; Zhang, H.
Title: Research on fault diagnosis method of turbofan engine fuel actuator based on ST-DPO-LSTM inverse model
Abstract: The fuel inverse model can map the actual fuel flow rate based on the state of the aero-engine, and plays a critical role in the fault diagnosis of Fuel Metering Unit (FMU). The modeling accuracy of fuel inverse model affects the accuracy of fault diagnosis. However, the fuel actuator/engine is a strongly nonlinear and strongly coupling object, which restricts the accuracy of fuel inverse model. Therefore, this study proposes a fault diagnosis method for the FMU of a turbofan engine based on the ST-DBO-LSTM (Similarity Transformation- Dung Beetle Optimizer-Long Short Term Memory) fuel inverse model. Initially, we esTablelish a nonlinear mathematical model of the actuator/engine based on AMESim/Simulink Co-simulation to improve the modeling accuracy of the actuator. Then, a fuel inverse modeling method based on ST-DBO-LSTM is proposed, which uses Similarity Transformation (ST) to process data and improve the generalization performance of the inverse model. This method uses the Dung Beetle Optimizer (DBO) algorithm to optimize the Long-Short-Term-Memory (LSTM) network parameters, and an inverse model from engine state to fuel flow rate is esTablelished to improve the dynamic model accuracy of the inverse model. Finally, this work esTablelishes fault diagnosis models for FMU jamming, Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT), and other related issues. Simulation under large envelope and large states show that compared with DBO – LSTM, ST - NN and NN, ST - DBO - LSTM reduces the maximum error by 1.64 %,15.63 %, and 76.93 %, and the average error by 42.0 %, 42.8 % and 79.4 % respectively. The proposed method has a high fault detection rate, low false alarm rate, and the faster detection speed, which is superior to above methods and verifies the effectiveness of the method. It privades a reference direction for fault diagnosis of FMU. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yao, B.; Jin, R.; Shang, X.; Yao, L.; Wu, Y.; Wu, B.; Yuan, Q.; Qi, N.; Huo, M.
Title: Analytical approximation of the propulsion dynamics of magnetic sail with fixed solar incidence angle
Abstract: Magnetic sails are a highly promising propellantless propulsion method for interplanetary spaceflight. This study investigated the approximate analytical solution for the two-dimensional equations of motion of a magnetic sail, employing the latest single-loop magnetic sail thrust model with a fixed solar incidence angle. Based on linear perturbation theory (neglecting higher-order perturbations), an analytical expression for the heliocentric transfer orbit of a magnetic sail was derived. A trajectory obtained via numerical integration was used as the reference for assessing the proposed method’s level of approximation precision. A simulation probed the effects of varying the magnetic sail coil radius and the parameters of the initial parking orbit, and the results indicated that the proposed analytical method demonstrates excellent approximation accuracy for small-scale magnetic sails and general elliptical orbits. During the early stages of the mission, the approximate relative error was as low as 10−4, and the computation time was only 1 % of that required by the numerical integration method. Furthermore, to address long-duration missions and large-scale magnetic sails, the analytical solution was corrected by updating the orbital parameters at each step of the approximation process. This method achieved an approximation accuracy that was 100 times more precise than that of the uncorrected analytical solution. Even for a long-duration mission spanning 30 orbits, the corrected analytical solution maintained an accuracy on the order of 10−5. The approximation method proposed in this study is a valuable tool for the preliminary design and optimization of trajectories in deep space missions employing magnetic sails, particularly for long-duration missions and large-scale transfer scenarios. © © 2025. Author full names: Wang, Dingding (57925273000); Zheng, Longxi (55428897100); Yang, Yudong (59168549500); Wang, Bowen (59134843500)
Title: Experimental study on the effect of fuel supply methods on pulse detonation combustor performance
Abstract: Pulse Detonation Combustors (PDCs) represent a transformative propulsion technology capable of achieving substantial thermodynamic efficiency gains through constant-volume combustion and self-pressurization. However, conventional adaptive fuel supply methods remain fundamentally limited by their passive coupling with chamber dynamics, resulting in uncontrolled injection during critical phases such as deflagration-to-detonation transition (DDT) and exhaust. To overcome these limitations, this study introduces an actively controlled low-pressure intermittent fuel supply system, systematically examining its performance relative to conventional adaptive fueling in a gasoline/air U-bend PDC. Experimental results confirm stable detonation across 10–30 Hz under both strategies, yet the intermittent method significantly shortens the DDT distance from 733 mm to 616 mm with increasing frequency. Moreover, it enables precise temporal fuel management, yielding an 8–14 % reduction in fuel consumption while maintaining detonation stability. A notable innovative finding is the consistently shorter and stable blue flame observed at the combustor outlet under intermittent supply, indicating more complete combustion and enhanced energy release—a direct result of suppressed over-rich mixture formation during non-optimal cycles. These results underscore the critical role of active injection synchronization in detonation cycle optimization and provide a theoretical basis for advanced control strategies in next-generation PDCs. © © 2025. Authors: Yang, F.; Kong, C.; Liu, J.; Lv, C.; Wang, Z.; Chang, J.
Title: Deep reinforcement learning-based optimization of sparse sensor placement for supersonic isolator flowfield monitoring
Abstract: Neural networks are typically employed to establish a mapping relationship between local observational quantities and the entire flowfield, thereby enabling flowfield prediction. In supersonic inlets, the optimization of sparse sensor placement involves whether the flowfield prediction model (FPM) can accurately perceive the flowfield using the minimal number of sensors. In this study, deep reinforcement learning (DRL) is used to optimize the equidistantly arranged sensors on the wall of a supersonic isolator. A neural network model that takes wall pressure sensors data as input and produces schlieren images of the flowfield as output serves as the FPM, which also functions as the environment in DRL. The agent, based on the Actor-Critic model, explores in this environment with goal of minimizing the number of sensors while maintaining accurate flowfield prediction. After each interaction, the FPM demonstrates excellent performance in the test set with the agent-explored scheme serving as input. Using the effective sensor scheme provided by DRL, among the structure similarity index measure of all samples in the test set between predicted and real flowfield schlieren images, both the mean and median are no <0.75, and the lower bound is no <0.65. These schemes generate a series of flowfield schlieren images with the assistance of the FPM. The images are then processed by the YOLO model, a target detection method, to determing shock train leading edge (STLE) locations. Based on the tracking results of the STLE, a better scheme can be further selected. Through the analysis of sensors data and STLE locations, it can be concluded that the sensors selected by DRL and located near the STLE exhibit broadband and high-frequency oscillation characteristics. This characteristic facilitates the neural networks in establishing a relationship between the sensors and the full flowfield. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, S.; Chen, R.; Zhu, M.; Wang, S.; XiaogangDeng
Author full names: Wang, Shengye (57191221827); Chen, Rong (59331298100); Zhu, Mengyuan (60052463900); Wang, Shunshun (57887381500); XiaogangDeng (60209853000)
Title: A simplified Reynolds-stress model based on moving equilibrium relation and symbolic regression training
Abstract: The Reynolds stress model (RSM), considered a higher-level Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) turbulence model, typically outperforms the eddy-viscosity model (EVM) in simulating complex flows. However, constrained by the limitations of the traditional RANS framework, the RSM still exhibits deficiencies in predicting streamwise separation flows, resulting in an over-prediction of recirculation length and irregular streamline patterns near reattachment points. Given that both are related to Reynolds shear stress transport equations, this study proposes a data-driven algebraic expression for Reynolds shear stresses based on moving equilibrium relation and symbolic regression (SR) training. Furthermore, a novel turbulence model (four-equation model) is derived by coupling the algebraic Reynolds shear stress relation with three Reynolds normal stress transport equations and a scale-providing equation, and the so-called semi-differential Reynolds stress model (Semi-RSM). Validation across two-dimensional periodic hill, iced airfoil, and three-dimensional hill cases demonstrates that the trained SR expression is effective and interpretable. Based on this, the Semi-RSM successfully predicts recirculation length and resolves irregular streamline defects. On the other hand, tests on delta wing and wing-body configurations confirm that the Semi-RSM retains the advantages of the original RSM in terms of swirl and corner flows. Meanwhile, some results show an improvement, indicating the favorable generalization of the SR expression. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Author full names: Pattanayak, Tavish (59243025300); Gautier, Raphaël H. (57192229628); Mavris, Dimitri N. (57201567978)
Title: Hybrid-electric Turboprop Performance under Battery Degradation: An Uncertainty Quantification Study
Abstract: Hybrid-electric propulsion (HEP) has been identified as a key technology for reducing aviation emissions in the short term. However, battery degradation over time introduces uncertainty in performance, particularly as batteries approach replacement thresholds. This study evaluates the benefits of hybridization under a worst-case scenario - when battery-specific energy is at 80 % of its nominal value, the widely accepted threshold for battery replacement. Using uncertainty quantification methodologies, the impact of reduced battery performance on fuel savings, powertrain architecture, and operational feasibility for a 50-passenger regional short takeoff and landing turboprop aircraft is assessed. Additionally, a sensitivity analysis is performed to identify the most influential constraints and design variables affecting performance. Results indicate that even at this reduced battery performance, hybridization offers significant fuel savings compared to a conventional baseline. The findings inform strategies for the viability of hybrid-electric turboprop architecture under degraded conditions. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Brouwer, K.R.; Perez, R.A.; Riley, Z.B.; Beberniss, T.J.; Spottswood, S.M.
Author full names: Brouwer, Kirk R. (56732618000); Perez, Ricardo A. (36017332500); Riley, Zachary B. (55805518700); Beberniss, Timothy J. (16202016600); Spottswood, Stephen Michael (6604036778)
Title: Measurements of a thermally buckled panel dynamically excited by a separated shock/boundary-layer interaction
Abstract: Experiments were conducted to investigate the effects of a separated shock/boundary layer interaction (SBLI) on the aeroelastic behavior of a thin, thermally buckled panel in turbulent flow. A suite of traditional sensors and non-contact measurement techniques, including high-speed, three-dimensional stereo digital image correlation (DIC), self-aligned focusing Schlieren (SAFS), and infrared (IR) imaging, were used to characterize the operating conditions and structural response. The impact of the temperature differential between the panel and frame as well as cavity pressure on the panel response were explored. For a majority of the operating conditions, measured time histories of the panel dynamics revealed small-amplitude, forced oscillations about a stationary deformation. The oscillations were due to the pressure fluctuations from the turbulent boundary layer and separated SBLI, whereas the stationary deformation was due to the time-averaged SBLI loading. Frequency shifting of the dominant peaks in the strain and deformation power spectral densities was also observed to occur for these conditions with variation in the static pressure and temperature differentials. However, for cavity pressure and temperature differential ranges of approximately [90, 93] kPa and [15, 20] K , respectively, the response transitioned to cross-well oscillations which were captured using simultaneous SAFS, DIC, and IR. The dynamic response was centered about the panel midpoint, coincident with the location of the shock-induced separation on the panel. As the temperature differential decreased, the oscillations evolved from intermittent to persistent snap-through. This behavior was associated with a significant increase in the magnitude of the power spectral density for lower frequencies indicating the onset of aperiodic behavior. While the snap-through motion did not always subside during the test window, the preferred equilibrium state was observed to shift as the panel approached and crossed the local buckling boundary. © 2025 .

Author full names: Li, Yuxuan (59071734300); Liu, Weihang (59988627300); Sun, Chengbao (57817538300); Cui, Miao (15064943200); Ren, Jiawen (59987664000); Nikolaitchik, Mikhail A. (57208760187)
Title: A physics-informed LSTM framework for solving fluid flow and heat transfer coupling problem of the turbine vane as well as the internal cooling optimization
Abstract: The investigation of a turbine vane’s cooling performance is essential for improving the efficiency and lifespan of gas turbines, which involves in solving the fluid flow and heat transfer coupling problem of the turbine vane. Up to now, numerical simulation serves as a valuable tool, offering high accuracy but requiring significant computational time when evaluating various cooling conditions. To improve the computational efficiency, this paper introduces a novel framework combining the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) with Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN), termed LS-PINN, to solve the fluid flow and heat transfer coupling problem of the turbine vane. The LS-PINN framework demonstrates superior performance to conventional data-driven methods by embedding physical principles in the learning process, thereby boosting accuracy while providing more explainable outcomes. Meanwhile, the LS-PINN framework facilitates rapid multi-physics field predictions with diverse cooling conditions and scarce data through transfer learning. The transfer learning-enhanced LS-PINN framework maintains prediction accuracy equivalent to the base model while reducing computational time by 75%. Furthermore, the influence of temperature, mass flow rate and turbulence intensity of the inlet coolant on cooling efficiency was studied based on the LS-PINN framework, and the optimized temperature distribution on the vane surface was successfully obtained. This work would provide valuable reference for the optimization design of turbine vane cooling, which has potentials in engineering applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, G.; Zhou, L.; Zhu, J.; Lan, P.C.; He, X.; Xiong, J.; Jin, L.
Title: Enhanced attention-mechanism-driven broad learning system for aircraft engine remaining useful life prediction
Abstract: Accurate prediction of Remaining Useful Life (RUL) for aircraft engines is critical to ensuring flight safety and optimizing maintenance strategies. To address the limitations of conventional self-attention mechanisms in existing Broad Learning System (BLS)-based approaches—specifically their limited ability to capture localized degradation features—this study proposes an enhanced BLS framework augmented with a multi-scale convolutional self-attention mechanism. The proposed method integrates multi-scale convolutional layers within the self-attention module to jointly capture global dependencies and local contextual features from engine sensor data, and incorporates a squeeze-and-excitation (SE) module to adaptively recalibrate channel-wise feature importance, emphasizing degradation-sensitive patterns. The refined features are then propagated to the BLS for efficient RUL regression. Experimental results on NASA's C-MAPSS dataset demonstrate that our method outperforms conventional BLS approaches, achieving a 45% reduction in Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and a 70% improvement in Prognostic Score. These significant advancements provide a novel paradigm for prognostics and health management (PHM) in aerospace engineering, effectively balancing computational efficiency with cutting-edge predictive performance to significantly enhance maintenance decision-making. © © 2025. Authors: Tadashi Katsuno, E.; Tödter, S.; Peters, A.; El Moctar, O.
Author full names: Tadashi Katsuno, Eduardo (60209250900); Tödter, Simon (57221384155); Peters, Andreas (57075630700); El Moctar, Ould (57203417194)
Title: Helicopter ditching in calm waters and waves: Simulation and validation
Abstract: Helicopter ditching is a critical emergency maneuver that may be required during offshore operations, which are essential for the safe transport of personnel and equipment to remote structures such as wind turbines and oil platforms. Understanding the dynamics of slamming, the impact of the aircraft on water, is fundamental to enhancing safety measures for personnel and maintaining structural integrity. Existing literature primarily focuses on the ditching of fixed-wing aircraft, while studies on helicopter ditching remain limited, particularly regarding realistic wave conditions and the accurate representation of helicopter geometry, including the emergency flotation system. This paper examines helicopter ditching using Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations. First, Verification and Validation of the numerical model are conducted by comparing experiments with flat plate slamming simulations and model-scale ditching-induced helicopter motion with forced descent, achieving close agreement in peak pressures and impulse calculations. Next, full-scale free-ditching simulations incorporate six degrees of freedom and rigid-body fluid-structure interactions to analyze different initial orientations (roll, pitch, and yaw angles), mass distributions, and autorotation effects. Finally, helicopter ditching in wave conditions is assessed: using a fifth-order regular wave model that represents rough sea states, simulations evaluate how wave crest, middle, and trough landings affect helicopter trajectory and loads. This study improves the understanding of helicopter ditching dynamics by integrating six degrees of freedom and evaluating performance in both flat and wave conditions. The findings contribute to improving safety standards and operational guidelines in offshore helicopter operations. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Li, Y.; Chen, Z.; Sun, S.; Hu, J.; Zhang, W.; Sun, J.
Title: Characteristics of the aerodynamic behavior and vortex evolution of a three-dimensional airfoil in dynamic proximity to the free surface
Abstract: This study investigates the characteristic of an airfoil in dynamic water effect. A numerical model of a three-dimensional airfoil in dynamic proximity to the water surface was developed using the overset mesh strategy combined with the volume of fluid method. Simulations were conducted at a constant angle of attack of 10° across three sinking velocities. A systematic analysis was performed on the aerodynamic forces, tunnel flow dynamics, vortex evolution, and free surface elevation. The results demonstrate that the lift coefficient of the windward surface increases as the sinking velocity enlarges. The effective ride height is decreased and the saddle point moves towards the leading edge due to the interaction between the free surface and separated downwash airflow. Furthermore, the wingtip vortex moves upward and inward as the sinking velocity increases. Additionally, the free surface amplifies the outward dispersion of the wingtip vortex and contributes to an increase in lift-induced drag. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jiang, Y.; Wang, X.; Li, H.; Huang, Y.; Xie, G.; Zhou, Z.
Title: Large eddy simulations and experimental study for the effect of hole exit filleted corners on film cooling effectiveness
Abstract: The inherent manufacturing-induced filleted corner at hole exits may reduce cooling margins and elevate turbine blade ablation risks. This study employs an integrated approach combining PSP and LES to quantify the effect of fillet radii and blowing ratios on cooling performance, thereby providing data support for evaluating real engineered cooling performance of designed holes. 210 cylindrical holes by EDM and 270 holes by AMT were processed to obtain the true and reliable filleted corner parameters. Through industrial CT scanning, non-constant radius curvature profiles of hole exits were obtained. Thus, the leading-edge fillet radius was adopted as the representative parameter and was quantified via high-precision image contour recognition technology. Based on statistical sampling method, six representative dimensionless fillet radii r / D (the fillet radius-to-hole-diameter ratio) was adopted as 5 %, 15 %, 25 %, 35 %, 45 %, 55 %. Results show that all holes exhibited progressive degradation of film cooling performance with the increasing blowing ratio. The r / D = 15 % configuration performed the maximum deterioration (68.2 %) at M = 0.5 - 1.25, while a 62.9 % reduction of baseline cylindrical hole. Besides, the film cooling effectiveness at M = 0.5 - 1.25 followed a non-monotonic (decreasing followed by increasing) trend as the fillet radius ratio ranging from 5 % to 55 %. Film holes with filleted corner exits generally exhibited reduced film cooling effectiveness compared to the cylindrical hole, showing a maximum reduction of 19.81 % at M = 1.0, though achieving a maximum enhancement of 26.22 % at M = 1.25. Notably, excessive hole exit fillet corners should be avoided to prevent hole leading-edge ablation. During the optimization design or manufacturing process of film cooling holes, the exit fillet radius should be constrained to no >5 % of the film hole diameter to improve film cooling effectiveness without the risk of ablation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ge, Y.; Wang, X.; Wang, Q.
Author full names: Ge, Yuyang (57411934100); Wang, Xiaoliang (36614724000); Wang, Quanbao (55544144900)
Title: Effects of arrangement-differentiated ballonet gas sloshing on airship stability and modes
Abstract: Airships rely on buoyancy to generate lift, necessitating the arrangement of large-volume ballonets. However, during ascent and descent, the sloshing of gas can significantly affect the airship's motion. This paper incorporates a multi-ballonet sloshing model into the airship's motion, yielding a sloshing-motion coupled model that enhances the fidelity of the airship's flight dynamics modeling. The considerable volume of gas within the airship is represented by spring-mass-dashpot systems, with the state information of the moving masses introduced as new state variables and integrated into the airship's linearized motion equations. Based on this model, the influence of sloshing on the dynamic stability characteristics of airships is investigated. The results reveal that sloshing has a pronounced effect on the Pendulum Oscillation Mode of airships. Reducing the number of ballonets may further compromise overall stability. Therefore, the use of at least four, and preferably more, independent ballonets is necessary—particularly for conventional airships with a slenderness ratio of approximately 4. This evaluation provides valuable recommendations for the appropriate ballonet arrangements for various airship configurations. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, Y.; Lu, G.; Zhang, K.; Zhou, P.; Meng, J.
Title: Cross-dimensional aircraft thermal protection performance control optimization based on a supercritical CO2 porous transpiration cooling system with the non-smooth fin groove design
Abstract: Owing to the limited coolant carried by aircraft, high-efficient heat transfer with less coolant Nconsumption loss is highly desired for the surface cooling. To cope with it, a novel nonlinear cross-dimensional control optimization method combining model-free predictive control and Tornado optimization algorithm is proposed to exploit the optimal porous surface structure with non-smooth fin groove and design the optimal coolant control strategy for the performance enhancement of the supercritical CO<inf>2</inf> porous transpiration cooling system. This method realizes the optimal porous transpiration cooling surface structure with minimum coolant consumption and the optimal cooling performance and control performance. In this work, the supercritical CO<inf>2</inf> porous transpiration cooling (SCPTC) system is first developed with preliminary mechanism analysis. Secondly, four typical surface structure schemes are further compared and discussed, the nonlinear empirical formulas for surface temperature of different structures are also developed. Finally, a model-free predictive control embedded cross-dimensional optimization method is implemented, in which the cooling performance, control performance, and coolant consumption are taken as the three contradictory objective function, the shape, number, and size of the contact surface grooves are defined as optimization variables. After optimization, a proper balanced optimal design scheme is achieved considering three crucial performance indicators. The main conclusions cover: The different designs of contact surface grooves can enhance the SCPTC system cooling performance. Through iterative optimization, better cooling and control performance, as well as lower coolant consumption of the SCPTC system are obtained. Concretely, the optimal design achieves the enhances of 6.04 % and 39.24 % in cooling and control performance, decrease of 61.34 % in coolant consumption, compared to the initial design scheme. This study highlights the application of cross-dimensional control optimization in transpiration cooling-based aircraft thermal protection enhancement: Optimal cooling performance at the cost of minimal coolant consumption. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, H.; Wang, C.; Hou, Z.; Lu, T.; Zheng, C.
Author full names: Li, Huipeng (58857465700); Wang, Congqing (55735865200); Hou, Zhiyao (60208793300); Lu, Tianhao (60208816200); Zheng, Chuanyao (60208859600)
Title: Few-shot aircraft skin defect segmentation via CLIP-guided prior masks and hierarchical purification decoder
Abstract: Skin defect detection is critical to aircraft safety and maintenance, as its accuracy directly impacts flight safety and aircraft lifespan. Traditional automated detection methods depend on substantial labeled data. However, the scarcity of real defect samples, high collection costs, and lack of diversity easily lead to model overfitting, which is difficult to meet the engineering needs. To address this problem, this paper proposes CHPDNet, enabling accurate defect segmentation with just one labeled sample. Firstly, the feature masking enhancement module (FMEM) is introduced to alleviate the loss of fine-grained spatial information and improve the segmentation accuracy for small defects by dual-channel mask complementation. Moreover, we generate reliable priori masks based on CLIP and use the optimized correlation matrix to enhance the mask quality, which enhances the model's adaptability and robustness to skin defects. In addition, the hierarchical purification decoder (HPD) is designed to utilize a progressive strategy from coarse to fine segmentation to reduce the missed and false detections. Extensive comparative experiments demonstrate that CHPDNet achieves superior performance on both ASD-2 i and MVTec-5 i datasets under 1-shot settings, with mIoU scores of 60.13 % and 62.31 %, respectively. The code is available at: https://github.com/lhpzfsc/CHPDNet . © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, Z.; Sun, Y.; Gu, Q.
Title: Experimental investigation of the effect of concave endwall profiling on the aerodynamic performance of a transonic highly-loaded turbine cascade
Abstract: Endwall profiling is an effective technique for improving the aerodynamic performance of turbines. Although many traditional endwall parameterization methods have been used to date, this technique still requires further update and development. This study conducts optimization design on the endwall of a highly-loaded turbine cascade with a large turning angle based on an in-house concave pit endwall optimization profiling platform. The parameterized endwall profiling is realized through a quartic concave curve function, and two types of endwalls are investigated through experimental methods. The experimental study is carried out under design conditions, variable Mach number conditions, and variable incidence angle conditions, with the aerodynamic data at the outlet section measured in each case. Meanwhile, numerical calculations are performed to complement the experimental findings and to provide additional insight into the flow field, and the numerical calculation results show good agreement with the experimental results in terms of loss and flow angle. Results indicate that under the design outlet Mach number condition, the total pressure loss of the optimized endwall (EW) is reduced by 17.22 %. Moreover, it also exhibits strong adaptability under variable Mach number and variable incidence angle conditions, with the total pressure loss reduced by 18.17 %. Additionally, the causes of high-loss regions and airflow over-deflection near the hub and shroud, as well as the changes in aerodynamic parameters in the cascade passage and on the blade surface, are analyzed. Through comparison, it is found that the reduction of total pressure loss is mainly attributed to the weakening of horseshoe vortices in the passage, the reduction of shock wave intensity, and the radial migration of passage vortices after endwall profiling. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, A.; Yang, D.; Tian, L.
Title: Experimental investigation on the effect of high-frequency discharge on the unsteady characteristics of cavity flow
Abstract: In this paper, we conducted a study on the effect of high-frequency discharge on the cavity flow in a Mach 2.5 wind tunnel, focusing on examining the transition of unsteady characteristics. The actuator is driven by a high-frequency power supply to operate at discharge frequencies of 5, 10, 15, and 20 kHz, while the cavity flow with and without excitation is visualized by time-resolved schlieren imaging at a sampling frequency of 50 kHz. Statistical analysis based on the schlieren image dataset elucidates the effect of discharge on the unsteady characteristics of the dynamic flow as well as the response features of the flow to discharge. The results indicate that high-frequency discharge exhibits different control effects and mechanisms on the shear layer and shock wave. On the one hand, high-frequency discharge promotes an increase in the fluctuating thickness of the shear layer by modifying the velocity profile, with the most pronounced increment reaching 40 % in the cavity midsection at 20 kHz. Attributed to the thicker shear layer, the pressure inside the cavity also reaches a maximum rise of 3.1 % at 20 kHz. On the other hand, the impact intensity dampens with the elevation of frequency, due to the larger average power of thermal bubbles produced by the actuator. In addition, the energy deposition significantly alters the distribution of unsteady modes, facilitates the Kelvin-Helmholtz instability and vorticity in the shear layer, and regulates the dominant frequency of unsteady modes to the discharge frequency. High-frequency discharge diminishes the low-frequency oscillation of the impact shock and amplifies the amplitude of the low-frequency oscillation in the shear layer. Concurrently, the flow field’s response to discharge can be captured at each frequency, whereas the capacity of different structures to respond to different discharge frequencies varies. Within the confines of this study, the optimal responsiveness of the shear layer and impact shock is observed at 10 kHz and 15 kHz, respectively. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhao(赵元褀), Y.; Yang(杨琦), Q.; Li(李德友), D.; Fu(付晓龙), X.
Title: Aerodynamic interference of mars coaxial rotors at low reynolds in transonic wind-sand flow
Abstract: The aerial exploration of Mars requires a rotorcraft to operate within a dust-laden atmosphere. However, the mechanisms governing flight stability under such extreme conditions remain poorly understood. In this study, a high-fidelity approach that couples delayed detached eddy simulation with a discrete phase model is used to show that dust particles induce a previously unrecognized and critical failure mode. Dust-vortex coupling actively suppresses high-frequency turbulence and drives powerful, low-frequency oscillations that compromise the entire system. This phenomenon manifests as severe torque and lift instabilities, with pressure fluctuations on blade surfaces increasing dramatically, indicating extreme unsteady loading. The analysis reveals that this instability is not a secondary effect but a primary consequence of prolonged particle retention within rotor-induced recirculation zones. This discovery reveals a critical design principle for future Martian aerial vehicles: shifting the focus from simple erosion protection to actively mitigating dust-induced instabilities to ensure mission safety. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, W.; Shi, J.; Wang, M.
Title: Design-for-demise-oriented modeling and testing of carbon fiber reinforced composites for safe satellite re-entry
Abstract: Carbon fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP), recognized for its potential to undergo complete degradation during atmospheric re-entry, serves as an ideal candidate for aerospace Design-for-Demise (D4D) strategies. However, uncontrolled thermal degradation, arising from multi-physics interactions involving heat transfer, mass loss kinetics, and pore evolution, compromises re-entry safety and space-debris mitigation. Critically, these interactions are inherently nonlinear, leading to pyrolysis mechanisms that cannot be deduced from the simple sum of individual effects. To address the poorly characterized pyrolysis behavior of CFRP, we developed a one-dimensional model that integrates porosity evolution and temperature-dependent thermophysical properties. Through validation against cone calorimetry experiments for 4.5mm and 10mm CFRP specimens, we reveal a two-stage pyrolysis process with distinct transitions at approximately 642 K and 712 K. Our results demonstrate that dynamic pyrolysis layer evolution and competing decomposition pathways yield multi-peak mass loss rate (MLR) profiles, with local MLR peak occurring when degradation fronts fully penetrate the sample thickness. By comparing the profiles of MLR across specimens of varying thicknesses, we show that increased thickness amplifies localized pyrolysis effects, altering local MLR variations. Besides, using global sensitivity analysis, we quantify how input parameters influence pyrolysis dynamics through individual effects and synergistic interactions. These findings explain the pyrolysis mechanism of CFRP under high-temperature conditions, and may provide valuable insights for the rational design and optimization of CFRP composites for D4D applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhao, J.; Dong, X.; Yuan, S.; Sheng, X.; Li, Z.; Han, G.; Lu, X.
Title: Thermodynamic characteristics and stability enhancement mechanisms of circumferential groove in transonic fans at varying Reynolds numbers
Abstract: This study investigates the influence of a single circumferential groove on the thermodynamic performance and stall margin of a transonic fan rotor across a range of Reynolds numbers ( Re ), using a validated numerical method. Results indicate that the optimal groove placement depends on Re and is tied to distinct flow mechanisms. At a high Re of 455,000, interaction between the tip leakage vortex (TLV) and the shock causes vortex breakdown and tip blockage, which triggers stall. Placing the groove to encompass this blockage region simultaneously reduces boundary-layer and secondary-flow losses and improves near-design efficiency. This configuration achieves optimal stall margin improvement by suppressing TLV development. By contrast, at a low Re of 34,000, stall originates from suction-side flow separation induced by the radial transport flow and from forward spillage of the tip leakage flow. In this regime, the optimal groove location aligns with zones of TLV initiation or with regions of radial transport vortex activity. Although implementing the groove increases boundary-layer losses and decreases secondary-flow losses—resulting in a negligible net efficiency change near the design point—it significantly expands the stable operating range by mitigating the interaction between the radial transport flow and the tip leakage flow. These findings offer crucial insights for Re –adaptive groove optimization in transonic rotors, advancing aerodynamic performance for high-altitude, low- Re aerospace applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Huang, Z.; Li, X.; Chen, J.; Hu, T.; Wang, J.; Li, J.; Hu, X.
Title: Analysis of vortex structures and flow losses in a supersonic expander-rotor
Abstract: This study employs numerical methods to analyze the internal flow structures and the mechanisms contributing to losses in a supersonic expander-rotor under typical operating conditions. The findings reveal that the tip leakage vortex is the predominant vortex structure, the leakage vortex core originates from the airflow at the leading edge, with the airflow from the mid-rear section, adjacent to the wall, and the suction side, circulating around the leakage vortex core, which fosters the development and intensification of the tip leakage vortex. It was shown that the primary contributors to endwall losses include the accumulation of low-energy fluid, as well as the oblique shock wave, shock-induced corner boundary layer separation, flow recirculation, and mixing losses. It was obtained that as the increasing of clearance height, the relative motion’s shear effect on the leakage flow diminishes, the losses associated with the annular wall significantly decrease, but the tip leakage losses escalate. The primary forms of internal flow losses in the supersonic expander-rotor were shown to be due to oblique shock wave and endwall losses. Thus, it was stated out that the effective control of these oblique shock wave and endwall boundary layer is imperative for substantially enhancing the supersonic expander-rotor’s performance. The present work clarifies the vortex structures, primary loss forms, and loss mechanisms within the supersonic expander rotor. These insights represent both a prerequisite for enhancing performance and a foundation for future design. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hur, J.; Nam, J.; Kim, J.; Kim, J.; Jeong, S.; Jik Lee, B.
Author full names: Hur, Jungmu (59332580500); Nam, Junhyuk (60190223000); Kim, Jinhwi (59332800400); Kim, Jinyoung (57419880200); Jeong, Seoghyeon (60205596700); Jik Lee, Bok (60190223100)
Title: Experimental investigation of flow enthalpy effects on boundary-layer transition over a sharp cone
Abstract: Boundary-layer transition and second-mode instability over a 7° half-angle cone are investigated in a hypersonic shock tunnel under varying total enthalpy conditions, while maintaining a fixed freestream Mach number and unit Reynolds number. Surface pressure and temperature measurements, along with schlieren imaging, were used to characterize the boundary-layer development. Under all test cases, boundary-layer transition and second-mode instability were observed. A convolutional neural network-based classifier was used to categorize laminar, second-mode instability, and turbulent boundary layer on the cone. The transition region consistently began near x=200mm across all enthalpy cases, while high-enthalpy flows exhibited transition regions with reduced streamwise extent and a higher frequency of turbulent packet generation. Turbulent intermittency rises rapidly in the streamwise direction under high-enthalpy cases, but more gradually under low-enthalpy cases. The rise in surface heat flux occurred near the same streamwise location as the increase in turbulent intermittency, indicating consistency between the two diagnostics. Spectral proper orthogonal decomposition of schlieren image sequences and power spectral density analysis of surface pressure measurements confirmed the dominance of second-mode instability, with their peak frequencies increasing under higher total enthalpy cases. Based on the pressure spectra, the second-mode peak persists farther downstream in the low-enthalpy cases than in the high-enthalpy cases, indicating a delayed breakdown. This delayed breakdown suggests a broader spatial extent of potentially unstable disturbances, which may contribute to a transition region with extended streamwise extent. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wei, B.S.; Yue, X.; Dai, H.; Zheng, Z.; Dang, Z.H.; Liu, Z.C.
Author full names: Wei, Bosong (57193602613); Yue, Xiaokui (9232396000); Dai, Honghua (45561036600); Zheng, Zixuan (57217011004); Dang, Zhaohui (40761165400); Liu, Zongcheng (56275546600)
Title: Two-layer optimal collision-avoidance enclosing control for multiple satellites against an escaping target under incomplete information condition and sensor faults
Abstract: Problem of collision-free target-enclosing (TE) control by a group of weak-maneuverability service satellites (SSats) against a noncooperative target satellite (TSat) whose dynamics and escaping behavior are fully unknown is studied in this paper. A two-layer prescribed-performance optimal cooperative target-enclosing (POCT) controller is proposed. To reduce the running load and measuring requirements of the low-cost SSats, by appointing a leader satellite (LSat), a decoupling mechanism is proposed, and it is proved to equivalently divide the studied TE task into two layers, including information-incomplete target pursuit (TP) layer and collision-free configuration tracking (CT) layer. In the inner TP layer, to overcome the challenges caused by the escaping unknown TSat under sensor faults, initial-condition-free boundaries are proposed which can directly constrain the TP error to perform prescribed transient and steady-state behaviors, and then the TP controller can be constructed with only using measurement data with sensor faults. Meanwhile, in the external CT layer, by describing the collision-free CT issue as a nonlinear nonzero-sum differential game and introducing an auxiliary variable into the Nash equilibrium approximation error, a high-precision optimal enclosing CT controller with collision avoidance is designed, which guarantees an asymptotical tracking for the desired configuration. The control objectives are proved to be achieved by the proposed POCT controller. Finally, the advantages on infinite initial constraint range, whole-process performance setting achieved by the inner layer design and better CT performance achieved by the external layer design are shown through simulation results. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kou, X.; Li, W.; Ouyang, Y.; Yang, Z.; He, S.
Title: Piezoelectric adaptive control for transonic wind tunnel test models based on error feedback FxLMS algorithm
Abstract: In transonic wind tunnel tests, the model tail support system is highly susceptible to low-frequency, large-amplitude resonance due to aerodynamic loads, which seriously affects the quality of test data and restricts the design and improvement of the aircraft. A piezoelectric active vibration reduction system based on the error feedback filtered-x least mean square (EF-FxLMS) algorithm is proposed to efficiently and reliably suppress flow-induced vibrations in transonic wind tunnel models. Firstly, to address the limitation of the classical filtered-x least mean square (FxLMS) algorithm that requires prediction of the reference signal related to disturbance, the EF-FxLMS algorithm is proposed by directly using the error signal as the reference signal. This approach effectively solves the problem of real-time reference signal acquisition during wind tunnel tests. Subsequently, to meet the practical vibration reduction requirements of transonic wind tunnel models, a piezoelectric active vibration reduction structure with a driving displacement amplification mechanism is developed. Finally, a piezoelectric active vibration reduction system for transonic wind tunnel models is established using the proposed EF-FxLMS algorithm and the developed piezoelectric active vibration reduction structure, and ground test research and wind tunnel test verification are conducted. The experimental results demonstrate that the EF-FxLMS algorithm effectively suppresses flow-induced vibration responses of the aircraft model, with advantages such as strong noise immunity and adaptive variable step. Additionally, the system significantly extends the experimental attack angle range for the transonic wind tunnel model, ensuring accurate evaluation of its aerodynamic characteristics. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, X.; Sethuraman, V.R.P.; Ajith Kumar, A.K.; Ma, C.; Wu, K.; Ge, X.; Fu, S.; Kim, H.D.
Author full names: Chen, Xin (59664765700); Sethuraman, Vignesh Ram Petha (60203320600); Ajith Kumar, S. (57220813619); Ma, Chi (57194336972); Wu, Kexin (57201559055); Ge, Xiaoyan (60203795500); Fu, Song (7402732551); Kim, Heuy Dong (57203008510)
Title: Study on self-excited and forced oscillations of shock train in a scramjet isolator
Abstract: The URANS method is employed to investigate the self-excited oscillation and forced oscillation of the shock train by varying the upstream turbulence intensity and applying pressure fluctuations at the upstream and downstream of the model, respectively. The current results demonstrate that while turbulence intensity is extremely low or high, the probability distribution of instantaneous shock train positions tends toward uniformity within the oscillation region. As the upstream turbulence intensity rises, the shock train leading edge shifts closer to the upstream. Meanwhile, the oscillation of the first shock is intensified, while the second shock exhibits the opposite behavior. The oscillation energy of the downstream shocks decreases. Upstream imposed disturbances propagate to the isolator exit, while downstream disturbances remain confined to propagation within the shock train. Peak frequencies are observed in the power spectral density analyses, where their harmonics serve as a critical factor causing the shock train to follow distinct trajectories during each perturbation cycle. Increasing perturbation frequency reduces shock train oscillation amplitudes and weakens the impact of dominant frequency oscillations on other frequency components. Upstream and downstream frequency variations of the isolator exhibit opposing effects on shock train length, though the length variation shows no correlation with perturbation amplitude. As the perturbation amplitude increases, a reduction in the gas acceleration zone behind the leading shock is observed. In comparison to upstream perturbations, downstream ones induce more pronounced impacts on the distance between the first two shocks. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Miccio, E.; Veneruso, P.; Opromolla, R.; Fasano, G.; Tiana, C.; Gentile, G.
Author full names: Miccio, Enrico (58504580600); Veneruso, Paolo (57411911800); Opromolla, Roberto (56368337300); Fasano, Giancarmine (16174945500); Tiana, Carlo L.M. (6507551644); Gentile, Giacomo (57412316700)
Title: Vision-aided Sensing Pipeline with AI-based vertiport detection for Precision Navigation in UAM Approach and Landing Scenarios
Abstract: This paper presents an original vision-aided, multi-sensor navigation architecture to support approach and landing procedures of Vertical Take-Off and Landing aircraft to vertiports in Urban Air Mobility scenarios. The architecture relies on an Extended Kalman filter integrating inertial measurements with positioning information provided by GNSS or GNSS/visual processing, depending on the distance from the landing pad. Indeed, visual-based measurements allow improving the accuracy and the integrity of the navigation filter output, especially at close distances from the landing areas. The visual processing pipeline relies on a two-camera configuration designed to ensure continuous coverage of the landing pad along an approach trajectory which is compliant with the existing guidelines. Regarding the landing pad, an original configuration with multi-scale fiducial markers is considered, which is obtained modifying the one proposed by the European Aviation Safety Agency so as to enable both manned and unmanned operations. From the algorithmic perspective, different processing blocks are customized and combined within the sensing pipeline paying particular attention to the timely detection and removal of anomalous measurements as well as to the compliance of the estimated uncertainty with navigation performance requirements during landing operations. Landing area detection is entrusted to an ad hoc Convolutional Neural Network trained on the basis of the operational domain. Then, custom motion-aware image processing methodologies are used to detect, match, and track visual landmarks which are then used to solve a perspective-n-points problem through an iterative non linear least squares strategy. Analysis of residuals, as well as covariance-based checks on the difference between predictions and estimates, are used before feeding the visual solution into the filter, thus providing protection against outliers in the visual-based pose estimation process. Performance assessment is first carried out by means of numerical simulations within a high-fidelity virtual environment able to realistically reproduce aircraft dynamics, sensors’ operation, illumination and weather conditions. Such analysis is then complemented by tests conducted on real images taken during an experimental flight test campaign with a small unmanned aerial vehicle. © © 2025. Authors: Wu, X.; Yang, C.; Yang, Y.; Liu, C.; Wang, Z.; Yang, Y.
Title: Generalized Rodrigues parameters-based fast in-flight alignment approaches with GNSS assistance for SUAV
Abstract: With the increasing diversification of small unmanned aerial vehicle (SUAV) launch methods, airdrops have emerged as a critical approach for large-scale deployment. In-flight alignment (IFA) is essential for enhancing airdrop efficiency and flexibility without relying on additional launch platform information. This paper proposes a novel strapdown inertial navigation system (SINS) error model based on generalized Rodrigues parameters (GRPs) to improve IFA performance with global navigation satellite system (GNSS) assistance. A uniform GRPs-based nonlinear error model for attitude and velocity is developed, which allows the filter to propagate GRPs directly without quaternion transformations, enhancing robustness and stability. An unscented Kalman filter (UKF)-based GNSS-aided IFA approach is constructed using indirect filtering. To reduce computational complexity, a GRPs-based linear error model is derived, leveraging micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS) inertial measurement unit (IMU) characteristics and indirect filtering principles, and a Kalman filter (KF)-based GNSS-aided IFA approach is established. Observability analysis is conducted under six flight maneuver modes, followed by hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulations and field flight tests. Results show that the proposed models outperform existing linear ψ-angle-based, nonlinear Euler angle-based, and nonlinear quaternion-based models, achieving alignment errors below 1.5° within 6 seconds under high maneuverability, with alignment accuracy under 0.7°. Field tests further validate the superiority of the approaches. The GRPs-based linear model reduces computational complexity by 60 % while maintaining comparable alignment performance, making it suitable for resource-constrained SUAV onboard processors. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Qiu, X.; Zou, F.; Tao, Y.; Li, X.; Wang, L.; Li, J.; Liu, Y.
Title: Shock waves, vortex structures and momentum transport of a jet in supersonic crossflow with different injection angles
Abstract: Direct numerical simulation (DNS) is conducted to investigate the flow field structures of a Jet in Supersonic Crossflow (JISC) at Ma<inf>∞</inf>=1.6, Re<inf>D</inf>=24000, and jet-to-crossflow momentum flux ratio J=1.7, under five jet injection angles (θ=30∘, 60∘, 90∘, 120∘, and 150∘). The study investigates the influence of injection on shock structures, recirculation zones, large scale vortices, and analyzes vortex evolution and surrounding flow dynamic behavior using momentum transport equations. Results show that with increasing injection angle, the bow shock transfers from oblique to normal and strengthens up to θ=90∘, reshaping the spatial distribution of recirculation zones; this distribution is found to be correlated with the streamwise and wall-normal jet momentum. Within the Counter-Rotating Vortex Pairs (CVPs), negative streamwise momentum transport driven by the adverse pressure gradient promotes vortex dissipation, which becomes more prominent as the injection angle increases. Positive spanwise transport, also driven by pressure, intensifies with angle, enlarging the scale of vortex along spanwise direction. Primary and Secondary Trailing Counter-Rotating Vortex Pairs (TCVPs) exhibit distinct transport features. The Primary TCVPs show negative streamwise and positive wall-normal transport, causing fluid to recirculate into the CVPs, while the Secondary TCVPs, near-wall attached structures, shows positive streamwise and negative wall-normal transport. These findings provide new insights into physical mechanisms in JISC systems under varying injection angles, contributing to the theoretical foundation for flow control strategies in supersonic transverse jet applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Song, T.; Ding, L.; Shan, Z.; Wu, C.
Title: Thermal-flow coupling characteristics and control of sampling probes under high temperature and pressure conditions
Abstract: Gas sampling enables multiple-parameter diagnostics of aircraft engine combustors. To improve the accuracy, this study numerically investigates the flow and heat transfer characteristics of a sampling probe in high-temperature and high-pressure gas environments, validated the model against experiment. The flow characteristics of the sampled gas, as well as the flow and heat transfer of the cooling water, vary along the length of the probe under different inlet conditions. The results show that the outlet pressure of the sampling tube significantly affects the flow along the tube. When the π<inf>0</inf> falls below a critical value (π<inf>0,cr</inf>= 0.72), a series of shock waves with multiple reflections forms at the probe inlet. Lower outlet pressures intensify the temperature and pressure drop regions, which promotes rapid quench chemical reactions in the sampled gas, ensuring that the sampled gas composition accurately represents the inlet gas compositions. Furthermore, when π<inf>0</inf>=0.24, the minimum sampled gas pressure can drop to 14.6 % of the inlet pressure. Adjusting the probe outlet pressure controls the sampled gas outlet temperature, e.g., increasing either the outlet pressure or the tube length lowers the outlet temperature. These findings offer insights into how to optimize the sampling probe performance in extreme conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, W.; Hu, Z.; ZHANG, P.
Title: Liquid-fueled oblique detonation waves induced by reactive and non-reactive transverse liquid jets
Abstract: The reliable initiation of oblique detonation waves (ODWs) in liquid-fueled oblique detonation wave engines (ODWEs) remains a significant challenge. This study presents a novel approach using transverse liquid jets—both reactive and non-reactive—to achieve and stabilize ODWs under conditions that are typically unfavorable for sustained detonation. While liquid jets are more easily implemented in practical applications compared to gas jets, the associated heat absorption can lead to ignition failure. Employing an in-house developed two-phase detonation solver built upon the OpenFOAM framework, this study demonstrates the first successful formation of liquid-jet-induced, liquid-fueled ODWs. Detailed chemical kinetic analyses and comparisons with the on-wedge strip method reveal that the jet-inflow momentum ratio ( J ) is a key parameter governing the transition from oblique shock waves to detonation. At low momentum ratios (0.1 ≤ J ≤ 0.3), the dominant mechanism is the chemical reactions driven by the evaporating jet. The heat release from reactions, which exceeds what the evaporation needs, facilitates the initiation of detonation. When J reaches or exceeds 0.4, the dominant effect shifts to the jet’s physical barrier, generating a strong jet-induced oblique shock wave and directly trigger detonation. However, excessively high momentum ratios can cause the jet to evaporate and lower the temperature, preventing it from participating in the reaction and leading to fuel waste. Leveraging the relative simplicity and flexibility offered by liquid transverse jets compared to gaseous jets, this study establishes the first computational framework for liquid-jet-induced ODW initiation. It provides a practical, adjustable method for igniting and sustaining ODWs in real-world ODWE applications and offers insights into how controlling the momentum ratio can enable stable detonation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, F.; Shang, Z.; Song, H.; Jia, M.; Wu, Y.; Zhang, Z.
Title: Experimental study on the characteristics of SiC enhanced plasma synthesis jet actuator
Abstract: Simplicity of structure and high energy utilization efficiency have always been the goals pursued in the design of plasma synthetic jet actuators. In this paper, a SiC-based plasma synthetic jet actuator (SiC-PSJA) was designed. The discharge characteristics of this actuator were comparatively studied with those of Cu₂O-PSJA and Al₂O₃-PSJA (control group). On this basis, the characteristics of jet evolution of the SiC-PSJA were investigated. The results indicate that under atmospheric pressure, when the electrode distance ranges from 2 to 6 mm, the breakdown voltage of the SiC-PSJA is lower than the Cu₂O-PSJA and the Al₂O₃-PSJA. When the electrode distance is 6 mm, the breakdown voltage of the SiC-PSJA is 1.3 kV, while those of the Cu₂O-PSJA and the Al₂O₃-PSJA are 3.8 kV and 9.2 kV, respectively. Under atmospheric pressure and with a capacitance of 1 μF, when the electrode distance is greater than 3 mm, an increase in the electrode distance does not result in a significant change in the jet penetration depth of the SiC-PSJA. The jet penetration depth enters a standstill. Under conditions of long electrode distance and large capacitance, both the jet penetration depth and the jet affected region will enter a standstill. By classifying the jet morphologies under different energy and electrode distances, six patterns can be obtained. The research presented in this paper provides a new method for designing simple and efficient plasma synthetic jet actuators and contributes to a better understanding of the working process and characteristics of the actuators. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, C.; Zhang, H.; Xie, Y.; Huang, F.; Wang, X.
Title: Design and implementation of a dual-core third order time-optimal tracking differentiator in the flight environment simulation system
Abstract: For advanced control systems, such as the flight environment simulation system (FESS), high-order derivatives contain richer dynamic information that is vital for high-performance controller design. Since high-order derivatives are more sensitive to noise, the accurate extraction of these derivatives with strong noise suppression is thus essential under practical, noise-affected conditions. In this paper, a new third-order tracking differentiator (TD) based on a third-order time-optimal control (TOC) law is proposed, aiming to achieve high-precision and low-noise estimation of signal derivatives in the FESS with the presence of noise. Specifically, a third-order TOC law is derived for integrator systems via a reverse-time method. Based on this control law, the Dual-Core TD is developed by introducing an innovative structure that integrates two distinct tracking differentiators. The convergence of the proposed method is rigorously proven through control-Lyapunov function theory. The effectiveness of the proposed TD is demonstrated through comprehensive simulations in both the time and frequency domains, which show superior noise suppression, tracking accuracy, and dynamic response compared to conventional approaches. To further validate the proposed method and highlight the contribution of high-order derivative estimation to control performance, a controller utilizing the Dual-Core TD is deployed on a hardware-in-the-loop platform of the FESS. Experimental results verify the effectiveness and engineering feasibility of the Dual-Core TD, indicating its strong potential for real-time, high-precision applications in complex control systems under noisy conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.
