Authors: Yang, Z.; Wang, S.; Li, J.; Song, W.
Title: Numerical investigation on the effect of cavity leading/trailing edge fillet design on mixing performance in a supersonic combustor
Abstract: This study proposes a novel approach that replaces the sharp leading and trailing edges in a conventional scramjet combustor cavity with rounded fillets. The leading-edge fillet promotes fuel diffusion inside the cavity by leveraging the Coandă effect, while the trailing-edge fillet mitigates wall ablation caused by concentrated thermal stress. The effects of edge filleting on hydrogen distribution and mixing performance are systematically examined via numerical simulations. Results indicate that cavity edge filleting significantly enhances hydrogen dispersion by expanding the recirculation zone and increasing the magnitude of vorticity and turbulent kinetic energy. Furthermore, this geometric modification improves the hydrogen mixing efficiency and substantially enlarges the flammable plume region. Specifically, the mixing efficiency at the cavity's rear wall is enhanced by up to 12.43 %, and the flammable plume area near the front wall increases by a maximum of 147.96 %. Both the mixing efficiency and flammable plume area exhibit positive correlations with the fillet radius. It is noteworthy that the total pressure recovery coefficient remains largely unchanged, with variations below 0.52 % across all tested radii. These findings demonstrate that filleting the leading and trailing edges effectively improves the ignition performance and flameholding stability of supersonic combustion chambers. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, Q.; Zhong, C.; Zhuo, C.; Liu, S.
Author full names: Zhang, Qingdian (58198454400); Zhong, Chengwen (15319814600); Zhuo, Congshan (35172208400); Liu, Sha (36701501800)
Title: Investigation of heat and drag reduction in hypersonic rarefied environments using spike-aerodisk-opposing jet configurations
Abstract: Heat and drag reduction are critical challenges in the aerodynamic and thermal design of near-space hypersonic vehicles. In this study, the implicit multiscale discrete velocity method is employed to investigate the aerodynamic and thermal characteristics of a spike-aerodisk-opposing jet configuration in rarefied hypersonic flows. The effects of flight altitude, Mach number, opposing jet intensity, and angle of attack are systematically analyzed. Results show that with increasing flight altitude, the freestream becomes more rarefied, leading to a gradual decline in the heat and drag reduction performance of the configuration. As the Mach number increases, the compressibility of the freestream gradually intensifies, resulting in a reduction in the heat and drag reduction capabilities of the spike-aerodisk-opposing jet configuration. In contrast, stronger opposing jets expand the influence region of the flow field, effectively improving both drag and heat reduction compared with the spike-aerodisk configuration alone. With increasing angle of attack, the effective windward area exposed to the flow enlarges, progressively degrading the mitigation performance; notably, when the angle reaches 25∘, drag augmentation is even observed. Overall, the present study demonstrates the feasibility and robustness of the spike-aerodisk-opposing jet configuration for integrated drag and heat reduction in rarefied hypersonic regimes, offering valuable guidance for the aerodynamic and thermal design of reusable near-space hypersonic vehicles. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xiao, Y.; Zhou, Y.; Xiao, J.; Yang, C.; An, Q.; Hou, X.; Chen, D.
Title: A dynamic decision-making simulation model for pilots considering risk preference heterogeneity
Abstract: With the increasing complexity of aviation safety decision-making, understanding pilots’ heterogeneous risk preferences and their influence on dynamic decision processes has become crucial. However, most studies largely rely on traditional models and lack rigorous mathematical predictive frameworks. To achieve this goal, a comprehensive risk preference classification model for pilots is proposed by integrating subjective scales with objective experimental data, categorizing pilots as risk-averse, risk-neutral, or risk-seeking. Subsequently, a dynamic decision-making framework grounded in the Extended Decision Field Theory (EDFT), interval-valued intuitionistic fuzzy number (IVIFN), and the OODA loop (Observe, Orient, Decide, Act) is developed to capture pilots’ cognitive and behavioral dynamics during task execution. Data from 30 participants, including scale assessments, behavioral measures, and EEG recordings, are used to validate the Pilot Risk Attitude Scale, classify pilots into three risk-preference categories, and extract EDFT parameters. Building on this foundation, a MATLAB-based simulation algorithm is then developed to model pilots’ cognitive and behavioral dynamics across different risk-preference categories during landing alternatives. Results show close agreement between simulated and experimental outcomes (Pearson’s r = 0.981), confirming the model’s predictive power. Furthermore, the model effectively discriminates dynamic decision-making behaviors among pilots with differing risk preferences. Meanwhile, extended decision-making intervals are found to enhance the selection of preferred alternatives, whereas elevated decision thresholds produce delays in choosing favored options. © © 2025. Authors: Annaz, F.Y.
Title: Robust equalization of multi-lane electromechanical actuators: Comparative analysis of torque-summed and velocity-summed architectures
Abstract: The increasing demand for energy-efficient, low-emission aircraft has accelerated the transition toward all-electric aircraft (AEA), driving the need for highly reliable electromechanical actuators (EMAs) in flight-critical systems. A major challenge in multi-lane EMA configurations lies in torque and velocity disparities caused by component mismatches, sensor drift, and mechanical tolerances. These discrepancies can result in force-fight, increased mechanical stress, and reduced control accuracy, necessitating robust equalisation strategies. This study investigates force equalisation and lane equalisation in torque-summed and velocity-summed EMA architectures to mitigate lane-torque disparities (ΔT) and enhance system robustness. A four-lane redundant EMA system, originally designed to actuate the inboard aileron of the Sea Harrier aircraft, was modelled in MATLAB–Simulink with a proportional–integral–derivative (PID) controller and Monitoring–Voting–Averaging Devices (MVADs) for feedback processing. Three-phase motor models were included to capture torque-ripple effects, and simulations were conducted across a range of inertial and aerodynamic load conditions. The results show that force equalisation effectively reduces lane-torque disparities in torque-summed architectures, improving load sharing and resilience under tachometer and potentiometer drift. In contrast, the velocity-summed architecture is inherently torque-balanced (lane-torque disparity ΔT is approximately zero), so lane equalisation has limited effect on ΔT. A potentiometer bias appears as a steady tracking offset that equalisation alone does not remove. These findings highlight critical trade-offs between architectures and underscore the importance of advanced equalisation and adaptive control strategies to further optimise EMA performance in next-generation all-electric aircraft. Although framed by an aerospace EMA, the methods and conclusions are not domain specific. The closed-form equalisation laws, cross-monitoring, and analysis of sensor-bias mechanisms generalise to multi-lane system architectures in robotics, automotive drive-by-wire, industrial automation, wind-turbine blade-pitch control, marine actuation, and medical and assistive devices. © © 2025. Authors: Jing, Q.; Ru, Y.; El-Hindi, K.E.
Title: Thermoelastic behavior of FG plates under intense thermal shock: Deep neural network-based validation of dynamical responses
Abstract: This article investigates the thermoelastic behavior of functionally graded plates under thermal shock relevant to aerospace applications. In this analysis, the finite element method (FEM) is employed using the Carrera unified formulation (CUF) with one-dimensional and two-dimensional models to analyze the complicated interplay of thermo-mechanical effects. The bottom surface is insulated, and thermal loading is applied to the top surface, which is representative of the high-temperature changes experienced in aerospace flight. The coupled thermoelasticity problem is discretized via the Galerkin method. In the discretization, the resulting FEM equations are solved numerically using the Newmark method to obtain a time-dependent, transient response for the boundary thermal shock. The study provides a thorough analysis of thermal stress distribution, displacement, and strain responses of functionally graded plates subjected to extreme thermal loading conditions. An important aspect of the study is the verification of the proposed model through deep neural networks (DNNs). The DNNs are trained on datasets obtained using the mathematical simulations to validate the prediction accuracy of the model. Results show the accuracy and efficiency of the 2D CUF-based FEM procedure in solving areas of complex thermoelastic problems. Benchmark comparisons validate that the method shows promise as a reliable approach to simulate aerospace structures under transient thermal gradients. The results offer significant understanding about an aerospace structure constructed with functionally graded plates, and their dynamic thermal response to thermal shocks, to help the optimization and design of aerospace components. The work illustrated the ability of the new 2D CUF-based FEM approach to perform advanced modeling of thermoelastic behavior in aerospace applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, X.; Hou, B.; Wang, J.; Wang, D.; Zhou, H.
Author full names: Zhang, Xiaohong (36538352900); Hou, Bowen (57193674435); Wang, Jiongqi (14030757500); Wang, Dayi (57223662725); Zhou, Haiyin (12752598800)
Title: Unified modeling of relative motion in eccentric orbits under J2 perturbation for non-cooperative targets
Abstract: To enhance the modeling accuracy of the relative motion for non-cooperative targets in eccentric orbits, a high-fidelity unified model is proposed. First, a novel second-order mapping from eccentric relative orbital elements to relative position coordinates is developed. Second, by introducing the J2 perturbation into the eccentric relative orbital elements, a closed-form analytical state transition matrix is derived and subsequently used to develop a long-term propagation model. Third, by embedding this model into the second-order mapping, a unified model is constructed that simultaneously accounts for the nonlinear dynamics of eccentric orbits and long-term perturbation effects. Finally, this unified model is further used to establish a conceptual framework that links the line-of-sight measurements to the eccentric relative orbital elements. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the unified model proposed achieves higher accuracy than existing methods. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tian, P.; CHEN, F.; Gou, H.; Shen, Q.
Title: Performance mapping and decoupled feedforward control for active torque regulation of a contra-rotating ducted fan
Abstract: The Contra-Rotating Ducted Fan (CRDF) is a promising propulsion technology for electric aircraft that offers an inherent capability for Active Torque Regulation (ATR) for attitude control. Practical implementation of ATR requires decoupling reaction-torque modulation from thrust generation when adjusting individual rotor speeds. This study reveals a fundamental relationship between the fan’s power consumption and reaction torque, based on analytical modeling using a velocity-triangle analysis and regression of performance maps within the CRDF’s feasible operating domain. Simulation results indicate that the thrust and power of the CRDF are primarily determined by the relative rotating speed (Ω), while torque is principally governed by the speed ratio (Λ); however, these relationships exhibit a degree of coupling. A practical feedforward control strategy is formulated based on linear regression of relevant parameter groups. This strategy computes the required adjustments in Λ and Ω from readily available system variables to achieve a desired reaction torque while maintaining either constant overall thrust or power consumption. The efficacy of the decoupled control is demonstrated through URANS simulations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, L.; Wang, Z.; Liu, X.; Mao, J.; Lv, C.; Zhang, D.; Liu, Y.
Author full names: Wang, Longfei (57203825383); Wang, Zicong (59803423200); Liu, Xinzi (57860330600); Mao, Junkui (7402727786); Lv, Chengliang (58642345300); Zhang, Dewei (58642345400); Liu, Yiming (59147627300)
Title: Investigation on flow and heat transfer characteristics of turbine blade cooling channel with non-uniform sinusoidal spoiler ribs
Abstract: To meet efficient uniform cooling demand for high-pressure turbine blades, a numerical investigation is conducted on flow and heat transfer characteristics of non-uniform sinusoidal spoiler ribs in cooling channel. Streamwise variations in geometric parameters amplitude, period, rib height and influence on cooling channel performance are elucidated to mitigate poor downstream heat transfer from streamwise cooling air temperature rise. With increase of the termination amplitude, disturbance of cooling air to near wall is significantly enhanced, and heat transfer performance is improved by 37.2 % compared with traditional uniform scheme. And large amplitude shows better heat transfer performance and does not depend on rib height. The best streamwise heat transfer uniformity is exhibited by moderate rib height and moderate termination amplitude, while high overall heat flux but poor uniformity is presented by large rib height and large termination amplitude. Compared with amplitude and rib height, the ability of non-uniform periodic arrangement to enhance heat transfer is relatively weak. When spoiler period varies drastically in streamwise direction, overall heat transfer reduces, but upstream flow separation is effectively suppressed, significantly reducing pressure loss. Among these, the thermal performance of large rib height and moderate periodic fluctuation scheme is the greatest. On this basis, the optimized arrangement of amplitude combination rib height increases streamwise both improves Nu/Nu <inf>0</inf> of downstream ribbed wall, and cooling air diversion effect suppresses pressure loss. Specifically, increasing rib height of streamwise at small amplitude relieves downstream heat transfer deficiency, and enhances the end heat transfer at large amplitude. © © 2025. Authors: Shen, Y.; Wang, Z.; Wang, K.; Zhou, Z.; Zou, X.
Author full names: Shen, Yuxin (60199568200); Wang, Zhengping (35070745100); Wang, Kelei (59901571800); Zhou, Zhou (35812630600); Zou, Xiaopeng (60199568300)
Title: Propulsion/aerodynamic coupling analytic modeling of distributed propulsion wing unit with induced wing
Abstract: This study addresses the propulsion/aerodynamic coupling characteristics of distributed propulsion wings (DPW) with induced wings, targeting the development of distributed electric propulsion (DEP) vertical take-off and landing (VTOL) unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). An aerodynamic analytic modeling approach is proposed to enable rapid computation and evaluation for the overall design and optimization of such aircraft. The DPW configuration is analyzed to establish modeling priorities, focusing on characterizing the aerodynamic behavior of the DPW unit with induced wing. Aerodynamic analytic models for the DPW unit are developed under both axial flow conditions and non-axial flow conditions with angles of attack, grounded in fluid dynamics theory and mathematical modeling. A corresponding model for the induced wing under ducted jet coupling is also established. The integrated characteristics of this complex system are then mathematically described by superimposing these component models. Validation through CFD simulations and experimental analysis under typical flight configurations demonstrates that the modular modeling approach effectively meets the requirements of aerodynamic design and computation, and the model’s solutions accurately characterize the aerodynamic behavior of the DPW unit under complex configurations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Long, L.; Zhao, Y.; He, X.
Title: Effect of flow field on the combustion performance of a triple-swirler combustor for advanced aircraft engine
Abstract: To investigate the effect of flow field, particularly specifically the large-scale axial vortex, on the combustion performance of a triple-swirler combustor, two triple-swirlers (referred to as S1 and S2) were developed to establish two distinct flow fields in a gas turbine combustor. The flow field of S1 is characterized by a larger recirculation zone at the center spanwise cross-section and a chaotic divergent flow at the axial cross-sections. In contrast, the flow field of S2 features a smaller recirculation zone at the center spanwise cross-section and a more uniform flow pattern with a prominent large-scale axial vortex at the axial cross-sections. Experimental investigations were conducted at atmospheric pressure and a temperature of 473 K to assess the combustion performance of the combustor under these differing flow fields. The evaluation of combustion performance included lean ignition performance, lean blowout limit, combustion efficiency, and pattern factor. The findings indicate that the combustion behavior of the combustor is strongly influenced by the flow field. S2 outperforms S1 in terms of lean ignition performance, lean blowout limits, and pattern factor under most operational conditions. However, when the fuel-air ratio (FAR) is below 0.02 or above 0.4, S1 exhibits superior combustion efficiency compared to S2. Conversely, for the remaining FAR conditions, S2 achieves higher combustion efficiency than S1. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, L.; Wang, X.; Zhang, J.; Ge, C.; Chu, R.; Wu, L.
Title: FTIM: An end-to-end flight trajectory imputation method
Abstract: Flight trajectory imputation plays a crucial role in optimizing aircraft management and enhancing safety performance. However, despite the availability of various imputation methods, these approaches often exhibit limitations in both applicability and performance. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an end-to-end method for Flight Trajectory Imputation (FTIM). FTIM takes incomplete short-term trajectories as input and outputs complete trajectories, which is composed of three core modules. The Trajectory Encoding Module introduces a novel data encoding technique that simplifies data distribution and reduces data range, thereby improving convergence abilities and imputation efficiency. The Trajectory Prediction Module proposes a novel encoder-decoder deep learning framework to accurately predict missing values in trajectories. Additionally, the Trajectory Reconstruction Module presents a near-source reconstruction approach to reconstruct trajectories from both original and imputed data, effectively mitigating error accumulation and further enhancing imputation accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that FTIM significantly outperforms numerous excellent methods in terms of performance and generalization capabilities, thereby demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving high-quality flight trajectory imputation. Moreover, FTIM can also significantly improve the performance of existing trajectory prediction methods, further highlighting its practical application value. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Gong, M.; Dong, H.; Jiang, Y.; Gu, Y.; Jiao, Y.
Title: Hypersonic boundary layer crossflow instability on a yawed cone with nose-tip heating
Abstract: In this work, the effects of nose-tip temperature and bluntness on the crossflow instabilities and their secondary instability have been studied experimentally at Mach 6 by infrared thermography and high-frequency pulsating pressure sensors. The investigations are conducted within the boundary layer of a 7° half-angle cone at a 6° angle of attack, with nose-tip bluntness radii (R = 0.5, 2, and 5 mm) and wall temperature ratio ( T <inf>w</inf>/ T <inf>0</inf>, the ratio of nose-tip temperature to total incoming temperature) ranging from 0.6 to 1.3. It is demonstrated that increasing the nose-tip temperature in the case of small bluntness reduces the amplitude of traveling crossflow waves while enhancing stationary crossflow vortices on the leeward side, resulting in a 16.2% increase in the maximum wave number. Increasing bluntness reduces the influence of nose-tip temperature on both traveling waves and stationary vortices, with a more pronounced effect on traveling waves. As bluntness increases, the high-frequency instability transitions from primarily secondary instability of traveling waves to secondary instability of stationary crossflow vortices. Additionally, the interaction between traveling and stationary waves transitions from initial suppression of traveling waves by stationary vortices to synchronous growth before being suppressed by the traveling waves. © © 2025. Authors: Long, W.; Xiong, D.; Wang, H.; Sun, M.; Chen, H.; Yang, Y.
Author full names: Long, Wenxiao (58933189200); Xiong, Dapeng (57216366912); Wang, Hongbo (24722069100); Sun, Mingbo (24465746000); Chen, Huifeng (57362662600); Yang, Yixin (56723061200)
Title: Numerical investigation on the flow characteristics of shock trains in supersonic square-to-circular transition ducts
Abstract: Square-to-circular transition ducts are widely used to connect square inlets with circular combustors in scramjet engines. These ducts often develop strong shock trains to sustain elevated backpressure from combustion. Understanding the flow characteristics in these ducts is critical for efficient combustion organization and improved flight performance. This study employs direct numerical simulation to investigate shock-train flow in a square-to-circular (STC) transition duct under Ma=2.7 freestream conditions and constant backpressure. Firstly, flow features at zero backpressure reveal that the STC duct exhibits not only Prandtl’s second-kind secondary flows in corner regions but also transverse motion near walls induced by geometric transition. Under constant backpressure, the shock train in the STC duct displays pronounced three-dimensional characteristics, including concave leading shock surfaces. Shock-boundary layer interactions in the STC duct cause flow separation predominantly concentrated near the wall central plane, with minimal corner separation—unlike the square duct, where significant corner separation propagates downstream. Analysis shows that the gradual geometric transition of the STC duct enhances corner momentum, thereby suppressing corner separation. Distributions of turbulent kinetic energy (TKE) along the bottom and top walls of the STC duct demonstrate that the shock train amplifies TKE within the boundary layer. The amplification mechanism is primarily attributed to mean shear and the interaction between mean flow deceleration and streamwise velocity fluctuations, with the latter being the dominant. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hui, Y.; Cheng, Y.; Jiang, B.; Tian, H.; Li, Z.; Wang, N.
Title: A full-lifecycle health monitoring and prognostics method for satellite components based on data distribution features and multi-kernel phase-adaptive Gaussian processes
Abstract: Health monitoring and prognostics of critical satellite components are essential for ensuring the long-term stability of space missions. Periodic orbital disturbances and gradual performance degradation introduce strong non-stationarity in on-orbit data, making it challenging for traditional methods to distinguish between cyclic fluctuations and actual degradation trends. To address this issue, this paper proposes a full-lifecycle health monitoring and prognostics approach that integrates data distribution features with a phase-adaptive Gaussian process framework. First, Gaussian kernel density estimation (KDE) is used to extract distributional features from baseline and current data, while Wasserstein distance is used to construct a health state indicator (HSI) for quantifying degradation. For short-term prediction, a multi-kernel short-term adaptive Gaussian process model (MK-STA-GP) is proposed, which improves the modeling and forecasting of short-term non-stationary behavior through kernel fusion and dynamic windowing strategies. For long-term remaining useful life (RUL) estimation, we introduce an uncertainty-regularized multi-kernel Gaussian process (URC-MK-GP) with trend-consistency constraints to improve robustness against local anomalies. Experiments on real satellite data show that the proposed method effectively tracks health evolution, improves short-term trend prediction, and enhances long-term RUL estimation stability, providing reliable support for intelligent spacecraft health management. © © 2025. Authors: Wen, Z.; Lian, Y.; Zeng, L.; Tian, C.; Li, J.; Shao, X.
Title: Airfoil-DDPM: A flexible airfoil generative design method using a multi-objective sampling based diffusion model
Abstract: Airfoil designs are crucial for the aerodynamic performance of airplanes. Multiple objectives under several flight conditions to satisfy engineering requirements, especially for wide-speed-range aircraft or morphing aircraft are always considered. Airfoil generative design method is an end-to-end method which inputs design objectives and outputs airfoil geometry directly. In this study, a novel airfoil generative design method using a conditional denoising diffusion probabilistic model was developed to solve the multi-objective design problem. Airfoil-DDPM can flexibly solve multi-objective design problems considering both geometric constraints and arbitrary number set of aerodynamic constraints. The classifier-free diffusion guidance and the multi-objective sampling method were combined in the diffusion model to realize the flexible multi-objective design. Trained on 50k airfoils across 200k flow cases(Ma=0.75, Re=7×106, α=0–3∘), the model generated airfoils with median drag coefficient and moment coefficient error of 0.0008 and 0.0069 respectively in multi-objective design. The accuracy Testing showed that the method can generate airfoils under geometric or aerodynamic constraints, while the airfoils generated under both constraints were better in line with the reference airfoil than those with only one kinds of constraint. Robustness testing showed that although the unreasonable input significantly deviates from the normal value, the generations satisfy the reasonable constraints well. The comparison resulted with other two generation models show that Airfoil-DDPM has absolute advantages over CVAE and CGAN in terms of generation quality and flexibility. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hu, C.; Chen, Y.; Wu, Y.; Yang, L.; Wang, J.; Hou, H.; Li, T.; Wang, Y.
Title: Experimental investigation on gliding arc plasma ignition under high-altitude ground-starting conditions in aero-engine combustor
Abstract: Low-temperatures and low-pressures at high-altitudes (Plateau) pose serious challenges for aero-engine combustor ignition. Plasma-assisted ignition has emerged as a promising technology to achieve reliable ignition in extreme conditions. Experiments were conducted in a dual-dome aero-engine combustor sector using RP-3 aviation kerosene, under high-altitude ground-starting conditions (altitude: 0→6 km, inlet pressure: 101.32→47.22 kPa, inlet temperature: 288.15→249.19 K). The ignition performances of conventional electric spark ignition, oxygen-enriched spark ignition, and gliding arc plasma ignition were systematically compared. Results showed that gliding arc ignition expanded the ignition boundary by 20.00 % and 10.20 % at 4 km altitude compared with electric spark ignition and oxygen-enriched ignition, respectively. Analyses of flow field, spray distribution, and cracking products provide direct evidence of the mechanisms by which gliding arc plasma improves atomization, evaporation, and ignition reliability. CH* chemiluminescence visualization revealed a distinct flame kernel propagation pathway unique to gliding arc ignition. In contrast, for electric spark ignition and oxygen-enriched ignition, the flame kernel exhibited a long propagation pathway and short duration time, resulting in high energy dissipation and low ignition success probability. The gliding arc plasma not only enhanced atomization, mixing, and evaporation at low-temperatures, but also enabled continuous heat release accumulation and high energy utilization efficiency, thereby facilitating multi-point ignition inside the combustion chamber. At 6 km altitude, gliding arc ignition reduced the ignition delay time by factors of approximately 33 and 13 times compared with electric spark and oxygen-enriched ignition, respectively. These findings demonstrate that gliding arc plasma ignition substantially elevates the effective ground ignition altitude of aero-engines and provides a reliable pathway for ignition under low-temperature, low-pressure conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, F.; Fu, C.; Tian, S.; Liu, J.; Zheng, L.
Title: Blade tip timing-based blade vibration measurement technology and its applications to aeroengine: A systematic review
Abstract: Blade tip timing (BTT) is a critical non-contact measurement technology for monitoring blade dynamics in rotating machinery, primarily due to its excellent ability to provide accurate, real-time data for all blades without interfering with blade structure or flow field. Driven by the demand for high-speed, high-precision monitoring in advanced aeroengines, the BTT technology is developing rapidly in the design and optimization of measurement systems, signal processing algorithms, and engineering applications in structural monitoring. To further enhance the applicability and reliability of the BTT in the complex operating environments of aeroengines, researchers have undertaken improvements and optimizations of the BTT technology from multiple perspectives. This paper presents a state-of-the-art review of the recent advancements in the BTT technology. Firstly, it briefly outlines the theoretical foundations of the BTT, followed by an assessment of its development trends. Secondly, five popular sensors for BTT measurement are presented. Particularly, optimization strategies related to the sensor placement and number of sensors are comprehensively reviewed. Thirdly, BTT signal processing methods are summarized, including their fundamental identifying principles of vibration characteristics parameters, strengths, and limitations. Additionally, special attention is given to the errors and uncertainties in BTT measurements. Then, various applications of the BTT in structural monitoring are reviewed. Finally, comments on the latest research progress are made, and outlooks on future research directions are highlighted. This review aims to provide researchers in this field with a timely, comprehensive, and systematic reference on the BTT technology, and to offer theoretical support to foster further innovation and application of this technology. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhu, Y.; Xuan, Y.
Title: A comprehensive model for energy management of adaptive cycle engine with integrating thermal inertia and variable mass effects
Abstract: As a leading candidate for next-generation aerospace propulsion, the adaptive cycle engine (ACE) can achieve both high thrust in turbojet engine and low specific fuel consumption in turbofan engine through mode transition. However, the variation of mass and energy within components and thermal inertial of solid structures in transient process is neglected in current engine simulation model. To increase transient calculation accuracy, this study innovatively proposes a performance model for an ACE integrated with the modified variable mass system (MVMS) and 2-D thermal inertial model (TIM). The response delay of solid temperature and heat transfer is characterized in TIM, and the MVMS further considers the impact of pressure on energy transition term compared with the traditional variable mass system (VMS). The relative error of the simulation results for physical model with MVMS and TIM is reduced from 1.13 % to 0.778 % compared to conventional model. In acceleration, the maximum thrust loss and delay caused by heat dissipation in TIM can be 2.9 % and 6.23 s respectively. During mode transitions, the engine instability caused by mass and energy storage in MVMS can be reduced by 2.3 % with the decrease of heat transfer in TIM. The comparison simulation with different heat transfer parameters in TIM reveals that the engine instability can be more obvious as the convective heat-transfer ratio decreases, and the response delay of performance and solid temperature increases. While revealing the dynamic features and mechanism during mode transitions of ACE, this novel approach establishes a theoretical foundation for designing ACE control laws. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Giral, F.; Gómez-Pérez, I.; Le Clainche, S.
Author full names: Giral, Francisco (59170903200); Gómez-Pérez, Ignacio (55427389500); Le Clainche, Soledad (55466824600)
Title: Intercepting unauthorized aerial robots in controlled airspace using reinforcement learning
Abstract: The proliferation of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) in controlled airspace presents significant risks, including potential collisions, disruptions to air traffic, and security threats. Ensuring the safe and efficient operation of airspace, particularly in urban environments and near critical infrastructure, necessitates effective methods to intercept unauthorized or non-cooperative UAVs. This work addresses the critical need for robust, adaptive systems capable of managing such threats through the use of Reinforcement Learning (RL). We present a novel approach utilizing RL to train fixed-wing UAV pursuer agents for intercepting dynamic evader targets. Our methodology explores both model-based and model-free RL algorithms, specifically DreamerV3, Truncated Quantile Critics (TQC), and Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). The training and evaluation of these algorithms were conducted under diverse scenarios, including unseen evasion strategies and environmental perturbations. Our approach leverages high-fidelity flight dynamics simulations to create realistic training environments. This research underscores the importance of developing intelligent, adaptive control systems for UAV interception, significantly contributing to the advancement of secure and efficient airspace management. It demonstrates the potential of RL to train systems capable of autonomously achieving these critical tasks. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Wang, H.; Zhang, H.; Guo, Z.; Li, Q.; Chu, W.
Title: Research on compressor slotted stator based on multi-objective optimization and shapley additive exPlanation (SHAP) method
Abstract: Slotted blade is an effective flow control strategy for suppressing boundary layer separation (BLS). However, the flow control mechanism of slotted blade remains to be further explored in stage environment, and its design has long been lacking a reference principle. Therefore, this study proposes for the first time a three-dimensional (3D) modeling method for slotted blade, and applies multi-objective optimization and the Shapley Additive exPlanation (SHAP) method to slotted blade. This paper investigates the mechanism underlying the influence of slotted blade on total performance of a single-stage compressor. Validated numerical methods were employed to perform numerical simulations at the design point (with a mass flow rate outlet boundary condition) and the near stall (NS) point (with an average static pressure outlet boundary condition). By taking the design point efficiency and the total pressure recovery coefficient at the NS point as the objectives for multi-objective optimization, optimal schemes were obtained. The optimal schemes achieve a 1.07 % improvement in efficiency at the NS point, with only a slight loss in design point efficiency. Flow field analysis revealed a novel flow control mechanism in stage environment: the slot-jet, with relatively low energy, promotes the radial flow of low-speed fluids and reduces flow losses near the casing. SHAP analysis indicates that inlet width, outlet axial position, radial position of the upper wall at slot inlet, and radial position of the upper wall at slot outlet exert the most significant influence on compressor performance. Finally, the paper proposes a design principle for slotted blade. This research provides a sufficient theoretical basis and valuable design guidance for the engineering application of slotted blade in stage environment. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Du, Q.; Zhang, Z.; Shi, S.; Zhou, D.; Chen, X.; Zhang, C.; Xie, T.; Yan, X.
Title: Thermal-fluid-solid interaction prediction and multi-objective optimization of helium turbine disc cavity based on deep learning
Abstract: Jet cooling with low-temperature working fluid is a primary method to enhance operational safety of high-temperature helium turbine discs for aerospace propulsion. However, this approach introduces complex thermal-fluid-solid interaction (TFSI) characteristics. This study conducts TFSI coupling analysis for helium turbine disc cavities and develops a deep neural network model named TFSI-DNN to reduce computational resource consumption. The model achieves real-time physical field reconstruction and accurate performance prediction for both fluid and solid domains. Multi-objective optimization is further performed to minimize axial deformation and equivalent stress. Results demonstrate that the TFSI-DNN model reconstructs pressure, temperature and velocity fields in the cavity fluid domain within 5.5 ms, and accurately identifies the average end face temperature, heat flow density and Nusselt number. For the disc solid domain, the TFSI-DNN model completes physical field reconstruction and strength performance prediction within 7.3 ms, with prediction accuracy exceeding 97.5 % for all key parameters. With the combination of the TFSI-DNN model and a gradient-based optimizer, robust Pareto results for axial deformation and stress are obtained in 15 s under different weights. This research provides a novel research paradigm and successful case for design optimization of turbine disc cavity structures in aerospace fields. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Niu, C.; Liu, S.; Wang, J.; Ding, Y.
Author full names: Niu, Chunwang (60096586900); Liu, Songjing (60095964000); Wang, Jialu (60096116100); Ding, Yanwei (57208751440)
Title: Modeling and differential suppression of thermistor 1/f noise for ultra-low frequency gravitational wave detection in TianQin mission
Abstract: The "Tianqin Project" aims to detect space gravitational waves in the ultra-low-frequency (ULF) band (10⁻⁴ Hz to 1 Hz) through the deployment of three satellites. The satellite temperature measurement system encounters significant challenges, including high overall temperature noise and insufficient temperature stability, primarily due to excessive 1/ f noise generated by thermistor sensors in the ULF range. This paper proposes, for the first time, a thermistor 1/ f noise physical model based on the Hooge model and electrical relationships, and designs a differential suppression method for thermistor 1/ f noise. First, a comprehensive physical model for thermistor 1/ f noise is developed, taking into account the effects of the total number of charge carriers, resistance, and frequency. In response to the challenge of quantifying the total number of charge carriers, a conversion relationship between the total number of carriers and the thermistor resistance was established. Second, a homologous bridge-based differential structure was developed, and a theoretical explanation of the dual-channel differential method was provided through nonlinear analysis. The feasibility of this method was further validated through simulations. Finally, comprehensive simulations of the 1/ f noise for two thermistors from the same manufacturer, batch, process, and model were conducted using the proposed differential method. The simulation results indicate that, within the temperature range of 288.15 K to 308.15 K, the proposed model and differential method reduce MF52B103F3435-100 thermistor's 1/ f noise in the frequency band of ULF to below 48.6 μΩ·Hz−0.5, achieving a noise suppression rate of over 95.5%. This method demonstrates significant advantages. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, D.; Chen, P.; Chen, K.; Wang, S.; Han, X.; Mao, J.
Author full names: Zhang, Dingcheng (58927972500); Chen, Pingting (55983148400); Chen, Kai (60200537500); Wang, Shanyou (57203838470); Han, Xingsi (15080739100); Mao, Junkui (7402727786)
Title: On the fundamental mechanisms of conjugate heat transfer and thermo-mechanical coupling in pin-fin arrays at low Reynolds numbers
Abstract: The conjugate heat transfer within the first-stage turbine airfoil of high-altitude UAV engines represents a uniquely challenging regime where low Reynolds number flow and a low Biot number create complex thermo-fluid phenomena not yet fundamentally understood. This study provides a deep mechanistic investigation of a pin-fin cooling array, a representative case for this environment. Through high-fidelity fluid-structure interaction simulations, we reveal a “dovetail-shaped” high-resistance zone forming downstream of the pin-fin, a previously unidentified thermal topology that governs the degradation of cooling performance. The mechanism is traced to a fundamental shift in flow physics at low Reynolds number: weakened turbulent mixing stabilizes wake vortices, while lifted hairpin and horseshoe vortices obstruct coolant from the surface. A counter-intuitive mechanical consequence is that peak Von Mises stress increases by 12 % to 380 MPa at low Reynolds number ( Re = 327), despite a more uniform temperature field. This is dominated by thermal expansion from the bulk temperature rise, which overrides the mitigating effect of reduced temperature gradients. This research provides a benchmark understanding of low- Reynolds number conjugate heat transfer under complex, bilateral thermal loading, highlighting how system-level boundary conditions can fundamentally alter local flow physics and structural integrity. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jiang, Z.; Ouyang, Z.; Xuan, H.; He, Z.; Zhu, Y.; Huang, B.
Title: Experimental study of rotor-casing impact and rubbing behavior in turboshaft engines following shaft broken failure: Effects of rotating speed and axial load
Abstract: Aero-engines operating under extreme dynamic loads may experience shaft broken failure, leading to hazardous rotor-casing interactions where the turbine rotor continues rotating due to residual gas thrust and impacts the exhaust casing. This study investigates the axial rubbing process and wear behavior between the turbine rotor and the exhaust casing under different flight conditions. Equivalent experimental designs were conducted, and multiple axial impact and rubbing tests between the turbine rotor and the exhaust casing were performed under three distinct flight conditions. The results indicate that after the impact between the turbine rotor and the exhaust casing, the exhaust casing exhibits varying degrees of noticeable rebound and vibration and the wear behavior varies significantly under different conditions. Under idle condition, the rubbing surface of the turbine rotor primarily experiences abrasive wear, accompanied by slight adhesive wear and oxidative wear. Under cruise condition, the wear mechanisms include abrasive wear, fatigue wear, and oxidative wear. Under takeoff condition, high-temperature oxidation and adhesive wear become prominent, resulting in a smoother surface with fewer scratches and accompanied by extensive peeling. These findings provide critical insights into post-failure turbine rotor-exhaust casing axial impact and rubbing behavior, supporting improved safety design for aero-engines. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ntouros, E.; Kelley, P.; Smeur, E.J.J.
Author full names: Ntouros, Evangelos (60199601200); Kelley, Pavel (60087307100); Smeur, Ewoud J.J. (57115491500)
Title: Airspeed estimation for UAVs using only propeller feedback
Abstract: This work introduces a novel analytical model for estimating the airspeed of fixed-wing Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) using solely propeller power and rotational speed measurements. The model can be used to replace Pitot-tube-based airspeed sensors, or contribute to redundancy in airspeed estimation. It does not require knowledge of the vehicle’s dynamic model and is computationally lightweight. It leverages power and rotational speed feedback, which is readily available from modern Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs), thereby enabling seamless integration with existing systems and off-the-shelf components. A systematic approach is followed to derive the model structure based on least squares optimization and regularization techniques on Blade Element Momentum (BEM) simulation, wind tunnel, and flight test datasets. The final model generalizes well achieving a normalized Root Mean Square Error (nRMSE) of 5 % on unseen flight data. The model coefficients can be identified either offline, using flight logs with airspeed measurements, or in-flight, using a lightweight identification method based only on Global Positioning System (GPS) velocity data. The resulting system provides a robust and computationally efficient solution for real-time airspeed estimation across diverse fixed-wing UAV platforms. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Liu, Y.; Hu, D.; Chi, R.; Cao, W.; Pang, B.; Wang, H.; Sun, W.
Title: Survivability assessment of constellation in space debris environment based on ARP model
Abstract: Amid the worsening space debris environment and the rapid development of large constellations, this paper proposes a constellation survivability assessment method based on the ARP (Availability, Recoverability and Performance) model in the space debris environment. This model can quantify the impact of space debris threats and resilience strategies on constellation system performance, particularly addressing the combinatorial explosion challenge found in traditional ADC (Availability, Dependability, Capability) model for heterogeneous mega-constellations. The proposed method integrates modeling for availability, recoverability, and performance. Detailed modeling procedures are presented for each component under the scenario of a sudden fragmentation event. In the availability part, a constellation flux method based on phase division is proposed, leveraging the characteristics of debris cloud propagation and constellation configuration. In the recoverability part, a recoverability model is provided grounded in constellation backup strategy. The performance analysis improves a 2D map method, enabling consideration of satellite failures. Finally, a case study of a constellation impacted by a break-up event is conducted for in-depth analysis. The results validate the feasibility of the proposed ARP-based survivability assessment method and its potential for guiding robust constellation design and protection strategies. © © 2025. Authors: Zhang, X.; Sun, D.; Liu, T.; Zhang, B.
Author full names: Zhang, Xiaodong (59763030700); Sun, Daobo (58509550100); Liu, Ting (60015086200); Zhang, Baoqiang (36563026000)
Title: Prediction of blade vibration response with uncertainty propagation using physics-informed neural networks and physics-informed Gaussian process regression
Abstract: Excessive resonance of aeroengine blades threatens airworthiness, making accurate frequency-domain prediction of vibration responses (frequency response functions, FRFs) imperative. We propose a resonance-aware hybrid surrogate—frequency-domain physics-informed neural network–physics-informed Gaussian process regression (PINN–PIGPR)—for FRFs under multi-source uncertainty. The PINN enforces frequency-domain physical constraints at discrete frequencies and outputs the FRF in one shot. A resonance-window strategy identifies the dominant peak and focuses learning on nearby frequencies, improving accuracy in the resonance band. The PINN prediction then serves as the prior mean of a Gaussian process (GP); a residual GP, trained with a composite kernel over the joint (frequency × parameter) space, achieves cross-frequency coherence and per-frequency corrections. The resulting surrogate supports uncertainty propagation via Monte Carlo simulation (MCS). Validation on a 3-DOF mass–spring benchmark and a dovetail-connected blade shows consistent gains over standalone PINN and GPR: lower peak-frequency and peak-amplitude errors, reduced FRF-normalized root-mean-square error (nRMSE), and sustained accuracy in resonance bands. Before uncertainty propagation, we validate the surrogate’s reliability via MCS, where PINN–PIGPR yields reliability estimates closest to the MCS benchmark among the methods compared. These results indicate a physics-consistent, resonance-aware frequency-domain approach suitable for blade health monitoring and frequency-domain safety assessment. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Meng, L.; Xiao, B.; Zhong, Z.; Tian, Y.; Wu, F.
Title: Intelligent identification method for combustion modes in scramjets based on small sample injection strategy
Abstract: The demand for predicting combustion modes in scramjet combustors during the engineering design phase has led to the development of advanced methodologies. This study proposes a few-shot combustion mode recognition (FS-CMR) model based on injection strategies, analyzing equivalence ratio, injection location, and boundary conditions to construct a spatial decision boundary for combustion mode recognition under small-sample conditions. The Flow Field Reconstruction (FFR) algorithm establishes a physical mapping between injection strategy vectors and combustion flow fields, enabling data augmentation. The Few-shot Feedback Augmentation (FFA) algorithm simulates physical disturbances, such as injection strategies and shock wave oscillations, through elastic distortion, spatial feature mixing, and noise injection, expanding the data distribution space. A dynamic feedback mechanism optimizes augmentation effectiveness. An Enhanced Swin Transformer (ESwinT) is proposed to extract combustion mode features, using adaptive aggregation and nonlinear decision boundaries to preserve spatial topological structures and improve wave pattern recognition. A dataset covering Mach numbers 4–7 and various hydrogen injection strategies validates the model. Experimental results show the FS-CMR model achieves 99.15% accuracy, providing a novel approach for scramjet combustor design. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yin, Y.; Wu, T.; Ji, H.; Tao, C.; Zhang, C.; Qiu, J.
Title: Inverse identification of coupled heat flux and random damage on thermal insulation tiles with sparse sensors
Abstract: Conventional inverse analysis schemes for thermal protection tile damage often neglect the coupling between surface heat flux and structural defects, and usually assume that damage occurs at the initial stage, which limits both accuracy and applicability. This study proposes an integrated inverse analysis scheme based on Tikhonov regularization and the Levenberg–Marquardt method, introducing time as an inversion variable. The proposed method enables simultaneous identification of surface heat flux, damage geometry and the time of damage occurrence. By explicitly considering the coupling between heat flux and damage, and by avoiding error accumulation from sequential inversion steps, the scheme substantially improves identification accuracy. Time inclusion also allows detection of damage occurring at arbitrary moments. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the scheme achieves an average relative error of less than 10% in heat flux estimation, and accurately reconstructs the damage geometry. Additionally, it reduces the required number of sensors by 57% compared to a conventional stepwise scheme. Overall, the proposed integrated scheme improves identification performance while reducing the demand for sensors, showing strong potential for application in damage diagnosis of thermal protection systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Mofid, O.; Mobayen, S.; Khodayar, M.
Author full names: Mofid, Omid (57201025805); Mobayen, S. (24822975200); Khodayar, Mahdi E. (57188814131)
Title: Robust fast-tracking of UAVs using command-filtered backstepping sliding mode control with disturbance observer
Abstract: This work investigates UAV fast-tracking under external perturbations and dynamic uncertainty. To achieve the goal of fast-tracking a UAV subjected to dynamic uncertainty and external disturbances, a nonlinear disturbance observer is combined with the command-filtered backstepping sliding mode control process. First, the nonlinear disturbance observer is used to effectively reject dynamic uncertainty and external disturbances, resulting in improved UAV performance when faced with unpredictability and disruptions. Second, the command-filtered backstepping sliding mode control technique is employed to quickly track the UAV, even under unreliable conditions and disturbances. The main advantages of the proposed control strategy are the absence of high-order derivatives in the controller design and the rapid convergence of the tracking error. Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop results, based on hardware-in-the-loop testing using the Speedgoat machine and the MATLAB/Simulink environment, respectively, are presented to validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed control scheme. The goal is to enable the UAV to be quickly tracked in the presence of external disturbances and dynamic uncertainty. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wu, H.; Chen, R.; Ye, Y.; Lou, J.; Yin, Z.; Zhou, S.; You, Y.
Title: Modeling of wall function based on symbolic regression under streamwise pressure gradient
Abstract: Wall function methods significantly reduce the dependence of Computational Fluid Dynamics(CFD) simulations on boundary layer grid density but exhibit considerable limitations in flow problems with pressure gradients. This study employs a symbolic regression approach centered on a multi-population asynchronous evolutionary algorithm for turbulent wall function modeling. It extracts wall function expressions under pressure gradient effects from flow data by introducing a dimensionless pressure gradient as a correction term. The reliability of these expressions is comprehensively evaluated based on interpolation performance, physical significance, as well as degradation and generalization behavior, with comparative analysis conducted against black-box machine learning methods. Results demonstrate that the constructed wall function model achieves velocity distributions closely matching numerical simulations under various pressure gradient conditions, relaxing the first-grid-layer height requirement by two orders of magnitude. Compared to black-box models, symbolic regression demonstrates superior small-sample modeling capability and generalization ability while effectively avoiding issues of trustworthiness and interpretability deficiencies. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yan, M.; Tian, Y.; Li, J.; Song, W.
Title: Research on the influence of self-circulation fluidic control on flow characteristics in a supersonic flow
Abstract: In scramjet engines, the transformation of combustion modes facilitates a wide range of flight operations but complicates the flow characteristics within the isolator. This paper introduces an innovative method called self-circulation fluidic control, which harnesses the advantages of boundary-layer suction and blowing. Based on a freestream Mach number of 2.0, we design controlled structures with varying geometric parameters. To explore the mechanism of self-circulation fluidic control, we investigate the three-dimensional effects of the rectangular isolator, the fundamental characteristics of the self-circulation structure, and the performance of different geometric parameters. The research reveals that, in the uncontrolled state, the asymmetric normal shock train exhibits strong three-dimensional characteristics due to flow separation from the side walls. When the back-pressure ratio is below the critical control value of 3.4, the C-1 self-circulation structure successfully obstructs the forward movement of the shock train, enhancing the isolator's maximum anti-back-pressure performance by 25%. As back pressure increases, the leading edge of the shock train shifts closer to the injection slot, resulting in improved uniformity and airflow quality in the supersonic flow field. Conversely, as back pressure decreases, a flow evolution hysteresis is observed in the isolator, with the leading edge shock displacement extending by 31% at a back-pressure value of 3.0. The distribution and motion behavior of the self-circulation airflow are influenced by the channel gap, channel length, and suction slot angle, which ultimately leads to suboptimal control performance. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Song, H.; Jeong, H.; Yim, J.
Author full names: Song, Hakyoon (60199721800); Jeong, Hyeseung (60199403900); Yim, Jinwoo (57222136228)
Title: Experimental study on aerodynamic interaction of tandem fan-in-body aircraft during transition flight
Abstract: The tandem fan-in-body configuration is a promising concept for urban air mobility application. This study experimentally investigates the aerodynamic interaction effects of this configuration during transition flight, focusing on the interplay among the ducted fans and the airframe. Tests were conducted by varying angles of attack, sideslip angles, and front and rear collective pitch angles at a unit Reynolds number of 2.05 × 10⁶ m⁻¹. Force, moment, and surface pressure measurements were acquired to characterize the global and local aerodynamic behavior. Results reveal that key aerodynamic characteristics of ducted fans—ram drag, nose-up pitching moment, and lip flow separation—remain present at the aircraft level. However, new issues—including loss of pitching moment control, the emergence of a positive rolling moment, and the front ducted fan’s preconditioning effect on the rear ducted fan—were observed. The front ducted fan’s effects remain localized, while the rear ducted fan’s influence extends globally across the airframe, amplifying aerodynamic complexity. The study also reveals that the tandem ducted fans interfere with each other’s performance, with varying degrees of interaction depending on their arrangement relative to the freestream. These findings offer critical insights for improving stability and control in urban air mobility aircraft. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Rostkowski, P.; Meurisse, J.B.E.; Panesi, M.
Author full names: Rostkowski, Przemyslaw (57208304566); Meurisse, Jeremie B.E. (57200959991); Panesi, Marco (22951799900)
Title: Non-Deterministic extension of plasma wind tunnel data calibrated model predictions to flight conditions
Abstract: This work proposes a novel approach for non-deterministic extension of experimental data that considers structural model inadequacy for conditions other than the calibration scenario while simultaneously resolving any significant prior-data discrepancy with information extracted from flight measurements. This functionality is achieved through methodical utilization of model error emulators and Bayesian model averaging studies with available response data. The outlined approach does not require prior flight data availability and introduces straightforward mechanisms for their assimilation in future predictions. Application of the methodology is demonstrated herein by extending material performance data captured at the HyMETS facility to the MSL scenario, where the described process yields results that exhibit significantly improved capacity for predictive uncertainty quantification studies. © © 2025. Authors: Sharmila, P.P.; Samad, A.
Title: Design optimisation of purge flow path in axial aero gas turbine engines using machine learning algorithm
Abstract: In high-pressure transonic axial turbines, the purge flow path is positioned downstream of the nozzle guide vanes. It allows compressor bleed air to flow through, preventing hot air from entering the wheel space. However, this purge flow interacts with the mainstream flow, adding to horseshoe vortices and increasing turbine losses. To address the aerodynamic inefficiencies caused by the purge geometry, numerical simulations using ANSYS have been conducted on a first-stage high-pressure transonic axial turbine with the purge flow path. This paper explores the multi-objective design optimisation of a single radial rim seal with four design variables: seal clearances (A<inf>1</inf>, A<inf>2</inf>, and A<inf>3</inf>) and flow direction (θ). The objectives are to minimise the total pressure loss in the purge flow path and to minimise the maximum temperature at the purge-rotor interface. A design space was developed using Latin Hypercube Sampling, and the Pareto front analysis defines the boundaries for optimised geometries, utilising the machine learning algorithm that runs on MATLAB. Kriging model, a type of surrogate model that works on NSGA -II, is chosen for the study. The results show that the optimised geometries improve sealing and cooling effectiveness, with a 14 % reduction in pressure losses and a 46 % decrease in maximum temperature compared to the baseline case. This optimisation enhances turbine performance through improved flow interaction and sealing efficiency. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, Y.; Dai, L.; Dong, J.
Title: Dual-coordinated optimal anti-saturation control for air-breathing hypersonic vehicles with interval-based barrier function under asymmetric AOA constraint and elevator saturation
Abstract: During longitudinal maneuvers of air-breathing hypersonic vehicles, intake efficiency is severely impacted by an improper angle of attack (AOA). A dual-coordinated strategy is proposed to address the challenges of asymmetric AOA constraint, elevator saturation, model uncertainty, and other strong nonlinearities. The coordination across planning and control layers is designed. At the planning layer, the AOA command is dynamically adjusted by an explicit reference governor. In contrast to model-based predictors, low-computation rules are employed without a precise model to enforce the AOA constraint and suppress chattering. Within the control layer, a basic robust anti-saturation tracking controller is first designed via dynamic surface control integrated with disturbance observers, so that accurate tracking of velocity and altitude is ensured under disturbances and elevator saturation. Furthermore, a novel interval-based barrier function is designed to quantify the AOA risk. The resulting controller is activated only when the AOA deviates from the safe interval, representing a fundamental distinction from always-active schemes and leading to enhanced tracking performance. Simulation results demonstrate that all closed-loop signals remain bounded, with a 3.14% improvement in AOA utilization efficiency compared to comparative methods, confirming advantages in constraint satisfaction and control smoothness. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kumar, R.; Velpula, P.; Ghosh, A.
Author full names: Kumar, Rajesh (58733039900); Velpula, Pranav (60199663000); Ghosh, Amardip (53876040000)
Title: Delayed response of isolator shocks to sudden changes in combustor pressure
Abstract: The response of pseudoshocks to sudden changes in combustor pressure is studied numerically in a 2D, cavity-based, scramjet combustor with a Mach 2 inflow, burning GH2 in vitiated air. The sudden change in combustor pressure is achieved by throttling the fuel flow rate through a transversely mounted fuel injector. The results are compared with pseudoshock response in a synthetic isolator comprising of a 2D rectangular channel where the pressure at the exit boundary could be numerically imposed. For both configurations, a finite delay in the response of pseudo-shocks to changes in exit pressure is observed. For an increase (or decrease) in exit pressure, a compression wave (or an expansion wave) is found to propagate upstream with individual shock cells responding sequentially as the incident and reflected waves pass by. Only when the compression (or rarefaction) waves arrive at the lead shock, the shock train extends upstream (or retracts downstream). The combustor-integrated isolator is seen to exhibit strong combustor-isolator coupling. For example, a sudden decrease in fuel flow rate for this isolator causes a downstream excursion of the lead shock, at the end of which, it momentarily moves upstream before settling into its final position. xt-diagrams and pressure plots show that this upstream movement of the pseudo-shock correlates with a momentary rise in combustor pressure, possibly resulting from an increase in fuel penetration as pseudoshocks retract towards the injector. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hong, S.H.; Kim, Y.J.; Cho, M.H.; Jung, S.N.
Author full names: Hong, Seong Hyun (57223093048); Kim, Young-jin (59622690400); Cho, Min-hyung (59543010000); Jung, Sungnam (7403676704)
Title: Vibration reduction of coaxial rotorcraft using multicyclic controller with fluid-structure coupling
Abstract: This study investigates the active vibration reduction of a coaxial rotor system in high-speed forward flight using an individual blade control (IBC) scheme with multicyclic control strategy. A high-fidelity simulation framework is developed based on a loosely coupling between a compressible three-dimensional computational fluid dynamics (CFD) solver and a comprehensive aeromechanics (CA) analysis program. The rotorcraft analysis model adopts an isolated rotor model and is validated against flight test data, resulting in reasonable agreements in predicting the rotor hub vibratory loads. Through the open-loop control, the effective actuation frequencies are identified which are used to design a closed-loop multicyclic controller. The closed-loop controller implemented using the identified system is demonstrated to significantly reduce the rotor hub vibration. A maximum reduction up to 84.7% in vibratory hub loads is achieved in reference to the uncontrolled case. The analysis results reveal that the multicyclic IBC actuation, integrated using CFD/CA-based analysis, provides an accurate and robust solution for the suppression of the rotor hub vibration for a lift-offset (L.O.) coaxial rotor system operating in high-speed flights. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Author full names: Liu, Chuang (55017803100); Luo, Yijie (58943523000); Yue, Xiaokui (9232396000); Capello, Elisa (26028530200)
Title: A hierarchical optimization approach for spacecraft swarm trajectories considering dynamic communication topologies
Abstract: This paper investigates the spacecraft swarm trajectory planning problem in the context of on-orbit assembly missions. Considering the limited communication equipment capacity, communication delay, limited control input, and inter-member interference in a spacecraft swarm, a hierarchical autonomous trajectory planning approach based on dynamic communication topology is developed. To address the problems of communication latency and communication resource waste within swarm in practical engineering applications, an improved Prim algorithm based on communication capacity priority criteria is developed to achieve a more reasonable swarm communication topology that satisfies multiple constraints. Furthermore, a hierarchical framework trajectory planning approach based on the crayfish-convex optimization algorithm (CCOA) is developed to achieve communication maintenance and safe collision avoidance among swarm members. This approach combines the solving speed of COA with the global optimization capability of convex optimization, achieving complementary advantages. Finally, numerical simulations are performed to verify the effectiveness and feasibility of the designed approach in communication topology optimization and trajectory planning. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, L.; He, M.
Title: Study on design of internal flowpath and dynamic characteristics of circulation actuator with dual-way offset at the inlet
Abstract: Circulation Control is an effective method for enhancing aerodynamic performance and flight control. The circulation actuator is a key aerodynamic component for achieving effective and efficient circulation control. This paper aims to develop a circulation actuator for a specific aircraft under stringent geometric space constraints. Based on the requirements for dual-channel fluidic actuation under these integration constraints, a dual-channel circulation actuator design with dual-way offset inlets is proposed. A study was conducted on the design of a separation-free flow path, the comprehensive development of a lightweight aerodynamic structure, and the dynamic response characteristics of the actuator. The results show that for a spanwise length of 300 mm, an aspect ratio of 1.5, and a jet outlet width-to-height ratio of 1000, the internal flow path of the proposed actuator achieves a separation-free flow. At input pressure ratios of 1.7 and 3.0, the maximum deviations in total pressure distribution at the actuator outlet are 1.05% and 0.98%, respectively. The combination of external stiffening ribs and built-in distributed ribs in the straight section effectively meets the design requirements for strength, stiffness, and lightweight structure within the installation space. Under a sinusoidal actuation signal, the maximum amplitude error between the input and actuator output is 2.6%, and the maximum phase difference is 10.8°. For a step actuation signal, the peak time is approximately 0.0015 s, the response delay is about 0.0100 s, and the maximum overshoot is approximately 39% of the step signal amplitude. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tang, X.Z.; Lee, J.R.; Hoi, S.M.
Author full names: Tang, Xiang Zhe (60196368600); Lee, Junrong (57192440334); Hoi, Sumin (57208665398)
Title: CFD analysis with Low-Fidelity design approach of Fixed-Wing unmanned VTOL aerial vehicle (UAV) body frame
Abstract: With the growing importance of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) in both commercial and defense sectors, there is an increasing demand for efficient and versatile platforms that address the limitations of multi-rotor UAVs. While conventional fixed-wing UAVs offers superior aerodynamic performance, they are limited by their dependence on runway for take-off and landing. Fixed-Wing UAVs with Vertical Take-off and Landing (VTOL) feature serve as an excellent solution that balances between the efficiency of fixed-wing UAVs and the versatility of multi-rotors UAVs. However, research on fixed-wing VTOL UAVs remains limited, particularly regarding systematic aerodynamic baselining prior to VTOL integration. This study addresses this gap by investigating the key design parameters associated with the aerodynamic performance of a fixed wing VTOL UAV body frame using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations in Ansys Fluent. The effects of airfoil selection, tapered wings, swept wings, washout wingtips, winglet installation, and the integration of canard swept wing configurations on aerodynamic performance, with the lift-to-drag ratio and stall angle as primary metrics are analyzed. Results show that a tapered wing with a ratio of λ=0.3 provides optimal efficiency, a Non-Swept wing offers superior lift, and a 6∘ Wingtip washout configuration outperforms conventional winglets. Furthermore, a 45∘Canard Swept wing significantly enhances stall characteristics by promoting flow reattachment. By consolidating these results into a validated aerodynamic design framework, this study forms a baseline body-frame configuration for medium-altitude long-endurance (MALE) UAVs, in establishing a foundation for future integration of VTOL features and refinement through high-fidelity modeling and experimental validation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, X.; Tian, S.; Li, H.; Yuan, Y.; Dai, J.; Wang, H.
Title: Prediction and experimental validation of temperature-time coupled compression recovery performance in high-temperature seals
Abstract: Thermal protection is a critical issue to be addressed for high-speed aircraft. High-temperature seals based on composite materials can effectively seal gaps between different components of high-speed aircraft and are widely used in high-speed flight applications. These seals exhibit favorable compression-recovery characteristics under high-temperature conditions, allowing them to compress and rebound in response to external load variations, thereby filling structural gaps and providing effective thermal insulation. However, long-term exposure to high-temperature environments leads to gradual degradation of seal performance, which may result in thermal sealing failure of high-speed aircraft and cause serious accidents. Therefore, accurate prediction of the compression-recovery behavior of high-temperature seals is essential to ensure reliable thermal sealing in high-speed aircraft. Current research on high-temperature seals primarily focuses on experimental testing and statistical analysis of compression-recovery properties, without establishing the relationship between compression-recovery characteristics and compression time or operating temperature. Furthermore, existing studies fail to describe the hysteresis behavior caused by plastic deformation during cyclic compression-recovery processes. As a result, the prediction accuracy remains insufficient to meet practical application requirements. In this study, a combined approach of finite element simulation and experimental investigation is employed to conduct a detailed analysis of the compression-recovery process of high-temperature seals. The nonlinear and hysteresis characteristics during the recovery process are identified and validated, and the variation patterns of compression-recovery properties with temperature and compression time are summarized. Based on these findings, a prediction method for the compression-recovery behavior of high-temperature seals is proposed using fuzzy evidence theory. By constructing fuzzy sets, high-precision interpolation and evidence theory-based fusion of compression-recovery data are achieved, enabling accurate prediction of the seal behavior. Comparison with experimental results shows a prediction error of only 5.2 %. Moreover, the proposed method improves prediction accuracy by 46.9 % and 73.7 % compared to traditional neural network and reference database algorithms, respectively, significantly outperforming existing methods. The research findings provide critical support for the reliable application of high-temperature seals in the aerospace field. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Akgün, G.; Kurtaran, H.
Title: Free vibration of laminated composite super-elliptic shells using generalized differential quadrature method
Abstract: Laminated composite shell structures are extensively utilized in many engineering applications due to their lightweight structure, low cost and satisfactory mechanical properties. This study presents, for the first time, an investigation of free vibrational characteristics of a complex shell structure which will be referred as super-elliptic (SE) shell structure made of laminated composite material. Unlike conventional cylindrical and elliptical shell formulations, SE shell formulation is capable of representing both cylindrical, elliptical and quasi rectangular shell structures. First order shear deformation theory (FSDT) and virtual work principle are employed to derive the equation of motion and compute the deformations. Spatial derivatives are computed via generalized differential quadrature (GDQ) method. Standard eigenvalue problem is solved to extract natural frequencies and mode shapes. Effects of ellipticity, ovality of SE shell structure, layer orientation and boundary conditions on free vibrational behavior are examined in detail. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kim, Y.; Park, J.; Kim, S.; Choi, O.; Kim, D.-C.
Author full names: Kim, Yongha (57191525609); Park, Jaehyeon (60196342800); Kim, Sungchan (58464943600); Choi, Oyoung (60196147100); Kim, Dong-chul (60196342900)
Title: Thermo-structural optimization of multiple composite layers with ablative materials and fasteners in passive thermal protection systems
Abstract: This paper presents an approximate method for the thermo-structural analysis of multiple composite layers incorporating ablative materials and fasteners, intended for passive thermal protection systems. This method utilizes a semi-analytical finite element method and incorporates custom subroutines to accurately simulate the progressive decomposition of pyrolyzing materials. Material properties were assessed through specimen testing. Optimization of the composite layers with ablative materials and fasteners was conducted to minimize mass per unit area, integrating both the proposed method and test results. The optimized multiple composite layers with ablative materials and fasteners have a mass per unit area of approximately 71.61 kg/m2, and the failure indices and temperature of structural layer satisfy the specified strength and temperature criteria. Furthermore, a detailed investigation into the thermo-structural performance of these layers was carried out using 3D finite element analysis. The findings offer valuable insights into the thermo-structural behavior of composite layers with ablative materials and fasteners in passive thermal protection systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.
