Authors: Zhou, H.; Wang, M.; Gan, Q.; Wang, X.; Cui, N.
Author full names: Zhou, Hong Yu (57218089143); Wang, Minzhao (60195931300); Gan, Qingzhong (59525623800); Wang, Xiaogang (56013451100); Cui, Naigang (57204331860)
Title: Online reentry trajectory planning for reusable rockets based on intelligent phase transition
Abstract: This paper researches the online three-dimensional entry trajectory optimization for the reusable rocket. Unlike the majority of existing studies that focus on the terminal vertical landing phase, typically within the altitude range of 0 to 4 kilometers, this paper extends the trajectory to a broader range, from 4 to 80 kilometers. The wider range leads to greater nonlinearity and coupling, presenting a comprehensive multi-phase trajectory optimization framework. Unlike other studies following the typical reentry optimization scheme for glide reusable vehicles, we take account of the actual powered and unpowered descending process and propose an online multi-phase hybrid optimization strategy. The reentry process is first divided into three subphases, that is the powered descent phase, the low dynamic pressure descent phase, and the aerodynamic descent phase. The first subphase is designed for decelerating the rocket with the minimum cost of propellant, which is accomplished with model predictive control. The second subphase approximates free falling for the engine shuts down and the aerodynamic drag force is tiny. The third subphase finally satisfies strict terminal state constraints by quickly solving a nonlinear planning problem based on a novel return profile and a set of analytic solutions. Meanwhile, determining the time of phase-transition is crucial to multi-phase trajectory optimization. We solve this issue using the maximum entropy reinforcement learning method based on a developed fast reachable area prediction algorithm. On the basis of Falcon 9 (Version 1.2, Block 5), we carry out numerical simulation with the presence of random disturbances to demonstrate the method performance. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Silva, J.C.; Brojo, F.
Author full names: Silva, João C. (59418376500); Brojo, Francisco P. (7801411528)
Title: Effects of swirl injection on detonation wave dynamics and rotating detonation engine performance
Abstract: Injection systems play a crucial role in the performance and stability of rotating detonation engines (RDEs), influencing detonation wave propagation, fuel mixing, and thrust generation. CFD provides an efficient and cost-effective method to study these complex phenomena, enabling the simplification of real-world injector behavior and the exploration of novel injection strategies. This study employs numerical simulations to investigate the effects of swirl injection on RDE operation, analyzing flowfield characteristics, combustion stability, and engine performance under varying injection angles. Small swirl angles ( ± 10°) minimally disrupt detonation waves, while larger angles ( ± 45°and ± 60°) introduce instability, including wave splitting and deflagrative losses. Swirl injection significantly reduces thrust (by over 50 %) and specific impulse (by 17 %). However, it offers a potential method for thrust modulation, with trade-offs in stabilization time and combustion efficiency. The study also highlights asymmetrical effects between positive and negative injection angles, with positive angles yielding higher wave velocities but requiring longer stabilization periods. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Zhang, P.; Yang, P.; Du, J.
Author full names: Zhang, Peng (57194346577); Yang, Pengcheng (60196056300); Du, Junping (59975619300)
Title: Mechanism of corner separation control via some bio-inspired slanting riblets in a highly loaded compressor cascade
Abstract: Corner separation in compressor cascades is a highly nonlinear flow phenomenon that significantly degrades aerodynamic performance and operational stability. This study numerically investigates a novel passive flow control method—bionic slanting riblets—for suppressing this complex, nonlinear flow separation in a high-speed, high-load NACA65-K48 compressor cascade. The effects of key riblet parameters, including height, yaw angle, and lateral placement, are systematically evaluated using Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) and Scale-Adaptive Simulation (SAS) approaches. The results demonstrate that optimally configured slanting riblets effectively suppress corner separation over the entire stable incidence range, reducing the mass-averaged total pressure loss coefficient by up to -5.23 % and increasing the static pressure recovery coefficient by up to 8.40 %. Formed through the nonlinear aggregation of small-scale vortices in multi-riblet channels, the resulting large-scale vortices enhance momentum transfer within the endwall boundary layer via nonlinear interactions, thereby restraining and delaying the accumulation of low-energy fluid and causing a portion of it to separate and shed earlier near the suction surface trailing edge, which consequently delays the formation of corner separation, weakens the reverse flow in the suction-side corner region, and reduces overall flow blockage. Transient analysis via Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) reveals that the slanting riblets effectively suppress the development of the passage vortex, nearly eliminating its characteristic high-frequency unsteady fluctuations. Simultaneously, the slanting riblets nonlinearly alter the structure and energy characteristics of the concentrated shedding vortex, resulting in a downstream shift of its initial position, a significant reduction in amplitude, and an increase in frequency, which collectively enhance flow stability. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Du, W.; Chen, Z.; Luo, L.; Zhou, H.; Yan, H.
Title: Three-dimensional conjugate heat transfer analysis on effects of impingement-hole arrangement and trailing-edge-pin-fin diameter on a novel double-wall turbine blade
Abstract: In this study, advanced cooling methods (double-wall effusion cooling and pin-finned slot) are applied to a novel turbine blade. Conjugate heat transfer simulations are performed on the 3D turbine blade to investigate the effects of impingement-hole arrangement and trailing-edge-pin-fin diameter through flow field and heat transfer performance under a certain set of boundary conditions. Numerical results indicate that the impingement channel’s turbulence offsets spanwise pressure reduction, controlling effusion-hole mass flow difference within 8 % and improving cooling effectiveness uniformity via periodic impingement hole mass flow. Leading-edge impingement hole position shifts spanwise maximum cooling effectiveness and affects local heat transfer; trailing-edge cooling relies on pin-fins, with larger pin-fins boosting vorticity, convective heat transfer (up to 10 %), and cooling uniformity. Limitations include uniform solid material assumption, and future work may explore transient characteristics and expanded boundary conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yoon, D.; Jung, K.-W.; Jeong, E.-T.; Lee, C.-H.
Author full names: Yoon, Dain (57233237200); Jung, Ki-wook (57444468600); Jeong, Euitaek (57579252500); Lee, Changhun (57203055994)
Title: Uncertainty-Aware adaptive sampling in MPPI for robust UAV path-Following
Abstract: Accurate path-following for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) in the presence of unknown external disturbances remains a significant challenge. This paper proposes an uncertainty-aware Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control framework that enhances robustness by leveraging Gaussian Process Regression (GPR). Unlike conventional approaches that primarily use the mean prediction of a disturbance model, our method actively incorporates the predictive variance from the GPR. This uncertainty measure is directly integrated into the MPPI formulation to adaptively scale the sampling distribution. This mechanism widens the sampling distribution in response to high prediction uncertainty, enabling the controller to explore a wider range of control actions. A theoretical analysis reveals that this scheme is equivalent to adaptively penalizing the control effort in the optimal control cost function. To ensure computational efficiency for practical implementation, a guidance law is embedded within the MPPI, and a Kalman filter-based GPR is employed. Simulation results with a 6-DOF quadrotor model demonstrate that the proposed controller significantly outperforms baseline methods in tracking accuracy and control energy efficiency under complex wind conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Tang, M.; Zong, H.; Wu, Y.; Wu, X.; Kong, Y.
Title: Effect of the spanwise pulsed spark discharge array on supersonic boundary-layer tripping
Abstract: In this study, a spanwise pulsed spark discharge array (SP-PSDA) is employed to force laminar boundary-layer tripping in a Mach 3.0 flat-plate flow. High spatio-temporal resolution nanoparticle planar laser scattering is utilized for flow visualization, enabling the observation of fine-scale structures in the boundary-layer evolution under plasma actuation. The results demonstrate that the SP-PSDA effectively triggers supersonic boundary-layer tripping at an actuation frequency of 8 kHz and an energy deposition of 20mJ. The process is dominated by artificial hairpin vortex-like structure, which evolve from plasma-induced thermal bulbs and play a critical role in forced boundary-layer transition. Furthermore, the effect of energy deposition and actuation frequency on boundary-layer tripping is investigated. Reduced energy deposition yields smaller initial thermal bulb scales, hindering the formation of artificial hairpin vortex-like structure and leading to unsuccessful tripping. Additionally, when the actuation frequency lies entirely outside the neutral curve, artificial hairpin vortex-like structure can still be generated, but effective boundary-layer tripping is not achieved. Conversely, when the actuation frequency partially or fully falls within the neutral curve, higher frequencies correlate with an increased likelihood of successful boundary-layer tripping. The frequency dependence indicates that there may exist other disturbances that may play equally important roles in boundary-layer tripping. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Shang, Y.; Du, W.; Luo, L.; Li, M.; Yan, H.
Title: Mechanism of turbine internal flow based on a high degree of freedom non-axisymmetric end wall modeling method
Abstract: The non-axisymmetric end wall contouring technique is an effective method for reducing secondary flow losses. This study aims to investigate the influence mechanism of non-axisymmetric end wall contouring on the internal flow characteristics of a highly loaded turbine rotor blade. To this end, a non-axisymmetric end wall contouring program based on Bézier surfaces has been developed, which offers a high degree of freedom and precise control. The end wall of the prototype was segmented along the flow direction, and the region from the leading edge to the trailing edge was selected for contouring. A surrogate model of the contouring program was then constructed with the objective of minimizing the total pressure loss coefficient to identify the optimal contouring scheme. Results show that the surrogate model constructed using this method can accurately predict the total pressure loss coefficient for different designs. The optimal design reduces the adverse pressure gradient in the mid-passage and decreases the size of the passage vortices, which led to a 0.5 % reduction in the total pressure loss coefficient compared to the prototype. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Lee, J.; Kim, S.; Jo, H.; Lee, A.; Kang, H.
Author full names: Lee, Jeongrak (58017228300); Kim, Seonghyeon (57196227781); Jo, Hanseong (58341193900); Lee, Anna (57188742999); Kang, Hongjae (56939558600)
Title: Plasma-assisted N2O/CH4 combustion in a rotating gliding arc thruster under ultra-lean conditions (Oxidizer-to-Fuel Ratio 5–1000)
Abstract: Plasma-assisted propulsion in space missions can promote new reaction pathways and reduce the activation energy, thereby enabling stable ignition and extended combustion limits under ultra-lean conditions, but it has not been applied to N<inf>2</inf>O-based premixed propellant thrusters. We investigated the effects of plasma-assisted combustion on an N<inf>2</inf>O/CH<inf>4</inf>-based propulsion system by introducing a rotating gliding arc (RGA)-based plasma thruster and analyzing its performance across an extensive oxidizer-to-fuel ratio (ROF) range (5–1000). The RGA enables instantaneous, catalyst-free ignition and N<inf>2</inf>O decomposition while maintaining a stable arc discharge at high pressure. Atmospheric flame visualization showed that RGA increased flame intensity and enabled stable combustion by extending the flammability limit into the ultra-lean region (ROF ≈ 1000). High-pressure plasma-assisted propulsion tests confirmed performance improvements through plasma-assisted combustion and N<inf>2</inf>O decomposition, particularly at ROFs above 50. Notably, as the applied power and after-chamber length increased, the propulsion performance enhancements became more pronounced in the ultra-lean region. Within the experimental range, efficiency improvements correlated positively with both the applied power and after-chamber length. With the longest chamber (70 mm), the system exceeded the theoretical characteristic velocity of the N<inf>2</inf>O monopropellant (1102.2 m/s) at ROFs below 300 with <200 W of applied power. At an ROF of 1000, it achieved a characteristic velocity of 967.7 m/s and 87.8 % efficiency. These findings highlight the potential of the proposed RGA-based thruster as a catalyst-free, rapidly responsive system with enhanced operational safety, offering strong promise as a next-generation green propulsion solution in an ultra-lean, premixed monopropellant configuration. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Sopek, T.; Zander, F.; Birch, B.; Buttsworth, D.
Author full names: Sopek, Tamara (56247394600); Zander, Fabian (36620657200); Birch, Byrenn J.C. (57204557603); Buttsworth, David R. (7004095560)
Title: Development of neuromorphic event-based imaging spectroscopy for hypersonic flight observation
Abstract: Enhanced capability to collect vital hypersonic flight data is required to better understand the physics of the flow around spacecraft entering Earth’s atmosphere. Daytime recording, while possible, remains particularly challenging and often requires compromises due to saturation and noise in this environment. Using novel, bio-inspired event-based cameras allows spectral measurements to be performed equally well during both day and night, without modifications to instrumentation - overcoming a major current limitation associated with daytime tests. Combining these cameras with high-resolution spectroscopy enables more reliable collection of critical data, such as temperature and species composition in the flow around re-entering spacecraft. Measurements were performed using a novel neuromorphic spectroscopy system and a range of light sources. These tests were conducted in the 400–900 nm spectral region to match the operational range of the event-based camera. The acquired data show that both broadband and discrete line spectra were obtained. Results for dynamic range, daytime performance, and long-range spectroscopy demonstrate improved performance of the event-based system compared to the conventional cameras typically used under similar conditions. This work demonstrates, for the first time, that a spectroscopy system based on neuromorphic technology is capable of successfully recording emission spectra. These results represent a landmark step toward developing advanced spectroscopy diagnostics for hypersonic flight observation, including Earth re-entry missions. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Sharma, V.; Kumar, P.
Title: Numerical investigation of thermal load on liquid rocket engine base plate
Abstract: In a stationary laboratory environment, rocket base plates are primarily heated by plume radiation. This study analyzes base plate heating in rockets by considering both convective and radiative heat transfer from the exhaust plume. Convective loads are determined through coupled fluid flow and combustion modeling, while radiative heat transfer is assessed using the Planck mean absorption coefficient and the Spectral Line Weighted-sum-of-gray-gases model (SLW). An in-house numerical subroutine is developed and integrated with a computational fluid dynamics solver for comprehensive analysis. The Space Shuttle Main Engine is examined at three altitudes - sea level, mid-altitude, and high altitude to evaluate the effect of plume expansion on base heating. Results reveal significant variations in base plate temperature predictions between radiation models across different altitudes. A comparative study between gray and non-gray models demonstrates that the Planck mean model overpredicts temperatures compared to the more accurate SLW model. Recirculation zones behind the base plate at sea level and high altitude reduce local temperatures; however, at mid-altitude, recirculation is confined to the nozzle exit region. The base plate temperature generally decreases with increasing altitude because of the pressure dependence of the emission coefficient, but increases at high altitude due to enhanced view factors between the expanded plume and the base plate. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yang, Z.; Xu, Y.; Huo, Z.; Chen, W.; Gao, M.; Feng, Y.; Li, X.; Wu, H.
Title: CA-DSMC: A conditional adaptive strategy for spacecraft station-keeping based on a state-triggered mechanism
Abstract: To address the problem of spacecraft deviating from their nominal orbits due to complex nonlinear perturbations in the deep space environment, this paper investigates a station-keeping strategy using impulsive thrust. A state-triggered conditional adaptive station-keeping algorithm is designed, which formulates a state-triggered mechanism to flexibly select the control reaching mode. This approach actively prevents control undershoot and overshoot, thereby balancing the trade-off between station-keeping cost and orbital state deviation. An adaptive sliding mode reaching law is employed, which incorporates real-time changes in the orbital state to ensure effective error convergence and suppress the chattering phenomenon during the steady sliding phase. Concurrently, to address the issue of unknown nonlinear disturbances degrading the precision of station-keeping control, a feedback iterative algorithm is developed. This algorithm reduces the post-maneuver error to a desired threshold, effectively mitigating the influence of a priori errors on subsequent control decisions. Under the same environmental constraints, the proposed conditional adaptive discrete sliding mode control (CA-DSMC) algorithm exhibits lower station-keeping costs and a faster error convergence speed compared to the target point method, the discrete linear quadratic regulator (DLQR) method, and a sliding mode method with a single control law. Further experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithm enhances the control system's flexibility and robustness while ensuring low maintenance costs. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Aslanov, V.S.
Title: A novel scenario of two-impulse Moon-Planet transfer
Abstract: The paper proposes a new scenario of two-impulse Moon-Planet transfer. The acceleration impulse is applied when the spacecraft starts from the moon’s surface and the braking impulse is applied when the spacecraft reaches a predefined point on the line connecting the centers of mass of the planet and the moon. After that, the spacecraft free-falls to the planet's surface. The equations of motion in the framework of the planar elliptic restricted three-body problem and the planet atmospheric entry dynamic equations are used to model the spacecraft motion. Numerical simulations for arbitrarily chosen trajectories demonstrate the feasibility of the new scenario for two-impulse Phobos-Mars and Moon-Earth transfers. For the Earth-Moon system, the total velocity increment for the proposed two-impulse transfer flight is comparable and the Moon-Earth transfer time is reduced by more than half compared to a traditional transfer that involves a near-lunar orbit. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, F.; Ye, L.; Wang, L.; Gao, W.; Zhou, T.; Li, G.; Liu, C.
Title: Decoupled assessment of the effect of upstream jets on downstream film cooling on a vane
Abstract: Film cooling is one of the most effective cooling methods for turbine vanes. Multi-row holes are usually used to inject coolant to reduce the vane surface temperature. The upstream film jet has both flow interference and cooling superposition on the downstream film jet. How to decouple and quantify these two effects is particularly important for conducting refined thermal analysis of turbine vanes. In this study, a double row of holes was set up on the pressure surface. By changing the cooling medium, the flow interference effect of the upstream jet on the downstream jet was separated. Then, the heat and mass transfer analogy is used to convert the concentration mixing process into a heat transfer process, thereby realizing the decoupling analysis of flow interference and cooling superposition. The study revealed that the injection of the upstream jet slightly improved the cooling effect behind the downstream hole. The hole spacing (p/d) and row spacing (S/d) affect the flow interference of the upstream film jet on the downstream film jet. Increasing the hole spacing reduces the flow interference of adjacent jets, and the influence of the upstream jet begins to appear. Increasing the row spacing causes the upstream film jet to dissipate more severely before reaching the downstream film hole, and its influence on the downstream film jet gradually decreases. When using laidback fan-shaped holes, the flow interference of the upstream jet on the downstream jet reduces the cooling effectiveness when the cooling superposition is still the main reason for the cooling gain. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Jia, X.; Dong, X.; Zhang, Q.; Zhang, Y.; Lu, X.
Title: Mechanisms and evolution of flutter stability for a transonic fan under linear reynolds number variations
Abstract: This study employs high-fidelity numerical simulations to systematically investigate the effect of linearly varying inlet Reynolds numbers (2.23×105∼1.78×106) on the flutter stability of the first bending mode for a wide-chord transonic fan. Analysis based on the energy method, influence coefficient method, and traveling wave method reveals that the Reynolds number significantly restructures the flow field, unsteady pressure response, and flutter stability. The results show that as the Reynolds number decreases linearly, the amplitude of aerodynamic damping attenuates synchronously, while the dominant traveling wave mode for flutter remains unchanged, with the most unstable nodal diameter (ND) consistently at 2ND. The pressure side and suction side exhibit markedly different responses to Reynolds number variations. On the suction side, shock forward movement and separation zone expansion increase the range of negative damping but reduce its intensity, with this range-intensity coupling effect maintaining a linear response. In contrast, the pressure side experiences forward movement and enhanced radial migration of the tip leakage flow, which restructures the unsteady pressure phase near the leading edge, leading to synchronous attenuation in both the intensity and range of positive aerodynamic damping and resulting in a nonlinear response. Governed by the pressure side behavior, the flutter stability exhibits strong nonlinearity with Reynolds number variation. Notably, this study is the first to observe a " low Reynolds number flutter phenomenon " when the Reynolds number drops below 4.46×105 characterized by local reversal of aerodynamic damping phase within the suction side recirculation zone. This phenomenon is closely associated with intensified laminarization and falls outside the predictive scope of existing high-Reynolds flutter models. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xie, J.; Wang, X.; Li, X.; Cai, J.; Zhao, Y.
Author full names: Xie, Juanzhang (57920411600); Wang, Xizhen (58546038400); Li, Xueshun (59519982000); Cai, Jinxu (60071003800); Zhao, Yongjun (57221285258)
Title: A Data-Driven Approach for Reliability Assessment and Remaining Life prediction of aero-engines based on a time-transformed bivariate wiener process and Copula dependency structure
Abstract: A data-driven degradation analysis based on stochastic processes is conducted for two types of degradation data from a specific aero-engine. A multivariate Wiener process is employed to characterize the stochastic degradation increments, with a particular focus on modeling the dependency between the degradation processes of two performance characteristics. Four types of Copula functions are used to construct the dependency structures. For parameter estimation, a Bayesian inference approach based on Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) is adopted. This method not only addresses the computational challenges associated with high-dimensional parameter estimation but also mitigates the error accumulation typically encountered when performing correlation modeling after separate single-feature degradation modeling. Furthermore, a model selection strategy is applied to determine the optimal model for aero-engine reliability assessment. Based on the established framework, a remaining useful life (RUL) prediction model is proposed, and the accuracy of the coupled model is validated through error analysis. The results demonstrate that the RUL prediction accuracy is acceptable and supports precise assessment of both the reliability and remaining lifetime of the studied aero-engine. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Teng, D.; Lu, C.; Fei, C.; Zeng, N.
Title: SEP-MRS: Synchronous enhanced PCE combining moving regression strategy for multi-objective reliability evaluation of aerospace structural systems
Abstract: To improve the problem of insufficient multi-objective reliability evaluating accuracy and efficiency of aerospace structural systems, the synchronous enhanced polynomial chaos expansions combining moving regression strategy (SEP-MRS, short for) is presented based on the matrix analytical theory, moving regression strategy, polynomial chaos expansions (PCE), Bayesian optimization technique, and Copula thought. In this approach, the matrix analytical thought is utilized to synchronously establish a multi-objective output response model; the moving regression strategy is applied to select effective training samples for local approximation; the PCE is employed as a basis function for capturing global features; the Copula thought is used to construct the correlation multi-objective reliability evaluation model. Besides, the multi-objective benchmark function, engine exhaust gas temperature, and turbine blade multi-failures are served as numerical and engineering cases to validate effectiveness of the developed SEP-MRS method. The results demonstrate that the SEP-MRS method has certain advantages in modeling characteristics (i.e., modeling efficiency and accuracy) and reliability evaluation performances (i.e., evaluation efficiency and precision) by comparing various approaches. The contribution of this paper is to provide powerful theoretical guidance for the long-life and high-reliability design of aerospace structural systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, C.; Jiao, G.; Li, J.; Wang, Y.; Li, J.; Song, W.
Title: Control-oriented experimental investigation of combustion mode transition hysteresis in dual-mode scramjet
Abstract: To ensure the safe operation of dual-mode scramjet across the entire flight envelope and to avoid instability working conditions such as inlet unstart or flameout, it is important to achieve a stable and controllable combustion mode transition. This study experimentally compares and analyzes the effects of two control strategies on the mode transition: open-loop control and wall-pressure-based closed-loop control. The open-loop control experiment achieves mode transition by linearly increasing or decreasing the equivalence ratio (ER). In contrast, the closed-loop experiment uses the optimally selected wall pressure as the controlled variable and performs real-time feedback control by linearly increasing or decreasing the expected wall pressure. Experimental results indicate that during open-loop control, two distinct hysteresis loops were observed as ER increase and decrease: a smaller hysteresis loop corresponding to the transition between pure scramjet and dual-mode scramjet combustion, and a larger one corresponding to the transition between dual-mode scramjet and ramjet combustion. Compared to the open-loop control case, the closed-loop control strategy significantly reduced the width of the hysteresis loops. Further investigations indicate that a higher ramp rate of the wall pressure setpoint leads to a wider hysteresis loop, and reducing the rate causes the transition paths during increasing and decreasing phases to converge. This phenomenon is reasonably explained from the perspective of control bandwidth. Overall, the wall-pressure-based closed-loop control demonstrates superior dynamic stability and effectively mitigates the hysteresis behavior during combustion mode transition in dual-mode scramjet. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, H.; Chen, Y.; Wang, Q.; Jin, H.; Huang, Y.; You, Y.
Title: Study on the flow and thrust performance of multi-ducted TBCC engine with ejector-rocket
Abstract: In order to investigate the cooperation characteristics and adjustment strategy of the ejector-rocket in the takeoff and acceleration climbing process of the combined-cycle engine, the numerical simulations of a full-flowpath model are carried out based on a specific multi-ducted TBCC configuration. The influence of the rocket mass flow rate on the intake, flow, and thrust performance of the engine under different flight conditions is analyzed. Moreover, the cooperative work strategy of the rocket and turbine in the takeoff and acceleration climbing process is eventually obtained. Results suggest that during zero-speed startup, pressure differentials induced by the ejector-rocket operation reduce the intake of turbine channel, with a maximum observed deficit of 11.7 %. Furthermore, cooperation between the turbine and ejector-rocket at flight Mach number M <inf>∞</inf> = 0 and 0.3 results in thrust losses, peaking at 16.7 %. Nevertheless, at transonic conditions ( M <inf>∞</inf> = 0.8 and 1.2), the cooperation between the turbine and the ejector-rocket with elevated rocket mass flow yields net thrust augmentation, reaching a maximum increase of 11.35 %. Notably, during supersonic flight, cooperation between the turbine and the ejector-rocket outperforms the standalone turbine operating with afterburner, with thrust gain exceeding specific impulse loss. Therefore, the activation of ejector-rocket should be avoided once the thrust of turbine engine suffices. In addition, it is better for the ejector-rocket to operate at a large propellant mass flow rate and delay activation until a faster flight speed during the transonic phase. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, X.; Hu, X.; Wang, E.; Zhou, W.
Title: Longitudinal flight performance improvement strategy for high-altitude long-endurance flexible aircraft: mass-actuated aeroelastic control
Abstract: For the high-altitude long-endurance (HALE) flexible aircraft, aeroelastic design and control have an important impact on enhancing the flight stability and control performance. In this study, a novel longitudinal flight performance improvement concept is proposed by employing mass-actuated aeroelastic control (MAAC) strategy, along with the mathematical modeling, performance analysis and control verification. Considering the couplings among longitudinal flight dynamics, structural dynamics, aerodynamic loads, and mass motion, an integrated mathematical model is firstly established for the HALE flexible aircraft with MAAC. Steady-state analyses reveal that the trimming thrust and elevator deflection can be reduced through MAAC, and the critical stable speed can also be adjusted without introducing drag penalty. Meanwhile, the control authority and variation tendencies are endowed with designable space by the variable spanwise and chordwise position of the mass. Integrating the mass-actuated aeroelastic wing, an auxiliary longitudinal control framework of the HALE flexible aircraft is finally established, which includes altitude control, wing deformation control, and stability augmentation control. With the proposed MAAC, simulation results show that lower energy consumption, smooth wing deflection control, and an extended flight envelope of the HALE flexible aircraft are achieved. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xu, A.; Wan, Z.; Wang, X.; Zhang, X.; Yang, C.
Title: Helicopter power and vibration reduction by segment control of active twist rotor
Abstract: Active twist rotor shows promising prospects in reducing rotor vibration and enhancing flight performance. However, current researches often concentrate on either power or vibration as a single objective, lacking in comprehensive optimization studies. To address this issue, this study first establishes a nonlinear aeroelastic analysis model capable of helicopter rotor power and vibration prediction, which is validated using the flight data of UH-60A. A multi-objective genetic algorithm assisted by a random-forests local surrogate model is employed for optimization. The Pareto frontiers and control schemes of the segment twist control are obtained. The mechanism of power and vibration reduction is studied through the distribution of aerodynamic loads and lift-to-moment ratio. In heavy load and high-speed flight conditions, active twist control can achieve better optimization results. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Hu, Y.-L.; Li, K.; Bao, L.; Liu, H.-B.
Title: Capturability analysis of three-dimensional true proportional navigation guidance law against arbitrarily maneuvering target under maneuverability limitation
Abstract: This paper investigates the capturability of a classical three-dimensional (3D) true-proportional-navigation (TPN) guidance law for an interceptor with limited maneuverability against an arbitrarily maneuvering target. First, for a maneuvering target with acceleration perpendicular to the line of sight and an unknown magnitude, a reasonable range for the navigation gain of this guidance law is derived using an inequality analysis. This ensures that the guidance command remains below the interceptor’s maximum allowable acceleration throughout the interception process. Subsequently, based on previous results for the capture region of 3D TPN against arbitrarily maneuvering targets, the interceptor’s acceleration constraints are incorporated. The resulting capture region is expressed in the form of a set of inequalities. Finally, numerical simulations validates the correctness and effectiveness of the theoretical results. © © 2025. Authors: Fan, Z.; Du, J.; LIU, Y.; Ba, D.; Xu, X.; Zhang, M.; Zhu, Z.
Title: Instability warning method via fast wavelet transform in a high-speed axial flow compressor: from surge precursors to early warning
Abstract: Instability warning was originally proposed to ensure the safe operation of compressor in engines by detecting the inception signal. In recent times, attention has been shifted to the irregularity of blade passing signatures to detect the early disturbances on the basis of understanding the instability mechanism. Taking into account the more serious consequences and less research of surge, the aim of this paper is to investigate the surge phenomenon, mechanism and warning methods in a high-speed two-stage compressor. Unsteady pressure is measured on the casing wall by using a cluster of time-resolved pressure transducers with axial and circumferential spatial resolution. The time and frequency analysis of unsteady signal demonstrates that deep surge with full passages backflow occurs at 100% design speed, while at 80% design speed, a classic surge with partial passage backflow is observed. Initially, inception disturbances develop gradually, forming rotating stall cells that subsequently trigger surge. During the surge process, the structure of shock wave is disrupted. Based on the disturbance distribution, the ideal location of transducer installation for warning is near the leading edge. Then, a warning strategy based on fast wavelet transform has been developed. By extracting features from the characteristic frequency bands, early instability warning is successfully achieved. This method has been further validated in a 1.5-stage transonic compressor, contributing to the prevention of surge occurrence and enhancing the robustness of anti-surge control systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Dashora, G.; Unnikrishnan, S.; Kumar, R.
Author full names: Dashora, Gargi (59139134800); Unnikrishnan, Sasidharan Nair (57111907300); Kumar, Rajan (57215215553)
Title: Effects of inflow boundary layers on performance and linear dynamics of supersonic inlets
Abstract: The role of inlets in ramjet engines is to decelerate and compress the incoming flow while maintaining appropriate mass flow rates into the engine. The present computational work aims to understand the impact of incoming boundary layer characteristics on the performance of a Mach 3 mixed compression inlet. The mean flow features and performance metrics of the inlet are evaluated using Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) simulations, while critical dynamics are estimated using a linear instability analysis. In the case of a thicker boundary layer inlet design, referred to here as an impacted inlet, compression waves over the external ramp shift upstream, losing the shock-on-lip condition and reducing engine mass flow rate. The impacted inlets display an upstream shifted separation bubble in the shock-wave/boundary layer interaction (SBLI) zone. The thicker boundary layer also results in an upstream shift in the separation and reattachment shock trains within the isolator, which plays a central role in the internal compression process, thereby reducing total pressure recovery. Linear stability analysis identifies the separation shock train residing in the most unstable zone in the baseline inlet. The flow fields of the impacted inlets are further destabilized through aggressive instabilities that appear in the reattachment shock train. In addition, thicker boundary layers amplify instabilities in the cowl SBLI zone, including Kelvin-Helmholtz and bubble instabilities. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, Z.; Liao, W.; Deng, H.; Yuan, Y.
Author full names: Zhang, Zhiyuan (57695496300); Liao, Wenhe (57199719900); Deng, Hanyu (56065864600); Yuan, Yichao (60178145700)
Title: Effect of oxidizer injection orifice arrangement on mixing and combustion characteristic in vortex flow pancake hybrid rocket motor
Abstract: The interaction mechanism between flow process and mixing combustion characteristics in vortex flow pancake hybrid rocket motor have been investigated through large eddy simulation based on a pressure-based solver in OpenFoam, which considers the heat transfer process between the solid fuel and combustion flow field in the motor. On this basis, the effect of six different oxidizer injection orifice arrangements on mixing and combustion characteristics are systematically explored and analyzed. The results indicate that the tangential injection of the oxidizer can create the swirling flow field with strong mixing properties. A series of large-scale vortex structures are formed with high turbulence properties, which effectively promotes the entrainment and mixing of pyrolysis fuel gas and oxidizer in combustion chamber. Compared to series arrangement of oxidizer injection orifice, parallel arrangement schemes exhibit better mixing characteristics with more intense vortices and higher turbulence kinetic energy. A larger distribution of high-temperature regions and lower mass fractions of unconsumed propellent are observed in combustion chamber. Among three different parallel arrangement schemes, parallel counter-rotating arrangement configuration exhibits the best mixing and combustion characteristics, and shows an increasing trend as the distance between two oxidizer injection orifice layers increases. At last, firing tests of vortex flow pancake hybrid thruster have been carried out for all cases, while the feasibility of the solver is also verified. Consistent with the flow field characteristics, the thruster with the parallel counter-rotating oxidizer injection scheme exhibits the best performance, achieving the combustion efficiency of 97.7%. In addition, pressure fluctuations during the stable operation period are significantly reduced under this configuration. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xu, W.; Liu, J.; Hou, M.; Tan, L.
Title: Coordinated intelligent control of morphing vehicles based on online correction intelligent identification
Abstract: To enhance the flight performance of morphing vehicles, this paper proposes a novel coordinated intelligent control (CIC) framework based on online correction intelligent identification (OCII). First, to address the challenges posed by the morphing and time-varying characteristics of the morphing vehicle, a novel time-varying parameter identification method, OCII, is proposed. The OCII algorithm first uses a neural network trained with offline data to obtain rough estimates, then employs online data to estimate the scaling factors to correct the neural network estimates, thereby achieving effective identification of time-varying parameters through online correction. Then, for the morphing system, a real-time differential evolution morphing (DEM) controller is designed based on the parameter identification results to fully leverage the morphing advantages of the vehicle. For the attitude control system, an improved robust block dynamic surface (IRBDS) controller is designed, integrating high-order power terms and nonlinear filters to enhance the control performance. The IRBDS controller is based on parameter identification results and introduces robust terms, endowing itself with exceptional robustness. Finally, the effectiveness and advantages of the CIC framework consisting of the OCII, DEM, and IRBDS algorithms are verified through simulations. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Qu, Z.; Luo, F.; Li, X.; Chen, W.; Long, Y.; Yang, F.; Wei, B.
Title: Flowpath performance improvement and evaluation of hydrogen-fueled scramjet with inlet pre-injection
Abstract: For high-Mach-number scramjet engines, inlet fuel pre-injection has shown practical potential as an effective strategy to address challenges in fuel–air mixing and combustion organization. This technique also offers the potential for wall thermal protection and drag reduction. Based on a previously developed rapid engine performance analysis model and baseline engine parameters, a hydrogen pre-injection scramjet is designed for a specific freestream condition. A set of three-dimensional Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes numerical simulations is conducted to investigate the internal flow characteristics of the engine with inlet pre-injection under over-rated operating conditions, and a comparative analysis is performed against a conventional injection scheme to evaluate the film cooling and skin friction reduction performance of inlet pre-injection. The turbulence is modeled by k-ω shear stress transport ( SST ) turbulence model. Results indicate that pre-injection jets can adjust the inlet shock structure, shifting the operating condition toward the design point and reducing non-uniformity at the inlet exit. Pre-injection enhances wall thermal protection and reduces skin friction within the inlet compression region and combustor isolator, primarily via film cooling and boundary-layer combustion. In particular, the average cooling efficiency exceeds 75.0 % in the external compression region, while the wall-averaged skin friction reduction along the centerline reaches approximately 32.0 %. The enhanced fuel–air mixing enabled by pre-injection improves overall combustion efficiency along the engine flowpath, resulting in a 5.4 % increase in combustion efficiency at the combustor exit. However, a localized increase in wall temperature and skin friction is observed in the combustor expansion section. By shortening the combustor length, the engine’s specific impulse can be further improved; the optimal configuration yields a 16.3 % improvement compared to the conventional injection case. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Shi, S.; Zhang, Y.; Wang, J.; Hu, L.; Liu, Y.; Lei, B.; Chen, H.; Liang, J.
Title: Pyrolysis and ablation behavior of an innovative thermal ablation/insulation gradient composite coating structure: From numerical model to design optimization
Abstract: Thermal protection coating is the one of ideal materials for large-area ablation regions of spacecraft. A thermal ablation/insulation gradient composite (TAIGC) coating structure is presented, which integrates the advantages of thermal ablation and insulation coatings. In this study, a multi-stage pyrolysis kinetics model was developed to investigate the pyrolysis characteristics based on the thermal analysis kinetics equations. The ablation and thermal protection mechanisms were revealed by analyzing the microstructural evolution of the TAIGC coating structure during thermal exposure tests. Based upon the principles of mass and energy conservation, a coupled ablation and heat transfer model with multi-stage pyrolysis and gas diffusion mechanisms was proposed. Based on the comparison of the temperature–time curves from the simulations and the experimental data, the proposed model could satisfactorily predict the pyrolysis and ablation response of the TAIGC coating structure undergoing ablation. Furthermore, the redesign and optimization of the TAIGC coating structure were conducted using the proposed model. This work can provide a theoretical basis for the high-fidelity design and ablation performance evaluation of multilayer thermal protection coating structures. © © 2025. Authors: Yu, T.; Chen, Q.
Title: Direct numerical simulation of shock strengths effects on multi-component mixing layers
Abstract: This study investigates the effects of varying shock wave strengths on the development of a component-asymmetric turbulent mixing layer at high Mach numbers using Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS), with a focus on analyzing the mechanism of shock-induced mixing enhancement. The simulated four cases involve spatially developing mixing layers formed by the high-speed mixing of H₂/N₂ and O₂/N₂ gas streams. Shock waves are introduced in three cases via wall deflection angles of 5°, 10°, and 15°, corresponding to oblique shocks of varying intensities. The study first examines how shock intensity influences the structural evolution of the mixing layer and the distribution of vorticity. The results indicate that greater shock intensity results in stronger baroclinic vorticity generation within the mixing layer, more intense perturbations along the shear interface, earlier disintegration of large-scale vortex structures, and a marked enhancement of turbulent mixing. Secondly, analysis of mixing efficiency shows that the presence of shocks significantly increases the mixing rate, with the strongest shock producing the highest efficiency—substantially exceeding that under shock-free conditions. Furthermore, the distribution of Reynolds stress suggests that shock-induced momentum injection intensifies local turbulence, with this effect positively correlated with shock strength. Notably, under strong shock conditions, the mixing layer exhibits pronounced turbulence enhancement and structural reorganization. In addition, by compiling key parameters under four shock conditions, a quantitative correlation between shock strength and mixing performance is established, providing a valuable reference for optimizing mixing in scramjet combustors for aerospace propulsion. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Du, Y.; Yang, L.; Shu, C.; Xiao, Y.; Song, Y.
Title: Wall stress model-based immersed boundary method for simulating incompressible turbulent flows around airfoils at high Reynolds numbers
Abstract: In this work, wall stress models are incorporated into the local virtual body-fitted grid-based immersed boundary method for simulating incompressible turbulent flows around airfoils at high Reynolds numbers ( Re > 10 6 ). The proposed method generates a local virtual body-fitted grid along the immersed boundary according to the distribution of Lagrangian points, and constructs a reference layer consisting of a series of reference points outside the virtual grid to implement the wall models. Both the equilibrium and parameterized non-equilibrium wall models are considered in the present work. The wall shear stress, calculated by wall models, is used to reconstruct the velocity at virtual points, which is then projected onto the underlying Eulerian mesh to update the flow field. Numerical simulations of turbulent flows around a NACA0012 airfoil, a NACA23012 airfoil, and a 30P30N three-element airfoil are conducted to assess the performance of the present method. The results demonstrate its capability to accurately capture turbulent flow features around complex geometries at high Reynolds numbers. In addition, the use of a local virtual body-fitted grid ensures a smooth flow field near the boundary, free of spurious oscillations. These advantages highlight the potential of the proposed method as an effective tool for aeronautical applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Fiore, M.; Barbato, V.; Bianchi, D.; Nasuti, F.
Author full names: Fiore, Matteo (57729643400); Barbato, Vincenzo (58902310900); Bianchi, Daniele (22952820200); Nasuti, Francesco (6701727901)
Title: Impact of module design on the performance of clustered aerospike nozzles for upper stage applications
Abstract: Annular aerospike nozzles offer a promising solution for upper-stage propulsion, combining compact design with the ability to achieve high expansion ratios within constrained envelope dimensions. Although the ring-shaped configurations deliver excellent theoretical performance, they also present significant challenges, such as narrow throat gaps and severe thermal loads. These issues can be mitigated by adopting clustered modular designs. However, using conventional bell-shaped modules may result in poor clustering efficiency. This is especially true for upper stage applications, where the high targeted expansion ratio may require an impractically large number of nozzles, compromising the feasibility of the system. Alternative module geometries are therefore explored in an effort to approximate the ring-shaped exit cross-sections of annular aerospikes, with the aim of improving the trade-off between single-module performance, clustering efficiency, and the total number of modules required. CFD simulations are performed for each geometry, considering both gapless arrangements and configurations with a fixed number of modules. Results indicate that a tailored geometry, characterized by a ring sector with rounded edges as the module exit cross-section, provides an interesting compromise, achieving performance comparable to the annular reference design. A comparison with conventional bell nozzles across a range of expansion ratios representative of upper-stage conditions confirms the potential gain in specific impulse, quantifying the advantages of the proposed configurations. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Xie, J.-B.; Liu, Z.-J.; Huang, H.-X.; Xu, Y.-Y.; Qin, Y.; Tan, H.; Qi, W.-C.
Title: Internal flow interaction between three parallel modules within a hypersonic inlet/isolator
Abstract: To uncover the flow interaction between adjacent channels when a single or double engine fails for multiple parallel engines, the asynchronous throttling of three parallel modules within inlet/isolator has been experimentally studied under a freestream Mach number of 6.0. The results show that excessive blockage in any single channel negatively affects the other channels, resulting in elevated pressure and intensified fluctuations. When one of the side channels is choked, the interaction intensity along the lateral direction gradually decreases. The spillage effect line shows a sweep backward trend, and as the area ratio decreases, the backward sweep angle also gradually decreases. Once the middle channel is choked, it causes interaction in the same synchronization and amplitude to the two side channels, and the effect on both side channels is basically the same. The spillage effect line presents a conical shape. Under bilateral throttling, transverse pressure gradients divert part of the choked flow into unthrottled channels, while the remainder propagates upstream and discharges through the bleed chamber. Throttling side channels generates higher pressure-rise amplitudes than throttling middle/left channels. Additionally, throttling influence length is defined: the influence length of the left channel exceeds that of the middle channel by 20.5 %, indicating less pronounced throttling effects in the middle channel. Under bilateral throttling, the influence length of side channels is 28.6 % longer than that observed during left/middle channel throttling. The pre-compression effect generated by lateral overflow in the near-wall region alters the upstream conditions at the inlet of an unthrottled channel, thereby constituting the primary mechanism for multi-channel flow interaction. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Park, H.; Lim, J.; Kim, S.; Suk, J.
Author full names: Park, Heetae (58897049000); Lim, Junhyun (57994657400); Kim, Seungkeun (8447576900); Suk, Jinyoung (7005601039)
Title: Control effectiveness estimation via null-space excitation in tiltrotor transition maneuvers
Abstract: This paper tackles the challenge of achieving a stable transition in tiltrotor aircraft, where control effectiveness varies significantly due to shifting flight regimes. We present a transition control framework that combines nonlinear dynamic inversion with online estimation of the control effectiveness matrix using recursive least squares. To design the transition control law, a trim analysis is first conducted to characterize feasible flight conditions, and a reference transition path is determined based on the resulting trim envelope. The control law is formulated to regulate the velocity along the direction of the tilting rotor and the angular rates about all three body axes. A quadratic programming-based control allocation scheme is employed to distribute control forces and moments, accounting for variations in actuator effectiveness and availability across flight modes. To facilitate reliable parameter identification without interfering with the nominal control allocation, a null-space input excitation strategy is incorporated into the allocation framework. Simulation results demonstrate that the presented approach enables stable forward and backward transition maneuvers, with real-time estimation of control effectiveness initialized from nominal values, without requiring full prior knowledge. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, E.; Zhao, Y.; Liu, B.; Cao, T.; Li, S.; Yao, J.; Xu, Y.
Title: A configuration design method for truss-deployable antenna mechanisms with non-uniform-height unit nodes
Abstract: Truss-deployable antennas represent a pivotal technology for large-aperture parabolic reflectors in satellite networks. Mainstream tetrahedral units including 3RR-RRR and 3RR-URU configurations have been successfully deployed on numerous domestic and international satellites. To overcome the technical challenges of intersecting coplanar rotation axes that impede deployment and the inherent conflict between single-DOF operation and complete stowage capability, this research introduces an innovative parabolic reflector support mechanism utilizing non-uniform-height nodal design. A groundbreaking centrosymmetric quadrilateral surface division methodology has been developed, integrated with conventional triangular division techniques, enabling the construction of large-aperture truss-deployable parabolic reflectors using pentahedral and tetrahedral unit configurations. Comprehensive simulations validate the mechanism's single-DOF characteristics and deployment kinematics, demonstrating significant advantages over existing truss-deployable antennas through single-DOF operation, enhanced folding ratio, structural simplicity, reduced joint variety, and superior surface reliability. Prototype testing further confirms the mechanism's configuration compactness and deployment reliability, establishing a novel technical pathway for next-generation ultra-large-aperture space-deployable antenna systems. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yang, X.; Song, H.; Liu, M.; Rao, J.
Title: Temperature non-uniformity monitoring and performance evaluation for aviation plate-fin heat exchangers based on fiber bragg grating arrays
Abstract: As a core component of aircraft thermal management systems, the plate-fin heat exchanger (PFHE) plays a decisive role in determining the system’s thermal safety limits. Local temperature non-uniformity within PFHEs is a key factor that degrades heat transfer efficiency. To overcome limitations of traditional performance evaluation models and localized thermal monitoring techniques, this study develops a comprehensive evaluation model incorporating non-uniformity parameters and implements a localized temperature monitoring system using fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensing arrays. Twenty-four compact, vibration-resistant FBG sensors with fast response characteristics were embedded inside the flow channels, facilitating real-time temperature field mapping. Experiments conducted on a customized aviation thermal simulation platform detected significant temperature non-uniformity on both fluid sides, yielding coefficients of variation of 0.130 (cold side) and 0.041 (hot side). The comprehensive model quantitatively demonstrates the degradation mechanism: temperature gradients cause substantial thermal efficiency reductions of 45% and 43% on the cold and hot sides, respectively. This integrated evaluation framework and monitoring methodology offer crucial insights for guiding PFHE geometric optimization and advancing aerospace thermal management strategies. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, B.-L.; Xia, J.; Tao, W.; Shen, Z.Q.; Zhang, F.Z.; Zhang, F.
Title: Film cooling characteristics of a pintle in a thrust-controlled solid rocket motor
Abstract: To further augment the specific impulse and power output of the thrust-controlled solid rocket motor (TCSRM), the solid-propellant combustion temperature is elevated to the range of 3000–3600 K, imposing an exceptionally high thermal load and pronounced ablation on the pintle, which has remarkable effect on the reliability of the TCSRM. It is essential to develop efficient cooling approach for pintle. In this study, film cooling is implemented for pintle thermal protection, and a comprehensive analysis is conducted to elucidate its underlying mechanism and its superiority over the uncooled baseline in enhancing pintle thermal endurance. The results show that relative to no-film-cooling configuration, pintle film cooling provides favourable coolant coverage upstream of the throat, thereby shielding the pintle from the hot core flow. In addition, as the blowing ratio increases, the overall film-cooling-effectiveness increases. Besides that, with an enhancement in the pintle phase, the cooling performance upstream of the throat region varies negligibly, while the overall film-cooling- effectiveness increases. Moreover, the cooling performance comparison among different coolants indicates that the helium shows the best overall film-cooling performance, followed by the nitrogen, and carbon dioxide. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yuan, H.; Wu, J.; Kang, G.
Author full names: Yuan, Haoran (59235448800); Wu, Junfeng (55559415900); Kang, Guohua (9235896300)
Title: Robust spatial stereo-distributed feature network with an orientation-based soft classification for the spacecraft pose estimation
Abstract: Accurate 6-DoF pose estimation of spacecraft remains a critical challenge under extreme lighting conditions and strict computational constraints, and nonlinear geometric transformations in space applications. To address this, we propose the Spatial Stereo-Distributed Feature Network (SSFN), an end-to-end deep learning framework that combines orientation-aware soft classification with spatial feature fusion for robust and precise pose estimation using a lightweight monocular camera. The method operates without prior geometric models or stereo vision setups. At its core, a Keypoint Detection Network (KDN) based on a CSPNet backbone employs multi-task learning to predict 2D keypoints with associated uncertainty measures, reinforced by coplanarity constraints to improve geometric consistency. By incorporating virtual ray parameterization and depth-agnostic scaling, the framework achieves camera-parameterized 3D reconstruction and fuses geometric and appearance features, enabling effective handling of non-coplanar keypoints and significant depth variations without explicit depth input. For orientation estimation, a probabilistic soft classification module discretizes continuous orientations into Gaussian-distributed bins. This representation is aggregated via VLAD and refined through weighted least-squares optimization, producing quaternion outputs with enhanced smoothness and robustness to noise. Extensive evaluations on SPEED, URSO, and LINEMOD datasets, supported by semi-physical simulations, demonstrate SSFN’s state-of-the-art performance: it achieves mean translation and rotation errors of 0.0045 and 0.0096 on SPEED, a localization error of 0.47 and angular error of 5.6° on the challenging URSO Soyuz_hard sequence, and 98.76 % accuracy under a 5 cm/5° threshold on LINEMOD. These results confirm SSFN’s efficacy in complex illumination and depth-varying scenarios, underscoring its potential as a reliable vision-based solution for autonomous spacecraft navigation. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Pirolley, M.; Couturier, R.; Salomon, M.; Ambert, F.
Author full names: Pirolley, Melvyn (60177753700); Couturier, Raphaël (8655841300); Salomon, Michel (9736770200); Ambert, Fabrice (55580956100)
Title: Machine learning techniques for detecting anomalies in ADS-B data in the context of low altitude aviation
Abstract: Today, the rise of simultaneously flying aircraft increases the constraints on air control systems. As air control systems are essential, they could be targeted by cyberattacks. The Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast (ADS-B) protocol enables the real-time tracking of aircraft. However, it is known for having serious cybersecurity issues. This is why finding a solution to secure ADS-B is important. This work focuses on the use of machine learning algorithms to detect or correct anomalies in ADS-B time series for low-altitude traffic. A set of four specific algorithms has been developed to face four realistic attack scenarios based on: spoofing, saturation, replay, and interpolated ghosts. The novelty of this work lies in the inclusion of realistic attack scenarios, where detection uses the context of ADS-B time series to enhance common machine learning algorithms. Spoofing attacks are detected using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifier that identifies incoherent trajectory shapes based on the aircraft type. Saturations are corrected with a Long-Short Term Memory model (LSTM) that verifies the normality of a trajectory by forecasting the future aircraft position. Replays are detected using a hash algorithm. Finally, interpolated ghosts are detected by a classifier trained to recognise overly smooth trajectories. This work has been packaged in a Python library easily usable to ensure anomaly detection in ADS-B data on any source. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, T.; Shan, Y.; Shi, Y.; Xu, G.; Cheng, L.; Hu, Z.
Title: Efficient ASM/CFD/CSD coupling method for helicopter numerical virtual flight simulation
Abstract: Numerical simulation of free-flight helicopter behavior poses a challenge for computational fluid dynamics (CFD). This study proposes an aeroelastic-motion coupled method to balance accuracy and efficiency in helicopter numerical virtual flight (NVF) simulations. In the aeroelastic calculation, an advanced actuator surface model (ASM) based on anisotropic three-dimensional Gaussian kernel is adopted. The influence of aerodynamic forces on the flowfield is taken into account by adding a momentum source term to the governing equations, avoiding the cost of body-fitted blade grids. A loose coupling strategy exchanges airloads and deformation between CFD and computational structural dynamics (CSD) solvers each revolution. A robust overset assembly technique enables six-degree-of-freedom motion. For the UH-60A Black Hawk helicopter, simulations were first conducted for hover and steady level-flight conditions: (1) low-speed blade–vortex interaction, (2) high-speed flight with negative advancing side lift, and (3) high-thrust flight with dynamic stall. The results were compared against test data from the UH-60A Airloads Program. Subsequently, an NVF simulation of a UTTAS pull-up maneuver was performed. The time histories of vertical loads on the rotor and fuselage showed strong agreement with test data, with peak overload factor error below 3.7%. In terms of efficiency, the coupling method demonstrated better performance than fully blade-resolved simulations, reducing computation time by 78.7%. The 9.3-second pull-up maneuver was completed in 58.2 wall-clock hours using a ten-million-cell mesh on a single GPU. The developed ASM/CFD/CSD method offers dual advantages in accuracy and efficiency, representing a significant step forward toward multi-physics NVF simulation of modern helicopters. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Carlson, K.; Renganathan, A.
Title: Multiobjective aerodynamic design optimization of the NASA common research model
Abstract: Aircraft aerodynamic design optimization must account for the varying operating conditions along the cruise segment as opposed to designing at one fixed operating condition, to arrive at more realistic designs. Conventional approaches address this by performing a “multi-point” optimization that assumes a weighted average of the objectives at a set of sub-segments along the cruise segment. We argue that since such multi-point approaches are, inevitably, biased by the specification of the weights, they can lead to sub-optimal designs. Instead, we propose to optimize the aircraft design at multiple sub-segments simultaneously – that is, via multiobjective optimization that leads to a set of Pareto optimal solutions. However, existing work in multiobjective optimization suffers from (i) lack of sample efficiency (that is, keeping the number of function evaluations to convergence minimal), (ii) scalability in the absence of derivative information, and (iii) the ability to generate a batch of iterates for synchronous parallel evaluations. To overcome these limitations, we apply a novel multiobjective Bayesian optimization methodology for aerodynamic design optimization that demonstrates improved sample efficiency and accuracy compared to the state of the art. Inspired by Thompson sampling, our approach leverages Gaussian process surrogates and Bayesian decision theory to generate a sequence of iterates according to the probability that they are Pareto optimal. Our approach, named batch Pareto optimal Thompson sampling ( q POTS ) 1 1 Here, q stands for selecting a batch of q iterates at every step. , demonstrates superior empirical performance on a variety of synthetic experiments as well as a 24 dimensional two-objective aerodynamic design optimization of the NASA common research model. We also provide open-source software of our methodology and experiments. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Gary, J.; McDermott, J.; Worden, K.; Hosder, S.; Viganó, D.
Author full names: Gary, Joshua (59220316500); McDermott, Josiah (60178144100); Worden, Kyle (59715414800); Hosder, Serhat (6507361155); Viganó, Davide (57189463342)
Title: A combined tomographic particle image velocimetry and numerical simulation approach for supersonic wind tunnel calibration
Abstract: Supersonic wind tunnels remain essential tools for high-speed aerodynamics research, yet the characterization of their free-stream conditions remains technically challenging and lacks standardized criteria for defining “good” flow quality. While traditional calibration methods rely on intrusive probes, recent advances in optical diagnostics offer new opportunities for non-intrusive characterization. In this work, we demonstrate a novel use of Tomographic Particle Image Velocimetry (Tomo-PIV), combined with numerical simulations, as a methodology for supersonic wind tunnel calibration. The approach is applied to the recently upgraded Missouri S&T Supersonic Wind Tunnel, where Tomo-PIV measurements reveal uniform flow with low angularity and low turbulent noise under three operating conditions. Results are corroborated by pitot probe data and CFD analysis, with agreement within 2 % across methods. These findings establish Tomo-PIV as a viable and generalizable diagnostic for the calibration of supersonic wind tunnels. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Yan, C.; Zhang, Z.; Bao, W.; Zhang, K.; Zhang, L.
Title: Collision-Free Robust Trajectory Tracking Control for Fixed-Wing UAV with Flexible Performance Constraints
Abstract: This paper investigates the safety-critical trajectory tracking for a fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) considering prescribed performance requirements, unknown disturbances, and both static and dynamic obstacles. First, a class of polynomial performance functions is designed to naturally satisfy the initial condition and achieve quantitative performance requirements. Based on this, a prescribed performance trajectory tracking controller is developed to ensure that the tracking error remains within the performance boundaries. Second, an obstacle avoidance method that couples control barrier function (CBF) theory with a predefined-time disturbance observer is proposed. This method attenuates the effect of disturbances on UAV flight within a predefined time, thereby rigorously ensuring obstacle avoidance safety and improving the disturbance rejection capability. Furthermore, within a quadratic programming framework, the prescribed-performance controller is integrated with CBFs to preserve tracking performance as much as possible while ensuring flight safety. Finally, to address potential violations of the performance boundaries during obstacle avoidance, adaptive adjustment signals are designed based on positive system theory to achieve flexible relaxation of the performance boundaries. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed scheme guarantees both obstacle avoidance safety and continuous tracking of the desired trajectory, even in the presence of unknown disturbances. Simulations validate the effectiveness and advantages of the proposed method in environments with obstacles and disturbances. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, S.; Jin, D.; Zhang, Y.; Wang, K.; Gui, X.
Title: Experimental study on stability enhancement of a novel centrifugal compressor for hydrogen recirculation in aviation proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) systems
Abstract: Conventional hydrogen recirculation in automotive PEMFCs typically relies on positive displacement compressors or ejectors. However, in high‑specific‑power applications such as aviation, where liquid hydrogen storage is preferred, displacement machines tend to be bulky and heavy, and ejectors lose effectiveness without a high‑pressure hydrogen source. In previous work, a magnetically levitated centrifugal compressor was therefore introduced as a lightweight, efficient and pulsation‑free alternative. Nevertheless, the operating range of centrifugal compressors is inherently constrained by the surge limit to protect themselves and PEMFCs. Moreover, acute variations in water‑vapor and nitrogen content significantly broaden the required operating envelope compared to fixed‑composition systems. Reconciling these conflicts forces an overly conservative surge margin to safeguard against rare extremes, yet shifts normal operation away from its peak‑efficiency sweet spot. To overcome these limitations, this study proposes a novel flow-control strategy based on blade-integrated recirculation jets to extend the surge limit of the prototype. High-frequency dynamic pressure measurements are conducted to locate critical flow regions of surge inception. Informed by these flow-physics insights, targeted structural modifications are developed and comparatively tested in helium and air. These modifications consistently improve surge margin and pressure ratio across varying gas properties and rotational speeds, with negligible impact on efficiency. The optimized configuration achieves a 40 % increase in surge margin over the baseline and enhances pressure ratio throughout the operating range. These benefits are attributed to momentum addition from steady recirculation jets, which counteract the strong streamwise adverse pressure gradient upstream of the volute tongue—the identified trigger for surge. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Li, T.; Wang, Z.; Huang, Z.; Xi, G.
Title: Aerodynamic performance of supersonic partial admission impulse turbine by quantifying the uncertainties of turbulence model
Abstract: Supersonic partial admission impulse (SPAI) turbines are critical for advanced aerospace power systems due to high thrust-to-weight ratio and compactness. However, their asymmetric supersonic admission poses serious challenges to accurate aerodynamic predictions. In numerical simulations, the Boussinesq eddy viscosity assumption in the Reynolds averaged Navier Stokes (RANS) model introduces uncertainties, which may lead to deviations in flow prediction and affect design reliability. To evaluate the aerodynamic prediction bias, this paper employs a non-uniform Reynolds stress tensor perturbation method to quantify the turbulence model uncertainty in a SPAI turbine using six specific perturbation cases. Firstly, deterministic simulations revealed key flow characteristics, including supersonic nozzle outflow, admission/non-admission region disparities, and loss mechanisms dominated by shock-induced separations and wake mixing. Then, uncertainty quantification revealed maximum deviations of 7.15 %, 4.26 %, and 2.34 % for expansion ratio, power output, and isentropic efficiency, respectively, across all perturbation cases, with the largest efficiency increase of 3.31 % in the 1C_min perturbation case. Flow sensitivity was highest in the rotor transition zones, admission regions, and nozzle wake regions, where perturbations altered shock-induced separation positions by up to 13 % of the chord length. Symmetric nozzle arrangements reduced lateral forces and overturning moments by over 99 % in each perturbation case but increased losses due to expanded wakes, demonstrating nozzle spacing dominates efficiency trends over turbulence uncertainty. This paper quantifies the uncertainty of the SST model and constructs an uncertainty prediction interval for the numerical simulations of the SPAI turbine, providing crucial support for high-fidelity turbine design. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Akbal, Ö.; Kaya, M.O.; Şahin, A.D.
Author full names: Akbal, Omer (59429134300); Kaya, Metin Orhan (57125510600); Şahin, Ahmet Duran (7202605017)
Title: An integrated CFD-based reduced-order model for rapid prediction of in-flight ice accretion and aerodynamic degradation on the NACA 0012 airfoil
Abstract: Ice accretion on aircraft surfaces can degrade aerodynamic performance and may lead to unsafe flight conditions. Although high-fidelity Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations are accurate, their computational cost limits rapid prediction and real-time decision-making. This study introduces a modular Reduced-Order Modeling (ROM) framework for the NACA 0012 airfoil that combines aerodynamic prediction, ice type classification, ice shape generation, and performance loss estimation. An ice-shape redistribution procedure is applied to standardize the ice geometry dataset for consistent ROM training. Separate proper orthogonal decomposition models for rime and glaze ice improve shape prediction, with notable gains for complex glaze formations. Targeted sampling expanded the dataset from 100 to 270 cases, reducing glaze model error by 32.8% and increasing the R² for drag prediction from 0.832 to 0.920 and for moment from 0.833 to 0.948. Rime ice and baseline aerodynamic prediction models achieved consistently low errors across lift, drag, and moment. Validation on four representative cases—rime ice, glaze ice with single and double horns, and a glaze-to-no-ice transition—showed that the ice classifier achieved 97.1% accuracy. Aerodynamic and ice accretion characteristics were also well reproduced. Computational cost analysis indicates that, while the offline model generation phase is resource-intensive, the online prediction stage completes in less than one second per case instead of 240 minutes, providing a speed-up of over three orders of magnitude compared to CFD. These results demonstrate the capability of the proposed framework for parametric studies and highlight its potential usage in preliminary design for assessing icing-induced aerodynamic degradation. © © 2025. Authors: Shekhar Pal, R.; Kumar, S.; Mukherjee, D.
Author full names: Shekhar Pal, Rajib (60172856700); Kumar, Shashi Ranjan (57197067742); Mukherjee, Dwaipayan (55489814600)
Title: Cooperative salvo guidance over leader-Follower network with free-Will arbitrary time convergence
Abstract: A cooperative salvo strategy is proposed in this paper which achieves consensus among the interceptors within a pre-defined arbitrary settling time. Considering non-linear engagement kinematics and a system lag to capture the effect of interceptor autopilot as present in realistic interception scenarios, the guidance schemes use the time-to-go estimates of the interceptors in order to achieve simultaneous interception of a stationary target at a pre-determined impact time. The guidance scheme ensures that consensus among the time-to-go estimates of the interceptors is achieved within a settling time whose upper bound can be pre-specified arbitrarily independent of the initial conditions or design parameters. The efficacy of the proposed guidance strategy is demonstrated using numerical simulations with varied conditions of initial position, velocities and heading angle errors of the interceptors as well as different desired impact times. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.
