Authors: Wang, Y.; Zheng, J.; Jin, Y.; Pang, Z.; Zhang, D.
Author full names: Wang, Yajuan (58757791400); Zheng, Jianhua (57871568100); Jin, Yang (57188854280); Pang, Zengyuan (55540343100); Zhang, Diantang (59973328700)
Title: Zero Poisson's ratio biomimetic jellyfish honeycomb structure for large deformation wing
Abstract: Honeycomb structure skin provides the possibility of wing deformation for adaptive aircraft. However, it still remain some challenges to overcome the problems of large deformation, high recovery and high load-bearing capacity at the same time. This work presented advanced bionic jellyfish honeycomb structures with exceptional large deformation and recoverable properties. Short-cut carbon fiber reinforced polyether ether ketone (CF/PEEK) was selected as the raw material, and the bionic jellyfish honeycomb structures with different cell element radii (R) and honeycomb wall thicknesses (t) was designed and fabricated using fused deposition 3D printing. Subsequently, the in-plane deformation and out-of-plane deformation behaviors were analyzed using a combination of quasi-static tensile tests and numerical simulations. In addition, its fracture damage morphology was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The results showed that this new structure achieved zero Poisson's ratio (ZPR) and 100% excellent deformation capacity with good load-bearing capacity. Further, it had remarkable recoverable properties, and the length of Mc-t0.6 increased by only 1.93% after 200 stretching cycles. Surprisingly, the bionic jellyfish's contraction-expansion motion achieved lower energy deformation through a combined mechanism of arc unfolding and hinge rotation, resulting in extraordinary deformation properties. © © 2025. Published by Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ru, Y.; Wang, F.; Wang, M.; Grüninger, M.; Saleem, K.
Author full names: Ru, Yi (57195546614); Wang, Fushuai (57354336200); Wang, Mengxia (60173444600); Grüninger, Michael (7003469467); Saleem, Kashif (24766676700)
Title: Nonlinear aerodynamic analyses of rotary composite tapered bladed disks (blisks) under subsonic and supersonic flow conditions
Abstract: Bladed disks (blisks) are being used more and more in the modern aerospace propulsion system because they tend to be lightweight, have higher efficiency, and greater structural integrity compared to traditional blade–disk assemblies. The current study examines the nonlinear aerodynamic response of a composite tapered rotary blisk in subsonic and supersonic flow conditions on the macro scale. The rotary blisk is modeled as a three-directional functionally graded material (TD-FGM) made of aluminum oxide and stainless steel with gradual changes in the radial, circumferential, and thickness directions. The effects of tapered geometry and porosity are incorporated to represent more realistic operational conditions under aerodynamic loading. To investigate the nonlinear vibrational features and flutter stability boundaries, numerical simulations are performed using both differential quadrature (2D-DQ) and finite element (FE) methods. The validation with benchmark data shows the dependability of the proposed computational framework. The parametric study indicated that increasing the grading intensity in the TD-FGM dramatically reduced nonlinear natural frequencies, particularly when airflow pressure was held constant. The findings from this study provide guidance toward the design of future blisks, with suggestions on methods to promote flutter resistance and enhance performance in emerging aerospace applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Duan, X.; Wang, X.; Luo, C.; Li, H.; Sun, S.; Li, L.
Title: Influence of structural parameters on the cooling and creep performance of double-wall turbine blade considering surface curvature
Abstract: In this study, a comprehensive numerical investigation is conducted to assess the influence of curvature radius on the thermal-mechanical performance of double-wall turbine blade structures. A coupled conjugate heat transfer computational fluid dynamics (CFD) and structural analysis approach is employed, where temperature fields obtained from computational fluid dynamics simulations are interpolated into structural models for creep behavior analysis. Four geometric parameters are optimized using a four-factor, five-level orthogonal design independently at each of four curvature radii ranging from 10mm to 60mm. The results demonstrate that increasing curvature radius promotes smoother geometric transitions and enhanced structural deformation coordination, thereby reducing stress concentrations and improving cooling effectiveness. Multi-objective optimization using response surface methodology achieves simultaneous minimization of structural stresses and maximization of cooling performance. The optimized configurations yield improvements including up to 10.59% enhancement in cooling effectiveness, 14.86% reduction in von Mises equivalent stress, 18.52% decrease in creep strain, and 14.38% reduction in maximum shear stress. This research establishes a high-fidelity multidisciplinary optimization framework that systematically incorporates surface curvature effects, providing practical guidelines for balancing aerodynamic, thermal, and structural requirements in double-wall turbine blade configurations and offering theoretical support for design strategies aiming to enhance blade longevity under extreme operating conditions. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Pourtakdoust, S.H.; Khodabakhsh, A.H.
Author full names: Pourtakdoust, Seid H. (55953294600); Khodabakhsh, Amir H. (57776017300)
Title: A deep learning density shaping model predictive gust load alleviation control of a compliant wing subjected to atmospheric turbulence
Abstract: This study presents a novel deep learning approach aimed at enhancing stochastic Gust Load Alleviation (GLA) specifically for compliant wings. The approach incorporates the concept of smooth wing camber variation, where the camber of the wing’s chord is actively adjusted during flight using a control signal to achieve the desired aerodynamic loading. The proposed method employs a deep-learning-based model predictive controller designed for probability density shaping. This controller effectively solves the probability density evolution equation through a custom Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) and utilizes Automatic Differentiation for Model Predictive Control (MPC) optimization. Comprehensive numerical simulations were conducted on a compliant wing (CW) model, evaluating performance of the proposed approach against stochastic gust profiles. The evaluation involved stochastic aerodynamic loads generated from Band-Limited White Noise (BLWN) and Dryden gust models. The evaluations were conducted for two distinct Compliant Chord Fractions (CCF). The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed probability density shaping model predictive control in alleviating stochastic gust load and reducing wing tip deflection. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Xiao, Z.; Meng, Y.; Chai, P.; Chen, Y.; Ouyang, H.
Author full names: Xiao, Zhicheng (57193706057); Meng, Yiming (57390679000); Chai, Pengfei (57221633744); Chen, Yong (55881280600); Ouyang, Hua (7101920722)
Title: Modal analysis methods of rotating mistuned bladed disks using blade tip timing
Abstract: This study establishes blade tip timing (BTT)-based methodologies for experimental modal analysis of rotating mistuned bladed disks, offering fundamental insights into vibration characteristics for turbomachinery integrity assessment and digital twin development. The central challenge addressed is the high-fidelity measurement of blade vibration response spectra based on under-sampled tip timing data. To resolve this, three algorithmic solutions are proposed: two time-domain approaches, namely discrete sinusoidal fitting and continuous track fitting, and a frequency-domain approach, namely spectrum deconvolution. The methodologies are validated rigorously by experiments where an integrally bladed rotor is operated in a vacuum environment and excited by piezoelectric actuators. Frequency response functions of both flexural (1F) and torsional (1T) mode families are measured. Comparison with strain gauge measurements demonstrates exceptional consistency with correlation coefficients up to 0.97. The time efficiency of the spectrum deconvolution method is revealed to be significantly higher than the time-domain approaches for a weakly damped system, confirming alignment with theoretical predictions. Mistuned modal parameters are identified through cross-validation of stabilization diagrams and complex mode indicator functions at various rotational speeds. BTT datasets enable more comprehensive identification of system modes by simultaneously monitoring the response of the whole cascade. Reconstructed FRFs using extracted parameters show mostly less than 8 % deviation from measurements. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Zhang, M.; Zhou, S.; Lei, X.; Dong, F.; Zhao, Z.; Lv, X.; Liu, H.
Title: Atomization dynamics of a converge-end gas-centered swirl coaxial injector
Abstract: Gas-centered swirl coaxial (GCSC) injectors are widely used in high-performance liquid rocket engines, making accurate atomization modeling essential. This study employs an enhanced compressible Eulerian-Lagrangian solver, CompressibleInterIso-LptFoam , to perform high-resolution simulations and experiments on liquid–gas mixing and liquid-only swirl atomization in a Converge-end GCSC injector. Results show that, compared to the conventional Open-end design, the Converge-end structure significantly improves atomization performance and suppresses self-pulsation. When gas is introduced, a counter-rotating vortex forms on the inner side of the liquid film, effectively dampening unstable waves of liquid-only swirl atomization and further promoting atomization. The liquid-gas atomization exhibits an “umbrella” shape with distinct inner and outer atomization angles; the outer angle decreases exponentially with increasing Weber number. Compared to liquid-only atomization, the droplet size in the liquid–gas mode is reduced by more than a factor of two, and the spray pattern transitions from a "hollow cone" to a more combustion-efficient "solid cone." The droplet size-axial velocity profile evolves from a “horizontal-L” to an “upright-L” shape, while the tangential–axial velocity distribution changes from a "linear" to a "petal-like" pattern. Experimental and numerical results agree closely, with a maximum deviation in atomization angle below 3°, confirming the accuracy of the numerical model. This study elucidates the atomization mechanisms of Converge-end GCSC injectors and provides a solid theoretical basis for future injector design optimization. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Aslam, N.; Masood, S.A.; Haider, B.A.; Rasul, A.
Author full names: Aslam, Nadeem (58777149600); Masood, Syed Athar (59267920100); Haider, Basharat Ali (36805990000); Rasul, Adnan (58114843100)
Title: Numerical investigation of diffuser sensitivity and test section flow in a supersonic free-jet wind tunnel
Abstract: This study presents a comprehensive numerical investigation of the influence of diffuser geometry on the nonlinear flow behavior within the test section of a supersonic free-jet wind tunnel. The research focused on flow development at the nozzle exit, pressure recovery at the diffuser outlet, and flow uniformity inside the test section. Large-scale CFD simulations were conducted in which operating conditions were kept constant whereas diffuser geometric parameters were systematically varied using design of experiments technique for an axisymmetric aerodynamic circuit. The results demonstrated that the diffuser geometry strongly governed the inherently nonlinear flow characteristics, where small geometric modifications led to disproportionate changes in pressure recovery and flow uniformity. Notably, nearly half of the simulated configurations failed to establish stable flow. The second-throat diameter and scoop angle were identified as critical parameters for initiating the flow, whereas the subsonic diffuser length was found to be the primary determinant of pressure recovery. The analysis of the test section confirmed that the jet assumes a natural conical shape, a phenomenon driven by the pressure imbalance between the jet core and the test chamber. Analytical expressions for diffuser efficiency and Mach uniformity index were introduced to quantify this behaviour. This study highlights nonlinear diffuser sensitivity as a key factor in achieving pressure recovery and flow uniformity, offering both theoretical insights and practical guidance for the design of supersonic free-jet wind tunnels. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Ren, J.; Du, J.; Xu, B.; Chen, Y.; Li, Z.
Title: Multi-degree-of-freedom reliable docking control for probe-and-drogue autonomous aerial refueling
Abstract: In probe-and-drogue refueling (PDR), it is challenging for the probe to dock with the drogue. This is due to the nonlinearity and various disturbances of the PDR system, which affect both precision and safety. This study fully considers these characteristics, focusing on achieving high-precision tracking and strong robustness against disturbances. To this end, a multi-degree-of-freedom reliable docking control method for PDR based on additive state decomposition (ASD) is proposed. The PDR docking system is initially decomposed into three subsystems of the same order using the ASD approach. Subsequently, three separate controllers are designed for these subsystems: a lyapunov-based controller, a terminal iterative learning controller, and an active disturbance rejection controller. Finally, the three individual controllers are integrated to form a comprehensive controller for the PDR docking system. By constructing a PDR docking simulation platform, it is verified that the proposed method can effectively handle complex disturbances and nonlinearities in the PDR docking system, achieving high-precision docking control. Furthermore, simulation experiments show that the control performance of the proposed docking controller is superior to that of the traditional terminal iterative learning docking controller. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Kong, L.; He, S.; Wu, P.; Cui, X.; Liu, C.
Title: Distributed state estimation with hybrid game-reinforced consensus for maneuvering target tracking
Abstract: For highly maneuverable targets, sudden changes in both the magnitude and direction of their maneuvers lead to model mismatch, causing a sharp decline or even divergence in the tracker’s performance. A CKF-Based AIMM Kalman Filtering with Hybrid Game-Reinforced consensus(HGRC-AIMMCKF) algorithm is proposed to address maneuvering target tracking. The fixed weight allocation rules of traditional consensus estimation amplify acceleration estimation errors by smoothing out abrupt acceleration changes.The proposed Hybrid Game-Reinforced Consensus (HGRC) framework addresses this problem. The framework models network agents as game players and communication weights as strategic variables. A non-cooperative utility function with cooperative constraints is formulated,and solved via the alternating direction method of multipliers to obtain Nash equilibrium-based weight allocation. This enables adaptive weight adjustment according to target maneuverability and estimation errors, with cooperative constraints ensuring global consistency and preventing local optimal. To address model mismatch induced by target high maneuverability, a maneuvering frequency adaptation law using innovation sequences is developed for the Singer model, complemented by an adaptive estimator for acceleration variance based on exponential moving average technique. This achieves self-tuning of state transition matrices and noise covariance matrices. Comprehensive numerical evaluations demonstrate statistically significant achieved by HGRC-AIMMCKF in tracking accuracy (27.7 % RMSE of acceleration reduction) and consensus convergence (41.6 % faster) compared to benchmark algorithms. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Can, E.
Title: UAV DC Motor behaviors control using new hybrid PSO-fuzzy logic with a dynamic error based optimization
Abstract: This study introduces a novel and comprehensive control architecture for DC motor speed regulation in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), leveraging a hybrid Particle Swarm Optimization–Fuzzy Logic (PSO–FL) strategy. UAV platforms inherently exhibit highly nonlinear, time-varying, and uncertain dynamics, which often challenge the efficacy of traditional control techniques such as Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) controllers and conventional fuzzy logic systems. To overcome these limitations, a dual-layer PSO–FL controller is proposed, wherein the PSO algorithm is utilized to optimize both fuzzification and defuzzification parameters, thereby enhancing controller adaptability and precision. Moreover, a Centralized Agent Optimization (CAO) framework is integrated to accelerate convergence and improve overall computational efficiency. The proposed controller dynamically adjusts membership functions and rule weights in real time, enabling robust, stable, and rapid system responses even under sudden aerodynamic perturbations and variable payload conditions. Extensive simulation and experimental validations confirm the superior performance of the proposed method over classical PID, PSO-tuned PID, and conventional fuzzy logic controllers, demonstrating faster rise times, reduced overshoot, and minimized steady-state error. These findings significantly advance the development of intelligent UAV control systems by effectively combining swarm intelligence with human-like reasoning methodologies. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Nekoo, S.R.; Sharf, I.; Arrue, B.C.; Ollero, A.
Author full names: Nekoo, Saeed Rafee (58355960200); Sharf, Inna (7004605208); Arrue, B. C. (35605607400); Ollero, Aníbal (7004325905)
Title: Momentum transfer by impact: A step towards grasping of non-cooperative space debris
Abstract: The impact between a free-floating or -flying space manipulator (SM) and an uncooperative target object is studied, for the purpose of transferring momentum from the object to the SM. The impact model employed to simulate contact between the robot end-effector and the object is discrete and impulsive, and it accounts for energy dissipation during impact by using the well-known Newton’s hypothesis and energetic’s definition of the coefficient of restitution (CoR). The resulting CoR is implemented to calculate the impact force, post-impact velocity of the object, and generalized rates of the SM after the collision. The motion of the SM is controlled before and after the collision via a nonlinear optimal approach, the so-called state-dependent Riccati equation (SDRE). The SDRE is a state-feedback controller design; however, a recent modification presented a design to apply the SDRE to the output feedback control. The output- and state-dependent Riccati equation (OSDRE) uses a transformation between the output variables and the states to bypass the kinematics of a system and control the outputs directly. This led to point-to-point motion control of SMs in free-floating mode using the base to push the end-effector towards the desired position. A combination of the OSDRE and the aforementioned impact modeling is introduced in this work to transfer the energy and momentum of a non-cooperative object in space to an SM, thus achieving a nearly stationary condition of the object, amenable to subsequent capture. The specific application directs attention to the collection of uncooperative space debris, which is a prominent topic in space robotics. A planar system case study is presented to discuss and demonstrate the application in various scenarios. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Yang, H.; Wang, C.; Lu, H.; Li, A.; Pavel, F.
Author full names: Yang, Hang (58682816400); Wang, Changqing (55684012600); Lu, Hongshi (57193195679); Li, Aijun (55751458600); Pavel, Fadeenkov (60172692100)
Title: Event-triggered sliding mode control of spinning tether system for fly-around observation
Abstract: The technology of small fly-around satellites plays an important role in observing the large spacecraft. Traditional small satellite fly-around technology suffers from contradictions between mission sustainability and observation accuracy. To address these challenges, this study introduces a novel tethered fly-around scheme based on a spinning tether system (STS), which can be used for observing large-scale structures such as space stations and defunct spacecraft. This research focuses on the parameter design and stability control of the STS during the fly-around process. First, to describe the large-scale spinning motion of the STS in space, a singularity-adjustable Lagrangian model is developed using the spinning plane coordinate system. Additionally, a flexible lumped model is created for controller validation. Second, the constraints of tether length and spinning rate are analyzed to determine the optimal parameters for fly-around mission. Third, a sliding mode controller is proposed to track the desired spinning motion of sub-satellites. Considering that the STS does not require continuous control during periodic fly-around, an event-triggered control mechanism is introduced to reduce communication consumption. Finally, a comparison of impulse consumption across various fly-around schemes is conducted. Simulation results show that the STS achieves stable fly-around configurations under the proposed control strategy. The two fly-around satellites experience reductions of 86 % and 84 % in control trigger events, respectively. Furthermore, energy consumption analysis shows that tethered schemes reduce impulse by 89.2 % in planar fly-around and 86.5 % in vertical fly-around compared to untethered schemes. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Sun, H.; Zhang, H.; Li, X.; Yuan, H.
Author full names: Sun, Hongyun (57209203514); Zhang, Hongyuan (52165064400); Li, Xinqi (60121791900); Yuan, Huiqun (7402446890)
Title: Modeling and optimization of parallel vibration reduction for multi-stage mistuned bladed disk systems in aeroengines
Abstract: This study presents a comprehensive investigation into the vibration characteristics and reduction strategies for multi-stage mistuned bladed disk systems in aeroengine applications. A refined dynamic model is established, incorporating bladed disk coupling, interstage stiffness, and mistuning perturbations, to accurately characterize both free and forced vibration behaviors. To alleviate the adverse effects of vibration localization, a novel Chaotic Dynamic Adaptive Sparrow Search Algorithm (CDASSA) is developed. By integrating chaotic mapping to enhance population diversity and combining Lévy flight with hybrid perturbation strategies, the proposed algorithm significantly improves global search efficiency and robustness, addressing common convergence limitations in traditional SSA variants. Furthermore, a parallel multi-objective optimization framework (MSCDASSA) is introduced to account for multi-stage coupling effects and enable coordinated optimization of blade arrangements across stages. Numerical simulations reveal that the optimized configurations achieve notable reductions in both maximum amplitude and response variability, with system performance approaching that of an ideally tuned reference. Monte Carlo analyses further validate the proposed algorithms' accuracy, stability, and computational efficiency, demonstrating superiority over conventional optimization techniques. Overall, this research advances the theoretical understanding of mistuned bladed disk dynamics and offers practical, high-efficiency solutions for vibration-resistant design, contributing to the improved fatigue life and structural reliability of advanced aeroengines. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Sun, J.; Zhu, H.; Li, H.; Xu, D.; Cai, G.
Title: Transient data-driven multi-objective intelligent optimization of counterflow jet configuration for reentry vehicles
Abstract: The development of supersonic vehicles is progressing toward long-endurance, high-Mach-number operation, excessive g-load tolerance, and reusability, imposing stringent demands on aerodynamic performance and thermal protection systems. Counterflow jet technology has emerged as an active and efficient approach for simultaneous drag and heat flux reduction. This investigation focuses on mechanically deployed reentry vehicles, employing transient numerical simulations to analyze counterflow jet flow fields. The study systematically categorizes the jet formation process into three distinct phases. Based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory neural networks (LSTM), a surrogate model for aerodynamic parameters (CNN-LSTM) was developed with transient aerodynamic simulation data. This hybrid architecture maintained computational accuracy while enhancing prediction efficiency compared to conventional CFD methods. To achieve a comprehensively better effect of drag and heat reduction, we adopted the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) optimization algorithm and combined it with the CNN-LSTM model to conduct multi-objective optimization design for the counterflow jet. Optimization results demonstrate that at 39.46 g total jet mass flow, the configuration achieves 39.9 % total drag reduction, 33.95 % decrease in wall heat flux integral, and 41.23 % reduction in peak heat flux density. The proposed methodology provides critical insights for engineering implementation of counterflow jet systems in supersonic applications. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Klein, M.; Dedic, C.; Goyne, C.
Author full names: Klein, Marissa Noelle (60134651200); Dedic, Chloe E. (54413928100); Goyne, Christopher P. (6602532441)
Title: Changes in optical transmission of fused silica windows in a supersonic combustion facility
Abstract: The development of hypersonic air breathing propulsion technology depends on wind tunnel testing, of which a large portion utilizes optical measurement techniques. Unfortunately, a change in transmission of fused silica windows is possible in combustion facilities with long run times. This change can affect the utility of optical diagnostics as this transmission change occurs in the spectral range of 350 to 850 nm. This can prevent or disrupt optical emission spectroscopy, particle image velocimetry, planar laser induced fluorescence, and other techniques. In this study, it was determined that the change in transmission was the result of devitrification of the window, resulting in a partial conversion of the silica from the amorphous form to cristobalite crystals. Cristobalite was detected through X-ray diffraction experiments and the amount was quantified for a used facility window. This is the first time window devitrification has been identified as the source of optical transmission degradation in a hypersonic ground testing facility. A sample of new fused silica was tested in a controlled oven environment to isolate elevated temperature as the main factor causing crystal formation. Identification of devitrification and its cause, in a hypersonic ground testing facility, will aid in the application and interpretation of future optical diagnostics and presents facility operators with potential options for mitigating it in the future. © © 2025. Authors: Shenghong, C.; Yanchu, Y.; Hangyue, Z.; Xiangqiang, Z.; Jingjing, C.; Rong, Z.; Zhu, Z.
Author full names: Shenghong, Cao (58789649400); Yanchu, Yang (58789607200); Hangyue, Zhang (60172936100); Xiangqiang, Zhang (60172936200); Jingjing, Cai (35177728000); Rong, Zhao (58789649500); Zhu, Rongchen (57214916269)
Title: Flight behavior and lifetime prediction of near space airship under system damage
Abstract: Airship technology has been progressively maturing, yet research on damage modeling and flight failure assessment remains limited. Accurately predicting the flight behavior and service life of damaged airships, and ensuring their long-endurance operation in high-altitude environments, poses considerable challenges in modern airship engineering. To address these challenges, damage models for the energy, propulsion, and envelope subsystems are developed. Additionally, comprehensive flight failure criteria are developed based on buoyancy-weight balance, energy generation-consumption balance, and thrust-drag balance. Furthermore, a predictive model for the flight behavior of damaged airships is constructed by integrating these damage models, failure criteria, and the airship’s geometric, thermodynamic, and dynamic models. This coupled model is then applied to forecast flight behavior and service life under various damage scenarios, revealing several notable phenomena: 1) Single-system damage: Helium leakage causes buoyancy-weight imbalance, photovoltaic array damage causes energy generation-consumption imbalance, and propulsion system damage causes thrust-drag imbalance. 2) Multi-system damage: Mixed damage inputs will accelerate the attenuation of service life. Meanwhile, helium leakage significantly affects vertical behavior, photovoltaic array damage will greatly impact the system's energy supply, and propulsion system damage has a larger impact on horizontal behavior. This study systematically reproduces the failure process under the coupling effect of multiple models. The model reveals the correlation between airship performance degradation and subsystems and also serves as an efficient tool for predicting service life. Based on the model developed in this study, the damage levels of subsystems can be quantitatively input to predict the flight life of airships, providing a reference for mission planning and real-time decision-making. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Chen, L.; Wang, Y.
Title: An effective local feature-augmented machine-learning optimization method for enhancing the compressible RANS model in simulating complex flows from transonic to hypersonic regimes
Abstract: Turbulence models provide the essential mathematical closures to the Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations, allowing for the prediction of complex, chaotic fluid behaviors in aeronautical and astronautical applications. However, their accuracy and universality still face significant challenges, especially for compressible flows that exhibit strong adverse pressure gradients, flow separation, or high swirl. This work presents an effective local feature-augmented machine-learning optimization method for enhancing the compressible RANS model. The present optimization framework optimizes both Reynolds stress and turbulent heat flux closures simultaneously by utilizing the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) approach and artificial neural networks (ANNs). The ANNs incorporate many key features of complex compressible flows as inputs, including shock waves, compressible intensity, and flow separation. By considering multiple flow conditions across the entire flow field, shared ANN parameters are trained and optimized with the flow field solver and sparse experimental data. In particular, joint and coupled field inversion training is performed with separated compression corner flows, covering local Mach numbers from 0 to 9.22. Since the resultant enhanced SST model only depends on physical flow features, its prediction capability can thus be ensured in diverse flow scenarios beyond the training cases as long as the flow features are similar. Extensive validations on compressible turbulence benchmarks ranging from transonic to hypersonic regimes, including various compression corners, a compressible flat plate, oblique SBLI, Onera M6 wing, and ASBLI, demonstrate that the data-assimilation-enhanced SST model outperforms the baseline SST remarkably in predicting pressure, skin friction, and heat flux distributions. It seems that the applicability of the present SST model can be effectively extended to various Mach numbers, which may shed light on the development of more reliable data-assimilated turbulence models. © © 2025. Authors: Shi, T.; Wu, W.; Zhong, J.
Author full names: Shi, Tingyi (60173267100); Wu, Wanyang (57015792600); Zhong, Jingjun (23981701500)
Title: Selection of arrangement position and rotation direction for the controlled speed casing in the low-speed three-stage axial compressor
Abstract: The controllable speed casing (CSC) represents an innovative casing treatment technology that has demonstrated effective enhancements in flow stability for individual rotors or stages in transonic compressors. This study explores the application of CSC in a low-speed three-stage axial compressor (LTAC) to address stall induced by tip blockage. By modifying the arrangement position and rotation direction of the CSC, this research assesses its impact on the stable operating margin and the initiation of stall in the compressor. Findings reveal that the tip of the second-stage rotor (R2) serves as the critical locus limiting the stable operation of the LTAC, and enhancing its flow capacity is essential for achieving stability expansion. Positioning the CSC at the second stage and implementing negative rotation (denoted as SRC-1.0) effectively inhibited the dissipation of the R2 tip leakage vortex, eliminated the resultant blockage area, significantly improved the flow capacity, and increased the stable operating margin by 48.67 %. This improvement in the R2 tip throughflow subsequently shifted the critical position restricting the stable operation of SRC-1.0 to the R3 tip. When the CSC was arranged in the third stage and also utilized negative rotation (denoted as TRC-1.0), it successfully suppressed the tip blockage of the third-stage rotor (R3). Benefiting from the inter-stage interference effect, this arrangement indirectly enhanced the flow capacity at the R2 tip, resulting in a 4.53 % increase in the stable operating margin. Despite these improvements, the R2 tip continued to be the primary location that limited the stable operation of TRC-1.0. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Liu, C.; Li, A.; Duan, G.; Hu, X.
Title: Optimal rotational speed rotor load reduction control in Coaxial High - Speed helicopter/engine integration system
Abstract: To satisfy coaxial helicopters' variable rotational speed control requirements, a flight condition feedforward integrated control strategy based on dynamic inversion is proposed. A coaxial high-speed helicopter/engine integration system is developed using the T700 turboshaft engine's high precision mathematical model. An optimal rotational speed flight strategy targeting minimum fuel flow is designed. To solve the rotor load surge caused by this strategy, a boundary protection controller using projection operator theory is proposed. Simulation results show that this control method can track optimal rotor speed, cutting fuel flow by over 8%. The boundary - protection flight controller, designed via projection operator theory, reduces upper and lower rotor loads. In low - speed flight, loads drop by over 11.8% and 12.4% respectively; in transition flight, by over 12% and 6% respectively. Thus, it effectively enables variable - speed flight control and rotor - load reduction for coaxial - helicopter integrated flight - propulsion systems. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Lu, Z.; Zhang, J.; Li, H.; Holzapfel, F.
Author full names: Lu, Zhidong (57215969717); Zhang, Jiannan (57203430473); Li, Hangxu (57216437746); Holzapfel, Florian (10641256100)
Title: A virtual command protection strategy for pseudo-inverse flight control allocation
Abstract: Over-actuated flight systems frequently encounter infeasible virtual commands outside the Attainable Moment Set (AMS), especially during aggressive maneuvers and failures. In such cases, pseudo-inverse-based control allocation can produce large allocation errors in magnitude and direction, degrading closed-loop performance. This paper proposes a dual protection mechanism for these control allocators in the dynamic inversion framework. Firstly, an ellipsoidal virtual command limiter is derived from the singular value decomposition of the control-effectiveness matrix. This limiter projects any infeasible command onto an ellipsoid that tightly approximates the AMS while preserving its original direction. Secondly, an anti-saturation feedback is designed to feed the allocation error into the reference model with a specific gain, removing persistent control allocation errors from the steady-state virtual command. Open-loop evaluations on an eVTOL model indicate that the ellipsoidal virtual command limiter effectively reduces directional allocation error, with significantly less computational complexity than online optimization. Closed-loop simulations demonstrate that the proposed protections reduce allocation error and input saturation, and improve tracking performance among coupled motion axes. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Zhu, Y.; Chen, Z.
Title: Improvement the performance of PEMFC by the cathode right-angled trapezoidal baffle for aviation applications
Abstract: Proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFC) are regarded as new and ideal energy devices in the aerospace field. However, the slow mass transfer limits the maximization of the output power. In this paper, a right-angled trapezoidal baffle structure is proposed. The influences of the number, spacing, height and bottom width of the baffles on the performance of PEMFC are studied in detail. In order to consider the impact brought by the increase in power consumption, the ratio of power consumption to output power is taken as the evaluation index M . Compared with the structure without baffles, the current density at 0.3 V can be increased by 10.68% to the greatest extent, and the peak power density is increased by 5.22% by adding baffles. The structure with 9 baffles with a depth of 0.4 mm and a bottom width of 0.5 mm shows the lowest M value which is 37.89% lower than that of base. In addition, the influence patterns of baffle parameters on performance parameters are summarized in detail, implying the number and the height of baffles are the most influential factors on the performance parameters. Moreover, the influence coefficient is established through the normalization method to uniformly compare the influence degree of different structural parameters on the performance parameters of PEMFC. Finally, through dimensionless analysis, the correlation between the performance parameters of PEMFC and the structural parameters of the baffle are established to guide the design of the baffle. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.

Authors: Wang, G.; Zhao, H.; Li, S.
Title: Three-stage training for cross-domain pose estimation of non-cooperative spacecraft
Abstract: Non-cooperative spacecraft pose estimation faces the challenge of data scarcity, which forces model training on synthetic data and generalization to real-world scenario. The synthetic-to-real domain gap has become a key bottleneck limiting performance improvement. This paper introduces a plug-and-play hierarchical training paradigm that extends curriculum learning from the dataset to the learning objective. This approach establishes a learning trajectory that guides the network to first prioritize domain-invariant geometry, then discriminative texture, and finally precise pose regression. To support this paradigm, Adaptive Cosine Annealing with Loss Feedback and Stage-Specific Gradient Manipulation are designed. Under the strict setting of using only source domain data, the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline approaches and achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods that are permitted to use target domain data, demonstrating competitive results. This work shows that generalization performance can be substantially improved by optimizing the training process alone, without increasing model complexity, offering a new perspective for related research. © 2025 Elsevier Masson SAS.
